Copenhagen Solo Travel Guide: Things to Do in Copenhagen
A guide to 24 hours in the beautiful city of Copenhagen

In June 2017, I went on my first ever solo trip—to Iceland and Denmark. While Iceland was meant to be the highlight, I had a quick layover in Copenhagen that turned into one of my most memorable city experiences. Looking back, it wasn’t just about sightseeing; it was about the feeling of freedom, discovery, and the [thrill of my first solo adventure](/post/a-solo-traveler-s-guide-to-iceland-without-a-car).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-1.jpeg)

Even though I usually prefer nature to cities, Copenhagen completely surprised me with its charm. From **Nyhavn **[Copenhagen](https://www.visitcopenhagen.com/?utm_source=) with its colorful waterfront houses to biking across cobblestone streets, this city had me hooked instantly. Maybe it was the midnight sun keeping the city alive at 11 p.m., or maybe it was just the energy of the people—it’s safe to say, this became one of my all-time favorite cities.

**(P.S. If you’ve read my Iceland blog, you’ll remember I lost all my camera photos. So, what I have here are just a few from my phone—but the memories are still golden!)**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-2.jpeg)


**Best Time to Visit Copenhagen**

I visited in June, during the summer, and I honestly think it’s the **best time to**[ visit ](https://www.visitcopenhagen.com/copenhagen/plan-your-trip/nyhavn-gdk586725?utm_source=)**Copenhagen**. The long summer days and the midnight sun make the city extra magical. Crowds gather in the main squares and harbors, and rather than being a downside, it added to the city’s lively charm.

If you prefer fewer crowds and a calmer vibe, spring and autumn are also lovely, but summer is when Copenhagen truly comes alive.


### **Top Things to Do in Copenhagen**

Whether you’re here for a day or a week, there are plenty of incredible **things to do in Copenhagen**. My short but packed itinerary covered everything from royal palaces to quirky neighborhoods, and I’ll share the highlights below.


### **1. Nyhavn Copenhagen**

This iconic spot was my first stop. Arriving early in the morning, I enjoyed the quiet before the rush of people filled the harbor. With its brightly painted 17th-century townhouses, buzzing cafes, and boats lining the canal, **Nyhavn Copenhagen** is the postcard-perfect heart of the city.

**Pro Tip:** Take a canal cruise from here. I did the 45-minute guided boat tour, which takes you past the Opera House, historic buildings, and even the **Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen**—perfect if you’re short on time.


### **2. Amalienborg Palace**

Just a short walk from Nyhavn, **Amalienborg Palace** is the residence of the Danish royal family. I arrived in time to see the changing of the guard at noon—a must-do if you love history and royal traditions. Don’t miss Frederik’s Church nearby, with its impressive dome.


### **3. Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen**

Of course, no trip is complete without visiting the **Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen**, inspired by Hans Christian Andersen’s fairy tale. While smaller than expected, it’s still one of the most photographed spots in the city and an essential stop on any **Copenhagen itinerary**.


### **4. Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen**

For a fun mix of history and entertainment, I made my way to **Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen**. Open since 1843, it’s one of the world’s oldest amusement parks, blending nostalgia with modern rides, live music, and stunning evening light displays. If you’re spending a full day in Copenhagen, I’d recommend going in the evening—it’s magical after dark.


### **5. Rosenborg Castle**

Another gem I explored was **Rosenborg Castle**, home to Denmark’s crown jewels. Built in the 17th century, the interiors are beautifully preserved, and the surrounding King’s Garden is perfect for a relaxing stroll. It’s also one of the most photographed castles in Europe.


### **Other Highlights for Your Copenhagen Itinerary**

- **Round Tower** – the oldest functioning observatory in Europe, with panoramic city views.
**Round Tower** – the oldest functioning observatory in Europe, with panoramic city views.

- **Christianshavn & Freetown Christiania** – a unique, colorful neighborhood with a completely different vibe.
**Christianshavn & Freetown Christiania** – a unique, colorful neighborhood with a completely different vibe.

- **Stroget Street** – perfect for shopping, street food, and people-watching.
**Stroget Street** – perfect for shopping, street food, and people-watching.

Schengen Visa needed to enter Denmark

All set to explore the former cultural capital of Europe. 

My first stop was **Nyhavn**. I wanted to beat the crowd, so I went around 8 and had an hour until  the crowds started pouring in. ![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-11.jpeg)

I had the chance to soak in the splendid morning sun and note down all the prime attractions that I can walk down to later. On the tour, we also learnt about the history of the country and the significance of the different buildings.  Oh you will also get to see the infamous "Little Mermaid Statue" (and all the tourists)!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-13.jpeg)

After the cruise, I got back to the harbor and started walking past the bridge to cross over on to the **PAPER ISLAND** with all the street eats and cool hip cafes.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-17.jpg)

After being left back at the canal, I started walking towards the beautiful **Amalienborg Palace, **the royal palace where the royal family still reside. If you visit around noon, you can also see the changing of the royal guard.  Don't forget to take a walk to **Frederik’s Church** (around the corner from the Palace).

Time for lunch. I walked my way over to the **Stroget** street - a street famous for food trucks and cute boutique shops. You can walk along the street for hours and stop for ice cream, coffee or just mindless window shopping.

There is an entrance fees to go up here (but it's worth it). After going up a short, winding path, you will reach the top of the tower where you will be treated to panoramic views of the city from up here..

Other places I visited - the King’s Garden. Rosenborg Castle. Our Savior’s Church. Christiansborg Palace (house or parliament).

My last stop was** Christhavn** aka the free town of Christiana. I was not allowed to click photos here.. if you have time, head to the **Tivoli gardens **for dinner. 

**Final Thoughts**

Copenhagen turned out to be so much more than just a layover. Between its royal palaces, historic castles, colorful neighborhoods, and vibrant vibe, the city left a mark on my heart.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-40.jpg)

If you’re wondering about the **best time to visit Copenhagen**, I’d say June. If you’re planning your trip and looking for a **Copenhagen itinerary**, include highlights like **Nyhavn Copenhagen**, **Amalienborg Palace**, the **Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen**, **Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen**, and **Rosenborg Castle**—you won’t regret it.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-24-hours-in-copenhagen-denmarkthingstodoincopenhagen-41.jpg)

For my first solo trip, I couldn’t have picked a better city. It was safe, friendly, and unforgettable. If you’ve never traveled solo before, Copenhagen is the perfect place to start.

In June 2017, I went on my first ever solo trip—to Iceland and Denmark. While Iceland was meant to be the highlight, I had a quick layover in Copenhagen that turned into one of my most memorable city experiences. Looking back, it wasn’t just about sightseeing; it was about the feeling of freedom, discovery, and the thrill of my first solo adventure.

Even though I usually prefer nature to cities, Copenhagen completely surprised me with its charm. From **Nyhavn **Copenhagen with its colorful waterfront houses to biking across cobblestone streets, this city had me hooked instantly. Maybe it was the midnight sun keeping the city alive at 11 p.m., or maybe it was just the energy of the people—it’s safe to say, this became one of my all-time favorite cities.

(P.S. If you’ve read my Iceland blog, you’ll remember I lost all my camera photos. So, what I have here are just a few from my phone—but the memories are still golden!)

Best Time to Visit Copenhagen

I visited in June, during the summer, and I honestly think it’s the best time to visit Copenhagen. The long summer days and the midnight sun make the city extra magical. Crowds gather in the main squares and harbors, and rather than being a downside, it added to the city’s lively charm.

If you prefer fewer crowds and a calmer vibe, spring and autumn are also lovely, but summer is when Copenhagen truly comes alive.

Top Things to Do in Copenhagen

Whether you’re here for a day or a week, there are plenty of incredible things to do in Copenhagen. My short but packed itinerary covered everything from royal palaces to quirky neighborhoods, and I’ll share the highlights below.

1. Nyhavn Copenhagen

This iconic spot was my first stop. Arriving early in the morning, I enjoyed the quiet before the rush of people filled the harbor. With its brightly painted 17th-century townhouses, buzzing cafes, and boats lining the canal, Nyhavn Copenhagen is the postcard-perfect heart of the city.

Pro Tip: Take a canal cruise from here. I did the 45-minute guided boat tour, which takes you past the Opera House, historic buildings, and even the Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen—perfect if you’re short on time.

2. Amalienborg Palace

Just a short walk from Nyhavn, Amalienborg Palace is the residence of the Danish royal family. I arrived in time to see the changing of the guard at noon—a must-do if you love history and royal traditions. Don’t miss Frederik’s Church nearby, with its impressive dome.

3. Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen

Of course, no trip is complete without visiting the Little Mermaid Statue Copenhagen, inspired by Hans Christian Andersen’s fairy tale. While smaller than expected, it’s still one of the most photographed spots in the city and an essential stop on any Copenhagen itinerary.

4. Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen

For a fun mix of history and entertainment, I made my way to Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen. Open since 1843, it’s one of the world’s oldest amusement parks, blending nostalgia with modern rides, live music, and stunning evening light displays. If you’re spending a full day in Copenhagen, I’d recommend going in the evening—it’s magical after dark.

5. Rosenborg Castle

Another gem I explored was Rosenborg Castle, home to Denmark’s crown jewels. Built in the 17th century, the interiors are beautifully preserved, and the surrounding King’s Garden is perfect for a relaxing stroll. It’s also one of the most photographed castles in Europe.

  • Round Tower – the oldest functioning observatory in Europe, with panoramic city views. Round Tower – the oldest functioning observatory in Europe, with panoramic city views.

  • Christianshavn & Freetown Christiania – a unique, colorful neighborhood with a completely different vibe. Christianshavn & Freetown Christiania – a unique, colorful neighborhood with a completely different vibe.

  • Stroget Street – perfect for shopping, street food, and people-watching. Stroget Street – perfect for shopping, street food, and people-watching.

Ultimate Gabon Safari Guide: Loango National Park & Libreville Tour
Discover Gabon's wild beauty on a 6-day safari in Loango National Park with this ultimate travel guide.

### Gabon Safari Tour: A Complete Gabon Travel Guide


Dubbed as “Africa’s last Eden”, Gabon stands as one of the few remaining untouched wilderness regions on the continent. We hopped on multiple planes and boats to reach our incredible lodge in the middle of Loango National Park.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-cover.jpg)

Set on the African equator, this country has been on my list for years. This stunningly beautiful destination has enormous potential for ecotourism. With astonishing wildlife like lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, endangered turtles, and whales, Gabon is endowed with nature’s bounty – which also means it comes at an exceptionally high cost for tourism[.](/post/the-ultimate-itinerary-for-madagascar)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-4.jpg)

Whether you’re exploring the dense forests, embarking on boat safaris across lagoons, or hiking to enjoy up-close wildlife experiences, this untamed nation has something for everyone. Forest elephants, dwarf crocodiles, mustache monkeys, hornbills, and hippos—prehistoric in sight and in sound—buzzed past us as we wandered into the forest floor.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-6.jpg)

It takes an extraordinary amount of pre-planning to ensure a [Gabon safari](https://www.visitgabon.com/) is seamless, and a healthy bit of homework to ensure reward. The effort it takes to design a Gabon expedition reassures you that this is far from the crowded safari in the Masai Mara. In theory, the harder it is to plan a safari and the longer it takes to formulate, the more remote you’re going. That is where this Gabon travel guide comes in handy

Flights + Visa

 We flew from Cameroon to Gabon via [Afrijet](https://www.afrijet.com/). You can also use Afrijet to travel between Libreville and Port Gentil (the base for Loango National Park). If you hold an Indian passport, you'll need an entry permit. While there’s an online portal, it’s often unreliable. I recommend having your travel agent handle the visa—ours took about 3 weeks.

Language 

French is the official language, though some people speak English. Keep Google Translate handy.

Currency

I used Euros but exchanged some for local currency for food and tips.


Best Time to Visit 

May to September is the dry season, ideal for outdoor exploration with less rainfall and cooler temperatures.


Guide

As with most safaris in Africa, having a reliable local guide is essential. Please feel free to get in touch with me for a local guide recommendation. 

I’m sharing this because I’m a huge believer in supporting this local team, they truly made our trip. To help them manage their bookings and give you the best service, please include your **confirmed travel dates and number of people** when you reach out. I love connecting fellow travelers with the best local resources to make sure your trip is as seamless as ours was!

A Gabon safari tour isn’t cheap. Expect around EUR 2500 per person for 4–5 days for 2 people sharing. But trust me - it’s worth every penny.

WHERE WE STAYED

Getting to Loango is an adventure in itself—plane from Libreville to Port Gentil, a 4-hour drive through jungle roads, then a boat ride through mangroves.

If you plan to visit [Loango National Park](https://gabonwildlifecamps.com/gabon-parks-camps/loango-national-park/), you’ll choose between three seasonal lodges. These camps are intimate, low-impact stays set deep in the forest or along the Louri Lagoon. You can take a boat into the lagoon or a jungle safari deep into the park.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-9.jpg)

These lodges perfectly reflect the essence of an eco safari in Africa—immersive, sustainable, and deeply connected to the untouched environment of Gabon.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-13.jpg)

At night, the lull of nocturnal wildlife and waves on the shore tell stories under the stars. The food was exquisite, even with dietary restrictions catered to in advance.

Just 40 minutes by boat from the lodge is the Atlantic Ocean. Here, you can walk on soft-sand beaches, surf, or gather around a log fire at sunset.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-15.jpg)


### **Vegan + Vegetarian Food**

Being vegetarian in Africa isn’t always easy. The best way to manage this is by choosing full-board safari packages and informing the camp ahead of time about dietary needs.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-18.jpg)


### **Highlights**


### **Libreville**

A small yet elegant capital city, Libreville has urban charm with a hint of wild adventure. It’s quieter and cleaner than most capitals, perfect for relaxing after your flight.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-20.jpg)

**Places to see in Libreville:**

- Sainte Marie Cathedral – One of the oldest churches with striking stained glass windows.
Sainte Marie Cathedral – One of the oldest churches with striking stained glass windows.

- National Museum of Arts and Traditions – Offers insight into Gabonese tribal culture through masks, carvings, and history.
National Museum of Arts and Traditions – Offers insight into Gabonese tribal culture through masks, carvings, and history.

- Point Denis Beach – Unwind here before your safari with golden sands and ocean dips.

- Marché du Mont-Bouet – Libreville’s largest market, full of local color, fresh produce, and handmade crafts
Marché du Mont-Bouet – Libreville’s largest market, full of local color, fresh produce, and handmade crafts

In the untouched wilderness of Loango there is something for everything. In the first part of the year, December to February, turtles come ashore to lay their eggs. Later in the year from July to September humpbacks are to be found just off the coast and the dry season from June to August is the best time to see wildlife such as elephants, buffalo, and hippos along the flood plains of the Ngové River.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-25.jpg)

The lagoon is vast, and the horizon merely a faint blur between water and sky. Thick walls of entangled green crowded down upon the water’s edge, where the dark waters of the river provided a brilliant reflection of the canopy. The swampy vegetation hides a multitude of animals. From baby crocodiles, mighty pythons, herds of buffalo, hippo, elephant and monkeys, which stared back at us before rushing back into the security of the forest. Egrets fluttered by us we puttered past. Ibises patrolled the river margins, and a fish eagle zoomed past.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-27.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-31.jpg)

Nearly three-quarters of the country is covered in tropical rainforests, which are among the richest and most diverse on Earth. The least densely populated country in Central Africa, Gabon has only one minuscule percent of its land falling under the agricultural axe. Although Gabon is principally known for its tropical rainforests it also has coastal woodland areas and open savannah grasslands where the coastal plains meet the forests. This is why I had come to Gabon & it didn’t disappoint. An AUTHENTIC wildlife experience in every sense of the word. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-37.jpg)

We have been lucky enough to see elephants in a variety of places and environments across Africa and yet this was as special as those other occasions. The ombre sky, the sound of the Atlantic crashing on the beach, the feel of the sea breeze on our faces and the sight of this lone elephant or even the small herd of elephants, adults and calves walking on the beach was incredible. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-41.jpg)

The remoteness and inaccessibility the swamps provide have inadvertently been a savior: protected by nature, elephant here have not to the same degree as elsewhere experienced poaching, know the area is safe, and consequently show their trust when you approach within a few feet by boat. Very few places in Africa offer forest elephant experiences of this kind. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-43.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-47.jpg)

For me, this makes Loango one of the most exciting safari destinations in Central Africa. The habituation process will be a long, slow process but then that is half the charm of Loango, it is raw and untouched. This is an area not for those with tick-lists but those with a thirst for adventure, those who wish to LIVE through an experience!


On paper, Gabon has it all. Which doesn’t mean it’s easy. It takes an extraordinary amount of pre-planning to ensure a Gabon safari is seamless, and a healthy bit of homework to ensure reward. The effort, however, it takes to design a Gabon expedition reassures you that this is far from the noisy charter plane skies of the Masai Mara. In theory, the harder it is to plan a safari and the longer it takes to formulate, the deeper and more remote you’re going.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-49.jpg)

The geographical location and low human population are the key driving forces behind the biomass's pristine condition. The challenge for intrepid travelers like us is both debilitating and stimulating. The feel of the ‘frontier-ness’ is palpable.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-6-day-safari-adventure-in-gabon-50.jpg)

Careful planning and patience are essential for a Gabonese adventure, which is as things should be. The unique wildlife and miles and miles of ‘broccoli’ are a testament to the country’s relative inaccessibility and to its enlightened forest conservation policies. Long may it all continue. For those in search of a true blue [African ](/post/saotomeandprincipe)experience where wildlife numbers outweigh that of humans, few places top Gabon. Hope I have convinced you to visit

Gabon Safari Tour: A Complete Gabon Travel Guide

Dubbed as “Africa’s last Eden”, Gabon stands as one of the few remaining untouched wilderness regions on the continent. We hopped on multiple planes and boats to reach our incredible lodge in the middle of Loango National Park.

Set on the African equator, this country has been on my list for years. This stunningly beautiful destination has enormous potential for ecotourism. With astonishing wildlife like lowland gorillas, chimpanzees, endangered turtles, and whales, Gabon is endowed with nature’s bounty – which also means it comes at an exceptionally high cost for tourism.

Whether you’re exploring the dense forests, embarking on boat safaris across lagoons, or hiking to enjoy up-close wildlife experiences, this untamed nation has something for everyone. Forest elephants, dwarf crocodiles, mustache monkeys, hornbills, and hippos—prehistoric in sight and in sound—buzzed past us as we wandered into the forest floor.

It takes an extraordinary amount of pre-planning to ensure a Gabon safari is seamless, and a healthy bit of homework to ensure reward. The effort it takes to design a Gabon expedition reassures you that this is far from the crowded safari in the Masai Mara. In theory, the harder it is to plan a safari and the longer it takes to formulate, the more remote you’re going. That is where this Gabon travel guide comes in handy

Flights + Visa

 We flew from Cameroon to Gabon via Afrijet. You can also use Afrijet to travel between Libreville and Port Gentil (the base for Loango National Park). If you hold an Indian passport, you’ll need an entry permit. While there’s an online portal, it’s often unreliable. I recommend having your travel agent handle the visa—ours took about 3 weeks.

Language 

French is the official language, though some people speak English. Keep Google Translate handy.

Currency

I used Euros but exchanged some for local currency for food and tips.

Best Time to Visit 

May to September is the dry season, ideal for outdoor exploration with less rainfall and cooler temperatures.

Guide

As with most safaris in Africa, having a reliable local guide is essential. Please feel free to get in touch with me for a local guide recommendation.

I’m sharing this because I’m a huge believer in supporting this local team, they truly made our trip. To help them manage their bookings and give you the best service, please include your confirmed travel dates and number of people when you reach out. I love connecting fellow travelers with the best local resources to make sure your trip is as seamless as ours was!

A Gabon safari tour isn’t cheap. Expect around EUR 2500 per person for 4–5 days for 2 people sharing. But trust me - it’s worth every penny.

WHERE WE STAYED

Getting to Loango is an adventure in itself—plane from Libreville to Port Gentil, a 4-hour drive through jungle roads, then a boat ride through mangroves.

If you plan to visit Loango National Park, you’ll choose between three seasonal lodges. These camps are intimate, low-impact stays set deep in the forest or along the Louri Lagoon. You can take a boat into the lagoon or a jungle safari deep into the park.

These lodges perfectly reflect the essence of an eco safari in Africa—immersive, sustainable, and deeply connected to the untouched environment of Gabon.

At night, the lull of nocturnal wildlife and waves on the shore tell stories under the stars. The food was exquisite, even with dietary restrictions catered to in advance.

Just 40 minutes by boat from the lodge is the Atlantic Ocean. Here, you can walk on soft-sand beaches, surf, or gather around a log fire at sunset.

Vegan + Vegetarian Food

Being vegetarian in Africa isn’t always easy. The best way to manage this is by choosing full-board safari packages and informing the camp ahead of time about dietary needs.

A small yet elegant capital city, Libreville has urban charm with a hint of wild adventure. It’s quieter and cleaner than most capitals, perfect for relaxing after your flight.

Taste of Panama: 72 Hours of Culture, Cuisine, and Coastlines
If it's your first time in Central America, Panama is an ideal gateway to the region. From rich culture to tropical coastlines and...

If it's your first time in Central America, Panama is an ideal gateway to the region. From rich culture to tropical coastlines and world-famous engineering feats, Panama offers a little bit of everything.

We’ve explored most of Central America, and while Panama was just a layover for us after a whirlwind trip through Honduras, it turned into a surprisingly enriching 72-hour adventure.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-1.jpg)

- Panama Canal
Panama Canal

- Amador Causeway (Panama Causeway)
Amador Causeway (Panama Causeway)

- Casco Viejo (Old Quarter)
Casco Viejo (Old Quarter)

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-1j1lu)

**FLIGHTS + VISA**

We flew into Panama with Copa Airlines, the national carrier. While our flight was smooth, reviews can be mixed — like with most airlines.

Visa Tip: If you hold a valid US visa (with at least 6 months' validity), you can enter Panama visa-free. This rule applies to many Central American countries.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-2.jpg)

**LANGUAGE IN PANAMA**

Spanish is the official language in Panama, but thanks to its popularity among travelers, many people especially in tourist zones speak basic English.

**BEST TIME TO VISIT PANAMA**

The dry season in Panama runs from December to April, making it the most popular time to visit. We traveled in early July, during the rainy season, but lucked out with dry, cloudy days and only occasional showers.

  
**CURRENCY**

Panama uses the US Dollar, so there's no currency conversion hassle for American travelers. Most places accept credit cards too.

**WHERE WE STAYED**

We spent our 3 nights in Panama City at Tryp by Wyndham — a great mid-range hotel with marts, cafes, and restaurants all within walking distance. Perfect for a short city trip.

**HOW TO GET AROUND PANAMA CITY**

Panama City has an affordable and efficient public transport system. We used the metro and buses to get around with ease. Taxis and ride-hailing apps are also available.


**SOLO TRAVEL + SAFETY IN PANAMA**

Panama felt a lot safer than some other Central American countries. That said, like any major city, there are certain neighborhoods you should avoid — your hotel concierge can point them out.

**VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD IN PANAMA**

Surprisingly, we found delicious vegan and vegetarian options in Panama. From local plant-based twists on traditional dishes to international cuisine, the food scene was diverse and satisfying.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-3.jpg)

**HIGHLIGHTS OF PANAMA**

**Panama Canal**

No trip to Panama is complete without visiting the legendary Panama Canal. At the Miraflores Locks, we watched massive ships pass through with mesmerizing precision — a living, breathing monument to engineering brilliance.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-4.jpg)

Our local guide explained the canal's rich history and the modern expansion project that now allows larger ships to cross. It's one of those places that makes you feel small in the best possible way.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-5.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-7.jpg)

It was mind-boggling how they managed to increase the canal's capacity to accommodate even larger ships. It's amazing to feel a sense of connection with the past and present – knowing that this incredible feat connects two vast oceans and plays a pivotal role in global trade. If you love history, engineering marvels, and breathtaking sights, the Panama Canal should definitely be at the top of your travel list when in Panama.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-9.jpg)

**Panama Causeway (Amador Causeway)**

The Panama Causeway is a scenic stretch connecting several islands to the mainland. Flanked by ocean views on one side and tropical greenery on the other, it’s perfect for a leisurely walk or bike ride.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-10.jpg)

We enjoyed the breeze, admired the yachts, and caught a stunning view of the Panama City skyline. Great for photos, casual dining, and sunset views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-15.jpg)

**Casco Viejo**

This historic gem of Panama City stole our hearts. Casco Viejo, the city’s old quarter, is bursting with colonial charm — cobbled streets, colorful facades, rooftop bars, art galleries, and cozy cafes.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-19.jpg)

Declared a UNESCO World Heritage site, Casco Viejo feels like a time capsule. Every building tells a story. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-23.jpg)

We spent hours wandering its alleys, shopping for local crafts, and soaking in the vibrant culture.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-cover.jpg)

While our time in Panama was short, it was rich in experiences. In just 3 days, we witnessed the country's iconic canal, explored its colonial past, and enjoyed its modern charm.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-for-48-hours-in-panama-28.jpg)

If you're already familiar with Latin America and want a quick but meaningful stop, Panama offers the perfect blend of adventure, relaxation, and culture — all within easy reach.

  • Highlights Panama Canal Causeway Casco Viejo Highlights Panama Canal Causeway Casco Viejo

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

FLIGHTS + VISA

We flew into Panama with Copa Airlines, the national carrier. While our flight was smooth, reviews can be mixed — like with most airlines.

Visa Tip: If you hold a valid US visa (with at least 6 months’ validity), you can enter Panama visa-free. This rule applies to many Central American countries.

LANGUAGE IN PANAMA

Spanish is the official language in Panama, but thanks to its popularity among travelers, many people especially in tourist zones speak basic English.

BEST TIME TO VISIT PANAMA

The dry season in Panama runs from December to April, making it the most popular time to visit. We traveled in early July, during the rainy season, but lucked out with dry, cloudy days and only occasional showers.

CURRENCY

Panama uses the US Dollar, so there’s no currency conversion hassle for American travelers. Most places accept credit cards too.

WHERE WE STAYED

We spent our 3 nights in Panama City at Tryp by Wyndham — a great mid-range hotel with marts, cafes, and restaurants all within walking distance. Perfect for a short city trip.

HOW TO GET AROUND PANAMA CITY

Panama City has an affordable and efficient public transport system. We used the metro and buses to get around with ease. Taxis and ride-hailing apps are also available.

SOLO TRAVEL + SAFETY IN PANAMA

Panama felt a lot safer than some other Central American countries. That said, like any major city, there are certain neighborhoods you should avoid — your hotel concierge can point them out.

VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD IN PANAMA

Surprisingly, we found delicious vegan and vegetarian options in Panama. From local plant-based twists on traditional dishes to international cuisine, the food scene was diverse and satisfying.

HIGHLIGHTS OF PANAMA

Panama Canal

No trip to Panama is complete without visiting the legendary Panama Canal. At the Miraflores Locks, we watched massive ships pass through with mesmerizing precision — a living, breathing monument to engineering brilliance.

Our local guide explained the canal’s rich history and the modern expansion project that now allows larger ships to cross. It’s one of those places that makes you feel small in the best possible way.

It was mind-boggling how they managed to increase the canal’s capacity to accommodate even larger ships. It’s amazing to feel a sense of connection with the past and present – knowing that this incredible feat connects two vast oceans and plays a pivotal role in global trade. If you love history, engineering marvels, and breathtaking sights, the Panama Canal should definitely be at the top of your travel list when in Panama.

Panama Causeway (Amador Causeway)

The Panama Causeway is a scenic stretch connecting several islands to the mainland. Flanked by ocean views on one side and tropical greenery on the other, it’s perfect for a leisurely walk or bike ride.

We enjoyed the breeze, admired the yachts, and caught a stunning view of the Panama City skyline. Great for photos, casual dining, and sunset views.

Casco Viejo

This historic gem of Panama City stole our hearts. Casco Viejo, the city’s old quarter, is bursting with colonial charm — cobbled streets, colorful facades, rooftop bars, art galleries, and cozy cafes.

Declared a UNESCO World Heritage site, Casco Viejo feels like a time capsule. Every building tells a story. 

We spent hours wandering its alleys, shopping for local crafts, and soaking in the vibrant culture.

While our time in Panama was short, it was rich in experiences. In just 3 days, we witnessed the country’s iconic canal, explored its colonial past, and enjoyed its modern charm.

If you’re already familiar with Latin America and want a quick but meaningful stop, Panama offers the perfect blend of adventure, relaxation, and culture — all within easy reach.

FAQ

  • **Is 3 days enough in Panama? **Yes, especially if you’re focusing on Panama City. You can cover major sights like the Panama Canal, Casco Viejo, and Causeway. **Is 3 days enough in Panama? **

Yes, especially if you’re focusing on Panama City. You can cover major sights like the Panama Canal, Casco Viejo, and Causeway.

  • **Is Panama safe for tourists? **Yes, but like any city, some areas are best avoided. Stick to well-known neighborhoods and you’ll be fine. **Is Panama safe for tourists? **

Yes, but like any city, some areas are best avoided. Stick to well-known neighborhoods and you’ll be fine.

  • **Do I need to know Spanish to visit Panama? **While knowing basic Spanish helps, most tourist areas in Panama have English-speaking staff. **Do I need to know Spanish to visit Panama? **

While knowing basic Spanish helps, most tourist areas in Panama have English-speaking staff.

  • **What’s the best way to get around Panama City? **Public transit is affordable and reliable. Taxis and ride-sharing apps also work well. **What’s the best way to get around Panama City? **

Public transit is affordable and reliable. Taxis and ride-sharing apps also work well.

May 2022

**Welcome to Part One of Our Southeast Asia Trip – Hello, Vietnam!**

If you’re looking for an itinerary that skips the usual tourist trail and dives straight into Vietnam’s raw, rural beauty, you’ve come to the right place.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-1.jpg)

Most people picture Vietnam as the land of the famous “H” cities—Hue, Hanoi, Hoi An, Ha Long Bay, and Ho Chi Minh—along with wild street food and bustling markets. But we went in a different direction.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-4.jpg)

Our short 5-day trip took us into lush valleys, misty caves, remote villages, and scenic rivers—**Vietnam off the beaten path.** 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-6.jpg)

In this itinerary, we skip all the usual touristy spots and head straight for the countryside.

**Day 2** – Thai Vi Temple, Bich Dong Pagoda, Mua Caves

**Day 3** – Day trip to Trang An

**Day 4** – Day trip to Mai Chau → Back to Hanoi

**Day 5** – Hanoi city walk + cultural experien

### Vegetarian options were limited, especially outside Hanoi. Best meal was at[ ](https://sadhurestaurant.com/)[Sadhu Vegetarian Restaurant](https://sadhurestaurant.com/) in Hanoi—an upscale, buffet-style spot with great variety. Booking is a must. 


### **Ninh Binh:** Boutique stay near Tam Coc lake – quiet & convenient

**Hanoi:**[ Acoustic Hotel & Spa](https://acoustichotel.com/) – centrally located, clean, with excellent service (highly recommended)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-7.jpg)

In Hanoi, I chose the centrally located [Acoustic Hotel & Spa](https://www.booking.com/hotel/vn/acoustic-amp-spa.html). What a pretty little place with the most delightful team to help with you everything you could need. Due to Covid, they were not serving breakfast, but there are a hundred cafes just 2 minutes from this quaint boutique hotel. Highly recommended if you are just looking for some place clean to crash in between your city runs. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-8.jpg)

**Ninh Binh – The Base of All Adventures**


### We skipped Hanoi on Day 1 and headed straight to **Ninh Binh**—a quiet town surrounded by limestone cliffs, pagodas, and endless rice fields.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-13.jpg)


### Short walk from Tam Coc. Peaceful setting amidst green fields and locals visiting to pray. Not the grandest temple, but perfect for solitude.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-15.jpg)

It may not be as popular as the colorful towns like Hoi An or Hue but it was the focal point of our trip to Vietnam. I was determined to escape city life on this trip and soak up the slow life of countryside Vietnam, and that is exactly what this town offers. The best part was it is only 90 minutes from Hanoi. We based ourselves here to explore the mind blowing caves, pagodas, infinite limestone cliffs and bike along striking green rice fields. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-20.jpg)

We started our exploration with the** Thai Vi Temple**. If you base yourself in central Ninh Binh area, this temple is just a short walk from the Tam Coc lake. The temple is hidden deep in the mountains, with many local villagers visiting through the day. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-22.jpg)

Though I will admit, the temple didn’t impress me as much as the surrounding areas did. It was pure serenity surrounded by dense green mountains & abundant rice fields. If you are like me and prefer places far flung, miles away from tourists, be sure to add this gem to your itinerary. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-25.jpg)


### A stunning three-tiered cave temple carved into a mountainside. Go early (6 a.m.) to avoid crowds and enjoy the sunrise. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-27.jpg)

The entrance is surrounded by water lilies and this lake. To me, this was one of the best places to experience the sunrise. The gateway stands tall with intricate designs adorning the entrance to the mountains. You should definitely take the short trail that cuts through the mountain for magical views of the valley, awe-inspiring peaks & the vast fields below! 

**Travel tip: the entrance is FREE but try to make it there by 6 a.m. to avoid crowds **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-30.jpg)


### A popular spot, but worth the 500-stair climb. Views over Tam Coc valley are breathtaking. Caution: the dragon statue area is sharp and windy.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-32.jpg)

With mountains that stretch as far as eyes can see and freshly laid fields below you, it could be one of the most knockout sights you can hope to see anywhere in Vietnam(in my humble opinion). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-35.jpg)

This is a sacred temple area where you need to climb over 500 stairs to reach different view points - one is the iconic Mua Caves view point of the Tam Coc valley and the other one is further up, called the Mua Peak, for the view of the dragon statue. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-37.jpg)

The dragon statue, though breathtaking, is a bit dangerous to get to - you will need to climb jagged rocks and stand very still. The rocks are sharp and you could easily hurt yourself. I would not recommend this one, if you are afraid of heights. It also gets windy up there, so be very mindful of all this before you venture out here. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-42.jpg)

**NOTE: **this is a popular spot where people actually dress up for a photo. If you go on a weekend or long weekend, expect to wait for half hour for your turn for photos. The dragon statue area is much more quieter and we had it to ourselves the whole time. 


### Called **"Halong Bay on Land"**, this 3-hour bamboo boat ride was the highlight of our trip. It’s peaceful, scenic, and led mostly by women rowers.


### **Cost:** VND 250,000 **Route:** We chose Route 3 (longest cave route)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-44.jpg)

Aptly called as 'Halong Bay On Land', this is yet another hidden gem that often gets exchanged for a cruise to visit Halong Bay, it's more attractive cousin. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-47.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-49.jpg)

As much as people told me NOT TO MISS Halong Bay, sitting on a cruise just didn't appeal to us (it never does!). Trang An felt like the right fit for us, a much more authentic experience far away from the tourist crowds who book day trips to Halong. The crowds here for only locals, also only because it was a long weekend. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-51.jpg)

The landscape in Trang An is second to none. Did you know this is the only place in Vietnam that is both a natural and cultural UNESCO World Heritage site? You will be surrounded by steep karst cliffs covered in thick moss. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-54.jpg)

This was our favorite day in Vietnam. Imagine just green cliffs for miles on end.. all you will hear is the swoosh of your paddle against the calm river. You gently float along on your bamboo boat to see mirror-like reflections for three hours. If that isn't the very definition of paradise, I don't know what is.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-56.jpg)

When you enter the complex, you need to pay and pick the tour you wish to go on **(costs VND 250,000 per person)**. There are few options to choose from but we went with route 3 as this one had the longest cave structures among the three options. No matter which tour you pick, you will get to hop on and off a few times to visit temples and caves but for the most part, all of you have to do is soak up this all natural environment in all it's zen glory. The entire scene was captivating beyond words. Once you make your way through the different caves, it is inevitable by how enthralling this place is. You are hit by the color of pure green goodness on every corner, each cliff connected by rice paddies and not just water. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-61.jpg)

My favorite part of the whole experience was also that 90% of the boats are rowed by women. They expertly bend and maneuver their way through the cliffs without breaking a sweat. We tried to do for about half hour and were left panting. Don't miss out on this magical opportunity to immerse yourself in this eco-tourism gem hidden deep in the middle of towering mountains and sacred rivers.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-63.jpg)

One our last day from Ninh Binh, we decided to go to yet another off beat destination called Mai Chau, a picturesque postcard valley in the northwest region of Vietnam. If you do this trip from Hanoi, it would be a 3 hour drive (one way).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-64.jpg)

This is again a fantastic place to escape the tourist crowds, more so than Sa Pa. If you enjoy taking it slow with a lot of eco-tourism touch points, this place is a must-do. With it's scenic vistas and undisturbed country life, this is the ideal day trip from Hanoi. Let your lungs soak up the fresh air. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-67.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-69.jpg)

What do to in Mai Chau? We did two things - the first one was hiking up to the **Chieu Cave**. 

This is a massive cave structure that sites on top of a hill. Note that there are a good 1000 stairs to climb to enter the cave but it is not very difficult. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-71.jpg)

Once inside, you will likely be the only ones there. What I also loved about this mini-adventure was the opportunity to catch the scenic vista of  the village below us. Postcard perfection. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-72.jpg)

Take your exploring the stalactite forest. As with any cave, the impressions, varying shapes & curves and patterns on these cliffs are fascinating. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-73.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-77.jpg)

Our last stop for the day was a dip in the **Goa Lao waterfall.** Just a 20 minute drive from the cave. Not very deep, it's an amazing place to cool off after your hike up. Likely to be filled with local kids having a ball but absolutely no tourists. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-79.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-81.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-83.jpg)

**Traveler note**: our original plane was to stay a day in Mai Chau but every place was booked for the long weekend, so we went back to Hanoi for the night. If you have the time, I would highly recommend spending one night in this village. 


### Back in the capital on Day 5, we took it slow. Here’s what we loved:

- **Morning stroll** around **Ngoc San Pagoda**

### **Morning stroll** around **Ngoc San Pagoda**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-85.jpg)

- **Snack stop:**[ King Roti](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Restaurant_Review-g293924-d15368867-Reviews-King_Roti-Hanoi.html) – iconic street bread under $1

### **Snack stop:**[ King Roti](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Restaurant_Review-g293924-d15368867-Reviews-King_Roti-Hanoi.html) – iconic street bread under $1


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-86.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-88.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-90.jpg)

- **Sunset:** St. Joseph’s Cathedral

### **Sunset:** St. Joseph’s Cathedral

- **Evening walk:** Train Street – funky, artistic, and lively
**Evening walk:** Train Street – funky, artistic, and lively


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-92.jpg)

Later in the evening, we caught the sunrise at the Ngoc Pagoda again. Before walking further down to the biggest church in the city to catch the sunset. Get here a bit early and enjoy the evening by the lake.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-93.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-95.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-97.jpg)

We also checked out all the cool street art along the way. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-99.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-101.jpg)

We later stopped at the Insta-famous **Train Street**. What a funky vibe here with the graffiti and lights. We loved this part of the town. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-103.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-105.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-107.jpg)

We then went for dinner at the best vegetarian restaurant in town. [Sadhu vegetarian](https://sadhu.vn/) restaurant comes highly rated in Hanoi and at a cost of almost $20 per person. The portions and variety were impressive though. It was a great way for us to experience the local cuisine without compromising on our principles. The setting is also marginally upscale. Ideal for date night. 

The staff are fantastic, they explain what to try and how everything works. Be mindful of the portions as it is buffet style and you could fill up quickly if you don't space out the courses, 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-109.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-five-day-guide-to-northern-vietnam-111.jpg)

### This Vietnam itinerary gave us a taste of **authentic countryside life**, far from tourist traps. From limestone mountains and serene rivers to friendly locals and ancient pagodas—it was pure magic.


### Ninh Binh and its surrounding gems like Trang An and Mai Chau are must-visits, not just stopovers. The best part? It was easy on the wallet and big on experience.


### We’ll be back for more.

The jagged mountains covered with dense shrubs, famous caves and vibrant Asian culture, the country stood out as a gem. If you choose the right area, you can have a sublime eco-tourism experience here. We look forward to visiting Vietnam again and exploring more of it's alluring culture.

Welcome to Part One of Our Southeast Asia Trip – Hello, Vietnam!

If you’re looking for an itinerary that skips the usual tourist trail and dives straight into Vietnam’s raw, rural beauty, you’ve come to the right place.

Most people picture Vietnam as the land of the famous “H” cities—Hue, Hanoi, Hoi An, Ha Long Bay, and Ho Chi Minh—along with wild street food and bustling markets. But we went in a different direction.

Our short 5-day trip took us into lush valleys, misty caves, remote villages, and scenic rivers—Vietnam off the beaten path.

In this itinerary, we skip all the usual touristy spots and head straight for the countryside.

Vietnam Itinerary Summary

Day 1 – **Vietnam **Arrive in Hanoi → Drive to Ninh Binh

Day 2 – Thai Vi Temple, Bich Dong Pagoda, Mua Caves

Day 3 – Day trip to Trang An

Day 4 – Day trip to Mai Chau → Back to Hanoi

Day 5 – Hanoi city walk + cultural experien

Local currency: Vietnamese Dong (VND) – carry cash

Best time to visit: March–May or September onwards to avoid the rainy season

Safety: Safe for solo travelers, especially women, with basic precautions

We hired a private driver with Hanoi Vietnam Transfer (non-English speaking) for 4 days. Cost approx. $400. Ideal if you’re short on time. Public transport (trains/flights) is reliable too.

  • Daily budget (off-season): $80–100

Hotel prices: Affordable boutique stays (~$30–60/night)

Vegetarian options were limited, especially outside Hanoi. Best meal was at Sadhu Vegetarian Restaurant in Hanoi—an upscale, buffet-style spot with great variety. Booking is a must.

Ninh Binh: Boutique stay near Tam Coc lake – quiet & convenient

Hanoi: Acoustic Hotel & Spa – centrally located, clean, with excellent service (highly recommended)

In Hanoi, I chose the centrally located Acoustic Hotel & Spa. What a pretty little place with the most delightful team to help with you everything you could need. Due to Covid, they were not serving breakfast, but there are a hundred cafes just 2 minutes from this quaint boutique hotel. Highly recommended if you are just looking for some place clean to crash in between your city runs.

Ninh Binh – The Base of All Adventures

We skipped Hanoi on Day 1 and headed straight to Ninh Binh—a quiet town surrounded by limestone cliffs, pagodas, and endless rice fields.

Short walk from Tam Coc. Peaceful setting amidst green fields and locals visiting to pray. Not the grandest temple, but perfect for solitude.

It may not be as popular as the colorful towns like Hoi An or Hue but it was the focal point of our trip to Vietnam. I was determined to escape city life on this trip and soak up the slow life of countryside Vietnam, and that is exactly what this town offers. The best part was it is only 90 minutes from Hanoi. We based ourselves here to explore the mind blowing caves, pagodas, infinite limestone cliffs and bike along striking green rice fields.

We started our exploration with the** Thai Vi Temple**. If you base yourself in central Ninh Binh area, this temple is just a short walk from the Tam Coc lake. The temple is hidden deep in the mountains, with many local villagers visiting through the day.

Though I will admit, the temple didn’t impress me as much as the surrounding areas did. It was pure serenity surrounded by dense green mountains & abundant rice fields. If you are like me and prefer places far flung, miles away from tourists, be sure to add this gem to your itinerary.

A stunning three-tiered cave temple carved into a mountainside. Go early (6 a.m.) to avoid crowds and enjoy the sunrise.

The entrance is surrounded by water lilies and this lake. To me, this was one of the best places to experience the sunrise. The gateway stands tall with intricate designs adorning the entrance to the mountains. You should definitely take the short trail that cuts through the mountain for magical views of the valley, awe-inspiring peaks & the vast fields below!

**Travel tip: the entrance is FREE but try to make it there by 6 a.m. to avoid crowds **

A popular spot, but worth the 500-stair climb. Views over Tam Coc valley are breathtaking. Caution: the dragon statue area is sharp and windy.

With mountains that stretch as far as eyes can see and freshly laid fields below you, it could be one of the most knockout sights you can hope to see anywhere in Vietnam(in my humble opinion).

This is a sacred temple area where you need to climb over 500 stairs to reach different view points - one is the iconic Mua Caves view point of the Tam Coc valley and the other one is further up, called the Mua Peak, for the view of the dragon statue.

The dragon statue, though breathtaking, is a bit dangerous to get to - you will need to climb jagged rocks and stand very still. The rocks are sharp and you could easily hurt yourself. I would not recommend this one, if you are afraid of heights. It also gets windy up there, so be very mindful of all this before you venture out here.

**NOTE: **this is a popular spot where people actually dress up for a photo. If you go on a weekend or long weekend, expect to wait for half hour for your turn for photos. The dragon statue area is much more quieter and we had it to ourselves the whole time.

Called “Halong Bay on Land”, this 3-hour bamboo boat ride was the highlight of our trip. It’s peaceful, scenic, and led mostly by women rowers.

Cost: VND 250,000 Route: We chose Route 3 (longest cave route)

Aptly called as ‘Halong Bay On Land’, this is yet another hidden gem that often gets exchanged for a cruise to visit Halong Bay, it’s more attractive cousin.

As much as people told me NOT TO MISS Halong Bay, sitting on a cruise just didn’t appeal to us (it never does!). Trang An felt like the right fit for us, a much more authentic experience far away from the tourist crowds who book day trips to Halong. The crowds here for only locals, also only because it was a long weekend.

The landscape in Trang An is second to none. Did you know this is the only place in Vietnam that is both a natural and cultural UNESCO World Heritage site? You will be surrounded by steep karst cliffs covered in thick moss.

This was our favorite day in Vietnam. Imagine just green cliffs for miles on end.. all you will hear is the swoosh of your paddle against the calm river. You gently float along on your bamboo boat to see mirror-like reflections for three hours. If that isn’t the very definition of paradise, I don’t know what is.

When you enter the complex, you need to pay and pick the tour you wish to go on (costs VND 250,000 per person). There are few options to choose from but we went with route 3 as this one had the longest cave structures among the three options. No matter which tour you pick, you will get to hop on and off a few times to visit temples and caves but for the most part, all of you have to do is soak up this all natural environment in all it’s zen glory. The entire scene was captivating beyond words. Once you make your way through the different caves, it is inevitable by how enthralling this place is. You are hit by the color of pure green goodness on every corner, each cliff connected by rice paddies and not just water.

My favorite part of the whole experience was also that 90% of the boats are rowed by women. They expertly bend and maneuver their way through the cliffs without breaking a sweat. We tried to do for about half hour and were left panting. Don’t miss out on this magical opportunity to immerse yourself in this eco-tourism gem hidden deep in the middle of towering mountains and sacred rivers.

One our last day from Ninh Binh, we decided to go to yet another off beat destination called Mai Chau, a picturesque postcard valley in the northwest region of Vietnam. If you do this trip from Hanoi, it would be a 3 hour drive (one way).

This is again a fantastic place to escape the tourist crowds, more so than Sa Pa. If you enjoy taking it slow with a lot of eco-tourism touch points, this place is a must-do. With it’s scenic vistas and undisturbed country life, this is the ideal day trip from Hanoi. Let your lungs soak up the fresh air.

What do to in Mai Chau? We did two things - the first one was hiking up to the Chieu Cave.

This is a massive cave structure that sites on top of a hill. Note that there are a good 1000 stairs to climb to enter the cave but it is not very difficult.

Once inside, you will likely be the only ones there. What I also loved about this mini-adventure was the opportunity to catch the scenic vista of the village below us. Postcard perfection.

Take your exploring the stalactite forest. As with any cave, the impressions, varying shapes & curves and patterns on these cliffs are fascinating.

Our last stop for the day was a dip in the Goa Lao waterfall. Just a 20 minute drive from the cave. Not very deep, it’s an amazing place to cool off after your hike up. Likely to be filled with local kids having a ball but absolutely no tourists.

Traveler note: our original plane was to stay a day in Mai Chau but every place was booked for the long weekend, so we went back to Hanoi for the night. If you have the time, I would highly recommend spending one night in this village.

Back in the capital on Day 5, we took it slow. Here’s what we loved:

  • Morning stroll around Ngoc San Pagoda

Morning stroll around Ngoc San Pagoda

  • Snack stop: King Roti – iconic street bread under $1

Snack stop: King Roti – iconic street bread under $1

  • Sunset: St. Joseph’s Cathedral

Sunset: St. Joseph’s Cathedral

  • Evening walk: Train Street – funky, artistic, and lively Evening walk: Train Street – funky, artistic, and lively

Later in the evening, we caught the sunrise at the Ngoc Pagoda again. Before walking further down to the biggest church in the city to catch the sunset. Get here a bit early and enjoy the evening by the lake.

We also checked out all the cool street art along the way.

We later stopped at the Insta-famous Train Street. What a funky vibe here with the graffiti and lights. We loved this part of the town.

We then went for dinner at the best vegetarian restaurant in town. Sadhu vegetarian restaurant comes highly rated in Hanoi and at a cost of almost $20 per person. The portions and variety were impressive though. It was a great way for us to experience the local cuisine without compromising on our principles. The setting is also marginally upscale. Ideal for date night.

The staff are fantastic, they explain what to try and how everything works. Be mindful of the portions as it is buffet style and you could fill up quickly if you don’t space out the courses,

**TIP: We went on a week day and the place was packed so a booking is a MUST. **

This Vietnam itinerary gave us a taste of authentic countryside life, far from tourist traps. From limestone mountains and serene rivers to friendly locals and ancient pagodas—it was pure magic.

Ninh Binh and its surrounding gems like Trang An and Mai Chau are must-visits, not just stopovers. The best part? It was easy on the wallet and big on experience.

We’ll be back for more.

I also loved how easy on the wallet it was - from food to stay to transport, very reasonable and the entire trip actually cost half of what it actually took us to fly there. Ha! The jagged mountains covered with dense shrubs, famous caves and vibrant Asian culture, the country stood out as a gem. If you choose the right area, you can have a sublime eco-tourism experience here. We look forward to visiting Vietnam again and exploring more of it’s alluring culture.

A Guide to Spending 24 hours In Stockholm, Sweden
A guide to making the most of your layover in Stockholm, Sweden

For our fourth anniversary back in 2017, we did a 10-day trip across Sweden, [Norway](/post/a-four-day-guide-to-spending-winter-in-norway), and Finland. You already know I love this part of the world – Scandinavia is absolutely beautiful. The first thing that pops into your mind when you think of Sweden is Ikea… or maybe Phoebe from FRIENDS trying to do a Swedish accent?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-1.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-3.jpg)

We only spent a day in Stockholm but we loved the cheerful vibes. The smell of fresh-baked bread, the festive cheer, the clean winter air… and all the happy, helpful people.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-5.jpg)


### **Must-See Places**

- Gamla Stan and Stortorget (a gorgeous street in Old Town)
Gamla Stan and Stortorget (a gorgeous street in Old Town)

- Riddarhuset
Riddarhuset

- Royal Palace
Royal Palace

- Vasa Museum
Vasa Museum

If you are staying for more than one night, you should also do Lake Mälaren and stop at[ ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birka)[Birka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birka), Sweden's first city.

**Best Time to Visit**

Since we went in the winter, it was very quiet. I would imagine it being just as beautiful in the summer. No matter when you travel, you will never be far away from adorable shops, cobblestone alleys, and clear blue skies.


**Visa**

We flew from Dubai straight to Stockholm. A[ ](https://www.schengenvisainfo.com/sweden-visa/)[Schengen Visa](https://www.schengenvisainfo.com/sweden-visa/) is needed to enter Sweden.


### **Language**

Swedish is the local language, but like most of Scandinavia, people speak good English.


### **Travel & Medical Insurance, Currency**

Highly recommended. Medical insurance is mandatory when you apply for a Schengen Visa.


### **Cost & Getting Around**

Since we only spent a day, we mostly walked around. Scandinavia is expensive, but you can pay by card in most places.


### **Safety**

Had no issues with safety.


### **Food**

Plenty of vegetarian and vegan options at cafes and restaurants.


### **Itinerary: Gamla Stan**

We landed at 5 a.m., grabbed a quick coffee, and took the train from the airport toward the old town. One of the best and most fun things to do on any European holiday is explore the colorful streets. By 7:30 a.m., we had a beautiful winter day ahead.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-6.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-8.jpg)

We started making our way through Södermalm and onwards to Gamla Stan, the city’s medieval heart. After seeing pictures of Gamla Stan, I was intrigued by how it looked eerily similar to Copenhagen. Needless to say, it was simply marvelous. This is the most picturesque (aka “gram-worthy”) square in Stockholm: Stortorget.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-10.jpg)

As someone naturally attracted to colors, I fell in love with the buildings here. The little corner was just gorgeous, filled with cute cafes and boutique shops. Since it was early, we were the only ones around, so we enjoyed our walk around the old town without crowds.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-11.jpg)

We continued walking down the narrow streets and picked up delicious treats – a must-try if you’re in Stockholm. Apart from the cobbled streets, we saw stunning medieval architecture dating back to the 13th century, including the[ ](https://www.svenskakyrkan.se/stockholm/domkyrkan)[Stockholm Cathedral](https://www.svenskakyrkan.se/stockholm/domkyrkan).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-5.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-14.jpg)

Since it was still pretty early in the day, we were the only ones here.. so we totally enjoyed our walk around the old town. It was the perfect place to spend a few hours exploring, with it's iconic landmarks and surreal river views. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-16.jpeg)

We continued walking down the narrow streets and picked up delicious treats. These were so good... definitely a must-try if you are in Stockholm.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-17.jpg)

Apart from the famous cobbled streets, we also got to see the stunning medieval architecture dating back to the 13th century in places like **the Stockholm Cathedral. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-18.jpg)


### **Stockholm City Hall**

Stockholm City Hall, where the[ ](https://www.nobelprize.org/about/nobel-prize-ceremony/)[Nobel Prize](https://www.nobelprize.org/about/nobel-prize-ceremony/) ceremony is held every year, is just a short walk from the train station. Guided tours are available for a deeper dive into the history.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-19.jpeg)


### **Royal Palace**

If you visit around midday, you can also catch the famous guard change at the[ ](https://www.kungligaslotten.se/english.html)[Royal Palace](https://www.kungligaslotten.se/english.html).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-22.jpeg)


### **Cafes & Restaurants**

The best part of Stockholm was its charming restaurants and cafes. We spent a few hours getting over jet lag while enjoying delicious local food.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-24.jpg)

**Vasa Museum**

If art and culture are your thing, don’t miss the[ ](https://www.vasamuseet.se/en)[Vasa Museum](https://www.vasamuseet.se/en), home to the iconic 17th-century ship.

It is never easy to see a city in 24 hours, but if you prioritize, you can do justice to this stunning city. It makes a great layover if you’re heading to other Scandinavian countries.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-cover.jpg)

People often overlook Stockholm in favor of Norway or Copenhagen. But as we walked over the bridges, we were blown away by the elegance of this colorful capital.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-guide-to-spending-24-hours-in-stockholm-sweden-26.jpg)

For our fourth anniversary back in 2017, we did a 10-day trip across Sweden, Norway, and Finland. You already know I love this part of the world – Scandinavia is absolutely beautiful. The first thing that pops into your mind when you think of Sweden is Ikea… or maybe Phoebe from FRIENDS trying to do a Swedish accent?

We only spent a day in Stockholm but we loved the cheerful vibes. The smell of fresh-baked bread, the festive cheer, the clean winter air… and all the happy, helpful people.

Must-See Places

  • Gamla Stan and Stortorget (a gorgeous street in Old Town) Gamla Stan and Stortorget (a gorgeous street in Old Town)

  • Riddarhuset Riddarhuset

  • Royal Palace Royal Palace

  • Vasa Museum Vasa Museum

If you are staying for more than one night, you should also do Lake Mälaren and stop at Birka, Sweden’s first city.

Best Time to Visit

Since we went in the winter, it was very quiet. I would imagine it being just as beautiful in the summer. No matter when you travel, you will never be far away from adorable shops, cobblestone alleys, and clear blue skies.

Visa

We flew from Dubai straight to Stockholm. A Schengen Visa is needed to enter Sweden.

Language

Swedish is the local language, but like most of Scandinavia, people speak good English.

Travel & Medical Insurance, Currency

Highly recommended. Medical insurance is mandatory when you apply for a Schengen Visa.

Cost & Getting Around

Since we only spent a day, we mostly walked around. Scandinavia is expensive, but you can pay by card in most places.

Safety

Had no issues with safety.

Food

Plenty of vegetarian and vegan options at cafes and restaurants.

Itinerary: Gamla Stan

We landed at 5 a.m., grabbed a quick coffee, and took the train from the airport toward the old town. One of the best and most fun things to do on any European holiday is explore the colorful streets. By 7:30 a.m., we had a beautiful winter day ahead.

We started making our way through Södermalm and onwards to Gamla Stan, the city’s medieval heart. After seeing pictures of Gamla Stan, I was intrigued by how it looked eerily similar to Copenhagen. Needless to say, it was simply marvelous. This is the most picturesque (aka “gram-worthy”) square in Stockholm: Stortorget.

As someone naturally attracted to colors, I fell in love with the buildings here. The little corner was just gorgeous, filled with cute cafes and boutique shops. Since it was early, we were the only ones around, so we enjoyed our walk around the old town without crowds.

We continued walking down the narrow streets and picked up delicious treats – a must-try if you’re in Stockholm. Apart from the cobbled streets, we saw stunning medieval architecture dating back to the 13th century, including the Stockholm Cathedral.

Since it was still pretty early in the day, we were the only ones here.. so we totally enjoyed our walk around the old town. It was the perfect place to spend a few hours exploring, with it’s iconic landmarks and surreal river views.

We continued walking down the narrow streets and picked up delicious treats. These were so good… definitely a must-try if you are in Stockholm.

Apart from the famous cobbled streets, we also got to see the stunning medieval architecture dating back to the 13th century in places like **the Stockholm Cathedral. **

Stockholm City Hall

Stockholm City Hall, where the Nobel Prize ceremony is held every year, is just a short walk from the train station. Guided tours are available for a deeper dive into the history.

Royal Palace

If you visit around midday, you can also catch the famous guard change at the Royal Palace.

Cafes & Restaurants

The best part of Stockholm was its charming restaurants and cafes. We spent a few hours getting over jet lag while enjoying delicious local food.

Vasa Museum

If art and culture are your thing, don’t miss the Vasa Museum, home to the iconic 17th-century ship.

It is never easy to see a city in 24 hours, but if you prioritize, you can do justice to this stunning city. It makes a great layover if you’re heading to other Scandinavian countries.

People often overlook Stockholm in favor of Norway or Copenhagen. But as we walked over the bridges, we were blown away by the elegance of this colorful capital.
