The Djúpalónssandur beach is different compared to the other beaches on the Southern Peninsula because it’s filled with lava pebbles. This place has an eerie vibe to it. I spent an hour walking along the beach but I wish I had the time to stay longer. This is also where I got to see for myself how cold the Atlantic Ocean is in June.

Our last stop for this mountain that I still can’t get over being so close to. You might have seen tons of pictures during the winter but believe me, nothing can compare to seeing it during the summer.

If it is your first time in the country, you have to do this to get a sense of the crazy diverse landscape this country offers.

This crater has red volcanic slopes and is nearly 3,000 year old. As a result of minerals from the volcanic soil, the water is a unique and strikingly vivid aquamarine shade of blue.

Gullfoss or The Golden Waterfall Gullfoss, meaning “golden waterfall,” is a massive waterfall on the river Hvita in western Iceland. The name is inspired by the phenomenon when glacial sediment in the water turns the falls golden in the sunlight. The water plunges 105 feet in two steps over a deep, dramatic crevasse in the river valley. As I climbed up, I was able to hear the falls well before I could see the wild, tumbling water. 

On the way back from Gullfoss, stopped by the beautiful Faxi waterfall, which is in the river Tungufljot..

**Icelandic horses ** Of course, I had to spot these cuties on our drive back to the city.. they are just like their country of origin - little but oh so fierce. They are smallish horses and are more like ponies.

Another 9 a.m. pick up - today we were going to be driving to the two most photographed falls in all of Iceland. Keep in mind that there’s an overwhelming amount of waterfalls, valleys, coastal retreats and more that months could be spent exploring them all; an amazing feat if you stop to think about it.

Fact: I think we all have a certain level of skepticism whenever we see stunning photos online or in calendars and books. In Iceland there’s no need for photo editing – it really is that gorgeous.

P.S. don’t miss the double rainbow!

For lunch, we stopped near the Vik area, Iceland’s southernmost village facing the Atlantic Ocean. What I loved most about this place were these lupines - they were in full bloom and were visible almost everywhere.

We then drove to the mind blowing black sand beach in Vik with surreal basalt column formations and these magnificent Reynisdrangar sea stacks.

On the way to Jokulsarlon, I saw enormous devastated areas of sandy plains covered with green moss. Not all effects of glaciation take place over thousands of years.

Pro Tip: take a small walk through the Skaftarhreppur as you drive up to Jokulsarlon.

At around 6, we were ready to call it a day at a lodge near the Jokulsarlon Glacier Lagoon. Stay at a cozy cabin and dinner was provided as a part of the tour.

DIAMOND BEACH** ** On the last day of the trip, we drove to the Diamond Beach (the main reason for my trip) In true Iceland form, the weather was cold and really windy even in summer BUT it was a clear day and the sunshine reflecting off of the glaciers on the beach was beyond beautiful. Our guide dropped us off to explore the area on our own for a few hours (honestly, I would have never left…)

I remember sitting here mesmerized at the way the glacial water gently lapped at black sand, and how the clouds appeared to be slicing the sky in two.

After lunch, we were ready for our Glacier Hike in the mighty Vatnajokull, the largest icecap in Europe. The hike was close to 3 hours. The hike was riddled with ice caves and sink holes. Blue Ice and Volcanic rocks appeared like a painting from another world.

I always pictured glaciers as big old blocks of ice floating in the middle of the ocean but Solheimajokull is a glacial tongue that is riddled with ice caves and sink holes. Blue ice and black volcanic rocks appeared like a painting from another world!

Lastly the **Gljufrabul. ** This hidden waterfall in Iceland deserves so much more recognition. I was envisioning a 10 feet drop when I walked to the other side of this waterfall but I was welcomed by an almost 30 feet drop that was spectacular.

**Pro tip: GET YOUR FEET WET. ** **You can walk all the way through to the other side in knee high water to get closer to the waterfall - just be careful with your camera. I left mine with a friend to avoid getting it wet. **

**FINAL THOUGHTS ** There are very few places that leave me with such vivid memories. Even after two years everything is as fresh as it was the day I visited. The afternoon glow, the “greener” than green moss, the fresh flowing water, the magical air.

This is a country where you will find surreal sights with every road bend. I took this trip all by myself - with complete strangers. On hindsight, I doubt I would have been as mesmerized by it had I gone with company. The landscape I saw all around me was beyond stunning and left me with goosebumps more often than not. When you’re in Iceland you just learn to appreciate the privilege you have to live on this beautiful planet.

I have always seen myself as a jungle person. The waterfalls, mountains, glaciers and pure beauty in you has me completely mesmerized. Thank for helping me find pieces of myself. Thank you the fantastic weather everyday. Forever grateful. Forever thankful. Till next time, I will be thinking of you, beautiful.

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

Turkey Travel Guide: A Journey Across Continents and History
An off beat guide to 7 beautiful days in Turkey. Itinerary for Istanbul, Cappadocia and Antalya.

Straddling two continents and the boundary between ancient and modern times, Turkey was an interesting destination. Was it one of my favorites? No, but it did surprise me more often than not.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-1.jpg)

I was determined not to visit the typical tourist spots on this trip, and to some extent, we did achieve that. It truly is amazing what you discover when you venture off the beaten track. We returned with wonderful memories and a thousand pictures. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-2.jpeg)

This **Turkey travel guide** will help with everything you need to plan your trip, from visa requirements to must-see attractions.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-3.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-5.jpg)

### Turkey Travel Guide: 7-Day Itinerary

- **Day 1** – Istanbul
- **Day 2** – Day trip to Princes' Islands and Bosphorus cruise
- **Day 3** – Day trip to Bursa
- **Day 4** – Cappadocia, part 1
- **Day 5** – Cappadocia, part 2 (including Cappadocia hot air balloon)
- **Day 6** – Antalya, part 1
- **Day 7** – Antalya, part 2 and departure

### Table of Contents

- [Best Time to Visit Turkey](#best-time-to-visit-turkey)
- [Know Before You Go](#know-before-you-go)
- [Where We Stayed](#where-we-stayed)
- [Costs and Getting Around](#costs-and-getting-around)
- [Vegan and Vegetarian Food](#vegan-and-vegetarian-food)
- [Highlights](#highlights): [Istanbul](#istanbul) · [Cappadocia](#cappadocia) · [Antalya](#antalya)
- [Final Thoughts](#final-thoughts)

## Best Time to Visit Turkey

I went in April – a lovely time to visit, at the start of spring. The weather was pleasant, making it easy to move around, and it was less touristy than expected. I would recommend visiting during this season for comfortable sightseeing and better experiences with local attractions like the **Blue Mosque Istanbul** and **Hagia Sophia Istanbul**.

## Know Before You Go

I flew direct with [Turkish Airlines](https://www.expedia.com/). Though expensive, I could not recommend it enough—food and in-flight entertainment were great.

We got a **Turkey visa for Indians** using our US visa. Be sure to double-check current [visa ](https://www.evisa.gov.tr/en/?utm_source=)requirements before traveling.

Turkish is the official language, but people also speak Arabic. In Istanbul, English is widely understood, but communication became more challenging once we left the city.

The Turkish Lira is the official currency. Dollars and Euros are sometimes accepted, but always check the exchange rate at the point of transaction.

## Where We Stayed

- **Istanbul** –[ Elysium Hotel](https://booking.tp.st/vJd3WZry)

- **Cappadocia** – [Tekkaya Cave Hotel](https://booking.tp.st/vJd3WZry)

- **Antalya** – [Best Western Khan](https://booking.tp.st/vJd3WZry)

## Costs and Getting Around

Turkey is not very expensive, but haggling is key. Local food is affordable, especially if you eat meat. Scams have increased for taxis and hotels in 2023, so stay vigilant. [Booking tours ahead of time ](https://tripadvisor.tp.st/kl8z2t3b)for major attractions like **Topkapi Palace** and a **Bosphorus cruise** can save time and stress.

Istanbul felt crowded, dirty, and occasionally unwelcoming, especially toward tourists from South Asia. Keep your belongings secure and avoid persistent street vendors.

## Vegan and Vegetarian Food

Vegetarian options are limited. We enjoyed Turkish pizza (**Pide**) and Baklava, along with surprisingly good Turkish ice cream.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-7.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-9.jpeg)

## Highlights

### Istanbul

#### Day 1 – Exploring Istanbul

Istanbul not Constantinople… this song was stuck in my head throughout our trip. Our first day was spent soaking in the history and traditions of the city while wandering streets filled with treats and trinkets.

**Blue Mosque Istanbul** – Iconic but under renovation during our visit. Six minarets, four supporting domes, and beautiful interiors. Trams stop at Sultanahmet, just a two-minute walk to the **Hagia Sophia Istanbul**.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-11.jpeg)

**Traver Tip:** you can take the TRAM to Sultanahemet stop and walk to the Blue Mosque... it is just another 2 minute walk to the Hagia Sofia. 

The mosque is supported by four domes or pillars but has six minarets (second only to the Mecca in Saudi Arabia). The inside of the mosque, though pretty, was crowded during the day so we decided not to spend too much time inside.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-12.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-14.jpeg)

**Hagia Sophia Istanbul** – Originally built as a basilica in the 4th century, rebuilt in the 6th, converted to a mosque, and now a museum. Stunning mosaics and architecture make this a must-visit spot. Pro Tip: climb the stairs for aerial views inside.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-16.jpeg)

I felt that the story of Hagia Sofia tells the story of this old city and of Turkey. Did you know that after Constantinople was conquered by the Ottomans, this impressive building was ordered to be converted from a church to a mosque? Now of course, it’s a decorated museum. 

**Tip:** head up the stairs for an aerial view of the inside of the museum. You can observe the intricate details on the walls... 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-17.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-19.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-21.jpg)

**Ortakoy Mosque & Bosphorus Views** – Sunrise or sunset along the Bosphorus makes for incredible photos.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-23.jpeg)

**Grand Bazaar Istanbul** – One of the largest markets in the world with 4,000 shops over 60 streets. Spices, lanterns, and colors everywhere.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-24.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-26.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-28.jpeg)

**Topkapi Palace** – Entrance: 40 TL. Must-see: Room of Relics, Imperial Treasury, and panoramic **Bosphorus** views.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-30.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-32.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-34.jpeg)

Day trip to **Princes’ Islands**: Heybeliada (horse carriage ride) and Buyukada (bikes and panoramic views). A peaceful escape from the busy city.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-36.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-38.jpeg)

Evening **Bosphorus cruise** – 7 PM to 11 PM, unlimited drinks and food. Views include Dolmabahce Palace, Bosphorus Bridge, Ortakoy, Rumeli Fortress, Fatih Bridge, Beylerbeyi Palace, and Maiden’s Tower.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-40.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-42.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-44.jpeg)

This Island felt like a paradise outside the city.. a place trapped in time. My favorite part was that these islands are traffic free and the only transport is either a bike, moped or horse-drawn carriages. Although just a few miles away from the Asian side of Istanbul, the Princes’ islands feel refreshingly different. They are charming, historic.. and a much needed escape from the touristy Istanbul.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-46.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-48.jpeg)

In the evening, we wanted to do a cruise on the Bosphorus. The cruise starts at 7 and ends close to 11 p.m. Includes unlimited drinks and food. We were able to catch all the historical places like Dolmabahce Palace, Bosphorus Bridge, Ortakoy, Rumeli Forthress , Fatih Bridge , Beylerbeyi Palace Maiden’s Tower. If you are looking for a party without the hassle, go for it BUT it could feel LONG after a whole day out. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-50.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-52.jpeg)

Crossed the Bosphorus bridge to the Asian side. Stops included:

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-54.jpeg)

- 600-year-old tree café. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-55.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-57.jpeg)

- Uludağ Mountain via cable car (2486 m above sea level)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-59.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-61.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-63.jpeg)

- Bursa town: Green Mosque & Green Mausoleum

A serene escape from Istanbul, rich with history and scenic views

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-65.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-67.jpeg)

Our last stop was the small town in Bursa where we saw the beautiful **Green mosque** and G**reen Mausoleum**. They were both built by 5th Ottoman Sultan Mehmet Çelebi in the 15th century. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-69.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-71.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-73.jpeg)

We had so many pictures to click in this pretty little town with it's little Alladin shops laden with carpets, spices and hot baths.. it was a lovely place to watch the sun go down. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-75.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-77.jpeg)

### Cappadocia

Morning flight to Cappadocia. Explored fairy chimneys, volcanic formations, and the **Nar Crater Lake**.

**Ortahisar Castle** – Tallest fairy chimney in Cappadocia, 78 m high.

**Three Beauties** – Mystical structures in Urgup with local legends.

**Underground Caves** – Incredible subterranean city exploration.

**Sunset Point, Red Valley** – Stunning sunset views.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-79.jpg)

There are day tours you can take to cover the city (Green, Blue and Red) .. since I had my own list, we hired a car through [MY TRIP TRAVEL AND TOURISM AGENCY TURKEY,](http://mytriptravelagency.com/). The service was spot on, you could message the owner, Cengiz at night and he would still respond. The car was also fantastic and spacious. Our driver was an old grandpa, kind and really sweet. He also clicked amazing pictures for us. If you are in Cappadocia and want to do a private tour (or need an airport pick up or drop), get in touch with these folks. They are awesome. 

**TRAVEL TIP: **If you fly into Kayseri, you will need to drive a good one hour to get to Goreme. You should arrange for an airport pick up with your tour agency or the hotel. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-80.jpeg)

If you have been reading about Cappadocia, you know it is famous for fairy chimneys. What are these chimneys? Well, years ago due to intense volcanic activity, there were strange rock formations.. but over thousands of years, the softer tufa eroded at a faster rate than he harder rock, creating these lunar-like spires with darker mushroom-like caps.  

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-81.jpeg)

However, Cappadocia isn’t just about the fairy chimneys and I was determined to see the best this place has to offer. Starting with the **Nar Crater Lake**, an hour drive from Goreme, is a crater lake that was formed when a volcano blew its top not so long ago. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-82.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-84.jpeg)

We went around 3 in the afternoon and had this place to ourselves - a definite win in my book.  
**Tip: **you can even walk to the other side of the lake along the paved path.. and catch the mountain up close. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-86.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-88.jpg)

**Ortahisar Castle **

Locals call this the tallest fairy chimney in Cappadocia (and it is, standing 78m high). It’s one of the most noticeable structures in town. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-90.jpeg)

**Three Beauties **

This unique structure lies in the Urgup region of the city... they are almost mystical (I will let the guide tell you about the legends behind these structures). 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-91.jpeg)

**Underground Caves**

To say that Cappadocia is a city below the ground is an understatement. It's really amazing to see how people in olden days made their lives under the rocks. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-92.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-94.jpeg)

**Sunset Point in Red Valley **
Our last stop was this beautiful spot in the red valley. It’s impossible to look at a sunset and not dream. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-96.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-98.jpeg)

It was actually super cloudy earlier that day but when we got to the sunset point, the clouds cleared and the sky lit up just for us. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-100.jpeg)

Hot air balloon canceled due to weather, but the city offered plenty to explore.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-101.jpeg)

**Pigeon Valley** – Historical pigeon “nests” carved into caves. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-102.jpg)

You can’t blame me for being fixated on Cappadocia. It’s an unreal feeling when you get to the city and start seeing the rock formations for yourself. There are so many beautiful vantage points here but my favorite was this one in the Pigeon Valley with a stellar view of the Uchisar Castle. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-103.jpeg)

**Uchisar Castle Hike** – Panoramic city views.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-104.jpg)

**Paradise Valley & Esentepe** – More panoramic views and hiking opportunities.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-105.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-107.jpeg)

**Esentepe**
For more panoramic views of the Cappadocia 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-109.jpeg)

**Pasabaglari or Fairy Chimneys** – Best views farther from the entrance to avoid crowds.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-110.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-112.jpeg)

**Pro Tip: **don't go to the chimneys closer to the entrance (it will packed with the selfie-stick "tourists"). As you walk further down, you will see a steeper but quieter hill with way better views of chimneys..

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-114.jpeg)

**Devrant Valley** – Lunar landscape, called Imagination Valley for its unique rock shapes.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-115.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-117.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-119.jpeg)

**Cappadocia hot air balloon** – Iconic and highly recommended for early morning sunrise views.

### Antalya

Flight to Antalya. Explored less touristy parts of Turkey:

**Perge** – Ancient Hellenistic city with Roman Theater and Greek wrestling school.

**Aspendos** – Oldest Amphitheater in Turkey, capacity 15,000.

Private tour recommended for convenience.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-121.jpg)

There is so much history in this place. I was fascinated by the vastly different landscapes in Turkey and wanted to explore the less touristy places in Antalya. We decided to hire a car  from the city and did a private tour in Kaleci to **Perge and Aspendos. **

**Tip: **keep in mind, this usually takes a whole day to do but we managed to squeeze it in under 5 hours. You can do a day tour to both places and Pamakulle, which I highly recommend. 

What’s your first thought when you see this picture? That it’s Greece? Or Rome? This was **Perge**. Just outside the city of Antalya you will find two stunning archaeological sites, now popular for their extensive ruins. Perge features a Roman Theater, an ancient Greek wrestling school as well as a church and temple dedicated to Greek deities.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-122.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-124.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-126.jpeg)

It is believed to have been founded by the heroes of the Trojan war.. it was the principal city in Hellenistic times. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-128.jpeg)

Our next stop was Aspendos, Turkey's oldest Amphitheater. Much like Perge, Aspendos is believed to have been founded by Greeks after the Trojan war. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-129.jpg)

It is essentially an Amphitheater with a capacity to hold over 15,000 people. These ruins are incredible and are definitely worth the drive up from the city. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-130.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-132.jpg)

**Disclaimer: **You can do both these places using public transport, though it will be time consuming and difficult to figure out. I would have done this had I been traveling with the husband.. but since I traveled with my parents I did not want the hassle of getting on and off a bus in the middle of nowhere. 

**Duden Waterfalls** – Upper waterfall in a lush valley; lower waterfall near the beach.

**Boat Cruise** – Scenic views along the turquoise coast.

**Kaleici (Old Town)** – Ottoman-style houses, cobbled lanes, Ataturk Statue, Hadrian's Gate, and Hidirilik Tower.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-134.jpg)

These waterfalls cascade in two different places - the upper waterfall is set in a lush valley and the lower waterfall is located near a beach. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-135.jpeg)

This place was like a painting. It actually felt a lot like Central Park - hidden in a cool valley right in the middle of the city. It's a peaceful haven away from the hustle and bustle...

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-136.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-138.jpeg)

When you are in Antalya, you have to do a **boat cruise during the day**. One a clear day, there is no better view than sailing along a blue sky.. as the world passes you by. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-140.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-142.jpeg)

After the boat ride, we spent afternoon in **old town of ‘Kaleici’** which winds up the hill from the harbor. It’s a gem of a place. Ringed by Roman walls, it’s all cobbled lanes, Ottoman-style houses and pretty cafés. Here we found the** Ataturk Statue in the Republic Square** - the symbol of Antalya and the founder of Antalya - Attalos. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-144.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-146.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-148.jpeg)

At dusk, we went to the infamous **Hadrian's gate in search of the Hidirilik Tower**. This didn't feel like Turkey but more like the alleys in Europe. Loved it. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-150.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-152.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-154.jpeg)

## Final Thoughts

Istanbul is full of history, but the real beauty lies outside the city. **Cappadocia** is otherworldly, with endless rock formations and fairy chimneys. **Antalya** was a pleasant surprise, offering history, beaches, and hidden gems. Turkey’s geographic diversity makes it worth multiple trips.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-156.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-158.jpeg)

It was Cappadocia that truly bowled me over. A land with endless sea of rock formations, it isn’t like anywhere else in the world. We felt like we had landed on another planet. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-160.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-162.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-164.jpeg)

Antalya was a surprise.. we did not expect to love it as much as we did but there is a lot more to do here. I wish we had more time to do it all but that's what I liked most about Turkey - the geographic diversity. Will be back for more.. 

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-166.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-168.jpeg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-off-beat-guide-to-7-days-in-turkeytravelguide-170.jpg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

Our travel journey—filled with immersive adventures, honest insights, and practical tips—has resonated deeply with readers who seek meaning beyond the map. From solo travel stories to offbeat discoveries, we’re proud to have been recognized by both Indian and international media.

[The National News ](https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/money/2022/07/28/how-to-save-money-so-you-can-afford-to-travel-more/),[Khaleej Times ](https://www.khaleejtimes.com/travel/uae-how-to-travel-the-world-despite-a-weak-passport-expats-share-tips),[Mint / Livemint](https://www.livemint.com/news/trends/this-visited-108-countries-while-holding-full-time-job/amp-11746251898742.html), [Times Now News](https://www.timesnownews.com/travel/travelogues/solo-trip-india-why-not-travel-vlogger-ankitha-rajendaran-on-travelling-solo-to-109-countries-and-the-joy-of-embracing-unseen-destinations-article-152114732)[,Outlook Traveller](https://www.outlooktraveller.com/destinations/international/travel-to-syria-uzbekistan-and-iran), [Hindustan Times ](https://www.hindustantimes.com/lifestyle/travel/woman-who-travelled-solo-to-109-countries-7-continents-shares-her-top-travel-hacks-every-female-should-know-101747298629619.html),[MSN India](https://www.msn.com/en-in/money/topstories/meet-ankita-rajendaran-the-woman-who-travelled-to-108-countries-while-working-full-time/ar-AA1DUMAu?ocid=spr_trending&businessvertical=News), [The Established ](https://www.theestablished.com/self/health/the-best-company-is-your-ownand-indian-youth-is-agreeing)[NDTV](https://www.ndtv.com/video/news/news/couple-who-have-explored-over-80-countries-share-tips-for-budget-travel-729052), [Times of India](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/life-style/travel/destinations/7-countries-you-should-visit-if-you-want-to-travel-differently/photostory/121286098.cms), [SheThePeople](https://www.shethepeople.tv/interviews/ankitha-rajendaran-why-not-book-interview-9426506)

Straddling two continents and the boundary between ancient and modern times, Turkey was an interesting destination. Was it one of my favorites? No, but it did surprise me more often than not.

I was determined not to visit the typical tourist spots on this trip, and to some extent, we did achieve that. It truly is amazing what you discover when you venture off the beaten track. We returned with wonderful memories and a thousand pictures.

This Turkey travel guide will help with everything you need to plan your trip, from visa requirements to must-see attractions.

Turkey Travel Guide: 7-Day Itinerary

  • Day 1 – Istanbul
  • Day 2 – Day trip to Princes’ Islands and Bosphorus cruise
  • Day 3 – Day trip to Bursa
  • Day 4 – Cappadocia, part 1
  • Day 5 – Cappadocia, part 2 (including Cappadocia hot air balloon)
  • Day 6 – Antalya, part 1
  • Day 7 – Antalya, part 2 and departure

Table of Contents

  • Best Time to Visit Turkey
  • Know Before You Go
  • Where We Stayed
  • Costs and Getting Around
  • Vegan and Vegetarian Food
  • Highlights: Istanbul · Cappadocia · Antalya
  • Final Thoughts

Best Time to Visit Turkey

I went in April – a lovely time to visit, at the start of spring. The weather was pleasant, making it easy to move around, and it was less touristy than expected. I would recommend visiting during this season for comfortable sightseeing and better experiences with local attractions like the Blue Mosque Istanbul and Hagia Sophia Istanbul.

Know Before You Go

I flew direct with Turkish Airlines. Though expensive, I could not recommend it enough—food and in-flight entertainment were great.

We got a Turkey visa for Indians using our US visa. Be sure to double-check current visa requirements before traveling.

Turkish is the official language, but people also speak Arabic. In Istanbul, English is widely understood, but communication became more challenging once we left the city.

The Turkish Lira is the official currency. Dollars and Euros are sometimes accepted, but always check the exchange rate at the point of transaction.

Where We Stayed

  • Istanbul – Elysium Hotel

  • Cappadocia – Tekkaya Cave Hotel

  • Antalya – Best Western Khan

Costs and Getting Around

Turkey is not very expensive, but haggling is key. Local food is affordable, especially if you eat meat. Scams have increased for taxis and hotels in 2023, so stay vigilant. Booking tours ahead of time for major attractions like Topkapi Palace and a Bosphorus cruise can save time and stress.

Istanbul felt crowded, dirty, and occasionally unwelcoming, especially toward tourists from South Asia. Keep your belongings secure and avoid persistent street vendors.

Vegan and Vegetarian Food

Vegetarian options are limited. We enjoyed Turkish pizza (Pide) and Baklava, along with surprisingly good Turkish ice cream.

Highlights

Istanbul

Day 1 – Exploring Istanbul

Istanbul not Constantinople… this song was stuck in my head throughout our trip. Our first day was spent soaking in the history and traditions of the city while wandering streets filled with treats and trinkets.

Blue Mosque Istanbul – Iconic but under renovation during our visit. Six minarets, four supporting domes, and beautiful interiors. Trams stop at Sultanahmet, just a two-minute walk to the Hagia Sophia Istanbul.

Traver Tip: you can take the TRAM to Sultanahemet stop and walk to the Blue Mosque… it is just another 2 minute walk to the Hagia Sofia.

The mosque is supported by four domes or pillars but has six minarets (second only to the Mecca in Saudi Arabia). The inside of the mosque, though pretty, was crowded during the day so we decided not to spend too much time inside.

Hagia Sophia Istanbul – Originally built as a basilica in the 4th century, rebuilt in the 6th, converted to a mosque, and now a museum. Stunning mosaics and architecture make this a must-visit spot. Pro Tip: climb the stairs for aerial views inside.

I felt that the story of Hagia Sofia tells the story of this old city and of Turkey. Did you know that after Constantinople was conquered by the Ottomans, this impressive building was ordered to be converted from a church to a mosque? Now of course, it’s a decorated museum.

Tip: head up the stairs for an aerial view of the inside of the museum. You can observe the intricate details on the walls…

Ortakoy Mosque & Bosphorus Views – Sunrise or sunset along the Bosphorus makes for incredible photos.

Grand Bazaar Istanbul – One of the largest markets in the world with 4,000 shops over 60 streets. Spices, lanterns, and colors everywhere.

Topkapi Palace – Entrance: 40 TL. Must-see: Room of Relics, Imperial Treasury, and panoramic Bosphorus views.

Day trip to Princes’ Islands: Heybeliada (horse carriage ride) and Buyukada (bikes and panoramic views). A peaceful escape from the busy city.

Evening Bosphorus cruise – 7 PM to 11 PM, unlimited drinks and food. Views include Dolmabahce Palace, Bosphorus Bridge, Ortakoy, Rumeli Fortress, Fatih Bridge, Beylerbeyi Palace, and Maiden’s Tower.

This Island felt like a paradise outside the city.. a place trapped in time. My favorite part was that these islands are traffic free and the only transport is either a bike, moped or horse-drawn carriages. Although just a few miles away from the Asian side of Istanbul, the Princes’ islands feel refreshingly different. They are charming, historic.. and a much needed escape from the touristy Istanbul.

In the evening, we wanted to do a cruise on the Bosphorus. The cruise starts at 7 and ends close to 11 p.m. Includes unlimited drinks and food. We were able to catch all the historical places like Dolmabahce Palace, Bosphorus Bridge, Ortakoy, Rumeli Forthress , Fatih Bridge , Beylerbeyi Palace Maiden’s Tower. If you are looking for a party without the hassle, go for it BUT it could feel LONG after a whole day out.

Crossed the Bosphorus bridge to the Asian side. Stops included:

  • 600-year-old tree café. 

  • Uludağ Mountain via cable car (2486 m above sea level)

  • Bursa town: Green Mosque & Green Mausoleum

A serene escape from Istanbul, rich with history and scenic views

Our last stop was the small town in Bursa where we saw the beautiful Green mosque and Green Mausoleum. They were both built by 5th Ottoman Sultan Mehmet Çelebi in the 15th century.

We had so many pictures to click in this pretty little town with it’s little Alladin shops laden with carpets, spices and hot baths.. it was a lovely place to watch the sun go down.

Cappadocia

Morning flight to Cappadocia. Explored fairy chimneys, volcanic formations, and the Nar Crater Lake.

Ortahisar Castle – Tallest fairy chimney in Cappadocia, 78 m high.

Three Beauties – Mystical structures in Urgup with local legends.

Underground Caves – Incredible subterranean city exploration.

Sunset Point, Red Valley – Stunning sunset views.

There are day tours you can take to cover the city (Green, Blue and Red) .. since I had my own list, we hired a car through MY TRIP TRAVEL AND TOURISM AGENCY TURKEY,. The service was spot on, you could message the owner, Cengiz at night and he would still respond. The car was also fantastic and spacious. Our driver was an old grandpa, kind and really sweet. He also clicked amazing pictures for us. If you are in Cappadocia and want to do a private tour (or need an airport pick up or drop), get in touch with these folks. They are awesome.

**TRAVEL TIP: **If you fly into Kayseri, you will need to drive a good one hour to get to Goreme. You should arrange for an airport pick up with your tour agency or the hotel.

If you have been reading about Cappadocia, you know it is famous for fairy chimneys. What are these chimneys? Well, years ago due to intense volcanic activity, there were strange rock formations.. but over thousands of years, the softer tufa eroded at a faster rate than he harder rock, creating these lunar-like spires with darker mushroom-like caps.

However, Cappadocia isn’t just about the fairy chimneys and I was determined to see the best this place has to offer. Starting with the Nar Crater Lake, an hour drive from Goreme, is a crater lake that was formed when a volcano blew its top not so long ago.

We went around 3 in the afternoon and had this place to ourselves - a definite win in my book.
**Tip: **you can even walk to the other side of the lake along the paved path.. and catch the mountain up close.

**Ortahisar Castle **

Locals call this the tallest fairy chimney in Cappadocia (and it is, standing 78m high). It’s one of the most noticeable structures in town.

**Three Beauties **

This unique structure lies in the Urgup region of the city… they are almost mystical (I will let the guide tell you about the legends behind these structures).

Underground Caves

To say that Cappadocia is a city below the ground is an understatement. It’s really amazing to see how people in olden days made their lives under the rocks.

**Sunset Point in Red Valley ** Our last stop was this beautiful spot in the red valley. It’s impossible to look at a sunset and not dream.

It was actually super cloudy earlier that day but when we got to the sunset point, the clouds cleared and the sky lit up just for us.

Hot air balloon canceled due to weather, but the city offered plenty to explore.

Pigeon Valley – Historical pigeon “nests” carved into caves.

You can’t blame me for being fixated on Cappadocia. It’s an unreal feeling when you get to the city and start seeing the rock formations for yourself. There are so many beautiful vantage points here but my favorite was this one in the Pigeon Valley with a stellar view of the Uchisar Castle.

Uchisar Castle Hike – Panoramic city views.

Paradise Valley & Esentepe – More panoramic views and hiking opportunities.

Esentepe For more panoramic views of the Cappadocia

Pasabaglari or Fairy Chimneys – Best views farther from the entrance to avoid crowds.

**Pro Tip: **don’t go to the chimneys closer to the entrance (it will packed with the selfie-stick “tourists”). As you walk further down, you will see a steeper but quieter hill with way better views of chimneys..

Devrant Valley – Lunar landscape, called Imagination Valley for its unique rock shapes.

Cappadocia hot air balloon – Iconic and highly recommended for early morning sunrise views.

Antalya

Flight to Antalya. Explored less touristy parts of Turkey:

Perge – Ancient Hellenistic city with Roman Theater and Greek wrestling school.

Aspendos – Oldest Amphitheater in Turkey, capacity 15,000.

Private tour recommended for convenience.

There is so much history in this place. I was fascinated by the vastly different landscapes in Turkey and wanted to explore the less touristy places in Antalya. We decided to hire a car from the city and did a private tour in Kaleci to **Perge and Aspendos. **

**Tip: **keep in mind, this usually takes a whole day to do but we managed to squeeze it in under 5 hours. You can do a day tour to both places and Pamakulle, which I highly recommend.

What’s your first thought when you see this picture? That it’s Greece? Or Rome? This was Perge. Just outside the city of Antalya you will find two stunning archaeological sites, now popular for their extensive ruins. Perge features a Roman Theater, an ancient Greek wrestling school as well as a church and temple dedicated to Greek deities.

It is believed to have been founded by the heroes of the Trojan war.. it was the principal city in Hellenistic times.

Our next stop was Aspendos, Turkey’s oldest Amphitheater. Much like Perge, Aspendos is believed to have been founded by Greeks after the Trojan war.

It is essentially an Amphitheater with a capacity to hold over 15,000 people. These ruins are incredible and are definitely worth the drive up from the city.

**Disclaimer: **You can do both these places using public transport, though it will be time consuming and difficult to figure out. I would have done this had I been traveling with the husband.. but since I traveled with my parents I did not want the hassle of getting on and off a bus in the middle of nowhere.

Duden Waterfalls – Upper waterfall in a lush valley; lower waterfall near the beach.

Boat Cruise – Scenic views along the turquoise coast.

Kaleici (Old Town) – Ottoman-style houses, cobbled lanes, Ataturk Statue, Hadrian’s Gate, and Hidirilik Tower.

These waterfalls cascade in two different places - the upper waterfall is set in a lush valley and the lower waterfall is located near a beach.

This place was like a painting. It actually felt a lot like Central Park - hidden in a cool valley right in the middle of the city. It’s a peaceful haven away from the hustle and bustle…

When you are in Antalya, you have to do a boat cruise during the day. One a clear day, there is no better view than sailing along a blue sky.. as the world passes you by.

After the boat ride, we spent afternoon in old town of ‘Kaleici’ which winds up the hill from the harbor. It’s a gem of a place. Ringed by Roman walls, it’s all cobbled lanes, Ottoman-style houses and pretty cafés. Here we found the** Ataturk Statue in the Republic Square** - the symbol of Antalya and the founder of Antalya - Attalos.

At dusk, we went to the infamous Hadrian’s gate in search of the Hidirilik Tower. This didn’t feel like Turkey but more like the alleys in Europe. Loved it.

Final Thoughts

Istanbul is full of history, but the real beauty lies outside the city. Cappadocia is otherworldly, with endless rock formations and fairy chimneys. Antalya was a pleasant surprise, offering history, beaches, and hidden gems. Turkey’s geographic diversity makes it worth multiple trips.

It was Cappadocia that truly bowled me over. A land with endless sea of rock formations, it isn’t like anywhere else in the world. We felt like we had landed on another planet.

Antalya was a surprise.. we did not expect to love it as much as we did but there is a lot more to do here. I wish we had more time to do it all but that’s what I liked most about Turkey - the geographic diversity. Will be back for more..

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

Armenia Visa for Indians + 7 Day Itinerary & Travel Guide
November 2021 On this blog post, we talk about the less frequently visited Armenia (unless you live in Dubai and hop on a flight to go...

November 2021

On this blog post, we talk about the less frequently visited Armenia (unless you live in Dubai and hop on a flight to go here for sport). After all, a short 3 hour flight from Dubai will take you to this small country in the Caucasus region.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-1.jpg)

Armenia is the second country I have visited in this region that is nestled in the crossroads between Eastern Europe and Western Asia.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-2.jpg)

It is also surprisingly historic and it is believed that they were one of the first countries in the world to adopt Christianity as their state religion. Be prepared for a whole lot of beautiful **Armenia monasteries** scattered around the region amongst dense national park forests and snow-capped alps.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-3.jpg)

So let me sum up the trip for you with a list of must-see places!

From Dubai, I flew directly with [Fly Dubai.](https://www.expedia.com/?locale=en_US&siteid=4401&semcid=AE.B.GOOGLE.BT-c-EN.GT&semdtl=a118255096623.b1140881547719.g1kwd-12670071.e1c.m1CjwKCAiAtt2tBhBDEiwALZuhACVHjFPfsfGYfe3jjpdhUQSTFwlZW8jESR1S5Licds1WGKaIrlzOWBoCwxAQAvD_BwE.r19ceb9faab37e8ca276f38bcaf0ac8520664e2246a5fde8ee1016452c1cd03caf.c1HcZGe3UbzM66omL2N8lvMA.j11000013.k1.d1624927061187.h1e.i1.l1.n1.o1.p1.q1.s1.t1.x1.f1.u1.v1.w1&gad_source=1&gclid=CjwKCAiAtt2tBhBDEiwALZuhACVHjFPfsfGYfe3jjpdhUQSTFwlZW8jESR1S5Licds1WGKaIrlzOWBoCwxAQAvD_BwE)

You can no longer get a visa on arrival. You will need a visa. You can apply for this yourself. **Armenia visa for Indians** requires applying in advance. Heads up - sometimes, you could be rejected if you have an Indian passport. The agency I went with applied for me. You can directly apply for it [here](https://evisa.mfa.am/) too.

NOTE: Due to the conflicts in the region, if by any chance you have been to Azerbaijan in the recent past, expect to be questioned about this at the immigration counter. Keep your responses short and be clear about the reasons for your trip there. if you went for tourist reasons, explain that clearly and be ready to produce a detailed set of documents for your trip to Armenia from **Armenia itinerary**, hotel reservations, and return ticket.

Expect your typical developing country culture. Most certainly expect a few leers from strange men on the street if you decide to walk outdoors in a fancy dress in the city. Go with your guide/driver and be mindful of your surroundings — especially if you’re considering **Armenia **

April, May, June and October, November is best due to the slightly cooler weather. The **best time to visit Armenia** really depends on whether you prefer snowy landscapes or mild spring hikes.

Everyone is helpful and fluent in English.

Travel Insurance is highly recommended. USD accepted at all resorts. Carry local currency for small purchases.

For this trip with an amazing local guide. The owner, set us up wonderfully. No hassles and extremely hands-on. They have a location in Yerevan, Armenia as well as Dubai. Smooth transactions and she was happy to accommodate any requests at a marginal cost.

This helped us cover all the highlights quickly as part of our **Armenia travel guide** experience.

Food is ridiculously cheap, so is transportation and hotel. Including hotel, flights and transportation, the trip should cost no more than $650-800, depending on where you stay and time/length of travel.

The food was great in the city. There were a lot of wonderful upscale restaurants just around Ramada. Some so fancy that we needed a reservation on a weekday. Ha!

The food on the road was a miss, as always. Nothing special. On our last night though, we went to a beautiful restaurant called Zaitoon in downtown Yerevan. The ambiance and service was upbeat accompanied by some delicious vegan food. Definitely give it a try.

Top tip: we found this joint called Fresh House. They have some epic milkshakes, coffee and hot chocolate. It's just a small shack in the middle of the city.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-12.jpg)

[Ramada in Yerevan](https://www.booking.com/searchresults.en-gb.html?aid=356980&label=gog235jc-1DCAsoB0IgcmFtYWRhLXN1aXRlcy1ieS13eW5kaGFtLXllcmV2YW5IM1gDaAKIAQGYAQm4ARfIAQzYAQPoAQGIAgGoAgO4AqzL3q0GwAIB0gIkZmI2ODFjYjctZTk0MS00NzgzLThkY2UtNTNkZDFhNjM5YTE32AIE4AIB&no_rooms=1&highlighted_hotels=4807580&redirected=1&city=-2325645&hlrd=user_sh&group_adults=2&source=hotel&group_children=0&expand_sb=1&keep_landing=1&sid=f08c842dbab42a93655ecaba13793263). Your usual four star hotel with the perks.Breakfast was good and had a wide veggie spread. Even better if you have eggs, but was a loss for me.

Up the mountains, we stay at[ Harsnadzor Eco Resort](https://www.booking.com/searchresults.en-gb.html?aid=311984&label=harsnadzor-MWGkvQn24%2A17PU0Gsxm52gS393024905675%3Apl%3Ata%3Ap1%3Ap2%3Aac%3Aap%3Aneg%3Afi%3Atikwd-301766326747%3Alp1000013%3Ali%3Adec%3Adm%3Appccp%3DUmFuZG9tSVYkc2RlIyh9YXwxhKG0pUU-mcMVT-JwQpc&highlighted_hotels=1180862&redirected=1&city=-2324769&hlrd=no_dates&source=hotel&expand_sb=1&keep_landing=1&sid=adc025dbc0f949bf975d19222a2d3d55) . Honestly, having stayed at a few other magnificent locations just in the last 12 months, this one wasn't close to being the best. The views were pretty epic, I will give you that. So if just cute pictures are your thing, by all means go for it. If not, I don't think there was anything to rave about... but hey, that's just me!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-14.jpg)

Charents Arch, Garni Temple, **Symphony of Stones Armenia**. Dinner at India Palace.

After a fairly quick immigration, we were picked up by our driver. Went straight to the airport to freshen up. Then drive to Charents Arch.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-15.jpg)

This place is named after a famous Armenian Poet. Of course, it is also known for its stunning views of the biblical Mount Ararat. There is not much to do around here but don't miss out on those postcard perfect shots.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-20.jpg)

Next stop, Garni temple. It is believed that this a pagan temple built in 1st century AD.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-22.jpg)

What I loved about it was the surprising Greek style architecture and the vast magnificent views of the surrounding mountains.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-26.jpg)

Quite the show stopper with intricate details on every column. Don't miss this one.

Next stop was to my favorite spot of the day - Next stop was to my favorite spot of the day - **Symphony of Stones Armenia**. Not a lot of guides include it but being just a stones throw away from Garni, it is a must-see in this side of the country.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-28.jpg)

This is sort of like a natural wonder in Armenia. Basically giant basalt rock formations in a hexagonal cylindrical shape. You can walk close to them and really admire nature - it really is quite magical, right?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-31.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-33.jpg)

This place is aptly named Symphony of Stones because the Agat river flows just beside the monument.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-35.jpg)

Back to the city. Dinner was at India Palace (just around the corner from Ramada), since we were cranky from no sleep and had no energy left to explore the city!

Today we explored the Southern region of the country. Starting with Noravank Monastery. Just a short drive from Yerevan, it was so busy even at 11 a.m. in the morning. Expect crowds no matter when you go.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-36.jpg)

I read that apart from it's centuries old history, what sets Noravank apart is that unlike some other churches in the region, Noravank was spared destruction when the Mongols swept through ancient Armenia in the 13th century and, hence, the intricate stonework that decorates the exterior of the complex is still relatively intact.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-37.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-cover.jpg)

The stairs are narrow, making it a little tricky to go up and even more so while going down. It is worth climbing up to the top for a closer look at the red-rocky gorge that surrounds this monastery.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-43.jpg)

Next stop post lunch was Shaki Waterfall. This is one of the most picturesque places in Armenia for me. The waterfall is easily accessible. It was formed when the Vorotan river flowed from 18m height.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-44.jpg)

Fun Travel Tip: There is a small steep hill that you can climb toward the right of the waterfall. Go up for even better views of the cascading falls from the top. Added perks - you will have it all to yourself. It was blissful to just be there without another soul, just listening to the sound of flowing water.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-47.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-49.jpg)

Our last stop was going up **Wings of Tatev Cable Car** - the world's longest reversible cable way in the world, recorded in the Guinness Books of Records. This takes you right up to the Tatev Monastery, built in the 9th century. It also happens to be one of the most far-flung ones in the country.. which means most people don’t make it here.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-2.jpg)

I recommend you don’t miss it. The view is breathtaking, with the monastery set high upon a hill over looking a massive valley and old village. Would you look at that? It’s like walking back in time!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-52.jpg)

Stayed at the eco resort. It was pretty cold and the icy blankets did nothing to keep us warm. There was a heater but it wasn't enough for the big room.

Exploring a few hidden places like this in an otherwise touristy country was the highlight of my trip to Armenia. Here I am, sinking my hands into the warm mineral water at the Devil’s pool.

The picturesque natural bridge is situated in this deep canyon, surrounded with many mountainous springs, becoming a geyser in the winter.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-53.jpg)

You can also see stalactites made by the mineral water hanging from the edges of this stone arch, and there was a beautiful falls rushing down from the rock caves under the natural bridge!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-54.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-56.jpg)

There was absolutely no trail here, some daredevilery went into getting to this point, so it was a day I thoroughly enjoyed. Go as far in as your brave heart and great shoes take you.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-58.jpg)

I loved all the random pitstops along the way. What a beautiful mountainous setting on every corner.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-59.jpg)

We then went to the town of Jermuk and waterfall, this was huge miss for me. Skip it.

Post lunch, we went to Areni Cave. One of the world's oldest winery. Excavations unearthed large complexes which showed that these complexes were once used for worship and economic purposes.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-60.jpg)

Last stop for the day was a wine tasting at a local join. I don't drink alcohol but I hear the pomegranate wine is a must-try.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-61.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-63.jpg)

Back to Ramada in Yerevan for the last two nights of stay.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-67.jpg)

This is a lot like ski resort in Georgia. You go up but there is not a lot to do. Just take nice pictures with the snow and enjoy more mountain views.


We then drove to Dilijan Monastery. The town of Dilijan itself is worth a visit, so is the national park but we didn't have time for that... because we wanted to head to our next stop for sunset.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-69.jpg)

Behold the largest high-altitude lakes in Eurasia. **Lake Sevan Armenia** is a massive body of water, which I found to my delight can look particularly beautiful in the late afternoon light. There are many reasons to visit Sevan but most of all it should be for it's breathtaking beauty. It is quite a sight - any time of day.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-70.jpg)

Of course, along with the lake you also find the Sevanavank Monastery. You can easily walk up to the church. This place offers some of the most unobstructed views of the lake. Just be prepared for the crazy cold and lots of wind!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-71.jpg)

With the slightly overcast clouds over the monument, it was quite the sight to behold.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-72.jpg)

As we made our way back to the city, we decided to step out for a stroll. Just a mile away from our hotel was the iconic Republic Square of **Yerevan travel guide** highlights. This part of the city is actually established when Armenia was part of the Soviet Union. The theme was a sort of "new look" Armenia. You cannot miss the pink tuff stones that line these streets.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-73.jpg)

Our last morning was relaxed and lazy. We started at 11 and made our way to the Yerevan market to pick a few souvenirs. The market was pretty big and really gorgeous to walk in with the fall foliage on either side of the street. You will find things big and small - though it is not easy haggle to with the Armenians!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-74.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-76.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-78.jpg)

Next stop was the Cascade tower for panoramic views of the city. You will need to climb up 1300 steps to get there though


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-80.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-84.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-86.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-88.jpg)

After that workout, we headed straight for lunch at a local restaurant on the way to the airport.

Last stop was the Zvartnots temple. This temple was constructed in the 7th century, it is mostly ruins now but you can still see the footprints of this four shells church. One of its most iconic feature is that the columns are with Ionic capitals, commonly seen in the Roman empire. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-90.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-92.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-94.jpg)

There isn’t much to do around there. However, I highly recommend adding this one to your itinerary on the way to the airport. Since it was hazy, it was difficult to capture Mount Ararat but if you zoom in behind me, you can see faint traces of it.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-96.jpg)

You don't need a lot of time to explore this country. It is compact and easy to travel around. It isn't necessarily a hidden gem any more but still worth a visit if you are looking for a hassle-free getaway for a few days.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-97.jpg)

It is a wonderful place if you want a mix of the bustling city life, with a whole lot of history and culture thrown in to the mix.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-98.jpg)

Last but not the least, if you love mountains, you will dig this country. You are only a breath away from one at any given time!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-99.jpg)

How long to spend? Well you can spend 5 days or 5 months, it isn't a particularly vast country but it does hold its own little surprises. I hope this write up inspired you to visit this grand country. Ta!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/armenia-visa-for-indians-guide-100.jpg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers, Anki

On this blog post, we talk about the less frequently visited Armenia (unless you live in Dubai and hop on a flight to go here for sport). After all, a short 3 hour flight from Dubai will take you to this small country in the Caucasus region.

Armenia is the second country I have visited in this region that is nestled in the crossroads between Eastern Europe and Western Asia.

It is also surprisingly historic and it is believed that they were one of the first countries in the world to adopt Christianity as their state religion. Be prepared for a whole lot of beautiful Armenia monasteries scattered around the region amongst dense national park forests and snow-capped alps.

So let me sum up the trip for you with a list of must-see places!

From Dubai, I flew directly with Fly Dubai.

You can no longer get a visa on arrival. You will need a visa. You can apply for this yourself. Armenia visa for Indians requires applying in advance. Heads up - sometimes, you could be rejected if you have an Indian passport. The agency I went with applied for me. You can directly apply for it here too.

NOTE: Due to the conflicts in the region, if by any chance you have been to Azerbaijan in the recent past, expect to be questioned about this at the immigration counter. Keep your responses short and be clear about the reasons for your trip there. if you went for tourist reasons, explain that clearly and be ready to produce a detailed set of documents for your trip to Armenia from Armenia itinerary, hotel reservations, and return ticket.

Expect your typical developing country culture. Most certainly expect a few leers from strange men on the street if you decide to walk outdoors in a fancy dress in the city. Go with your guide/driver and be mindful of your surroundings — especially if you’re considering **Armenia **

April, May, June and October, November is best due to the slightly cooler weather. The best time to visit Armenia really depends on whether you prefer snowy landscapes or mild spring hikes.

Everyone is helpful and fluent in English.

Travel Insurance is highly recommended. USD accepted at all resorts. Carry local currency for small purchases.

For this trip with an amazing local guide. The owner, set us up wonderfully. No hassles and extremely hands-on. They have a location in Yerevan, Armenia as well as Dubai. Smooth transactions and she was happy to accommodate any requests at a marginal cost.

This helped us cover all the highlights quickly as part of our Armenia travel guide experience.

Food is ridiculously cheap, so is transportation and hotel. Including hotel, flights and transportation, the trip should cost no more than $650-800, depending on where you stay and time/length of travel.

The food was great in the city. There were a lot of wonderful upscale restaurants just around Ramada. Some so fancy that we needed a reservation on a weekday. Ha!

The food on the road was a miss, as always. Nothing special. On our last night though, we went to a beautiful restaurant called Zaitoon in downtown Yerevan. The ambiance and service was upbeat accompanied by some delicious vegan food. Definitely give it a try.

Top tip: we found this joint called Fresh House. They have some epic milkshakes, coffee and hot chocolate. It’s just a small shack in the middle of the city.

Ramada in Yerevan. Your usual four star hotel with the perks.Breakfast was good and had a wide veggie spread. Even better if you have eggs, but was a loss for me.

Up the mountains, we stay at Harsnadzor Eco Resort . Honestly, having stayed at a few other magnificent locations just in the last 12 months, this one wasn’t close to being the best. The views were pretty epic, I will give you that. So if just cute pictures are your thing, by all means go for it. If not, I don’t think there was anything to rave about… but hey, that’s just me!

Charents Arch, Garni Temple, Symphony of Stones Armenia. Dinner at India Palace.

After a fairly quick immigration, we were picked up by our driver. Went straight to the airport to freshen up. Then drive to Charents Arch.

This place is named after a famous Armenian Poet. Of course, it is also known for its stunning views of the biblical Mount Ararat. There is not much to do around here but don’t miss out on those postcard perfect shots.

Next stop, Garni temple. It is believed that this a pagan temple built in 1st century AD.

What I loved about it was the surprising Greek style architecture and the vast magnificent views of the surrounding mountains.

Quite the show stopper with intricate details on every column. Don’t miss this one.

Next stop was to my favorite spot of the day - Next stop was to my favorite spot of the day - Symphony of Stones Armenia. Not a lot of guides include it but being just a stones throw away from Garni, it is a must-see in this side of the country.

This is sort of like a natural wonder in Armenia. Basically giant basalt rock formations in a hexagonal cylindrical shape. You can walk close to them and really admire nature - it really is quite magical, right?

This place is aptly named Symphony of Stones because the Agat river flows just beside the monument.

Back to the city. Dinner was at India Palace (just around the corner from Ramada), since we were cranky from no sleep and had no energy left to explore the city!

Today we explored the Southern region of the country. Starting with Noravank Monastery. Just a short drive from Yerevan, it was so busy even at 11 a.m. in the morning. Expect crowds no matter when you go.

I read that apart from it’s centuries old history, what sets Noravank apart is that unlike some other churches in the region, Noravank was spared destruction when the Mongols swept through ancient Armenia in the 13th century and, hence, the intricate stonework that decorates the exterior of the complex is still relatively intact.

I recommend you don’t miss it. The view is breathtaking, with the monastery set high upon a hill over looking a massive valley and old village. Would you look at that? It’s like walking back in time!

Costa Rica Travel Guide: Best Time to Visit: Explore La Fortuna, Arenal Volcano & Rio Celeste
Over the summer, we set off on a South American adventure, visiting Ecuador, Colombia, and finally Costa Rica. The draw to Costa Rica was...

Over the summer, we set off on a South American adventure, visiting Ecuador, Colombia, and finally Costa Rica. The draw to Costa Rica was twofold: the stunning waterfalls and the country’s dedication to eco-tourism. Eco-friendly travel is increasingly popular here, and Costa Rica perfectly combines adventure, nature, and sustainability.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-itinerary-to-mesmerizing-costa-rica-costaricatravelguide-1.jpeg)

In this **Costa Rica travel guide**, I’ll share our itinerary, travel tips, and highlights from **San Jose Costa Rica**, **La Fortuna Costa Rica**, **Arenal Volcano**, and the famous **Rio Celeste Waterfall**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-itinerary-to-mesmerizing-costa-rica-costaricatravelguide-2.jpeg)


### **Costa Rica Travel Guide: Best Time to Visit**

The best time to visit Costa Rica is from **June to September**, when temperatures are mild. The busiest months start in July, when U.S. and [European](/europe) families travel for summer vacations. While slightly warmer, this period allows you to enjoy the lush landscapes in their full glory. Avoid December’s rainy season unless you enjoy tropical downpours.

We flew with [Avianca](https://www.expedia.com/), which offers comfortable flights within South America, including great in-flight entertainment and friendly service.

For Indians, a **visa on arrival** is valid for a maximum of 90 days. Make sure to carry your return ticket for immigration purposes.

Spanish is the official language, so learning a few key phrases helps. Words like Buenos Días (good morning), Hola (hello), and Cuánto (how much) make interactions easier.

Travel insurance is highly recommended, and while the **Costa Rican Colón** is the official currency, U.S. dollars are widely accepted.

Getting around Costa Rica is easier with a car, especially if you plan to visit **La Fortuna Costa Rica** and **Rio Celeste Waterfall**. We rented a car from **Hertz at San Jose Costa Rica airport**, presented our Dubai/US driving licenses, got insurance, and were on our way within 45 minutes.

Spanish is the official language in all Central and South American countries. Mr. A and I are quick to learn languages. We enjoyed learning a few useful Spanish words. Buenos Dias (good morning), adios (bye), hola (hello or hi), Si (yes), cuanto (how much), salida (exit), entrada (entrance), caliente leche (hot milk) and numbers from 1 to 10. 

Travel Insurance is always recommended. 
**Costa Rican Colon** is the official currency. They also accepted dollars though. 

Our trip focused on nature, so we stayed at the [Chachagua Rainforest Eco-Lodge](https://www.booking.com/searchresults.html?city=-1107229;expand_sb=1;gclid=CjwKCAiA5L2tBhBTEiwAdSxJX2GE0QHHcDrPove29-ryi5veWQQ74mjtizDy35WaA7na6C3nCQNqixoCgPgQAvD_BwE;highlighted_hotels=377215;hlrd=no_dates;keep_landing=1;redirected=1;sid=2c91221561facfee3d6615d3d1424f9e;source=hotel&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-6fd32734507240e0885c8e52e-437527_cjevent-534e2539989011f0812802cc0a82b821&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287) near La Fortuna. This eco-lodge was like stepping into the Amazon rainforest—with lush greenery, wild animals, lakes, and a pool. The lodge offered great food and easy access to **La Fortuna Costa Rica** and **Arenal Volcano**, making it the perfect base for our adventures.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-itinerary-to-mesmerizing-costa-rica-costaricatravelguide-3.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-itinerary-to-mesmerizing-costa-rica-costaricatravelguide-5.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/an-itinerary-to-mesmerizing-costa-rica-costaricatravelguide-7.jpeg)

Costa Rica seemed to be slightly more expensive than some of the other countries we visited during our trip. It is also easy to book custom [day trips](https://tripadvisor.tp.st/ALerpWNn) in Costa Rica. 

[Driving](https://www.expedia.com/) in Costa Rica made our trip easier, especially for accessing remote locations like Rio Celeste. Standard rental procedures apply, and insurance is highly recommended.

Costa Rica is generally safe, but standard travel precautions are advised:

- Keep your belongings secure.
Keep your belongings secure.

- Carry essential documents.
Carry essential documents.

- Avoid large sums of cash.
Avoid large sums of cash.

- Being alert lets you enjoy Costa Rica without worries.
Being alert lets you enjoy Costa Rica without worries.


### **Things to Do in Costa Rica **

Beyond waterfalls and volcanoes, Costa Rica offers a variety of activities for all travelers:

This country epitomizes pure, unadulterated love for nature. Especially the waterfalls. I haven’t seen as many impressive waterfalls anywhere else (besides Iceland). Costa Rica Travel Guide: Best Time to Visit

The best time to visit Costa Rica is from June to September, when temperatures are mild. The busiest months start in July, when U.S. and European families travel for summer vacations. While slightly warmer, this period allows you to enjoy the lush landscapes in their full glory. Avoid December’s rainy season unless you enjoy tropical downpours.

We flew with Avianca, which offers comfortable flights within South America, including great in-flight entertainment and friendly service.

For Indians, a visa on arrival is valid for a maximum of 90 days. Make sure to carry your return ticket for immigration purposes.

Spanish is the official language, so learning a few key phrases helps. Words like Buenos Días (good morning), Hola (hello), and Cuánto (how much) make interactions easier.

Travel insurance is highly recommended, and while the Costa Rican Colón is the official currency, U.S. dollars are widely accepted.

Getting around Costa Rica is easier with a car, especially if you plan to visit La Fortuna Costa Rica and Rio Celeste Waterfall. We rented a car from Hertz at San Jose Costa Rica airport, presented our Dubai/US driving licenses, got insurance, and were on our way within 45 minutes.

Spanish is the official language in all Central and South American countries. Mr. A and I are quick to learn languages. We enjoyed learning a few useful Spanish words. Buenos Dias (good morning), adios (bye), hola (hello or hi), Si (yes), cuanto (how much), salida (exit), entrada (entrance), caliente leche (hot milk) and numbers from 1 to 10.

Travel Insurance is always recommended. Costa Rican Colon is the official currency. They also accepted dollars though.

Our trip focused on nature, so we stayed at the Chachagua Rainforest Eco-Lodge near La Fortuna. This eco-lodge was like stepping into the Amazon rainforest—with lush greenery, wild animals, lakes, and a pool. The lodge offered great food and easy access to La Fortuna Costa Rica and Arenal Volcano, making it the perfect base for our adventures.

Costa Rica seemed to be slightly more expensive than some of the other countries we visited during our trip. It is also easy to book custom day trips in Costa Rica.

Driving in Costa Rica made our trip easier, especially for accessing remote locations like Rio Celeste. Standard rental procedures apply, and insurance is highly recommended.
