Slovakia Travel Guide: Bratislava, Castles & Danube River
For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to Europe . We prefer to go to Europe during the off season to avoid the crowds. We...

For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to [Europe](/europe). We prefer to go to Europe during the off season to avoid the crowds. We covered three countries during this trip - **Czech Republic, Slovakia and Slovenia. **

We started our trip at the beautiful capital city, [Prague](/explore-destinations/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-town). True to our travel style, we ventured out of the city on our second day in Czech Republic. We did a day trip to [Cesky Krumlov](/explore-destinations/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov), one of the most magical fairy tale small town in South Bohemia. Followed by another trek in [Narnia](/explore-destinations/day-trip-to-narnia-labyrinth-and-bastei-bridge). 

After Czech, our next stopover was at **Slovakia** - for two rather short days. We arrived by train from Czech (because it is efficient and cost effective). 


**Full disclosure:** I had not considered adding Slovakia to our plan.. we could only do two nights here, which wasn’t going to be enough to hike the high Tatra. The hubs then told me the history of the Danube river and I knew we couldn’t leave Europe this time without hiking along the river.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-1.jpeg)

Blue Church
Bratislava Castle 
The UFO Tower
Old Town Hall
Devin's Castle and Danube river 
St. Martin's Cathedral
St. Nicholas Church 
Street Art and local street food 
Christmas Markets 


I would imagine the weather is pretty amazing in the summer but the winters are rainy, gloomy with snow and temperatures in the low 20s (Fahrenheit not Celsius). That being said, visiting Europe during the winter is absolutely magical and something everyone must experience at least once. So if you haven't done that already I would highly recommend visiting Slovakia during the Christmas season to soak in all the festivities. Luckily when we visited, there wasn't a lot of snow but the temperatures were below freezing during the night. 


We arrived by train from Czech. The central station is centrally located in the city and I would recommend taking the train to get here, if you are already in Europe. 
Schengen Visa required for entry into Slovakia. 

The official language spoken in the country is** Slovak. **

Travel and Medical Insurance is mandatory to travel to all Schengen countries. 
Currency is** Euro.**

We stayed at the[ ](https://www.booking.com/)[Elisabeth Old Town](https://booking.tp.st/B5bZNBzQ) close to the city center. The rooms were spacious for European standards. The breakfast was nothing to write home about.. that being said, there were quite a few good restaurants within walking distance. 

For lunch, we even tried a vegan Indian place called the [Thali.](http://www.thali.sk/vegetarianska-restauracia/)  You pay according to the weight.. not bad for being so far from India! :) 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-2.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-4.jpeg)

Bratislava was not very expensive, probably because this place is not touristy (yet). Tours are reasonably priced (during the off season). Cabs are expensive, as you would expect them to be anywhere in Europe. I loved that the buses were running frequently, super convenient and of course, cheap. A proper meal on average will be around EUR 10 per person. You can also easily [book walking tours](https://tripadvisor.tp.st/ePk3SiTi) or [get a guide](https://getyourguide.tp.st/q8d79xrR) for a day trip to Austria - this is just perfect for any European country. 

Had no issues with safety. We felt everyone we met was warm and helpful. 


As always, I was able to find lovely vegan friendly restaurants around the area. Yes, there was a cheese loaded Pizza and a lot of junk food at the Christmas markets. 


We arrived at the Bratislava Central station (aka we took the choo choo train). A quick 20 minute walk took us to our hotel in the Old Town. 

I was surprised to find lingering fall colors around town.. so pretty. It felt like we had finally arrived in Central Europe - there was a sense of faded beauty here. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-8.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-10.jpeg)

After checking into the hotel, we quickly went around old town Bratislava before we lost anymore light. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-12.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-14.jpeg)

Our first stop was the **The Blue Church**. The church is unique and is painted a baby blue with white accents. Even as you are walking down the street, you can't possibly miss this fairy tale church. 

As we strolled down the streets of Old Town, we were blown away by the magnificent **street art. **They were in almost every other building - big, bold and beautiful! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-16.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-18.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-20.jpeg)

By the time we were done street strolling, it was sun down and the Christmas markets were LIT. I loved how the markets here were more authentic and subdued compared to the ones in Prague. Grabbed all the junk food we could get our hands on and headed back to our room to call it a day.

**Fun tip:** if you are vegetarian, you must try the** Plaka - **which is basically like a hash brown but topped with mayo, cheese, onions and jalapeno.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-22.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-24.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-26.jpeg)

A packed day ahead of us. We started our day at 8 30 by catching a bus to the beautiful Devin's castle. We picked up a one way ticket on **Bus 29** for a short 20 minute ride up the hills. This is one of the easiest day trips you can do from the city. 

**Tip: to pick up the bus ticket, use change and pay at the ticket collection pole right next to bus stops. You will need to know the bus numbers and routes to do this. **

The bus dropped us off on the main street, from here, we walked towards the castle. There were clear signs, so we didn't get lost. There is a fee to enter the castle - EUR 3. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-32.jpeg)

There are two parts to the castle, the castle itself and the area outside it. The ruins in the castle date back to the 9th century and are well preserved. The castle hangs on top of the cliff at the confluence of Danube and Morava rivers. It was one hell of a beautiful winter morning and there wasn't another soul in sight. Which meant only one thing - picture time! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-38.jpeg)

We walked through the ruins, soaking in the medieval history mirrored in the castle's ruins. Once we got to the top, we were welcomed by panoramic views of the Danube - the second longest river in Europe. We couldn’t have asked for better weather to see this amazing place.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-40.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-44.jpeg)

As we walked down from the castle, we entered the castle grounds which is also home to a small memorial site for the 400 lives that were lost during the war. We did a small picnic since we just didn't have the heart to leave. 

This castle is a must do if you want to see the frontier that stands between Slovakia and Austria. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-48.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-50.jpeg)

We walked back half way to the city before taking the same bus back to city, getting off close to the **UFO tower**. This was clearly the most iconic location in the city. You can spend EUR 7.50 and go up the tower for a complete view of the city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-52.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-54.jpeg)

Once we walked back towards the old town, we stopped to take in of the colorful government buildings and of course, more street art. Oh and lunch was thumped down with much gusto at [Vegan Kiosk](https://www.happycow.net/reviews/vegan-kiosk-bratislava-78335) - easily the best meal we had during our trip! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-56.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-58.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-60.jpeg)

Right next to the Old Town Hall and St Martin's Cathedral, we came across a beautiful Christmas market; everyone was being jolly at 4 in the afternoon. Gosh, I fell in love with the vibes in this place. 'Tis really is the season. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-62.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-64.jpeg)

Our last stop for the day was for the sunset views over the **Bratislava castle**. It was a 30 minute walk up towards the castle from the Old Town. This is a monumental tourist location. You can also see the **St Nicholas Church** from here. We spent a few hours walking through the castle grounds. 


**Pro Tip:** if you don't want to spend money going up the UFO tower, you can get iconic city views from up here. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-66.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-68.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-70.jpeg)

Grabbed dinner at the **Christmas markets** before heading to the hotel. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-72.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-74.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-76.jpeg)

While researching Slovakia, there was not enough information on doing stuff outside the city. If you want to do something a little offbeat but don’t want to venture too far out, I recommend the Devin’s Castle - the perfect spot to see amazing views of the Danube River (and there is a good chance you’ll have the place to yourself in winter)! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-78.jpeg)

I wish we had more to spend here.. though the capital city is small and can be done in a day, I really wanted to head to the high Tatra mountains for a 2 day hike. Can’t wait to get back to this pretty little country and explore further. 

**One last fun fact: **

Did you know the **Danube River **originates in Germany but is 1780 miles and actually flows through 10 countries before emptying into the Black Sea? Yup, nature is incredibly fascinating! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-79.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-81.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/best-of-bratislava-slovakia-in-two-days-83.jpg)

 Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback. 

Cheers,

Anki

We walked back half way to the city before taking the same bus back to city, getting off close to the UFO tower. This was clearly the most iconic location in the city. You can spend EUR 7.50 and go up the tower for a complete view of the city.

Once we walked back towards the old town, we stopped to take in of the colorful government buildings and of course, more street art. Oh and lunch was thumped down with much gusto at Vegan Kiosk - easily the best meal we had during our trip!

Right next to the Old Town Hall and St Martin’s Cathedral, we came across a beautiful Christmas market; everyone was being jolly at 4 in the afternoon. Gosh, I fell in love with the vibes in this place. ‘Tis really is the season.

Our last stop for the day was for the sunset views over the Bratislava castle. It was a 30 minute walk up towards the castle from the Old Town. This is a monumental tourist location. You can also see the St Nicholas Church from here. We spent a few hours walking through the castle grounds.

Pro Tip: if you don’t want to spend money going up the UFO tower, you can get iconic city views from up here.

Grabbed dinner at the Christmas markets before heading to the hotel.

While researching Slovakia, there was not enough information on doing stuff outside the city. If you want to do something a little offbeat but don’t want to venture too far out, I recommend the Devin’s Castle - the perfect spot to see amazing views of the Danube River (and there is a good chance you’ll have the place to yourself in winter)!

I wish we had more to spend here.. though the capital city is small and can be done in a day, I really wanted to head to the high Tatra mountains for a 2 day hike. Can’t wait to get back to this pretty little country and explore further.

**One last fun fact: **

Did you know the **Danube River **originates in Germany but is 1780 miles and actually flows through 10 countries before emptying into the Black Sea? Yup, nature is incredibly fascinating!

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

**9. Cambodia Travel Highlights**


### **🏛️ Phnom Penh – Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum**

A moving visit to the **S21 Prison**, where we met survivor **Mr. Bou Meng**, one of only 7 to live through the horrors of Khmer Rouge rule.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-23.jpg)

Around mid day to we visited the **Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum**, a tragic reminder of the genocide of the communist Khmer Rouge led by Pol Pot in the 1970s. We sought to see and understand this senseless reign of terror and at the end there was the meeting with one of only 7 survivors of the genoice, Mr. Bou Meng. In his book, he talks about how he was miraculously spared because of his paintbrush. Even though he could not save his wife or children, he was determined to bear witness and stand in a courtroom to tell the world of the horrors that unfolded behind these prison cells. Around 2 million Cambodians were killed between 1975 and 1979 by the Khmer Rouge. In this high school converted to a prison, around 18,000 were tortured and killed. Only 7 survived and Mr. Bou Meng was one of them. A story of courage, survival and truth. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-27.jpg)

An alternative to the busy Angkor Wat complex, **Phnom Bakheng** offers a scenic hilltop view over the temples.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-31.jpg)

There are many temples to catch the sunset in Angkor wat, including the main complex itself but we went here as it is was the closest for us after. It was such a gorgeous, albeit overcast, sky.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-37.jpg)

Absolutely magical! The **sunrise experience** was the highlight of our trip.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-39.jpg)

**🌅 Sunrise at Angkor Wat**

Absolutely magical! The **sunrise experience** was the highlight of our trip.

**Tips for Sunrise:**

- Book tickets online the day before
Book tickets online the day before

- Arrive by **5 a.m.**
Arrive by **5 a.m.**

- Use the **reflection pool** for iconic shots
Use the **reflection pool** for iconic shots

Bring bug spray & water

As one of the largest religious monuments in the world, this is the one temple you need at least 4 hours to complete What I found fascinating was the architectural influence of the Chola and Pallavas in Angkor Wat. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-42.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-44.jpg)

With its huge pavilions, towering spires and larger-than-life sculptures, this temple has a plethora of similarities with the South Indian temples I visited with mom & dad as a kid. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-48.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-50.jpg)

Though the temple was built by the Khmer rulers between the 9th and 14th century, the unmistakable link between South India and Cambodia is evident in every pillar.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-54.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-56.jpg)

**🌳 Ta Prohm – The Jungle Temple**

Made famous by **Tomb Raider**, this temple is slowly being reclaimed by nature. Ancient stones wrapped in giant tree roots — simply breathtaking.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-58.jpg)

I am not big on architecture or history. This place won me over simply because it was a place that was losing it’s battle to nature. Once you start making your way through the maze of the temple complex, you will realize that the entire temple is being swallowed by the jungle. I believe this is how the world was meant to be. One with the natural world around in every way.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-61.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-63.jpg)

The gigantic monster twigs adds to the allure and mystery of this ancient temple. I also loved that we were the only ones there early in the morning. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-67.jpg)

**🧘 Chill Time at Templation Villa**

After intense temple hopping, we relaxed in our villa — Khmer massage, fresh food, and calm surroundings.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-69.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-71.jpg)


### **😊 Bayon Temple – The Smiling Faces**

Known as the **Face Temple**, Bayon Temple has **216 smiling Buddha faces** carved into 54 towers.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-73.jpg)

Known as the 'face temple' thanks to its iconic structures, huge heads glared down at us, exuding power and a hint of all-knowing kindness in the smile! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-74.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-76.jpg)

**🏞️ Phnom Bok – Hidden Gem**

Off the beaten track, this mountaintop temple is **quiet, scenic, and worth the 600-step climb**. Ideal for sunset and reflection.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-78.jpg)

This place is truly a hidden gem that sits on top of a hill that overlooks all of Siem Reap. There is a sense of mystery surrounding these ruins, that was once a flourishing temple. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-79.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-81.jpg)

A visit to Phnom Bok is the best way to experience the Khmer region in all its (former) glory. You will get a sense of what once was. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-83.jpg)

This trip proved that timing really is everything. Post-COVID, Cambodia is still seeing fewer tourists, and it’s the best time to visit.

We spent **4 days in Cambodia**, covering all major temples without feeling rushed. It was the perfect mix of history, adventure, nature, and rest.

Whether you’re a history buff, a photographer, or a cultural explorer — **Cambodia travel** is sure to win your heart.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-cover.jpg)

**We thought four days was great and ample time to explore the temples. **
**You will probably need three days for the temples, unless you plan to explore them all. We went with a plan and covered the most unique temples in the complex. Making sure each one was different in it's own way. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-85.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-87.jpg)

Whether it's the history, the architecture or just another place you want to tick off, the Angkor wat temples won't disappoint you. You can relax, soak up the culture and much more in just a few days. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-89.jpg)

Drop your comments and feedback on [Instagram](http://www.instagram.com/wayfarer_anki) if you wish to get in touch. 

Cheers,

Anki

🏛️ Phnom Penh – Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum

A moving visit to the S21 Prison, where we met survivor Mr. Bou Meng, one of only 7 to live through the horrors of Khmer Rouge rule.

Around mid day to we visited the Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum, a tragic reminder of the genocide of the communist Khmer Rouge led by Pol Pot in the 1970s. We sought to see and understand this senseless reign of terror and at the end there was the meeting with one of only 7 survivors of the genoice, Mr. Bou Meng. In his book, he talks about how he was miraculously spared because of his paintbrush. Even though he could not save his wife or children, he was determined to bear witness and stand in a courtroom to tell the world of the horrors that unfolded behind these prison cells. Around 2 million Cambodians were killed between 1975 and 1979 by the Khmer Rouge. In this high school converted to a prison, around 18,000 were tortured and killed. Only 7 survived and Mr. Bou Meng was one of them. A story of courage, survival and truth.

An alternative to the busy Angkor Wat complex, Phnom Bakheng offers a scenic hilltop view over the temples.

There are many temples to catch the sunset in Angkor wat, including the main complex itself but we went here as it is was the closest for us after. It was such a gorgeous, albeit overcast, sky.

Absolutely magical! The sunrise experience was the highlight of our trip.

🌅 Sunrise at Angkor Wat

Absolutely magical! The sunrise experience was the highlight of our trip.

Tips for Sunrise:

  • Book tickets online the day before Book tickets online the day before

  • Arrive by 5 a.m. Arrive by 5 a.m.

  • Use the reflection pool for iconic shots Use the reflection pool for iconic shots

Bring bug spray & water

As one of the largest religious monuments in the world, this is the one temple you need at least 4 hours to complete What I found fascinating was the architectural influence of the Chola and Pallavas in Angkor Wat.

With its huge pavilions, towering spires and larger-than-life sculptures, this temple has a plethora of similarities with the South Indian temples I visited with mom & dad as a kid.

Though the temple was built by the Khmer rulers between the 9th and 14th century, the unmistakable link between South India and Cambodia is evident in every pillar.

🌳 Ta Prohm – The Jungle Temple

Made famous by Tomb Raider, this temple is slowly being reclaimed by nature. Ancient stones wrapped in giant tree roots — simply breathtaking.

I am not big on architecture or history. This place won me over simply because it was a place that was losing it’s battle to nature. Once you start making your way through the maze of the temple complex, you will realize that the entire temple is being swallowed by the jungle. I believe this is how the world was meant to be. One with the natural world around in every way.

The gigantic monster twigs adds to the allure and mystery of this ancient temple. I also loved that we were the only ones there early in the morning.

🧘 Chill Time at Templation Villa

After intense temple hopping, we relaxed in our villa — Khmer massage, fresh food, and calm surroundings.

😊 Bayon Temple – The Smiling Faces

Known as the Face Temple, Bayon Temple has 216 smiling Buddha faces carved into 54 towers.

Known as the ‘face temple’ thanks to its iconic structures, huge heads glared down at us, exuding power and a hint of all-knowing kindness in the smile!

🏞️ Phnom Bok – Hidden Gem

Off the beaten track, this mountaintop temple is quiet, scenic, and worth the 600-step climb. Ideal for sunset and reflection.

This place is truly a hidden gem that sits on top of a hill that overlooks all of Siem Reap. There is a sense of mystery surrounding these ruins, that was once a flourishing temple.

A visit to Phnom Bok is the best way to experience the Khmer region in all its (former) glory. You will get a sense of what once was.

This trip proved that timing really is everything. Post-COVID, Cambodia is still seeing fewer tourists, and it’s the best time to visit.

We spent 4 days in Cambodia, covering all major temples without feeling rushed. It was the perfect mix of history, adventure, nature, and rest.

Whether you’re a history buff, a photographer, or a cultural explorer — Cambodia travel is sure to win your heart.

**We thought four days was great and ample time to explore the temples. ** **You will probably need three days for the temples, unless you plan to explore them all. We went with a plan and covered the most unique temples in the complex. Making sure each one was different in it’s own way. **

Whether it’s the history, the architecture or just another place you want to tick off, the Angkor wat temples won’t disappoint you. You can relax, soak up the culture and much more in just a few days.

Drop your comments and feedback on Instagram if you wish to get in touch.

Cheers,

Anki

7-Day Bhutan Itinerary: Best Places to Visit, Hikes & Travel Tips
Discover the best places to visit in Bhutan with our Bhutan 7-day travel guide! Explore stunning landscapes, and a beautiful culture!

Perched on the eastern edge of the Himalayas, this tiny kingdom has always been wrapped in mystique. Bhutan isn’t the easiest country to visit, with limited flights and long drives, but the effort is well worth it. If you're seeking a unique and authentic travel experience filled with tranquility and pristine nature, Bhutan is the perfect destination.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-cover.jpg)

In a world increasingly dominated by globalization and sameness, where cities feature the same stores found worldwide, Bhutan offers a refreshing escape. As the world’s only Buddhist Monarchy, it boasts a long-standing green legacy, making it the first carbon-neutral country on Earth. For an unforgettable journey, explore my guide "Best Places to Visit in Bhutan: A 7-Day Bhutan Itinerary:" to discover the highlights of this enchanting land.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-4.jpg)

The logistics of getting to Bhutan is one of the reasons it has been a well kept secret. Somethings that aren't always shared - there are just three flights that fly into Bhutan, which means that you are willing work around a huge budget, your options of getting to Bhutan are limiting.

If you choose to fly - then fly into Paro on the national carriers [Druk](https://www.drukair.com.bt) or Bhutan Air from Singapore, Delhi, Bangkok, Kathmandu.

If you want to keep costs low, you go via India and cross the border by road. Fly from anywhere in India to Bagdogra, then cross the border. This is a long but scenic journey that can take around 10 hours with stops for lunch and dinner - but you can always sleep through it; which is what we did.

If you hold an Indian passport, the visa is on arrival and free of charge, for most other nationalities the process is simple but comes at an additional cost. There is a small mandatory daily [Sustainable Development Fund](https://www.bhutan.travel)[ fee](https://www.bhutan.travel) for all travelers coming into Bhutan. This is included in the costs of the tour for Indian passport holders and foreigners. The cost, however, is much higher for foreigners.

The official and national language of Bhutan is **Dzongkha**. It belongs to the Tibeto-Burman language family and is written using the Tibetan script. In the villages, you will have difficulty communicating with locals, but if you are with a guide, no issues at all.

The best time to visit Bhutan depends on what you're looking for in your trip:

**Peak Season (Spring and Autumn):**

- **March to May (Spring):** Pleasant weather with blooming rhododendrons, clear skies, and stunning Himalayan views. Ideal for trekking and hiking.
**March to May (Spring):** Pleasant weather with blooming rhododendrons, clear skies, and stunning Himalayan views. Ideal for trekking and hiking.

- **September to November (Autumn):** Comfortable temperatures, clear skies, and vibrant
**September to November (Autumn):** Comfortable temperatures, clear skies, and vibrant

- **May and November: **Less crowded than peak season, with comfortable weather. Perfect for sightseeing and cultural experiences.
**May and November: **Less crowded than peak season, with comfortable weather. Perfect for sightseeing and cultural experiences.

- **June to August (Monsoon):** Lush greenery, fewer crowds, and discounted rates. However, be prepared for occasional rain showers (we didn't really encounter any rain in August)
**June to August (Monsoon):** Lush greenery, fewer crowds, and discounted rates. However, be prepared for occasional rain showers (we didn't really encounter any rain in August)

**Bhutanese Ngultrum (BTN)** is the primary currency. Here are some key tips:

- Indian Rupees are widely accepted in Bhutan, except for ₹2,000 notes.
Indian Rupees are widely accepted in Bhutan, except for ₹2,000 notes.

- Cash is essential in Bhutan, especially outside major cities like Thimphu and Paro. Credit and debit cards are accepted only in select high-end hotels, restaurants, and shops.
Cash is essential in Bhutan, especially outside major cities like Thimphu and Paro. Credit and debit cards are accepted only in select high-end hotels, restaurants, and shops.

- ATMs are few and far between, and they may not always support international cards.
ATMs are few and far between, and they may not always support international cards.

Did you know that hiring a local guide is mandatory for entry into Bhutan? Beyond the regulatory requirement, engaging a local guide can significantly enhance your travel experience. You'll gain deeper insights into the culture, history, and traditions of this mystical Himalayan kingdom. Plus, you'll directly contribute to the local economy.

Interested in planning your own Bhutan adventure or joining an upcoming group trip? Reach out to me for personalized assistance.

Bhutan is one of the safest countries in the world, and its warm, peaceful environment makes it an excellent destination for solo travelers and women. Women travelers are treated with respect in Bhutan. The culture promotes gender equality, and harassment is virtually unheard of. While Bhutan is liberal compared to some neighboring countries, dressing modestly. Bhutan requires international tourists to travel with a licensed tour operator. This rule ensures that you are always accompanied by a knowledgeable guide, which enhances safety and convenience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-6.jpg)

**VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD**

Bhutan offers a variety of flavorful vegetarian and vegan dishes deeply rooted in its culture and tradition. Some of the dishes to try - Ema Datshi, Kewa Datshi, Shamu Datshi, Puta, Goen Hogey. Don't miss Suja (Butter Tea)!


Our Bhutan trip started in Thimphu. The capital city is a little more commercial than I would have liked but it is still peaceful and a fabulous place to kick start your Bhutanese adventure before venturing into the countryside.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-7.jpg)

These are the historic places we covered and ones you shouldn’t miss.

📌 The Simtoka Dzong: one of the oldest Dzong’s in Bhutan, this place is a stunner, inside and outside. Mesmerizing details and Buddhist sculptures left is wanting more


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-12.jpg)

📌 Buddha Dordenma Statue: one of the world’s largest and tallest sitting Buddha statues, at over 52m. It’s almost like Buddha is protecting the city and all its inhabitants


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-16.jpg)

📌 Simply Bhutan: a living museum that gives you a glimpse of the Bhutanese culture, and how they still protect their old traditions with gusto. From singing, dancing, to rice wine tasting, it’s absolutely fabulous!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-20.jpg)

📌 Takin reserve: of course I had to include something with wildlife. The Takin reserve was built as a natural reserve to protect a rare animal called Takin, the national animal of Bhutan. We also found fee other animals inside the park. The forest area is actually gorgeous and lush green.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-26.jpg)

📌 The view of the double rainbow over Tashichho Dzong. This is an old palace that tells us stories of the glorious past of Bhutan!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-28.jpg)

📌 Local food: if you are in the mood for great local food that’s veggie friendly, get the ema datshi (chillies with cheese)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-29.jpg)

From the commercial Thimphu to Paro, a quaint town, is one of the widest valleys in Bhutan. It is a land of forgotten customs and pristine wilderness. From fortresses to ruins, from sacred monasteries to holy temples, and from soul-quenching treks to picturesque landscapes, Paro has something for all types of travelers!

These are the must-see places:

📌 while driving from Thimphu, travel via Wangchu River - it is an amazingly scenic drive!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-32.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-34.jpg)

📌 Click pictures of the dzongs and Nya Mey Zam bridge at night


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-40.jpg)

📌 Rinpung Dzong aka Paro Dzong occupies a strategic site on the top of a small hill with a panoramic view of the Paro valley around it. It looks like a castle in the air. Dress in Bhutanese National Dress, find the best shop right opposite Paro Dzong!

Undoubtedly the most popular place in Bhutan, Paro Taktsang, often referred to as Tiger's Nest, has become almost synonymous with the country itself—and with good reason.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-42.jpg)

Perched majestically at a breathtaking height of 9,678 feet above sea level, it’s impossible not to fall in love with the picturesque facade of this iconic monastery, which is set against the dramatic backdrop of a steep, leaning cliff.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-43.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-45.jpg)

The stunning views of the surrounding valleys and mountains only add to its allure, making it a must-visit destination for travelers seeking both adventure and spiritual enlightenment. The site is believed to be one of the thirteen Tiger’s Nest caves in Tibet, which adds to its mystique, hence it is best known as Tiger’s Lair caves. This rich history and cultural significance make it a central point of interest for those who wish to explore the deep spiritual roots of Bhutanese Buddhism.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-49.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-51.jpg)

I had lots of questions about how difficult this trail is, as the trek to reach this remarkable destination is often a topic of discussion among visitors. If you are a beginner hiker, the trail can certainly appear daunting at first glance, with its steep inclines and uneven terrain.

However, there is no reason you cannot complete the loop if you approach it with patience and determination. Just take your time, enjoy the journey, and remember that the experience is just as important as the destination. For those who find their fitness level to be above average, the trek can be completed in a brisk 90 to 120 minutes or even less, but it is advisable to pace yourself. Regardless of your fitness level, make sure to leave ample time for rest breaks along the way.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-53.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-55.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-57.jpg)

These pauses not only allow you to catch your breath but also provide a wonderful opportunity to soak up all that spiritual energy that permeates the air around you. The serene atmosphere, combined with the breathtaking views, creates a truly enriching experience that will leave a lasting impression on your heart and mind.

PRO TIP: highly recommend doing a Bhutanese stone bath after the trek - so good for those sore muscles!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-59.jpg)

True to style, one the last few days from Paro, we went to Chelela Pass and Haa Valley. Routes you won’t find on just any regular trip. I found this after a bit of reading and so happy I did, as we were the only tourists there.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-60.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-62.jpg)

At the dizzying height of 3,988 meters, Chelela Pass feels like the top of the world, it is the highest passes in Bhutan. The prayer flags flutter in the wind, sending blessings across the Himalayas as you take in the sweeping views of the Paro and Haa valleys.Quaint and deserted, Haa Valley is where the earth kisses the sky, wrapped in blankets of green and dotted with wildflowers. The air here feels different, crisp and pure, untouched by the modern world. It’s a place where nature reigns supreme, offering solace to those who seek peace away from the chaos.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-64.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-66.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-68.jpg)

I loved both these places the most during our week long Bhutan trip. It’s incredible to find ancient temples tucked away in corners only the mountains know. This valley is Bhutan’s best-kept secret, an untouched paradise.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-70.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-72.jpg)

**FOODIE TIP:** if you visit Haa Valley, you have to try “hoentay” which is a buckwheat dumpling stuffed with radish, spinach, cheese and pepper. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-74.jpg)

Fortress. Green Mountains. Monasteries. Untouched valleys. Traditions & authenticity. These words barely scratch the surface of what it means to embark on a journey through the breathtaking landscapes and rich cultural tapestry of Bhutan. The moment we set foot in this enchanting country, I was captivated by its unique blend of natural beauty and spiritual depth.

Any culture that is deeply rooted in Buddhism has always been fascinating to me, so when the opportunity to head to Bhutan to lead a group trip came by, I knew I had to go for it.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-75.jpg)

The allure of Bhutan is not just in its picturesque scenery but also in its commitment to preserving its cultural heritage and environment. Add that to the fact that it’s an amazing place for active adventures—from challenging hikes that take you to ancient monasteries perched on cliffs, to exhilarating rafting experiences on pristine rivers, and opportunities for wildlife viewing in lush national parks—I was completely sold!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-76.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-78.jpg)

The experience of being in Bhutan was nothing short of incredible. I was fortunate to spend several days in a land where the air is refreshingly pure, devoid of pollution, and where the sounds of nature take precedence over the noise of modern life. The tranquility and serenity of the environment felt like a much-needed escape from the chaos of everyday existence. It was such a privilege to visit yet another destination frozen in time, unchanged by the whims of mass tourism.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-80.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/bhutan-itinerary-7day-travel-guide-84.jpg)

The people of Bhutan, with their warm smiles and welcoming nature, embody the essence of their rich cultural traditions, which remain intact and vibrant against the backdrop of the stunning Himalayan landscape. Each moment spent in this remarkable country was a reminder of the beauty that comes from living in harmony with nature and honoring one's heritage.

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Lots of love,

Anki

  • Indian Rupees are widely accepted in Bhutan, except for ₹2,000 notes. Indian Rupees are widely accepted in Bhutan, except for ₹2,000 notes.

  • Cash is essential in Bhutan, especially outside major cities like Thimphu and Paro. Credit and debit cards are accepted only in select high-end hotels, restaurants, and shops. Cash is essential in Bhutan, especially outside major cities like Thimphu and Paro. Credit and debit cards are accepted only in select high-end hotels, restaurants, and shops.

  • ATMs are few and far between, and they may not always support international cards. ATMs are few and far between, and they may not always support international cards.

Did you know that hiring a local guide is mandatory for entry into Bhutan? Beyond the regulatory requirement, engaging a local guide can significantly enhance your travel experience. You’ll gain deeper insights into the culture, history, and traditions of this mystical Himalayan kingdom. Plus, you’ll directly contribute to the local economy.

Interested in planning your own Bhutan adventure or joining an upcoming group trip? Reach out to me for personalized assistance.

Bhutan is one of the safest countries in the world, and its warm, peaceful environment makes it an excellent destination for solo travelers and women. Women travelers are treated with respect in Bhutan. The culture promotes gender equality, and harassment is virtually unheard of. While Bhutan is liberal compared to some neighboring countries, dressing modestly. Bhutan requires international tourists to travel with a licensed tour operator. This rule ensures that you are always accompanied by a knowledgeable guide, which enhances safety and convenience.

VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD

Bhutan offers a variety of flavorful vegetarian and vegan dishes deeply rooted in its culture and tradition. Some of the dishes to try - Ema Datshi, Kewa Datshi, Shamu Datshi, Puta, Goen Hogey. Don’t miss Suja (Butter Tea)!

Our Bhutan trip started in Thimphu. The capital city is a little more commercial than I would have liked but it is still peaceful and a fabulous place to kick start your Bhutanese adventure before venturing into the countryside.

These are the historic places we covered and ones you shouldn’t miss.

📌 The Simtoka Dzong: one of the oldest Dzong’s in Bhutan, this place is a stunner, inside and outside. Mesmerizing details and Buddhist sculptures left is wanting more

📌 Buddha Dordenma Statue: one of the world’s largest and tallest sitting Buddha statues, at over 52m. It’s almost like Buddha is protecting the city and all its inhabitants

📌 Simply Bhutan: a living museum that gives you a glimpse of the Bhutanese culture, and how they still protect their old traditions with gusto. From singing, dancing, to rice wine tasting, it’s absolutely fabulous!

📌 Takin reserve: of course I had to include something with wildlife. The Takin reserve was built as a natural reserve to protect a rare animal called Takin, the national animal of Bhutan. We also found fee other animals inside the park. The forest area is actually gorgeous and lush green.

📌 The view of the double rainbow over Tashichho Dzong. This is an old palace that tells us stories of the glorious past of Bhutan!

📌 Local food: if you are in the mood for great local food that’s veggie friendly, get the ema datshi (chillies with cheese)

From the commercial Thimphu to Paro, a quaint town, is one of the widest valleys in Bhutan. It is a land of forgotten customs and pristine wilderness. From fortresses to ruins, from sacred monasteries to holy temples, and from soul-quenching treks to picturesque landscapes, Paro has something for all types of travelers!

These are the must-see places:

📌 while driving from Thimphu, travel via Wangchu River - it is an amazingly scenic drive!

📌 Click pictures of the dzongs and Nya Mey Zam bridge at night

📌 Rinpung Dzong aka Paro Dzong occupies a strategic site on the top of a small hill with a panoramic view of the Paro valley around it. It looks like a castle in the air. Dress in Bhutanese National Dress, find the best shop right opposite Paro Dzong!

Undoubtedly the most popular place in Bhutan, Paro Taktsang, often referred to as Tiger’s Nest, has become almost synonymous with the country itself—and with good reason.

Perched majestically at a breathtaking height of 9,678 feet above sea level, it’s impossible not to fall in love with the picturesque facade of this iconic monastery, which is set against the dramatic backdrop of a steep, leaning cliff.

The stunning views of the surrounding valleys and mountains only add to its allure, making it a must-visit destination for travelers seeking both adventure and spiritual enlightenment. The site is believed to be one of the thirteen Tiger’s Nest caves in Tibet, which adds to its mystique, hence it is best known as Tiger’s Lair caves. This rich history and cultural significance make it a central point of interest for those who wish to explore the deep spiritual roots of Bhutanese Buddhism.

I had lots of questions about how difficult this trail is, as the trek to reach this remarkable destination is often a topic of discussion among visitors. If you are a beginner hiker, the trail can certainly appear daunting at first glance, with its steep inclines and uneven terrain.

However, there is no reason you cannot complete the loop if you approach it with patience and determination. Just take your time, enjoy the journey, and remember that the experience is just as important as the destination. For those who find their fitness level to be above average, the trek can be completed in a brisk 90 to 120 minutes or even less, but it is advisable to pace yourself. Regardless of your fitness level, make sure to leave ample time for rest breaks along the way.

These pauses not only allow you to catch your breath but also provide a wonderful opportunity to soak up all that spiritual energy that permeates the air around you. The serene atmosphere, combined with the breathtaking views, creates a truly enriching experience that will leave a lasting impression on your heart and mind.

PRO TIP: highly recommend doing a Bhutanese stone bath after the trek - so good for those sore muscles!

True to style, one the last few days from Paro, we went to Chelela Pass and Haa Valley. Routes you won’t find on just any regular trip. I found this after a bit of reading and so happy I did, as we were the only tourists there.

At the dizzying height of 3,988 meters, Chelela Pass feels like the top of the world, it is the highest passes in Bhutan. The prayer flags flutter in the wind, sending blessings across the Himalayas as you take in the sweeping views of the Paro and Haa valleys.Quaint and deserted, Haa Valley is where the earth kisses the sky, wrapped in blankets of green and dotted with wildflowers. The air here feels different, crisp and pure, untouched by the modern world. It’s a place where nature reigns supreme, offering solace to those who seek peace away from the chaos.

I loved both these places the most during our week long Bhutan trip. It’s incredible to find ancient temples tucked away in corners only the mountains know. This valley is Bhutan’s best-kept secret, an untouched paradise.

FOODIE TIP: if you visit Haa Valley, you have to try “hoentay” which is a buckwheat dumpling stuffed with radish, spinach, cheese and pepper.

Fortress. Green Mountains. Monasteries. Untouched valleys. Traditions & authenticity. These words barely scratch the surface of what it means to embark on a journey through the breathtaking landscapes and rich cultural tapestry of Bhutan. The moment we set foot in this enchanting country, I was captivated by its unique blend of natural beauty and spiritual depth.

Any culture that is deeply rooted in Buddhism has always been fascinating to me, so when the opportunity to head to Bhutan to lead a group trip came by, I knew I had to go for it.

The allure of Bhutan is not just in its picturesque scenery but also in its commitment to preserving its cultural heritage and environment. Add that to the fact that it’s an amazing place for active adventures—from challenging hikes that take you to ancient monasteries perched on cliffs, to exhilarating rafting experiences on pristine rivers, and opportunities for wildlife viewing in lush national parks—I was completely sold!

The experience of being in Bhutan was nothing short of incredible. I was fortunate to spend several days in a land where the air is refreshingly pure, devoid of pollution, and where the sounds of nature take precedence over the noise of modern life. The tranquility and serenity of the environment felt like a much-needed escape from the chaos of everyday existence. It was such a privilege to visit yet another destination frozen in time, unchanged by the whims of mass tourism.

The people of Bhutan, with their warm smiles and welcoming nature, embody the essence of their rich cultural traditions, which remain intact and vibrant against the backdrop of the stunning Himalayan landscape. Each moment spent in this remarkable country was a reminder of the beauty that comes from living in harmony with nature and honoring one’s heritage.

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Lots of love,

Anki

Discover Cameroon: A 4-Day Itinerary to Ekom-Nkam Waterfalls & Douala’s Hidden Gems
Discover the best of Cameroon with our travel guide, featuring the stunning Ekom-Nkam Waterfalls and a 4-day itinerary for Douala.

Have you ever dreamed of experiencing the raw beauty of [Africa](/post/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda), but have often felt overwhelmed by the vastness of the continent or the country you are planning an adventure to? I know that is always the case with me. That's why I'm thrilled to share our recent adventure to Cameroon, a hidden gem that packs a punch in just a few days.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-cover.png)

Cameroon is often called “Little Africa” because it contains the diversity of all of [Africa ](/post/zambia)within its borders. Cameroon is the quintessential African country because it has got varied terrain, from volcanoes, beaches, Savannah, rainforests and rocky mountain ranges. But here's the thing - planning a trip to Cameroon can be daunting. Where do you start? What should you see? How do you make the most of your limited time?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-4.jpg)

Our trip focused on the iconic Ekom-Nkam waterfall, and if you watched the stories over the weekend, you already know that it more than lives up to hype. She is a stunner. In this guide, I'll take you through a short itinerary for Cameroon. From the bustling streets of Douala to the serene beauty of Ekom-Nkam Waterfalls, we'll explore the highlights this diverse country has to offer.

We flew from Dubai to Cameroon by Ethiopian Airlines. 

Visa is [e-visa](https://www.evisacam.cm/ords/dl_portal/r/public_portal/home) for most nationalities. I was surprised by how efficient and quick the process was. We got our visa back in 24 hours. 

French and English are the official language. Some people speak English but keep your google translate on hand. 

Rainy season is from March to October, if your goal is to see the Ekom-Nkam waterfall, then this is the best time to visit. Dry season runs from November to February, and it is ideal for wildlife viewing and hikes. 

I used Euros but we did exchange for local currency for tips, food and tips. 


As is always the case in Africa, I highly recommend having a driver or a local guide to take you around. Contact me if you need a reliable one for your trip. 

As long as you have a local guide with you, you should have no issues getting around in Cameroon. 


It is not easy being vegetarian in Africa - but there are ways you can get around it. We found local bread, and we stayed in a good hotel where Pizza and fries were available. 

Douala, often referred to as the economic capital of Cameroon, boasts a variety of attractions that showcase its historical significance and modern development. Here are some must-visit sites:

Standing tall in the Deido district, La Nouvelle Liberté is an iconic sculpture that has become a symbol of Douala's artistic spirit. Created by Joseph-Francis Sumégné, this 12-meter-high statue is made entirely from recycled materials, reflecting the city's commitment to sustainability and creativity. 

For art enthusiasts, Doual'Art is an essential stop. This contemporary art center, founded in 1991, is dedicated to promoting urban art and culture. The center regularly hosts exhibitions, workshops, and cultural events that showcase both local and international artists. Doual'Art plays a crucial role in shaping the city's cultural landscape and offers visitors a unique insight into Cameroon's vibrant art scene.

To delve into Cameroon's maritime history, visit the Musée Maritime de Douala. This museum, housed in a colonial-era building, offers a fascinating glimpse into the country's naval past. Exhibits include ancient maps, nautical instruments, and model ships, providing a comprehensive overview of Cameroon's relationship with the sea. The museum also highlights the importance of the Wouri River and the Port of Douala in shaping the city's development.

For an authentic local experience, head to Eko Market, one of Douala's largest and most vibrant marketplaces. This bustling bazaar offers a sensory overload with its colorful stalls, aromatic spices, and lively atmosphere. Here, you can find everything from fresh produce and traditional crafts to modern electronics. Eko Market is not just a shopping destination but a cultural hub where you can observe daily life in Douala and interact with friendly locals.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-6.jpg)

[The Cathédrale Saint-Pierre-et-Saint-Paul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Troyes_Cathedral) stands as a testament to Douala's religious heritage. This impressive Gothic-style cathedral, built in the early 20th century, features stunning stained-glass windows and intricate architectural details. The cathedral serves as a spiritual center for Douala's Catholic community and offers visitors a moment of tranquility amidst the city's hustle and bustle.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-7.jpg)

To appreciate Douala's colonial past, take a stroll through the Bonanjo district. This area is home to several well-preserved colonial-era buildings, including the Pagoda House and the former Palace of the German Governor. The district's architecture provides a stark contrast to the modern skyline, offering a glimpse into Cameroon's complex history.

No visit to Douala is complete without experiencing the Wouri River. Take a boat tour to appreciate the city's skyline from a different perspective and observe the bustling port activities. The river plays a crucial role in Douala's economy and offers a refreshing escape from the urban landscape.

Nestled in the heart of the forest, the mesmerizing waterfalls plunge from a towering height of about 80 metere, delivering a spellbinding spectacle that seems straight out of a storybook. 

The name Ekom Nkam comes from 2 words: Ekom which is the name of the village where the falls are located, and Nkam which is the name of the river from which the falls originate. The tallest waterfalls in the country, the falls are characterized by two falls: a “male” fed continuously whatever the season, and a “female” fed only in the rainy season, which is why JUNE is the best time to visit this waterfall.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-8.jpg)

It is one of the largest waterfalls in Cameroon, and it’s absolutely stunning. Imagine a powerful torrent of water cascading down from a height of 80 meters (262 feet), surrounded by dense, emerald-green forest. The water crashes into the rocky basin below, creating a misty spray that glistens in the sunlight. It’s wild, untamed, and completely mesmerizing. This is nature at its most raw and beautiful.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-11.jpg)

We spent hours just walking around, taking in the views from different angles. There are several lookout points, each offering a unique perspective of the falls. From one spot, you can see the entire drop of the waterfall, and from another, you can get up close to feel the cool mist on your face. If you’re lucky, you might even catch a rainbow forming in the spray, a picture, perfect moment. Ekom Nkam is not just about the waterfall either. The area around the falls is lush and teeming with life. I saw birds swooping overhead, colorful butterflies fluttering by, and even a few curious monkeys peeking out from the treetops. If you love nature, this place will be paradise for you. You can wander through the forest, breathe in the fresh air, and just feel a sense of peace and tranquility wash over you. If you’re up for a little adventure, you can also take a short hike down to the base of the falls. It’s a bit slippery, so make sure you have the right footwear. But once you’re down there, you’ll get an entirely different perspective of the falls—one that makes you feel tiny and insignificant in the best possible way. Standing at the bottom, with the water crashing down in front of me, I felt like I was witnessing the sheer power and majesty of nature.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-15.jpg)

One thing that makes Ekom Nkam particularly special is its appearance in the classic movie Greystoke: The Legend of Tarzan. If you’re a movie buff like me, you’ll be excited to know that scenes from the film were shot right here. As I stood by the falls, I couldn’t help but imagine Tarzan swinging through the trees, with the roar of the waterfall echoing in the background. It added a touch of cinematic magic to an already incredible place.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-17.jpg)

You have discovered the best of Cameroon with our travel guide, featuring the stunning Ekom-Nkam Waterfalls and a 4-day itinerary for Douala. 

Our trip to Ekom Nkam Waterfalls was just a small part of what Cameroon has to offer, but it left a lasting impression. This country is a hidden gem, with so much to discover for those who are willing to venture off the beaten path. From the bustling city life of Douala to the serene and untouched beauty of Ekom Nkam, Cameroon is a place that surprises you at every turn. If you’re an adventurer who loves exploring places that haven’t been overrun by tourists, Cameroon is for you. It’s a place where you can connect with nature, immerse yourself in local culture, and experience the warmth of African hospitality. The people are incredibly friendly, and the landscapes are stunningly diverse. Whether you’re hiking up Mount Cameroon, relaxing on the beaches of Kribi, or marveling at the grandeur of [Ekom Nkam](https://mintoul.gov.cm/en/site/ekom-nkam-falls/) Waterfalls, there’s something for everyone.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cameroontravelguide-18.jpg)

So, if you’re looking for your next travel destination, why not make it Cameroon? 

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Lots of love,

Anki

The people are incredibly friendly, and the landscapes are stunningly diverse. Whether you’re hiking up Mount Cameroon, relaxing on the beaches of Kribi, or marveling at the grandeur of Ekom Nkam Waterfalls, there’s something for everyone.

So, if you’re looking for your next travel destination, why not make it Cameroon?

Central Asia Road Trip: Kazakhstan & Kyrgyzstan 2026 Complete Self-Drive Itinerary
A Central Asia road trip through Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan in 2026 is the kind of adventure most travellers dream about but ...

A Central Asia road trip through Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan in 2026 is the kind of adventure most travellers dream about but few actually experience. You'll drive through red-rock canyons that resemble Martian landscapes, alpine lakes with crystal-clear water, mountain passes above 3,000 metres, and valleys bursting with wildflowers.

Unlike traditional group tours, this journey gives you complete freedom. Your vehicle, your route, your pace.

Two weeks is the ideal timeframe—long enough to fully appreciate the region and short enough to maintain momentum throughout the journey.

Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan remain refreshingly untouched by mass tourism. You'll often spend entire days without seeing another international traveller, camp beneath some of the clearest night skies in the world, and explore landscapes that feel genuinely wild.

![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-1.jpg)

## Why Choose a Self-Drive Central Asia Road Trip Instead of a Guided Tour?

Self-driving offers several advantages over organised group tours.

**Benefits of Self-Driving**

•	Lower overall costs  
•	Flexible itinerary  
•	Freedom to stop anywhere  
•	More authentic local interactions  
•	Opportunity to explore hidden viewpoints  
•	Greater sense of adventure

**Accommodation Options**  
•	Guesthouses: ₹2,000–4,000 per night  
•	Yurts: Traditional Central Asian accommodation  
•	Campsites: Remote wilderness experiences  
•	Hotels: Available in major cities

**Typical Daily Cost**  
₹8,000–12,000 per person for a comfortable self-drive trip.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-3.jpg)

## Complete 14-Day Kazakhstan & Kyrgyzstan Road Trip Route

**Suggested Route**  
**Almaty → Charyn Canyon → Kolsai Lakes → Lake Kaindy → Karkara Border → Karakol → Issyk Kul → Bishkek**

**Total Distance**  
Approximately 1,200 km

**Duration**  
14 Days

**Main Highlights**  
•	Desert canyons  
•	Alpine lakes  
•	Mountain passes  
•	Remote valleys  
•	Nomadic culture  
•	Traditional yurt stays

This route combines some of Central Asia's most spectacular scenery into one unforgettable journey.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-5.jpg)

### What Are the Best Roads for Self-Driving in Central Asia?

Road quality varies significantly throughout the region.

**Main Highways**  
•	Generally paved  
•	Suitable for most vehicles  
•	Easier navigation

**Mountain Roads**  
•	Gravel sections  
•	Sharp hairpin bends  
•	Minimal guardrails  
•	Limited road markings

**Most Challenging Route**
**Karkara Pass (2,200m)**  
Features:  
•	Gravel surfaces  
•	Narrow roads  
•	Steep sections  
•	Remote conditions

**Recommended Vehicles**  
•	Suzuki Jimny  
•	Toyota Land Cruiser  
•	Other reliable 4WD vehicles

Driving experience in mountainous terrain is highly recommended.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-6.jpg)

### How Difficult Is the Kazakhstan–Kyrgyzstan Border Crossing at Karkara?

Karkara Pass is one of the most remote and scenic border crossings in Central Asia.

**Key Facts**  
•	Elevation: 2,200 metres  
•	Open: June to September  
•	Road surface: Dirt and gravel  
•	Mobile coverage: Very limited

**Challenges**   
•	Rough road conditions  
•	Weather changes  
•	Border processing delays  
•	Isolation

**Alternative Option**  
Fly between:     
•	Almaty  
•	Bishkek

**Flight duration:**  
Approximately 1 hour

**Average cost:**  
₹2,500–4,000

![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-8.jpg)

### Which Lakes Offer the Best Autumn Colours in Central Asia?

Autumn transforms Central Asia's mountain landscapes into vibrant displays of colour.

**Kolsai Lakes**  
•	Turquoise water  
•	Pine forests  
•	Excellent hiking trails
•	Peak colours in September and October  

**Lake Kaindy**  
•	Famous submerged pine forest  
•	Unique photography opportunities  
•	Crystal-clear water

**Lake Ala-Kol**   
•	Less crowded  
•	Dramatic alpine scenery  
•	Ideal for trekking 

All three lakes can be visited as part of a 2–3 day excursion from Almaty.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-10.jpg)

•	Suzuki Jimny

•	Similar 4WD SUVs

**Rental Costs**  
₹5,000–12,000 per day

International Driving Permit (IDP)  
Required for foreign travellers.

One-way rentals are often available for an additional fee.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-12.jpg)

**Vehicle Rental**  
₹70,000–1.2 lakh

**Accommodation**  
₹21,000–56,000

**Food**

**Activities & Park Fees**

![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-16.jpg)

**Budget Traveller**

**Comfort Traveller**  
₹4 lakh+

Compared to guided tours, self-driving remains significantly more affordable

![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-18.jpg)

#### Visas, Health & Practical Information for Central Asia

**Visa Information**

**Kazakhstan**  
Visa-free or simplified entry for many nationalities.

**Kyrgyzstan**  
Visa-free entry available for many travellers.

Always verify current visa regulations before booking.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-20.jpg)

**Health Considerations**

**Altitude**

Maximum elevations on this itinerary generally remain manageable.  
Typical elevations:  

•	Almaty: 800–1,000m  
•	Kolsai Lakes: 1,800–2,300m  
•	Karkara Pass: 2,200m  
•	Issyk Kul: 1,600m

**Travel Insurance**  
Medical and evacuation coverage is highly recommended.

**Essential Supplies**  
•	First aid kit  
•	Extra drinking water  
•	Power banks  
•	Offline maps  
•	Emergency food supplies



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-22.jpg)

#### Is Altitude Sickness a Risk During This Road Trip?  
For most travellers, altitude sickness risk is relatively low.

**Why?**  
Most destinations remain below 3,000 metres.

**Common Symptoms**  
•	Mild headaches  
•	Fatigue  
•	Shortness of breath

**Prevention Tips**  
•	Stay hydrated  
•	Avoid excessive alcohol  
•	Rest during the first day  
•	Increase elevation gradually  
Most travellers adapt without difficulty.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-24.jpg)

##### What Is the Best Season for a Central Asia Road Trip?

**June – July**  
**Pros**  
•	Wildflowers  
•	Open mountain passes  
•	Warm weather

**Cons**  
•	More tourists

**August – September**  
**Pros**  
•	Pleasant temperatures  
•	Fewer crowds  
•	Clear skies

**Cons**  
•	Cooler evenings

**Recommended Period**  
**August to September**  
This offers the best balance of weather, accessibility, and crowd levels.

October Onwards  
•	Increasing snowfall  
•	Mountain roads begin closing  
•	Karkara Pass typically closes  
Not recommended for first-time visitors.

##### FAQs: Central Asia Road Trip Kazakhstan & Kyrgyzstan 2026   
**Can Inexperienced Drivers Complete This Road Trip Safely?**  
Generally, no.

**The route includes:**  
•	Remote mountain roads  
•	Steep passes  
•	Gravel tracks  
•	Limited emergency support

**Recommended Alternative**  
Hire a professional local driver.

**Average cost:**  
₹4,000–6,000 per day
 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-29.jpg)

**Is Camping Required During the Trip?**  
No.

**Accommodation options include:**  
•	Hotels  
•	Guesthouses  
•	Yurts  
•	Campsites

Many travellers combine all four for a more varied experience.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-31.jpg)

**What's the Most Challenging Day of the Journey?**  
**Day 7 – Karkara Border Crossing**

Challenges include:  
•	Long driving hours  
•	Rough roads  
•	Border formalities  
•	Limited services  
•	Weak mobile signal

Proper planning is essential.

**What Happens If Your Vehicle Breaks Down?**  
Roadside assistance can be limited in remote areas.

**Always Carry:**  
•	Spare tyre  
•	Basic tools  
•	Water  
•	Emergency food  
•	Offline navigation

**Recommended**  
Choose a reputable rental company that includes emergency support.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-33.jpg)

**Is This Road Trip Suitable for Solo Female Travellers?**  
Yes, but extra precautions are advised.

**Recommendations**  
•	Choose reliable accommodation  
•	Share your route with family or friends  
•	Consider hiring a local driver  
•	Avoid driving remote mountain roads after dark

Many solo female travellers successfully explore Central Asia each year and report positive experiences.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/your-ultimate-guide-to-traveling-in-uzbekistan-35.jpg)

**Final Thoughts**   
A Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan road trip offers some of the most rewarding landscapes and adventures in the world. From the red cliffs of Charyn Canyon to the alpine beauty of Issyk Kul and the remote passes of the Tian Shan Mountains, this journey combines freedom, nature, and cultural discovery in a way few destinations can match.

For travellers seeking an unforgettable self-drive adventure in 2026, Central Asia remains one of the world's last great road-trip frontiers.

Not many cities make it to my travel list — but Cape Town had long been on it. After spending 72 hours here, I can confidently say: it was worth every bit of the hype.

Unlike many [African](/africa) cities we’ve explored, Cape Town had a surprisingly cosmopolitan feel. It reminded us of Dubai and parts of [Europe](/europe) — from clean roads and modern eateries to stunning coastal drives.

### **Flights + Visa**

We flew with[ ](https://www.emirates.com/)[Emirates](https://www.emirates.com/). For Indian passport holders: apply for a **South African visa** (multiple-entry recommended). It took just 72 hours to process. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/capetownsouthafrica-5.jpg)

Cape Town isn’t ideal for solo exploration, especially at night. Stick to group tours and drive only during daylight. Common sense goes a long way.

We visited in **winter** (June–August) to avoid crowds — and it worked like a charm. No queues at major attractions.

1. Why Cape Town Was Worth the Hype

Not many cities make it to my travel list — but Cape Town had long been on it. After spending 72 hours here, I can confidently say: it was worth every bit of the hype.

Unlike many African cities we’ve explored, Cape Town had a surprisingly cosmopolitan feel. It reminded us of Dubai and parts of Europe — from clean roads and modern eateries to stunning coastal drives.

 Add to that: pristine beaches, thrilling wildlife, and jaw-dropping scenery

** Vegetarian Food in Comoros**

- At **Hotel Escale**, the chef prepared **custom vegetarian meals** daily.
At **Hotel Escale**, the chef prepared **custom vegetarian meals** daily.
