Prague: Cesky Krumlov from Prague & South Bohemia Highlights

For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to [Europe](/europe). We prefer to go to Europe during the off season to avoid the crowds. We covered three countries during this trip - **Czech Republic, Slovakia and Slovenia. **

We started our trip at the beautiful capital city, [Prague](/post/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-town)... you can check out the itinerary [here.](/explore-destinations) True to our travel style, we ventured out of the city on our second day in Czech Republic. We did a day trip to one of the most magical fairy tale small town in South Bohemia - **Cesky Krumlov**. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-cover.jpeg)

This town is located in the Southern Bohemian region of Czech republic and is known for being to home to a spectacular castle over the river Vltava.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-2.jpeg)

**Places you can't miss: **

State Castle 

Cesky Krumlov Old Town 

Castle Tower 

Local Pastry shops 

Oh and local beer, if you are into it

**Best time to visit**

I would imagine the weather is pretty amazing in the summer but the winters are extreme with snow and temperatures in the low 20s (Fahrenheit not Celsius). That being said, visiting Europe during the winter is absolutely magical and something everyone must experience at least once. So if you haven't done that already I would highly recommend visiting Prague during the Christmas season to soak in all the festivities. 

Luckily when we visited, there wasn't a lot of snow (we just had one night of snow in Prague) but the temperatures were below freezing during the night. 


**Flights and Visa **

We flew with [Smart Wings](https://www.expedia.com/Flights)** **- short, direct flights all through the week. 


Schengen Visa required for entry into Czech Republic. Immigration was simple and quick. 


**Language**

The official language spoken in the country is** Czech. **You will also find that the second most common language is German, followed by Russian. 


**Travel and Medical Insurance, Currency **

Travel and Medical Insurance is mandatory to travel to all Schengen countries. 


Currency is** Czech Crown. **Restaurants and other places don't accept Euro but most big restaurants accept cards. 


**Where we stayed **

We stayed at the [Ametyst Hotel](https://booking.tp.st/CSBjvvuF) in Prague. This hotel is in the New Town of Prague. 

The rooms were spacious for European standards with a beautiful view of the city.  The staff are really helpful and knowledgeable. The hotel is just a 15 minute walk to all the major attractions. The breakfast was repetitive but was a pretty good spread (even for us vegetarians). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-3.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-5.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-7.jpeg)

**Tip:** There is a small grocery right next to the hotel, which is incredibly convenient after a tiring day. 

**Cost and getting around **

Prague is expensive but not ridiculously so. Hotels are reasonably priced (during the off season). Cabs are expensive, as you would expect them to be anywhere in Europe. 

A proper meal on average will be around EUR 10-15 per person.

**Safety **

Had no issues with safety. We felt everyone we met was warm and helpful. 


**Food **

Cesky Krumlov is a small town, so apart from your pastries there isn't a lot of food for vegetarians. However, I would recommend eating at the Travel Hostel Restaurant - super chill vibes, local cuisine and authentic experience. We had the Pesto Pasta, Veggie Salad and dessert. YUM. Hearty portions too. Double win! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-11.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-13.jpeg)

**What to pack** 

Toiletries. Medicines. 

Winters are cold. Pack rain jackets, winter jackets, scarves and beanies if you are going around the same time as us. 


**Itinerary: ** 
The drive up from Prague is just around 2 hours, so you can rent a car and do this on your own. Personally, I prefer tours. If you are like me and want to avoid driving during the unpredictable winter season (because let's face it - ain't no fun in doing that), you can book a day tour from Prague to Cesky. 

We did a tour with [Prague Sightseeing Tours](https://getyourguide.tp.st/ZwBTYwI0). It was very professional with free Wi-Fi on board the bus, great food included during the trip and lots of wonderful information shared. I do recommend doing a tour with them. 


**Note: during the summer, the entrance fees to the castle is included in the tour cost but during winter the castle is closed, so we got lunch instead. **

After a 15 minute slippery walk up a narrow street, we were welcomed by endless rows of gothic houses and bright colored roof tops. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-15.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-17.jpeg)

We then gradually walked into the castle grounds - preserved from the 14th century. The castle complex has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site. In winter the castle itself is closed but you can still walk around the complex. You can even walk up the castle for some amazing views of the town. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-19.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-21.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-23.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-25.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-27.jpeg)

As you walk around the castle, you will notice that there are window displays of Bohemian garnets everywhere, which really adds to the enigma of the town. If you’re planning a trip, taking a day trip **cesky krumlov from prague** is the perfect way to experience this magical winter town.

We then made our way down from the castle towards the old town, crossing a small bridge with the Vltava River running just below it. Just imagine the entire river glistening under the winter sun, with music playing in the background, giving the entire place a magical feel—yeah, it’s like time stands still here.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-29.jpeg)

We continued walking along the bohemian streets until we reached the city center, which was completely festive and bustling with activity. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-30.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-32.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-34.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-36.jpeg)

After a scrumptious lunch, we got a EUR 7 (per person) ticket to climb the watch tower. The stairs leading up are rickety but there are beautiful views along the way...

and the views from the top are out of this world. Clear blue skies, a flowing river, crisp winter air and magic in the air... 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-38.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-40.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-42.jpeg)

We got incredibly lucky with the weather that day as the clouds cleared by the time we got to the place.. it made exploring this cute town very easy and comfortable even in winter. 

Never a bad time to grab snacks from a local pastry story. 

Fun fact: We were also told the local brew here is world famous!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-44.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-46.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-48.jpg)

**Final thoughts** 

Cesky Krumlov is easily one of Central Europe's most picturesque towns and tells you a story of Europe's majestic past. There is history, art, cuisine, and old castle tales... 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-50.jpeg)

You can blitz through the town in a day or stay for a few days and venture out further to explore South Bohemia.  

As for us? We were thrilled to have celebrated our fifth wedding anniversary in this whimsical winter town with the crowds gone and the place blanketed in pristine white snow. 

Happity boppity! :) 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-51.jpeg)

**One final fun fact: **

Did you know that BATA (the world famous shoe manufacturer) was actually founded in Czech Republic? Yeah, we didn't either. I think all countries like to think they founded Bata! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/day-trip-to-the-fairy-tale-town-of-cesky-krumlov-52.jpeg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback. 

Cheers,

Anki

For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to Europe. We prefer to go to Europe during the off season to avoid the crowds. We covered three countries during this trip - **Czech Republic, Slovakia and Slovenia. **

We started our trip at the beautiful capital city, Prague… you can check out the itinerary here. True to our travel style, we ventured out of the city on our second day in Czech Republic. We did a day trip to one of the most magical fairy tale small town in South Bohemia - Cesky Krumlov.

This town is located in the Southern Bohemian region of Czech republic and is known for being to home to a spectacular castle over the river Vltava.

**Places you can’t miss: **

State Castle

Cesky Krumlov Old Town

Castle Tower

Local Pastry shops

Oh and local beer, if you are into it

Best time to visit

I would imagine the weather is pretty amazing in the summer but the winters are extreme with snow and temperatures in the low 20s (Fahrenheit not Celsius). That being said, visiting Europe during the winter is absolutely magical and something everyone must experience at least once. So if you haven’t done that already I would highly recommend visiting Prague during the Christmas season to soak in all the festivities.

Luckily when we visited, there wasn’t a lot of snow (we just had one night of snow in Prague) but the temperatures were below freezing during the night.

**Flights and Visa **

We flew with Smart Wings** **- short, direct flights all through the week.

Schengen Visa required for entry into Czech Republic. Immigration was simple and quick.

Language

The official language spoken in the country is** Czech. **You will also find that the second most common language is German, followed by Russian.

**Travel and Medical Insurance, Currency **

Travel and Medical Insurance is mandatory to travel to all Schengen countries.

Currency is** Czech Crown. **Restaurants and other places don’t accept Euro but most big restaurants accept cards.

**Where we stayed **

We stayed at the Ametyst Hotel in Prague. This hotel is in the New Town of Prague.

The rooms were spacious for European standards with a beautiful view of the city. The staff are really helpful and knowledgeable. The hotel is just a 15 minute walk to all the major attractions. The breakfast was repetitive but was a pretty good spread (even for us vegetarians).

Tip: There is a small grocery right next to the hotel, which is incredibly convenient after a tiring day.

**Cost and getting around **

Prague is expensive but not ridiculously so. Hotels are reasonably priced (during the off season). Cabs are expensive, as you would expect them to be anywhere in Europe.

A proper meal on average will be around EUR 10-15 per person.

**Safety **

Had no issues with safety. We felt everyone we met was warm and helpful.

**Food **

Cesky Krumlov is a small town, so apart from your pastries there isn’t a lot of food for vegetarians. However, I would recommend eating at the Travel Hostel Restaurant - super chill vibes, local cuisine and authentic experience. We had the Pesto Pasta, Veggie Salad and dessert. YUM. Hearty portions too. Double win!

What to pack

Toiletries. Medicines.

Winters are cold. Pack rain jackets, winter jackets, scarves and beanies if you are going around the same time as us.

**Itinerary: ** The drive up from Prague is just around 2 hours, so you can rent a car and do this on your own. Personally, I prefer tours. If you are like me and want to avoid driving during the unpredictable winter season (because let’s face it - ain’t no fun in doing that), you can book a day tour from Prague to Cesky.

We did a tour with Prague Sightseeing Tours. It was very professional with free Wi-Fi on board the bus, great food included during the trip and lots of wonderful information shared. I do recommend doing a tour with them.

**Note: during the summer, the entrance fees to the castle is included in the tour cost but during winter the castle is closed, so we got lunch instead. **

After a 15 minute slippery walk up a narrow street, we were welcomed by endless rows of gothic houses and bright colored roof tops.

We then gradually walked into the castle grounds - preserved from the 14th century. The castle complex has been declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site. In winter the castle itself is closed but you can still walk around the complex. You can even walk up the castle for some amazing views of the town.

As you walk around the castle, you will notice that there are window displays of Bohemian garnets everywhere, which really adds to the enigma of the town. If you’re planning a trip, taking a day trip cesky krumlov from prague is the perfect way to experience this magical winter town.

We then made our way down from the castle towards the old town, crossing a small bridge with the Vltava River running just below it. Just imagine the entire river glistening under the winter sun, with music playing in the background, giving the entire place a magical feel—yeah, it’s like time stands still here.

We continued walking along the bohemian streets until we reached the city center, which was completely festive and bustling with activity.

After a scrumptious lunch, we got a EUR 7 (per person) ticket to climb the watch tower. The stairs leading up are rickety but there are beautiful views along the way…

and the views from the top are out of this world. Clear blue skies, a flowing river, crisp winter air and magic in the air…

We got incredibly lucky with the weather that day as the clouds cleared by the time we got to the place.. it made exploring this cute town very easy and comfortable even in winter.

Never a bad time to grab snacks from a local pastry story.

Fun fact: We were also told the local brew here is world famous!

Final thoughts

Cesky Krumlov is easily one of Central Europe’s most picturesque towns and tells you a story of Europe’s majestic past. There is history, art, cuisine, and old castle tales…

You can blitz through the town in a day or stay for a few days and venture out further to explore South Bohemia.

As for us? We were thrilled to have celebrated our fifth wedding anniversary in this whimsical winter town with the crowds gone and the place blanketed in pristine white snow.

Happity boppity! :)

Azerbaijan Itinerary: Naxcivan, Qabala & Baku (6 Days)
In one of the most remote corners of Central Asia lies an autonomous republic that very few tourists ever explore — Naxcivan. This...

In one of the most remote corners of Central Asia lies an autonomous republic that very few tourists ever explore — Naxcivan.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-1.jpg)

This exclave of Azerbaijan, bordering Turkey, Iran, and Armenia, is separated from the mainland yet deeply connected through history and culture


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-cover.jpg)

We decided to hop on a plane, took another one hour flight from Baku and landed in this unbelievably beautiful little corner of a mainstream country. We wandered through the empty roads, crushed on gorgeous minarets and fell in love with every part of Naxcivan. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-3.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-5.jpg)


### **Here’s our complete Azerbaijan itinerary 6 days:**

- Naxcivan – 2 days
Naxcivan – 2 days

- Qabala – 2 days
Qabala – 2 days

- Baku – 2 days
Baku – 2 days

- [FAQ ](#viewer-c6qpn)
[FAQ ](#viewer-c6qpn)

- [Guide Information](#viewer-7g71p) 
[Guide Information](#viewer-7g71p) 

- [Where We Stayed ](#viewer-ernsv) 
[Where We Stayed ](#viewer-ernsv) 

- [Highlights](#viewer-bsmo4)
[Highlights](#viewer-bsmo4)

- [Final Thoughts ](#viewer-b7pes) 
[Final Thoughts ](#viewer-b7pes) 

**1. Planning Your Trip to Azerbaijan**


### **Flights & Visa**

We flew with[ FlyDubai ](https://www.expedia.com/Flights)to Baku (2.5 hours). For Naxcivan, flights are available via Azerbaijan Airlines from Baku (1 hour, ~$80 return). We had our local guide book the tickets, which is recommended since online booking can be tricky.

📌 **Azerbaijan**[ visa for Indians](https://evisa.gov.az/en/) – $26 eVisa (processed in 24 hours)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-7.jpg)

**Currency**

Use local currency (Azerbaijani manat) in rural areas. USD is accepted in cities.

**Best Time to Visit**

- **Spring & Fall** – Ideal for hiking and sightseeing
**Spring & Fall** – Ideal for hiking and sightseeing

- **Winter (Feb)** – Snowy landscapes in Naxcivan
**Winter (Feb)** – Snowy landscapes in Naxcivan

- **Summer** – Best for Snake Mountain hikes
**Summer** – Best for Snake Mountain hikes

If you’re wondering about the **best time to visit Azerbaijan**, spring and autumn are perfect for exploring cities and cultural sites, while summer is best for hikes in Qabala and Naxcivan.

**Currency**

Use local currency (Azerbaijani manat) in rural areas. USD is accepted in cities.

**Independent vs. Guided Travel**

- Baku: Can be done solo.
Baku: Can be done solo.

- Naxcivan & Qabala: Hire a local guide + driver for logistics, especially for booking internal flights and remote site visits.
Naxcivan & Qabala: Hire a local guide + driver for logistics, especially for booking internal flights and remote site visits.

**Where We Stayed**

In Baku we stayed at the[ Landmark hotel](https://www.booking.com/searchresults.en-gb.html?aid=311984&label=the-landmark-baku-Q9mRYuor3ugNF8xtx5NXaQS393297877096%3Apl%3Ata%3Ap1%3Ap2%3Aac%3Aap%3Aneg%3Afi%3Atikwd-13677235125%3Alp1000013%3Ali%3Adec%3Adm%3Appccp%3DUmFuZG9tSVYkc2RlIyh9YTQUGSsRwx9_3qo3uPTHyoo&gclid=Cj0KCQiA2eKtBhDcARIsAEGTG42rLd4WzVScFf96yxkwIeHit471xbr-yTiDD92Ot2cEhdftK2JOgNkaAhCWEALw_wcB&highlighted_hotels=402903&redirected=1&city=-2705195&hlrd=no_dates&source=hotel&expand_sb=1&keep_landing=1&sid=82486b0758c55e2863eebe1973d6642a). It is correctly priced, and located right in the middle of the city with easy access to the old city, perfect for anyone looking for a **Baku travel guide** starting point. In Naxcivan, you have two choices - both are about the same price too. We stayed in Grand Naxcivan hotel.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-12.jpg)

**Vegetarian Food in Azerbaijan**

Like with most of Central Asia, the cuisine is meat based. As a vegetarian, you can ask to customize the dishes. We tried the local saj and qutabs. Both delicious and freshly made. You can also find a whole lot of hot tea and bakhalava at every stop. If you’re looking for **vegetarian food in Azerbaijan**, Baku and Qabala have more options, but in Naxcivan it’s best to request customizations.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-16.jpg)

**Top Destinations in Azerbaijan**


### **Naxcivan – Offbeat and Magical**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-18.jpg)

**Alinja Fortress**

Known as the “Machu Picchu of Eurasia,” this reconstructed fortress on a mountaintop offers panoramic views of the Iranian mountains.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-21.jpg)

On a freezing day, we hiked 1,800 steps to reach this historic stronghold. I could imagine how well-guarded it once was, but the real highlight was the sweeping panoramic views of the northern Iranian mountains—absolutely worth every step.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-27.jpg)

**Qarabaglar Mausoleum**

A visual twin to Uzbekistan’s blue-tiled wonders, located in a small village, this monument is stunning with twin minarets and mosaic artistry.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-29.jpg)

Resembling the blue-tiled marvels of Uzbekistan, this hidden gem in a quiet village is a masterpiece of Islamic architecture. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-32.jpg)

With its striking twin minarets, intricate mosaic artistry, and timeless elegance, it offers a glimpse into the region’s rich cultural and historical heritage—well worth the visit for architecture lovers and history buffs alike.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-34.jpg)

**Noah’s Mausoleum & Yezidabad Castle**

Believed to be the final resting place of the biblical Noah, this sacred site is steeped in legend and history.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-37.jpg)

 From the fortress walls of Yezidabad Castle, you can also spot the oldest mosque in Naxcivan—a breathtaking juxtaposition of spiritual heritage and ancient fortification that speaks volumes about the region’s deep cultural roots.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-39.jpg)

**Momine Khatun Mausoleum**

Dedicated to a ruler’s wife, this structure features a single support pillar, and the word Allah written over a thousand times. The **Momine Khatun Mausoleum** is one of the most important historical highlights of Naxcivan and a must for architecture lovers.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-42.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-44.jpg)

**Khan’s Palace**

Next to the Momine Khatun Mausoleum, this former royal residence showcases artifacts from Naxcivan’s past, with the dazzling mirror room as its unforgettable highlight.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-48.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-50.jpg)

**Gulustan Mausoleum**

One more distinct monument, this one stands out in two ways - the pinkish color of the tiles and the bright red mountains in the background.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-54.jpg)

**Snake Mountain (Ilandag Mountain)**

If you love outdoor hikes, summer is the best time to conquer Snake Mountain in Naxcivan.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-56.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-58.jpg)

 Nearby, you can explore one of the region’s oldest salt mines, adding a fascinating historic twist to your adventure. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-60.jpg)

**Qabala – Nature and Slow Travel**

Just three hours from Baku, Qabala is the kind of place where time slows down and nature takes center stage.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-61.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-63.jpg)

If you’re planning **things to do in Qabala Azerbaijan**, highlights include Lake Nohur, forest drives, waterfalls, and even skiing in winter


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-67.jpg)

Along the roadside, you’ll often spot local grandmas making fresh veggie qutabs right before your eyes—flaky, golden, and bursting with flavor. Qabala isn’t just a summer retreat; in winter, its slopes invite skiers, while spring transforms the valley into a haven for wine lovers. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-69.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-71.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-73.jpg)

Whether you’re here for adventure or simply to breathe deeply and unwind, Qabala is a year-round invitation to embrace slow travel at its finest.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-77.jpg)

**Baku – Where Old Meets New**

Our trip ended in Azerbaijan’s vibrant capital—a city of long coastlines, bold modern architecture, and centuries-old history.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-79.jpg)

**Coastal Strolls & the Carpet Museum**

A walk along the seaside boulevard is a must. The iconic **Carpet Museum Baku**, shaped like a rolled-up carpet, makes for a striking photo backdrop. Inside, the craftsmanship is mind-blowing.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-80.jpg)

**Views from Highland Park**

Perfect for panoramic city views and sunset shots of the Flame Towers.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-81.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringnakchivan-azerbaijan-83.jpg)

**Icherisheher Fortress Walls & The Old City**

Walking the old cobblestone streets is the best way to soak in history..

[The National News ](https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/money/2022/07/28/how-to-save-money-so-you-can-afford-to-travel-more/),[Khaleej Times ](https://www.khaleejtimes.com/travel/uae-how-to-travel-the-world-despite-a-weak-passport-expats-share-tips),[Mint / Livemint](https://www.livemint.com/news/trends/this-visited-108-countries-while-holding-full-time-job/amp-11746251898742.html), [Times Now News](https://www.timesnownews.com/travel/travelogues/solo-trip-india-why-not-travel-vlogger-ankitha-rajendaran-on-travelling-solo-to-109-countries-and-the-joy-of-embracing-unseen-destinations-article-152114732)[,Outlook Traveller](https://www.outlooktraveller.com/destinations/international/travel-to-syria-uzbekistan-and-iran), [Hindustan Times ](https://www.hindustantimes.com/lifestyle/travel/woman-who-travelled-solo-to-109-countries-7-continents-shares-her-top-travel-hacks-every-female-should-know-101747298629619.html),[MSN India](https://www.msn.com/en-in/money/topstories/meet-ankita-rajendaran-the-woman-who-travelled-to-108-countries-while-working-full-time/ar-AA1DUMAu?ocid=spr_trending&businessvertical=News), [The Established ](https://www.theestablished.com/self/health/the-best-company-is-your-ownand-indian-youth-is-agreeing)[NDTV](https://www.ndtv.com/video/news/news/couple-who-have-explored-over-80-countries-share-tips-for-budget-travel-729052), [Times of India](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/life-style/travel/destinations/7-countries-you-should-visit-if-you-want-to-travel-differently/photostory/121286098.cms), [SheThePeople](https://www.shethepeople.tv/interviews/ankitha-rajendaran-why-not-book-interview-9426506)

In one of the most remote corners of Central Asia lies an autonomous republic that very few tourists ever explore — Naxcivan.

This exclave of Azerbaijan, bordering Turkey, Iran, and Armenia, is separated from the mainland yet deeply connected through history and culture

We decided to hop on a plane, took another one hour flight from Baku and landed in this unbelievably beautiful little corner of a mainstream country. We wandered through the empty roads, crushed on gorgeous minarets and fell in love with every part of Naxcivan.

Here’s our complete Azerbaijan itinerary 6 days:

  • Naxcivan – 2 days Naxcivan – 2 days

  • Qabala – 2 days Qabala – 2 days

  • Baku – 2 days Baku – 2 days

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Guide Information Guide Information

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

1. Planning Your Trip to Azerbaijan

Flights & Visa

We flew with FlyDubai to Baku (2.5 hours). For Naxcivan, flights are available via Azerbaijan Airlines from Baku (1 hour, ~$80 return). We had our local guide book the tickets, which is recommended since online booking can be tricky.

📌 Azerbaijan visa for Indians – $26 eVisa (processed in 24 hours)

Currency

Use local currency (Azerbaijani manat) in rural areas. USD is accepted in cities.

Best Time to Visit

  • Spring & Fall – Ideal for hiking and sightseeing Spring & Fall – Ideal for hiking and sightseeing

  • Winter (Feb) – Snowy landscapes in Naxcivan Winter (Feb) – Snowy landscapes in Naxcivan

  • Summer – Best for Snake Mountain hikes Summer – Best for Snake Mountain hikes

If you’re wondering about the best time to visit Azerbaijan, spring and autumn are perfect for exploring cities and cultural sites, while summer is best for hikes in Qabala and Naxcivan.

Currency

Use local currency (Azerbaijani manat) in rural areas. USD is accepted in cities.

Independent vs. Guided Travel

  • Baku: Can be done solo. Baku: Can be done solo.

  • Naxcivan & Qabala: Hire a local guide + driver for logistics, especially for booking internal flights and remote site visits. Naxcivan & Qabala: Hire a local guide + driver for logistics, especially for booking internal flights and remote site visits.

Where We Stayed

In Baku we stayed at the Landmark hotel. It is correctly priced, and located right in the middle of the city with easy access to the old city, perfect for anyone looking for a Baku travel guide starting point. In Naxcivan, you have two choices - both are about the same price too. We stayed in Grand Naxcivan hotel.

Vegetarian Food in Azerbaijan

Like with most of Central Asia, the cuisine is meat based. As a vegetarian, you can ask to customize the dishes. We tried the local saj and qutabs. Both delicious and freshly made. You can also find a whole lot of hot tea and bakhalava at every stop. If you’re looking for vegetarian food in Azerbaijan, Baku and Qabala have more options, but in Naxcivan it’s best to request customizations.

Top Destinations in Azerbaijan

Naxcivan – Offbeat and Magical

Alinja Fortress

Known as the “Machu Picchu of Eurasia,” this reconstructed fortress on a mountaintop offers panoramic views of the Iranian mountains.

On a freezing day, we hiked 1,800 steps to reach this historic stronghold. I could imagine how well-guarded it once was, but the real highlight was the sweeping panoramic views of the northern Iranian mountains—absolutely worth every step.

Qarabaglar Mausoleum

A visual twin to Uzbekistan’s blue-tiled wonders, located in a small village, this monument is stunning with twin minarets and mosaic artistry.

Resembling the blue-tiled marvels of Uzbekistan, this hidden gem in a quiet village is a masterpiece of Islamic architecture.

With its striking twin minarets, intricate mosaic artistry, and timeless elegance, it offers a glimpse into the region’s rich cultural and historical heritage—well worth the visit for architecture lovers and history buffs alike.

Noah’s Mausoleum & Yezidabad Castle

Believed to be the final resting place of the biblical Noah, this sacred site is steeped in legend and history.

 From the fortress walls of Yezidabad Castle, you can also spot the oldest mosque in Naxcivan—a breathtaking juxtaposition of spiritual heritage and ancient fortification that speaks volumes about the region’s deep cultural roots.

Momine Khatun Mausoleum

Dedicated to a ruler’s wife, this structure features a single support pillar, and the word Allah written over a thousand times. The Momine Khatun Mausoleum is one of the most important historical highlights of Naxcivan and a must for architecture lovers.

Khan’s Palace

Next to the Momine Khatun Mausoleum, this former royal residence showcases artifacts from Naxcivan’s past, with the dazzling mirror room as its unforgettable highlight.

Gulustan Mausoleum

One more distinct monument, this one stands out in two ways - the pinkish color of the tiles and the bright red mountains in the background.

Snake Mountain (Ilandag Mountain)

If you love outdoor hikes, summer is the best time to conquer Snake Mountain in Naxcivan.

 Nearby, you can explore one of the region’s oldest salt mines, adding a fascinating historic twist to your adventure.

Qabala – Nature and Slow Travel

Just three hours from Baku, Qabala is the kind of place where time slows down and nature takes center stage.

If you’re planning things to do in Qabala Azerbaijan, highlights include Lake Nohur, forest drives, waterfalls, and even skiing in winter

Along the roadside, you’ll often spot local grandmas making fresh veggie qutabs right before your eyes—flaky, golden, and bursting with flavor. Qabala isn’t just a summer retreat; in winter, its slopes invite skiers, while spring transforms the valley into a haven for wine lovers.

Whether you’re here for adventure or simply to breathe deeply and unwind, Qabala is a year-round invitation to embrace slow travel at its finest.

Baku – Where Old Meets New

Our trip ended in Azerbaijan’s vibrant capital—a city of long coastlines, bold modern architecture, and centuries-old history.

Coastal Strolls & the Carpet Museum

A walk along the seaside boulevard is a must. The iconic Carpet Museum Baku, shaped like a rolled-up carpet, makes for a striking photo backdrop. Inside, the craftsmanship is mind-blowing.

Views from Highland Park

Perfect for panoramic city views and sunset shots of the Flame Towers.

Icherisheher Fortress Walls & The Old City

Walking the old cobblestone streets is the best way to soak in history..

The Old City

Narrow cobblestone streets, historic mosques, and hidden courtyards—this was my personal favorite part of Baku. Every turn hides a story.

Heydar Aliyev Center

The Heydar Aliyev Center Zaha Hadid creation is a futuristic masterpiece. Its sweeping curves and bold architecture make it one of Baku’s most iconic landmarks.

Nizami Street Vibes

Baku’s shopping and nightlife hub is alive with energy—perfect for people-watching, grabbing coffee, or just soaking up the city buzz.

Bibi Heybat Mosque

Green-and-gold interiors with Caspian Sea views, rebuilt after Stalin’s destruction.

Final Thoughts

Azerbaijan surprised us at every turn. From the shimmering mirror rooms of ancient palaces to snow-capped fortresses, every corner tells a story. One moment you’re hiking rugged mountains, the next you’re exploring modern marvels like the Heydar Aliyev Center Zaha Hadid design or browsing the Carpet Museum Baku.

Whether you’re here for history, culture, or food (yes—even vegetarian food in Azerbaijan), this Baku travel guide combined with offbeat stops like Naxcivan and nature escapes in Qabala makes the perfect Azerbaijan itinerary 6 days.

If you’re based in the Middle East or passing through Central Asia, Azerbaijan is an easy, accessible gem that deserves a place on your travel list. It’s close enough for a quick getaway yet rich enough to keep you exploring for weeks.

Exploring Somaliland: Visa Info, FlyDubai Flights & Solo Travel Tips
Negative emotions about a place are often rooted in fear and it holds us hostage, preventing us from discovering magical places like Somalil

On a whim, I took off and landed in a country that (technically) doesn’t even exist. This is **Somaliland**. No, it’s actually NOT a part of Somalia. **Somaliland is now a country on its own**, with its own currency and government. This is yet another country that shattered my every preconception this year. It was a pleasant surprise and beyond beautiful.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-1.jpg)

It was a far cry from the usual Western media fear mongering you see in the media. **Is Somaliland safe for tourists?** Surprisingly, yes. It is now an independent country with its own government, currency, and story—but it’s not still recognized by the international community as a country.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-4.jpg)

It really shakes up your preconceptions too—you can’t go 10 meters without a smile from a stranger, offering you qat or khat (more on this later). It was an enthralling and surprisingly fun trip.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-6.jpg)

In this **Somaliland travel guide**, I will tell you how you can go here safely and have a fantastic travel experience discovering **offbeat African destinations**.

**Highlights:**

**Hargeisa. Berbera. Sheikh Mountains. Laas Geel cave paintings.**

- [FAQ ](#viewer-7c3nb) 
[FAQ ](#viewer-7c3nb) 

- [Solo Travel + Safety](#viewer-dksah) 
[Solo Travel + Safety](#viewer-dksah) 

- [Budget ](#viewer-evrjv) 
[Budget ](#viewer-evrjv) 

- [Where We Stayed ](#viewer-rgn7) 
[Where We Stayed ](#viewer-rgn7) 

- [Highlights](#viewer-e6qkt)
[Highlights](#viewer-e6qkt)

- [Final Thoughts ](#viewer-a4vo1) 
[Final Thoughts ](#viewer-a4vo1) 

**Flights + Visa**

I flew directly with [FlyDubai ](https://www.flydubai.com/en/flights-to-hargeisa)**Hargeisa flights**—a short direct flight to Hargeisa, the capital.

For the[ ](https://visaguide.world/africa/somaliland-visa/)[Somaliland visa](https://visaguide.world/africa/somaliland-visa/)** from Dubai**, you will need to apply at a Somaliland mission; remember you CANNOT travel to Somaliland with a Somalia visa and vice versa. Your visa has to be through Somaliland. If in Dubai, visit their office with a confirmed return ticket, tour + hotel booking. Pay $60 and get an e-visa copy by email in 48–72 hours. Once you land, you won’t be asked to pay extra—if you are, it’s likely a bribe. Carry your payment receipt with you and get a stamp. Simple.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-7.jpg)

**Solo traveling. Are you required to wear a scarf?**

As with any country in the world, be aware of your surroundings. Even more so in a country like this since you are not allowed to venture out without a guard irrespective of your gender.

People don’t see a lot of tourists, so there will be some curiosity. If they are up for a chat, be friendly. Most people are kind and welcoming of travelers since it’s a rare sight in these parts of the world. **Solo female travel in East Africa** is doable with the right mindset and precautions.

**Best time to visit**

Avoid the summer. I went in November and it was still warm, but it’s still the best time to visit.


**Currency**

Dollar is best in most parts of Africa. Change to local currency for tips + souvenirs.

**Is it true you need an armed guard?**

A guide/local help is better to avoid unnecessary confusion after you land. Someone who knows the local language and ways is highly recommended unless you have unlimited time to do this trip... in which case, independent traveling is doable. Although remember, as a tourist, you cannot venture out of the capital without an armed escort. This is the law.

The armed guard will just be with you in case of any emergencies; they don’t really do much. Rest assured this is merely a precaution set in place after a kidnapping of a foreigner many years ago. Either way, it’s a cool story to tell when you experience something like this for the first time.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-8.jpg)

**Cost**

Costs vary depending on the number of people. Supply-demand is real. Expect to shell out $50–60 for a hotel/night. For the tour, if you are three people, it could cost between $300–400 for a short 3/4 day trip; the math is $150/day for a guard + car with fuel.

**Vegetarian Food**

Not much fresh veggie food is available once you leave Hargeisa, but on one night, I got very lucky finding a veggie pizza in **Berbera beach Somaliland**. Heaven after no food for a while.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-11.jpg)

**Where I stayed**

[Mansoor Hotel](https://maan-soor.com/contact-page/) in Hargeisa. The manager, Khalid, will be happy to set you up with a local guide + armed guard. DM me on[ Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/wayfarer_anki/) if you need his info.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-15.jpg)

In Berbera, I stayed at[ Allore Hotel](http://facebook.com/alloorehotelberbera/). Nothing to rave about and a bit steep at $30 for one night, but what can you do?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-19.jpg)

**Highlights**

**Hargeisa**

As I got my passport stamped and exited the airport, I was greeted with cheerful smiles of “Welcome to Somaliland.” I was now officially in a country that wasn’t recognized by any government entity. So what’s the story? The world recognizes Somalia as being made up of one country with three areas—Somalia, Puntland & Somaliland. However, in the early '90s, Somaliland fought a war with Somalia, won, and declared themselves an independent country. While Somalia continues to have violent terrorism issues, this country has largely restored law and order and is a safe place to visit. There is not a lot to do in this chaotic capital, but you can take a walk around if you fancy. A visit to the camel market and the money market is kind of cool. Great inclusion for your list of **things to do in Somaliland**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-23.jpg)

**Sheikh Mountains**

It’s hard to believe places like this exist in a country like Somaliland. This mountain is off the guidebooks even for a country as off the beaten track as this.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-27.jpg)

The drive up to Sheikh Mountains in Somaliland came as a breath of fresh air and a reprieve from the arid landscapes we have at home in Dubai.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-29.jpg)

There are a few different viewpoints you can hike to as well. The lush green mountains, the winding drive up, the picturesque scenery, and to top it off, the moody weather. What an exciting experience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-32.jpg)

If you happen to make it to Somaliland, I highly recommend taking this long route on the way to Berbera. As I mentioned on stories, you will need a guide/driver + convoy + armed guard(s) to venture anywhere in the country. It turned out to be the favorite part of my trip. The jungle girl in me was happiest.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-38.jpg)

**Berbera**

Berbera, a partly ruined seaport city that sits amidst beautiful empty beaches. The first picture is me hopping onboard a boat laying still on the Somalian waters.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-42.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-44.jpg)

It was once an important trading hub in the region, the largest of its kind. As you can see here, it stands out for its colorful crumbling buildings.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-48.jpg)

It was damaged in the Somali civil war, so it reminded me of our trip to Massawa, [Eritrea.](/post/visiting-the-north-korea-of-africa-eritrea)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-50.jpg)

The beach could have easily been one in Dubai—so deserted and so incredibly cool to hang all by your lonesome.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-51.jpg)

From meeting Tariq the fisherman to the random men on the port, I got to meet many strangers who insisted on taking a picture and said, “Tell the world you met people in Somaliland and that they are very nice!” I get to interact with so many people from all walks of life on these trips—and as I often say, they help keep life in perspective. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-54.jpg)

**Laas Geel**

As the convoy car with an armed guard made a stop on an empty road, I finally arrived at the last stop on the trip—[Laas Geel ](https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/5380/)**cave paintings**, an isolated site of prehistoric cave art. Discovered by a French team in 2002, this site is one of Africa’s (and perhaps the world’s) greatest prehistoric art sites


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-cover.jpg)

It is undoubtedly the highlight and the biggest “tourist attraction” in a country as off the beaten track as Somaliland.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-61.jpg)

The best part was that the cave was still in impeccable condition—given they are exposed to air, the colors and patterns are so vivid. This is said to be one of the oldest and most beautiful cave paintings in Africa, estimated to be somewhere between 5,000–10,000 years old. What blows my mind is how are these colors so well preserved? Incredible, right?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-63.jpg)

There are a few different caves you can walk to, but the first one is the most impressive with over 300 simple drawings of human relations with cattle—a depiction of their lives back then. I thoroughly enjoyed hiking to the different caves and couldn’t recommend it enough.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-64.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-66.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-68.jpg)

- 🍃 This is a must-do on any trip to Somaliland. It’s the most touristy thing to do in the country.
🍃 This is a must-do on any trip to Somaliland. It’s the most touristy thing to do in the country.

- 🍃 It’s open to travelers BUT you need to apply for a permit to visit. It costs $25 per person and could take 2 days to process.

- 🍃 It’s an easy day trip from Hargeisa or you can stop on the way back from the coast.
🍃 It’s an easy day trip from Hargeisa or you can stop on the way back from the coast.

**Final Thoughts**

Unreal, unequivocally beautiful, and as untouched as it gets. That defined my trip to this unheard-of country.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-70.jpg)

Tourism still barely exists here. In fact, more people travel to[ Antarctica](/post/how-to-plan-a-trip-to-antarctica) than they do to Somaliland in a year. But IF you are adventurous enough to cross its borders, you will discover its long struggle for freedom and nomadic heritage. In just a few days, you can travel from its busy capital Hargeisa to the ancient port of Berbera, to its vast deserts and empty beaches, to discovering beautifully preserved rock art. Pretty great going for a country that isn’t even a country, right?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-71.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-73.jpg)

Negative emotions about a place are often rooted in fear and it holds us hostage, preventing us from discovering magical places like Somaliland. As I left the smiling people behind, there was hope that this would be a super epic trip—and it was definitely all that.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoveringsomaliland-75.jpg)

Go with an open mind!

Cheers,

Anki

From solo travel stories to offbeat discoveries, we’re proud to have been recognized by both Indian and international media.

[The National News ](https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/money/2022/07/28/how-to-save-money-so-you-can-afford-to-travel-more/),[Khaleej Times ](https://www.khaleejtimes.com/travel/uae-how-to-travel-the-world-despite-a-weak-passport-expats-share-tips),[Mint / Livemint](https://www.livemint.com/news/trends/this-visited-108-countries-while-holding-full-time-job/amp-11746251898742.html), [Times Now News](https://www.timesnownews.com/travel/travelogues/solo-trip-india-why-not-travel-vlogger-ankitha-rajendaran-on-travelling-solo-to-109-countries-and-the-joy-of-embracing-unseen-destinations-article-152114732)[,Outlook Traveller](https://www.outlooktraveller.com/destinations/international/travel-to-syria-uzbekistan-and-iran), [Hindustan Times ](https://www.hindustantimes.com/lifestyle/travel/woman-who-travelled-solo-to-109-countries-7-continents-shares-her-top-travel-hacks-every-female-should-know-101747298629619.html),[MSN India](https://www.msn.com/en-in/money/topstories/meet-ankita-rajendaran-the-woman-who-travelled-to-108-countries-while-working-full-time/ar-AA1DUMAu?ocid=spr_trending&businessvertical=News), [The Established ](https://www.theestablished.com/self/health/the-best-company-is-your-ownand-indian-youth-is-agreeing)[NDTV](https://www.ndtv.com/video/news/news/couple-who-have-explored-over-80-countries-share-tips-for-budget-travel-729052), [Times of India](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/life-style/travel/destinations/7-countries-you-should-visit-if-you-want-to-travel-differently/photostory/121286098.cms), [SheThePeople](https://www.shethepeople.tv/interviews/ankitha-rajendaran-why-not-book-interview-9426506)

On a whim, I took off and landed in a country that (technically) doesn’t even exist. This is Somaliland. No, it’s actually NOT a part of Somalia. Somaliland is now a country on its own, with its own currency and government. This is yet another country that shattered my every preconception this year. It was a pleasant surprise and beyond beautiful.

It was a far cry from the usual Western media fear mongering you see in the media. Is Somaliland safe for tourists? Surprisingly, yes. It is now an independent country with its own government, currency, and story—but it’s not still recognized by the international community as a country.

It really shakes up your preconceptions too—you can’t go 10 meters without a smile from a stranger, offering you qat or khat (more on this later). It was an enthralling and surprisingly fun trip.

In this Somaliland travel guide, I will tell you how you can go here safely and have a fantastic travel experience discovering offbeat African destinations.

Highlights:

Hargeisa. Berbera. Sheikh Mountains. Laas Geel cave paintings.

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Solo Travel + Safety Solo Travel + Safety

  • Budget Budget

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

Flights + Visa

I flew directly with FlyDubai Hargeisa flights—a short direct flight to Hargeisa, the capital.

For the Somaliland visa** from Dubai**, you will need to apply at a Somaliland mission; remember you CANNOT travel to Somaliland with a Somalia visa and vice versa. Your visa has to be through Somaliland. If in Dubai, visit their office with a confirmed return ticket, tour + hotel booking. Pay $60 and get an e-visa copy by email in 48–72 hours. Once you land, you won’t be asked to pay extra—if you are, it’s likely a bribe. Carry your payment receipt with you and get a stamp. Simple.

Solo traveling. Are you required to wear a scarf?

As with any country in the world, be aware of your surroundings. Even more so in a country like this since you are not allowed to venture out without a guard irrespective of your gender.

People don’t see a lot of tourists, so there will be some curiosity. If they are up for a chat, be friendly. Most people are kind and welcoming of travelers since it’s a rare sight in these parts of the world. Solo female travel in East Africa is doable with the right mindset and precautions.

Best time to visit

Avoid the summer. I went in November and it was still warm, but it’s still the best time to visit.

Currency

Dollar is best in most parts of Africa. Change to local currency for tips + souvenirs.

Is it true you need an armed guard?

A guide/local help is better to avoid unnecessary confusion after you land. Someone who knows the local language and ways is highly recommended unless you have unlimited time to do this trip… in which case, independent traveling is doable. Although remember, as a tourist, you cannot venture out of the capital without an armed escort. This is the law.

The armed guard will just be with you in case of any emergencies; they don’t really do much. Rest assured this is merely a precaution set in place after a kidnapping of a foreigner many years ago. Either way, it’s a cool story to tell when you experience something like this for the first time.

Cost

Costs vary depending on the number of people. Supply-demand is real. Expect to shell out $50–60 for a hotel/night. For the tour, if you are three people, it could cost between $300–400 for a short 3/4 day trip; the math is $150/day for a guard + car with fuel.

Vegetarian Food

Not much fresh veggie food is available once you leave Hargeisa, but on one night, I got very lucky finding a veggie pizza in Berbera beach Somaliland. Heaven after no food for a while.

Where I stayed

Mansoor Hotel in Hargeisa. The manager, Khalid, will be happy to set you up with a local guide + armed guard. DM me on Instagram if you need his info.

In Berbera, I stayed at Allore Hotel. Nothing to rave about and a bit steep at $30 for one night, but what can you do?

Highlights

Hargeisa

As I got my passport stamped and exited the airport, I was greeted with cheerful smiles of “Welcome to Somaliland.” I was now officially in a country that wasn’t recognized by any government entity. So what’s the story? The world recognizes Somalia as being made up of one country with three areas—Somalia, Puntland & Somaliland. However, in the early ’90s, Somaliland fought a war with Somalia, won, and declared themselves an independent country. While Somalia continues to have violent terrorism issues, this country has largely restored law and order and is a safe place to visit. There is not a lot to do in this chaotic capital, but you can take a walk around if you fancy. A visit to the camel market and the money market is kind of cool. Great inclusion for your list of things to do in Somaliland.

Sheikh Mountains

It’s hard to believe places like this exist in a country like Somaliland. This mountain is off the guidebooks even for a country as off the beaten track as this.

The drive up to Sheikh Mountains in Somaliland came as a breath of fresh air and a reprieve from the arid landscapes we have at home in Dubai.

There are a few different viewpoints you can hike to as well. The lush green mountains, the winding drive up, the picturesque scenery, and to top it off, the moody weather. What an exciting experience.

If you happen to make it to Somaliland, I highly recommend taking this long route on the way to Berbera. As I mentioned on stories, you will need a guide/driver + convoy + armed guard(s) to venture anywhere in the country. It turned out to be the favorite part of my trip. The jungle girl in me was happiest.

Berbera

Berbera, a partly ruined seaport city that sits amidst beautiful empty beaches. The first picture is me hopping onboard a boat laying still on the Somalian waters.

It was once an important trading hub in the region, the largest of its kind. As you can see here, it stands out for its colorful crumbling buildings.

It was damaged in the Somali civil war, so it reminded me of our trip to Massawa, Eritrea.

The beach could have easily been one in Dubai—so deserted and so incredibly cool to hang all by your lonesome.

From meeting Tariq the fisherman to the random men on the port, I got to meet many strangers who insisted on taking a picture and said, “Tell the world you met people in Somaliland and that they are very nice!” I get to interact with so many people from all walks of life on these trips—and as I often say, they help keep life in perspective. 

Laas Geel

As the convoy car with an armed guard made a stop on an empty road, I finally arrived at the last stop on the trip—Laas Geel cave paintings, an isolated site of prehistoric cave art. Discovered by a French team in 2002, this site is one of Africa’s (and perhaps the world’s) greatest prehistoric art sites

It is undoubtedly the highlight and the biggest “tourist attraction” in a country as off the beaten track as Somaliland.

The best part was that the cave was still in impeccable condition—given they are exposed to air, the colors and patterns are so vivid. This is said to be one of the oldest and most beautiful cave paintings in Africa, estimated to be somewhere between 5,000–10,000 years old. What blows my mind is how are these colors so well preserved? Incredible, right?

There are a few different caves you can walk to, but the first one is the most impressive with over 300 simple drawings of human relations with cattle—a depiction of their lives back then. I thoroughly enjoyed hiking to the different caves and couldn’t recommend it enough.

  • 🍃 This is a must-do on any trip to Somaliland. It’s the most touristy thing to do in the country. 🍃 This is a must-do on any trip to Somaliland. It’s the most touristy thing to do in the country.

  • 🍃 It’s open to travelers BUT you need to apply for a permit to visit. It costs $25 per person and could take 2 days to process. 🍃 It’s open to travelers BUT you need to apply for a permit to visit. It costs $25 per person and could take 2 days to process.

  • 🍃 It’s an easy day trip from Hargeisa or you can stop on the way back from the coast. 🍃 It’s an easy day trip from Hargeisa or you can stop on the way back from the coast.

Final Thoughts

Unreal, unequivocally beautiful, and as untouched as it gets. That defined my trip to this unheard-of country.

Tourism still barely exists here. In fact, more people travel to Antarctica than they do to Somaliland in a year. But IF you are adventurous enough to cross its borders, you will discover its long struggle for freedom and nomadic heritage. In just a few days, you can travel from its busy capital Hargeisa to the ancient port of Berbera, to its vast deserts and empty beaches, to discovering beautifully preserved rock art. Pretty great going for a country that isn’t even a country, right?

Negative emotions about a place are often rooted in fear and it holds us hostage, preventing us from discovering magical places like Somaliland. As I left the smiling people behind, there was hope that this would be a super epic trip—and it was definitely all that.

Go with an open mind!

Cheers,

My 8 day journey across Gobi Desert, Mongolia

Every month Instagram tells you about a new and "undiscovered" destination tipped to be the next big thing. Often in Europe or Southeast Asia. But to me, unusual adventures are less about chasing trends and more about truly seeing another side of the world. On that note, welcome to country number 77 – Mongolia. Have you ever dreamt of going somewhere so far-flung and traveling so differently that it redefines how you see the world? Mongolia  is just the place. I recently took a solo trip...

Every month Instagram tells you about a new and "undiscovered" destination tipped to be the next big thing. Often in Europe or Southeast Asia. But to me, unusual adventures are less about chasing trends and more about truly seeing another side of the world.

On that note, welcome to **country number 77 – Mongolia.**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-1.jpg)

Have you ever dreamt of going somewhere so far-flung and traveling so differently that it redefines how you see the world? **Mongolia** is just the place. I recently took a solo trip through one of the world’s least densely populated nations, and it was everything I imagined – vast, wild, extreme, and deeply humbling


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-4.jpg)

.**Mongolia will push you.** It’s as tough as it is beautiful. Not many tourists make it here, but those who do never leave the same. Here's a glimpse into my 8-day road trip across the **Gobi Desert**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-6.jpg)

** Itinerary**

- **Day 1:** Land in Ulaanbaatar ➝ Drive to White Stupa ➝ Stay at Ger Camp
**Day 1:** Land in Ulaanbaatar ➝ Drive to White Stupa ➝ Stay at Ger Camp

- **Day 2:** Drive to Yol Valley ➝ Ger Camp
**Day 2:** Drive to Yol Valley ➝ Ger Camp

- **Day 3:** Drive to Khongor Sand Dunes ➝ Ger Camp
**Day 3:** Drive to Khongor Sand Dunes ➝ Ger Camp

- **Day 4:** Drive to Flaming Cliffs ➝ Ger Camp
**Day 4:** Drive to Flaming Cliffs ➝ Ger Camp

- **Day 5:** Drive to Ongi Monastery ➝ Ger Camp
**Day 5:** Drive to Ongi Monastery ➝ Ger Camp

- **Day 6–8:** Return to Ulaanbaatar ➝ Explore & Rest
**Day 6–8:** Return to Ulaanbaatar ➝ Explore & Rest

**TABLE OF CONTENTS **

- [FAQ ](#viewer-4c8pj)
[FAQ ](#viewer-4c8pj)

- [Where We Stayed](#viewer-ag9iu) 
[Where We Stayed](#viewer-ag9iu)

[Highlights](#viewer-eqphg)

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-7aa73) 

**FLIGHTS + VISA IF TRAVELING FROM DUBAI WITH AN INDIAN PASSPORT**

There are limited flights to **Mongolia**. From the Middle East or Europe, [Turkish Airlines](https://www.expedia.com/Flights) is your best bet (prepare for a long journey).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-7.jpg)

Mongolia has introduced[ visa-on-arrival](https://immigration.gov.mn/en/) for many nationalities, but **Indian passport holders** still need to apply in advance. I emailed my documents (flight & tour bookings) to the [Mongolian Embassy in Abu Dhabi](https://www.eoimn.gov.in/) and got a single-entry 30-day visa for $50. The process was smooth and fast.

**LANGUAGE**

Almost **no one speaks English** in Mongolia, including in the capital. Be ready to use sign language, Google Translate, or—better yet—travel with a local guide.

**BEST TIME TO VISIT**

Ideal: **May** (no crowds, pleasant weather) Popular: **June–August** (hotter but more active) Chilly but manageable: **September** Avoid: **October onwards** – many roads become inaccessible

**CURRENCY**

Use **USD** for major expenses, but keep **Mongolian Tugrik** for daily needs. ATMs are rare in rural areas.


**LOCAL GUIDE**

Mongolia is not a DIY country. Distances are vast. I pre-arranged a fabulous local guide through my trusted contacts. Please note that costs quickly add up given the massive distances you have to cover in a short period of time. 

Feel free to email me for guide details. **I don’t receive any commissions** - I’m happy to support a hardworking local team and help you have a great trip. To help them out, please include your **confirmed travel dates and number of people** when you get in touch so they can give you an accurate response right away!

**SOLO TRAVEL + SAFETY**

I traveled solo with a local guide and always felt safe. That said, **alcoholism** is a real issue in Mongolia - be alert in rural areas. Most people were welcoming and kind.

**GER CAMPS - WHAT TO EXPECT?**

Basic but authentic. Think:

- Single beds
Single beds

- Dry toilets
Dry toilets

- No showers
No showers

- Stunning views & silence
Stunning views & silence

If you aren’t used to roughing it out, this trip might not be for you (especially with kids).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-10.jpg)

**VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD. HOW HARD IS IT?**

Hard, but not impossible. Thanks to my guide, I had access to a stove. I cooked simple vegan dishes (rice, noodles, veggies). Also packed **Maggi** and protein bars. In Ulaanbaatar, check out **Loving Hut** for delicious vegan Mongolian food.. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-16.jpg)

**HIGHLIGHTS**

**WHITE STUPA**

A Martian-like landscape carved by centuries of erosion. The rocks appear red from afar, but up close, they burst with colors. Absolutely surreal.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-20.jpg)

Contrary to what you would think, the gobi desert is not just about sand dunes. It is also home to a whole set of diverse landscapes from mountains to green valleys. My first stop was this otherworldly martian looking landscape shaped from thousands of years of wind and water erosion. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-26.jpg)

Though it looks like a red rock, as I hiked closer, I found so many vibrant colors on the rocks. This was one of my favorite places on the trip.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-28.jpg)

Ice-capped canyons in the middle of the desert. Grazing yaks, narrow trails, hidden streams—**unexpected magic in the heart of Mongolia**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-31.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-33.jpg)

I hiked past grazing yaks and horses to a stream hidden deep in the valley. This valley is known for its shade clad canyons that prevent ice from melting well into the summer. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-37.jpg)

It’s astounding to find places like this, hard to believe all of this is smack in the middle of a desert. Still SO untouched by mass tourism. Will never get tired of exploring the world like this. My whole heart. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-41.jpg)

[Travel Tip](/post/travel-tips-for-beginners-your-guide-to-stress-free-adventures)**:** Visit in May or June to spot ice and avoid crowds.

**KHONGOR SAND DUNES**

Known as the **“singing dunes”**—as wind moves across them, they hum. I climbed to the top and watched horses graze in the green pastures below the desert. Pure poetry.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-43.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-47.jpg)

Said to be one of the largest and most spectacular dunes in all of Mongolia, the grandeur of the scenery is quite spellbinding. This place is called the “singing dunes” because the sands make an eerie tune as the wind blows over shifting sand. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-49.jpg)

As I huffed and puffed my way up this humongous pile of sand, I can confirm that this is indeed true.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-50.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-52.jpg)

Something you need to know about Mongolia is the weather can change in a few minutes. I could hear the rattling windows in my ger camp as a sandstorm that raged on for 15 hours straight. It's not scary as the camps are sturdy but if you are a light sleeper, you are in for a long night. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-54.jpg)

As someone who was raised in the Middle-East, there was one other thing I found fascinating - the horses grazing on a green field in front of a barren Gobi dessert. Just Mongolia things!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-55.jpg)

**FLAMING CLIFFS**

Where **dinosaur fossils** were first discovered. The fiery red clay glows at sunset. Feels like walking through prehistoric history. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-56.jpg)

At first glance, the place is an intense evocation of the great emptiness of the Mongolian landscapes. The hardened soil, which has mostly remained unchanged since the Cretaceous period, is set ablaze any time of day. Starkly different to the White Stupa, these cliffs are made up of red clay soil. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-61.jpg)

The region is called Bayanzag meaning it is rich in Saxual buses, the only plant that can survive both the extreme heat and cold of the country. It's just incredible how we can find such marvels in hidden corners of the planet. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-65.jpg)

**ONGI MONASTERY**

Ruins of a once-grand monastery destroyed in 1939. Quiet, moving, spiritual. The landscape here is equally breathtaking.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-67.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-69.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-71.jpg)

This place is a historically significant. Once a monastery that housed over 1000 monks, the structures were destroyed in 1939 to the anti-religious purges of communism. Walking around the deserted village is a surreal experience. You can just imagine what once was - the crowds, perhaps the happy smiles, the prayers, the families, the monks.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-73.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-75.jpg)

Today the monastery is mostly empty, with a few local families taking care of the temples as a homage to people who lost their lives. As I hiked and explored the monastery, I find lots of connection to Dalai Lama in Tibet here. Monks still visit the village during the summer and the winter. The whole atmosphere, while a solemn reminder of the tragedy, is still extremely tranquil.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-77.jpg)

**ULAANBAATAR**

**FINAL THOUGHTS**

**Mongolia**—land of the Eternal Blue Sky, Genghis Khan, and soul-stirring silence.

These aren’t your average tourist destinations.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-112.jpg)

 It takes **grit, patience, and planning**, but the rewards are once-in-a-lifetime memories.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-113.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-115.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-117.jpg)

I feel lucky to travel like this—with a weak passport, yet a strong sense of wonder.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-119.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-121.jpg)

If you're looking to explore places few dare to go, **Mongolia should be on your list**. Rugged, raw, and real.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermongolia-cover.jpg)

Our travel journey—filled with immersive adventures, honest insights, and practical tips—has resonated deeply with readers who seek meaning beyond the map. From solo travel stories to offbeat discoveries, we’re proud to have been recognized by both Indian and international media.
