Every month Instagram tells you about a new and “undiscovered” destination tipped to be the next big thing. Often in Europe or Southeast Asia. But to me, unusual adventures are less about chasing trends and more about truly seeing another side of the world.

On that note, welcome to country number 77 – Mongolia.

Have you ever dreamt of going somewhere so far-flung and traveling so differently that it redefines how you see the world? Mongolia is just the place. I recently took a solo trip through one of the world’s least densely populated nations, and it was everything I imagined – vast, wild, extreme, and deeply humbling

.Mongolia will push you. It’s as tough as it is beautiful. Not many tourists make it here, but those who do never leave the same. Here’s a glimpse into my 8-day road trip across the Gobi Desert.

** Itinerary**

  • Day 1: Land in Ulaanbaatar ➝ Drive to White Stupa ➝ Stay at Ger Camp Day 1: Land in Ulaanbaatar ➝ Drive to White Stupa ➝ Stay at Ger Camp

  • Day 2: Drive to Yol Valley ➝ Ger Camp Day 2: Drive to Yol Valley ➝ Ger Camp

  • Day 3: Drive to Khongor Sand Dunes ➝ Ger Camp Day 3: Drive to Khongor Sand Dunes ➝ Ger Camp

  • Day 4: Drive to Flaming Cliffs ➝ Ger Camp Day 4: Drive to Flaming Cliffs ➝ Ger Camp

  • Day 5: Drive to Ongi Monastery ➝ Ger Camp Day 5: Drive to Ongi Monastery ➝ Ger Camp

  • Day 6–8: Return to Ulaanbaatar ➝ Explore & Rest Day 6–8: Return to Ulaanbaatar ➝ Explore & Rest

**TABLE OF CONTENTS **

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Vegan + Vegetarian Food Vegan + Vegetarian Food

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

FLIGHTS + VISA IF TRAVELING FROM DUBAI WITH AN INDIAN PASSPORT

There are limited flights to Mongolia. From the Middle East or Europe, Turkish Airlines is your best bet (prepare for a long journey).

Mongolia has introduced visa-on-arrival for many nationalities, but Indian passport holders still need to apply in advance. I emailed my documents (flight & tour bookings) to the Mongolian Embassy in Abu Dhabi and got a single-entry 30-day visa for $50. The process was smooth and fast.

LANGUAGE

Almost no one speaks English in Mongolia, including in the capital. Be ready to use sign language, Google Translate, or—better yet—travel with a local guide.

BEST TIME TO VISIT

Ideal: May (no crowds, pleasant weather) Popular: June–August (hotter but more active) Chilly but manageable: September Avoid: October onwards – many roads become inaccessible

CURRENCY

Use USD for major expenses, but keep Mongolian Tugrik for daily needs. ATMs are rare in rural areas.

LOCAL GUIDE

Mongolia is not a DIY country. Distances are vast. I pre-arranged a fabulous local guide through my trusted contacts. Please note that costs quickly add up given the massive distances you have to cover in a short period of time.

Feel free to email me for guide details. I don’t receive any commissions - I’m happy to support a hardworking local team and help you have a great trip. To help them out, please include your confirmed travel dates and number of people when you get in touch so they can give you an accurate response right away!

SOLO TRAVEL + SAFETY

I traveled solo with a local guide and always felt safe. That said, alcoholism is a real issue in Mongolia - be alert in rural areas. Most people were welcoming and kind.

GER CAMPS - WHAT TO EXPECT?

Basic but authentic. Think:

  • Single beds Single beds

  • Dry toilets Dry toilets

  • No showers No showers

  • Stunning views & silence Stunning views & silence

If you aren’t used to roughing it out, this trip might not be for you (especially with kids).

VEGAN + VEGETARIAN FOOD. HOW HARD IS IT?

Hard, but not impossible. Thanks to my guide, I had access to a stove. I cooked simple vegan dishes (rice, noodles, veggies). Also packed Maggi and protein bars. In Ulaanbaatar, check out Loving Hut for delicious vegan Mongolian food..

HIGHLIGHTS

WHITE STUPA

A Martian-like landscape carved by centuries of erosion. The rocks appear red from afar, but up close, they burst with colors. Absolutely surreal.

Contrary to what you would think, the gobi desert is not just about sand dunes. It is also home to a whole set of diverse landscapes from mountains to green valleys. My first stop was this otherworldly martian looking landscape shaped from thousands of years of wind and water erosion.

Though it looks like a red rock, as I hiked closer, I found so many vibrant colors on the rocks. This was one of my favorite places on the trip.

Ice-capped canyons in the middle of the desert. Grazing yaks, narrow trails, hidden streams—unexpected magic in the heart of Mongolia.

I hiked past grazing yaks and horses to a stream hidden deep in the valley. This valley is known for its shade clad canyons that prevent ice from melting well into the summer.

It’s astounding to find places like this, hard to believe all of this is smack in the middle of a desert. Still SO untouched by mass tourism. Will never get tired of exploring the world like this. My whole heart.

Travel Tip: Visit in May or June to spot ice and avoid crowds.

KHONGOR SAND DUNES

Known as the “singing dunes”—as wind moves across them, they hum. I climbed to the top and watched horses graze in the green pastures below the desert. Pure poetry.

Said to be one of the largest and most spectacular dunes in all of Mongolia, the grandeur of the scenery is quite spellbinding. This place is called the “singing dunes” because the sands make an eerie tune as the wind blows over shifting sand.

As I huffed and puffed my way up this humongous pile of sand, I can confirm that this is indeed true.

Something you need to know about Mongolia is the weather can change in a few minutes. I could hear the rattling windows in my ger camp as a sandstorm that raged on for 15 hours straight. It’s not scary as the camps are sturdy but if you are a light sleeper, you are in for a long night.

As someone who was raised in the Middle-East, there was one other thing I found fascinating - the horses grazing on a green field in front of a barren Gobi dessert. Just Mongolia things!

FLAMING CLIFFS

Where dinosaur fossils were first discovered. The fiery red clay glows at sunset. Feels like walking through prehistoric history.

At first glance, the place is an intense evocation of the great emptiness of the Mongolian landscapes. The hardened soil, which has mostly remained unchanged since the Cretaceous period, is set ablaze any time of day. Starkly different to the White Stupa, these cliffs are made up of red clay soil.

The region is called Bayanzag meaning it is rich in Saxual buses, the only plant that can survive both the extreme heat and cold of the country. It’s just incredible how we can find such marvels in hidden corners of the planet.

ONGI MONASTERY

Ruins of a once-grand monastery destroyed in 1939. Quiet, moving, spiritual. The landscape here is equally breathtaking.

This place is a historically significant. Once a monastery that housed over 1000 monks, the structures were destroyed in 1939 to the anti-religious purges of communism. Walking around the deserted village is a surreal experience. You can just imagine what once was - the crowds, perhaps the happy smiles, the prayers, the families, the monks.

Today the monastery is mostly empty, with a few local families taking care of the temples as a homage to people who lost their lives. As I hiked and explored the monastery, I find lots of connection to Dalai Lama in Tibet here. Monks still visit the village during the summer and the winter. The whole atmosphere, while a solemn reminder of the tragedy, is still extremely tranquil.

ULAANBAATAR

Top City Spots:

  • Ghandan Kiid Monastery – A spiritual center of Mongolian Buddhism Ghandan Kiid Monastery – A spiritual center of Mongolian Buddhism

  • Sukhbaatar Square – The heart of modern Mongolian identity Sukhbaatar Square – The heart of modern Mongolian identity

  • **Genghis Khan Statue Complex – **World’s largest equestrian statue **Genghis Khan Statue Complex – **World’s largest equestrian statue

  • Winter Palace Museum – Historic home of Mongolia’s last king Winter Palace Museum – Historic home of Mongolia’s last king

  • **Choijin Lama Temple – **Buddhist architecture meets city skyline **Choijin Lama Temple – **Buddhist architecture meets city skyline

  • Zaisan Hill Memorial – Climb for sweeping sunset views over UB  Zaisan Hill Memorial – Climb for sweeping sunset views over UB 

FINAL THOUGHTS

Mongolia—land of the Eternal Blue Sky, Genghis Khan, and soul-stirring silence.

These aren’t your average tourist destinations.

 It takes grit, patience, and planning, but the rewards are once-in-a-lifetime memories.

I feel lucky to travel like this—with a weak passport, yet a strong sense of wonder.

If you’re looking to explore places few dare to go, Mongolia should be on your list. Rugged, raw, and real.

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Lots of love,

Anki

Malawi: The Underrated Safari Destination You’ve Never Heard Of
We’ve taken many trips as a family, but this was my first solo journey with mom —just us girls—and to no surprise, it was to my favorite...

We’ve taken many trips as a family, but this was my **first solo journey with mom**—just us girls—and to no surprise, it was to my favorite continent: [Africa](/africa). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-1.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-3.jpg)

While she had already experienced the luxury side of **South Africa**, this time I wanted her to see the raw, real beauty of the continent. And we did it my way.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-5.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-9.jpg)

We chose a place few venture into—**Malawi**—a destination that doesn't often make it to mainstream safari bucket lists. That’s exactly what makes it **special**. No traffic jams of safari jeeps, no packed tourist lodges—just serene wilderness, charming towns, and wildlife in its truest form.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-11.jpg)

** Itinerary Overview**

- **Day 1:** Lake Malawi
**Day 1:** Lake Malawi

- **Days 2–4:** Liwonde National Park Safari
**Days 2–4:** Liwonde National Park Safari

- **Days 5–7:** Zomba Plateau or Dedza
**Days 5–7:** Zomba Plateau or Dedza

- [FAQ ](#viewer-4c8pj)
[FAQ ](#viewer-4c8pj)

[Guide + Costs](#viewer-24gbk)

[Highlights ](#viewer-1p6q4)

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-9as24)

**Flights + Visa**

We flew with[ ](https://www.ethiopianairlines.com/)[Ethiopian Airlines](https://www.ethiopianairlines.com/), facing long layovers and airport naps—part of the adventure! The **Malawi eVisa** can be obtained online for $50. 👉[ ](https://www.evisa.gov.mw/)[Apply for your Malawi visa here](https://www.evisa.gov.mw/).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-16.jpg)

**Pro tip:** You’ll need an invitation letter from a local tour agent or hotel, and confirmed return tickets. Ours was processed in under 48 hours.

 **Yellow Fever Certificate** is often asked for at immigration, even if not strictly required based on travel history—so carry it.

**Language & Currency**

English is widely spoken, especially in cities and tourist zones. We carried **USD**, which worked well for our 7-day trip since we had a local driver throughout.

**Best Time to Visit**

The **dry season from May to October** is ideal for safaris and lake days. We went in **March**—a lush, post-rainy season with zero crowds, but it comes with the risk of cyclones (like Freddy, which hit during our stay!).

**Local Transport + Guide**

We hired a **local driver** who also helped arrange budget stays and guides. For 5 days, expect to pay **$450-$500 per car**, not per person. Safari guides were included in our lodge stay. Reach out to **Mvuu Lodge** (details below) for trusted recommendations.

**Where We Stayed**

**Lake Malawi: Norman Carr Cottage**

A charming eco-friendly beach cottage where we woke to waves and bird songs. Ideal for **kayaking, diving** and stargazing. The food was delicious and fully vegetarian-friendly.

[ ](https://www.normancarrcottage.com/)[Norman Carr Cottage](https://www.normancarrcottage.com/)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-22.jpg)

**Liwonde National Park: Mvuu Lodge**

Set inside the park on the **banks of the Shire River**, this solar-powered safari lodge blew us away.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-26.jpg)

 The luxurious tents came with open baths, huge decks for birdwatching, and no fences—meaning elephants and hippos strolled freely.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-32.jpg)

A special shoutout to the chef for creating the **best vegetarian meals we've had on safari**. Hot cocoa + cookies mid-day? Yes, please!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-38.jpg)

[ ](https://cawsmw.com/lodges/mvuu-lodge/)[Mvuu Lodge - Central African Wilderness Safaris](https://cawsmw.com/lodges/mvuu-lodge/)What I loved about it was that there are no fences, which means you will find hippos, elephants casually wandering the grounds. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-42.jpg)

**Lilongwe: Annavilla7 Aparthotel**

A basic but comfy place to crash with A/C, WiFi, and hot meals after our adventures. 🌐[ ](https://www.annavilla7.com/)[Annavilla7 Lilongwe](https://www.annavilla7.com/)

** Highlights**

**1. Lake Malawi**

One of Africa’s largest and deepest lakes, with crystal-clear water, mountain backdrops, and **over 700 endemic fish species**—a paradise for beach lovers and snorkelers.

Learn more:[ ](https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/289/)[Lake Malawi National Park - UNESCO](https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/289/)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-48.jpg)

**2. Liwonde National Park**

Our 3-day safari was the crown jewel of the trip — even with Cyclone Freddy making an uninvited appearance.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-cover.jpg)

 This was my very first forest safari, and the combination of moody skies, earthy scents, and soft rain made it feel straight out of a wildlife documentary.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-54.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-56.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-58.jpg)

We spotted baby elephants splashing in the shallows, graceful zebras grazing against the backdrop of palm-studded plains, herds of impalas leaping through the bush, and the elusive, rare sable antelope shimmering in the low light.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-60.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-62.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-64.jpg)

 There’s no rush here — you soak in the slow, hypnotic rhythm of nature, from leopard prints fresh on the trail to the distant, almost haunting sound of lions roaring in the night.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-66.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-68.jpg)

I could go on a hundred safaris and never get bored. Half the thrill is the surprise — a sable antelope darting from the bush, a family of elephants rustling through the trees, the distant roar of a lion or the screech of an unseen eagle. Sometimes, even fresh hippo prints on a riverbank are as exciting as the sighting itself.

Check out:[ ](https://www.africanparks.org/the-parks/liwonde)[African Parks - Liwonde](https://www.africanparks.org/the-parks/liwonde)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-70.jpg)

**Dedza Plateau, Malawi**

Our original plan was to hike Zomba Plateau, but Cyclone Freddy led us to Dedza — one of Malawi’s highest hillside towns and a stunning finale to our trip


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-71.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-73.jpg)

We visited the famous[ Dedza Pottery & Café](http://www.dedzapottery.com/), set in peaceful gardens with sweeping views. Local artisans showcased beautiful handmade pottery, and the café served fantastic coffee, vegetarian meals, and their legendary cheesecake — the perfect souvenir stop on the way back to Lilongwe.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-77.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-79.jpg)

Road-tripping through Malawi’s Great Rift Valley is pure magic. From green plateaus and highlands to forested mountains and open plains, the scenery shifts constantly.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-81.jpg)

Along the way, we passed maize grinding, cattle grazing, and roadside cooking — scenes that make Malawian drives unforgettable.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-84.jpg)

 For the most breathtaking route, drive from the Shire River to Livingstonia in the north, with sweeping views over Majete Wildlife Reserve as you descend the lush mountains.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-86.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-88.jpg)

**Road-Tripping in Malawi**

Malawi lies on Africa’s **Great Rift Valley**, so road trips here are stunning. You’ll pass **rolling green hills, villages, maize fields**, and mountain landscapes that will leave you speechless.

It reminded me of road trips in Lesotho—scenic, peaceful, and deeply human. If you love driving yourself, consider starting near the **Shire River** and continuing up to **Livingstonia** for epic views and untouched nature.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-90.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-92.jpg)


### **Final Thoughts**

Where did my love for Africa begin? Kenya in 2018? [Tanzania](/post/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar)? Rwanda with gorilla babies? Maybe it doesn't matter. What matters is the deep **sense of peace and perspective** this continent brings.Wildlife trips are my happy place. Watching baby elephants tumble, hearing the haunting roar of lions, and seeing eagles soar—all of it resets your soul.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-94.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discovermalawi-96.jpg)

**Malawi** isn’t on most safari lists—and that’s precisely the point. No tourist rush. No overcrowded lodges. Just you, nature, and the raw heartbeat of Africa. This trip wasn’t just a memory. It was **a gift I gave my mom—and myself**. So go. Pick places that are lesser-known. Support conservation. Be humbled by nature. Because rain or shine, in **Malawi**, the animals still come out to play.

**Thanks for reading. Got questions? Drop them below 👇🏽** 

**Love, Anki**

We’ve taken many trips as a family, but this was my first solo journey with mom—just us girls—and to no surprise, it was to my favorite continent: Africa.

While she had already experienced the luxury side of South Africa, this time I wanted her to see the raw, real beauty of the continent. And we did it my way.

We chose a place few venture into—Malawi—a destination that doesn’t often make it to mainstream safari bucket lists. That’s exactly what makes it special. No traffic jams of safari jeeps, no packed tourist lodges—just serene wilderness, charming towns, and wildlife in its truest form.

** Itinerary Overview**

  • Day 1: Lake Malawi Day 1: Lake Malawi

  • Days 2–4: Liwonde National Park Safari Days 2–4: Liwonde National Park Safari

  • Days 5–7: Zomba Plateau or Dedza Days 5–7: Zomba Plateau or Dedza

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Guide + Costs Guide + Costs

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

Flights + Visa

We flew with Ethiopian Airlines, facing long layovers and airport naps—part of the adventure! The Malawi eVisa can be obtained online for $50. 👉 Apply for your Malawi visa here.

Pro tip: You’ll need an invitation letter from a local tour agent or hotel, and confirmed return tickets. Ours was processed in under 48 hours.

Yellow Fever Certificate is often asked for at immigration, even if not strictly required based on travel history—so carry it.

Language & Currency

English is widely spoken, especially in cities and tourist zones. We carried USD, which worked well for our 7-day trip since we had a local driver throughout.

Best Time to Visit

The dry season from May to October is ideal for safaris and lake days. We went in March—a lush, post-rainy season with zero crowds, but it comes with the risk of cyclones (like Freddy, which hit during our stay!).

Local Transport + Guide

We hired a local driver who also helped arrange budget stays and guides. For 5 days, expect to pay $450-$500 per car, not per person. Safari guides were included in our lodge stay. Reach out to Mvuu Lodge (details below) for trusted recommendations.

Where We Stayed

Lake Malawi: Norman Carr Cottage

A charming eco-friendly beach cottage where we woke to waves and bird songs. Ideal for kayaking, diving and stargazing. The food was delicious and fully vegetarian-friendly.

 Norman Carr Cottage

Liwonde National Park: Mvuu Lodge

Set inside the park on the banks of the Shire River, this solar-powered safari lodge blew us away.

 The luxurious tents came with open baths, huge decks for birdwatching, and no fences—meaning elephants and hippos strolled freely.

A special shoutout to the chef for creating the best vegetarian meals we’ve had on safari. Hot cocoa + cookies mid-day? Yes, please!

 Mvuu Lodge - Central African Wilderness SafarisWhat I loved about it was that there are no fences, which means you will find hippos, elephants casually wandering the grounds.

Lilongwe: Annavilla7 Aparthotel

A basic but comfy place to crash with A/C, WiFi, and hot meals after our adventures. 🌐 Annavilla7 Lilongwe

** Highlights**

1. Lake Malawi

One of Africa’s largest and deepest lakes, with crystal-clear water, mountain backdrops, and over 700 endemic fish species—a paradise for beach lovers and snorkelers.

Learn more: Lake Malawi National Park - UNESCO

2. Liwonde National Park

Our 3-day safari was the crown jewel of the trip — even with Cyclone Freddy making an uninvited appearance.

 This was my very first forest safari, and the combination of moody skies, earthy scents, and soft rain made it feel straight out of a wildlife documentary.

We spotted baby elephants splashing in the shallows, graceful zebras grazing against the backdrop of palm-studded plains, herds of impalas leaping through the bush, and the elusive, rare sable antelope shimmering in the low light.

 There’s no rush here — you soak in the slow, hypnotic rhythm of nature, from leopard prints fresh on the trail to the distant, almost haunting sound of lions roaring in the night.

I could go on a hundred safaris and never get bored. Half the thrill is the surprise — a sable antelope darting from the bush, a family of elephants rustling through the trees, the distant roar of a lion or the screech of an unseen eagle. Sometimes, even fresh hippo prints on a riverbank are as exciting as the sighting itself.

Check out: African Parks - Liwonde

Dedza Plateau, Malawi

Our original plan was to hike Zomba Plateau, but Cyclone Freddy led us to Dedza — one of Malawi’s highest hillside towns and a stunning finale to our trip

We visited the famous Dedza Pottery & Café, set in peaceful gardens with sweeping views. Local artisans showcased beautiful handmade pottery, and the café served fantastic coffee, vegetarian meals, and their legendary cheesecake — the perfect souvenir stop on the way back to Lilongwe.

Road-tripping through Malawi’s Great Rift Valley is pure magic. From green plateaus and highlands to forested mountains and open plains, the scenery shifts constantly.

Along the way, we passed maize grinding, cattle grazing, and roadside cooking — scenes that make Malawian drives unforgettable.

 For the most breathtaking route, drive from the Shire River to Livingstonia in the north, with sweeping views over Majete Wildlife Reserve as you descend the lush mountains.

Road-Tripping in Malawi

Malawi lies on Africa’s Great Rift Valley, so road trips here are stunning. You’ll pass rolling green hills, villages, maize fields, and mountain landscapes that will leave you speechless.

It reminded me of road trips in Lesotho—scenic, peaceful, and deeply human. If you love driving yourself, consider starting near the Shire River and continuing up to Livingstonia for epic views and untouched nature.

Final Thoughts

Where did my love for Africa begin? Kenya in 2018? Tanzania? Rwanda with gorilla babies? Maybe it doesn’t matter. What matters is the deep sense of peace and perspective this continent brings.Wildlife trips are my happy place. Watching baby elephants tumble, hearing the haunting roar of lions, and seeing eagles soar—all of it resets your soul.

Malawi isn’t on most safari lists—and that’s precisely the point. No tourist rush. No overcrowded lodges. Just you, nature, and the raw heartbeat of Africa. This trip wasn’t just a memory. It was a gift I gave my mom—and myself. So go. Pick places that are lesser-known. Support conservation. Be humbled by nature. Because rain or shine, in Malawi, the animals still come out to play.

Thanks for reading. Got questions? Drop them below 👇🏽 

Love, Anki

Doha Tourism: Exploring Qatar Beyond a Layover
Bringing you a curated guide from this tiny palm-shaped country just an hour from our home base, Dubai. Whether you’re in transit or...

Bringing you a curated guide from this tiny palm-shaped country just an hour from our home base, Dubai. Whether you’re in transit or planning a quick escape, **Qatar** is the perfect blend of traditional Arab charm and modern elegance—and this blog will show you how to do justice to **Doha tourism** in just 3-4 days. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-1.jpg)

This trip was about diving beyond the shiny skyline to explore ancient villages, desert landscapes, rich culture, and of course, souqs and skyline views


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-4.jpg)

**Itinerary Summary (3 Nights, 4 Days)**

- **Day 0**: Arrive in Doha late evening, head out for a local dinner
**Day 0**: Arrive in Doha late evening, head out for a local dinner

- **Day 1**: Relax in the morning, explore North Qatar in the afternoon, dinner at The Pearl
**Day 1**: Relax in the morning, explore North Qatar in the afternoon, dinner at The Pearl

- **Day 2**: Spa day + desert safari & sunset at the Inland Sea
**Day 2**: Spa day + desert safari & sunset at the Inland Sea

- **Day 3**: Explore Souq Waqif, Katara, Falcon Souq, Pearl, fly back late night
**Day 3**: Explore Souq Waqif, Katara, Falcon Souq, Pearl, fly back late night

**COVID Regulations**

Proof of vaccination may still be requested.

**Flights, Visa and Immigration (if traveling with an Indian passport from Dubai)**

I flew with Air Arabia from Sharjah to Doha. It was a short one hour flight. 

Since I am a resident in the GCC, I was eligible for a free Visa On Arrival, provided I fulfilled certain travel requirements, which are as below: 

- Your occupation has to be approved by the Qatar Ministry

- You have to have reservation stay for a minimum of two nights in the hotels listed on Visa On Arrival hotels on [Discover Qatar](https://www.discoverqatar.qa/mandatory-hotels-for-visa-on-arrival/)

- You have a travel health insurance from the list of pre-approved Qatar companies. I went with [QIC](https://www.qicuae.com/insurance/travel/). 

**Language**

Arabic is the official language. English widely spoken in Doha.

**Best Time to Visit**

**November to March** offers mild desert weather.

**Currency**

Carried USD and exchanged a little; **cards work well** in Doha.

**Getting Around**

- In **Doha**: Use the **metro** or local taxis
In **Doha**: Use the **metro** or local taxis

Outside the city: Rent a car or book tours from providers like[ ](https://qatartourism.com/)[Qatar Inbound Tours](https://qatartourism.com/) or[ ](https://www.365adventures.me/)[365 Adventures Qatar](https://www.365adventures.me/)

**Where I Stayed**

🏨 **Four Points Sheraton Doha** Located near the Souq, metro, and restaurants. As a Marriott Bonvoy member, I was upgraded to a spacious room with skyline views. Great service and buffet breakfast!

An arts hub with:

- Local & international restaurants

**The Pearl + Qanat Quartier**

A pastel-colored, luxury waterfront community resembling **Notting Hill meets Venice**.

- Great for photos
Great for photos

- Boutique shops & cafés
Boutique shops & cafés


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-58.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-60.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-62.jpg)

**Final Thoughts**

“Why Qatar?” people ask. And my answer is: **Because it surprises you.**

Most see Qatar as a stopover or confuse it with its glitzier neighbors. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-64.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-66.jpg)

But **Doha tourism** is about so much more—historic forts, evolving art scenes, architectural gems, abandoned towns, and golden dunes meeting blue seas.While it’s one of the richest countries in the world, it didn’t feel showy. Instead, what stood out were the contrasts—**tradition meets innovation**, and **stillness meets progress**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverqatar-68.jpg)

If you only have a few days and are flying through the Gulf, don’t just pass through—**stay a while, explore, and let Qatar charm you**.

Love, **Anki**

Turkmenistan Tourism Guide: Visa, Itinerary & Highlights

What is it like visiting one of the least accessible countries in the world? Thought-provoking, tightly controlled… yet, surprisingly warm and welcoming. Long dubbed the “North Korea with oil,”  Turkmenistan receives five times fewer tourists than North Korea —which is precisely what intrigued me. It turned out to be a road trip filled with strange grandeur, flaming craters, ghost-white cities, ancient villages, and classic Central Asian hospitality. Turkmenistan Road Trip Itinerary Here’s...

What is it like visiting one of the least accessible countries in the world? Thought-provoking, tightly controlled… yet, surprisingly warm and welcoming.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-1.jpg)

Long dubbed the “North Korea with oil,” Turkmenistan receives **five times fewer tourists than North Korea**—which is precisely what intrigued me. It turned out to be a road trip filled with strange grandeur, flaming craters, ghost-white cities, ancient villages, and classic Central Asian hospitality.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-4.jpg)

**Turkmenistan Road Trip Itinerary**

Here’s our 5-day itinerary:

- **Day 1–2:** Land in Ashgabat, city tour
**Day 1–2:** Land in Ashgabat, city tour

- **Day 3:** Drive to Erbent Village, camp overnight at **Darvaza Gas Crater**
**Day 3:** Drive to Erbent Village, camp overnight at **Darvaza Gas Crater**

- **Day 4:** Visit **Nokhur Village**, overnight at local homestay
**Day 4:** Visit **Nokhur Village**, overnight at local homestay

- **Day 5:** Cave stop & return to Ashgabat
**Day 5:** Cave stop & return to Ashgabat

- **Day 6:** Fly out
**Day 6:** Fly out

We covered this comfortably in 5 days—but if your LOI gives you more time, add hikes in the Kopet Dag mountains or more time in local villages.

[Final Thoughts ](#viewer-9re9p) 

**Visa & Flights to Turkmenistan**

**Fly into Ashgabat** – We flew in from Dubai on **Fly Dubai**, a short 2.5-hour flight.


**Visa Process: **Turkmenistan has one of the [toughest tourist visa systems](https://caravanistan.com/visa/turkmenistan/)[.](https://caravanistan.com/visa/turkmenistan/) Most visitors need a **Letter of Invitation (LOI)** from a local tour agency. Here’s how to get it:

- Find a **reliable local guide **
Find a **reliable local guide **

- Share your travel dates; the guide applies for an **LOI** from the Ministry of Tourism
Share your travel dates; the guide applies for an **LOI** from the Ministry of Tourism

- Once approved (can take 3 weeks), use it to get a **Visa on Arrival ($99)** at Ashgabat Airport
Once approved (can take 3 weeks), use it to get a **Visa on Arrival ($99)** at Ashgabat Airport

- Visa validity: usually **7–10 days** only
Visa validity: usually **7–10 days** only

- **Carry USD in cash**—credit cards don’t work!

**Carry USD in cash**—credit cards don’t work!


We got our LOI just 2 days before departure, so opted for **Visa on Arrival**. If you're based in the UAE, there's also an embassy in Abu Dhabi.



![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-6.jpg)

[Essential Travel Tips](https://www.gov.uk/foreign-travel-advice/turkmenistan)

**Language**

Even in Ashgabat, English is rare. A **local Russian-speaking guide** is essential.

**Currency**

Carry **only USD**. Exchange through your guide—**black market currency exchange is illegal**.

**Best Time to Visit**

Avoid the scorching summer! Visit during **March–May** or **October–November**.

**Photography Rules**

Do NOT photograph **government buildings**, **police**, or **presidential palaces**. You’ll find guards watching everywhere—respect the law.

Guide
Feel free to get in touch with me for a great local guide. I’m sharing this because I’m a huge believer in supporting this local team - they truly made our trip. To help them manage their bookings and give you the best service, please include your **confirmed travel dates and number of people** when you reach out. I love connecting fellow travelers with the best local resources to make sure your trip is as seamless as ours was!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-7.jpg)

** Where We Stayed**

- **Ashgabat** – [Ak Atyln Hotel](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Hotel_Review-g293966-d6465489-Reviews-Hotel_Ak_Altyn-Ashgabat_Ahal_Province.html): Central location, markets & cafes nearby  
**Ashgabat** – [Ak Atyln Hotel](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Hotel_Review-g293966-d6465489-Reviews-Hotel_Ak_Altyn-Ashgabat_Ahal_Province.html): Central location, markets & cafes nearby  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-8.jpg)

- **Darvaza** – Yurt Camp: Pizza cooked in the sand, roaring fire under the stars  
**Darvaza** – Yurt Camp: Pizza cooked in the sand, roaring fire under the stars

**Ashgabat – The Marble City**

Nicknamed “The City of White Marble”, Ashgabat holds the Guinness World Record for the most marble-clad buildings. Only white or light-colored cars are allowed!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-27.jpg)

The city is grand, surreal, and filled with golden statues and futuristic architecture — each building symbolizing something from Turkmen history. Photography is restricted in many places, but once you settle in, the city leaves a lasting impression.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-31.jpg)

**Highlights:**

- 📸 Panoramic views of the all-white city
📸 Panoramic views of the all-white city

🏛 Independence Monument with golden statues

**Erbent Village Stopover**

On the way to Darvaza, we stopped in **Erbent**, a traditional desert village. Life here still reflects semi-nomadic customs.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-43.jpg)

**Saparmurat Hajji Mosque (Geokdepe)**

Built in memory of fallen defenders, it’s an international project — German tiles, Moroccan doors, French-built. A symbol of unity, history, and pride.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-53.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-55.jpg)

**Ruhy Mosque**

Commissioned by the former president and not a true mosque — its pillars quote the Ruhnama (his book), not the Quran. Still, the scale and interior are breathtaking. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-59.jpg)

**Darvaza Gas Crater – The Gates of Hell**

Not natural—but epic. A **Soviet drilling accident gone wrong**, still burning decades later. Best viewed while **camping nearby**—stars above, flames below. Eerie & unforgettable.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-cover.jpg)

Why is it still such a great place to be? Our stay in the yurt—just like in Kyrgyzstan—was wonderful and surprisingly spacious. We’re always up for camping, and with only the stars above and the fire roaring in the distance, it felt perfect. Camping at Darvaza is the best way to experience it. The view at night was my favorite—almost apocalyptic, don’t you think?

**Nokhur Village – Mountain Magic**

A remote valley where animist beliefs meet ancient traditions:

- Graves marked with **goat horns** to ward off evil
Graves marked with **goat horns** to ward off evil

- Local women cover **mouths with scarves**—a mystery yet unsolved
Local women cover **mouths with scarves**—a mystery yet unsolved

Underground **healing cave pool** nearby—cooling, calming, spiritual


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-74.jpg)

Nokhur is remote and steeped in ancient traditions, unlike anywhere else in Turkmenistan. We stayed with one of the oldest families, sleeping on carpets and sharing endless food and chai. I noticed women cover their mouths with headscarves—one of many unique customs here.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-77.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-79.jpg)

The cemetery is especially striking, with goat horns on graves to ward off evil and stones carved with steps to guide souls to heaven.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-81.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-83.jpg)

Hiking to the tallest mountain filled me with gratitude for discovering such an untouched place.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-85.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-87.jpg)

On the way back to Ashgabat, we visited an underground cave with a healing lake—perfect for a refreshing dip after the hike. Highly recommended.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-89.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-91.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-93.jpg)

Final Thoughts

Turkmenistan is **not an easy country to visit**—but that’s exactly why you should.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-95.jpg)

It’s a country where **time feels frozen**, shaped by presidential ego, Soviet ruins, and age-old beliefs. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-96.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-65.jpg)

And yet, it is filled with **humble, curious people** who are proud to share their stories. If you crave experiences that challenge your ideas of travel, this road trip through Turkmenistan will change the way you see the world.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/discoverturkmenistan-100.jpg)

**Anki**

What is it like visiting one of the least accessible countries in the world? Thought-provoking, tightly controlled… yet, surprisingly warm and welcoming.

Long dubbed the “North Korea with oil,” Turkmenistan receives five times fewer tourists than North Korea—which is precisely what intrigued me. It turned out to be a road trip filled with strange grandeur, flaming craters, ghost-white cities, ancient villages, and classic Central Asian hospitality.

  • Day 4: Visit Nokhur Village, overnight at local homestay Day 4: Visit Nokhur Village, overnight at local homestay

  • Day 5: Cave stop & return to Ashgabat Day 5: Cave stop & return to Ashgabat

  • Day 6: Fly out Day 6: Fly out

We covered this comfortably in 5 days—but if your LOI gives you more time, add hikes in the Kopet Dag mountains or more time in local villages.

Visa & Flights to Turkmenistan

Fly into Ashgabat – We flew in from Dubai on Fly Dubai, a short 2.5-hour flight.

**Visa Process: **Turkmenistan has one of the toughest tourist visa systems. Most visitors need a Letter of Invitation (LOI) from a local tour agency. Here’s how to get it:

  • Find a **reliable local guide ** Find a **reliable local guide **

  • Share your travel dates; the guide applies for an LOI from the Ministry of Tourism Share your travel dates; the guide applies for an LOI from the Ministry of Tourism

  • Once approved (can take 3 weeks), use it to get a Visa on Arrival ($99) at Ashgabat Airport Once approved (can take 3 weeks), use it to get a Visa on Arrival ($99) at Ashgabat Airport

  • Visa validity: usually 7–10 days only Visa validity: usually 7–10 days only

  • Carry USD in cash—credit cards don’t work!

Carry USD in cash—credit cards don’t work!

We got our LOI just 2 days before departure, so opted for Visa on Arrival. If you’re based in the UAE, there’s also an embassy in Abu Dhabi.

Essential Travel Tips

Language

Even in Ashgabat, English is rare. A local Russian-speaking guide is essential.

Currency

Carry only USD. Exchange through your guide—black market currency exchange is illegal.

Best Time to Visit

Avoid the scorching summer! Visit during March–May or October–November.

Photography Rules

Do NOT photograph government buildings, police, or presidential palaces. You’ll find guards watching everywhere—respect the law.

Guide Feel free to get in touch with me for a great local guide. I’m sharing this because I’m a huge believer in supporting this local team - they truly made our trip. Hassle-Free Dubai Desert Camping: Campr Dubai & Overnight Experiences
All you need to know to plan a hassle-free glamping trip in Dubai.

**Dubai Desert Camping**

What a year, eh? Honestly, this blog has been gathering dust while life—and work—kept us busy. At the start of the year, we had ambitious plans to hit nine countries. Now, if we manage even one, I’ll consider the year partially redeemed.

For those following along, the UAE—especially Dubai—began reopening in July. Life isn’t completely back to normal yet: masks and social distancing remain mandatory indoors and outdoors when around others. But that hasn’t stopped us from making a few exceptions to explore the beauty that surrounds us.

Our latest adventure? **Camping in Dubai**—but not just any camping. This was a star-lit, hassle-free desert experience organized by **Campr Dubai**, a service founded by a young South African couple who realized there was a gap in the market for convenient, well-planned desert escapes. Intrigued? So were we! I quickly reached out to the Campr team to plan our getaway. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-1.jpg)

I came across a cool article on [Whatson Dubai](https://whatson.ae/2020/10/campr-uae/)[ ](https://whatson.ae/2020/10/campr-uae/)about a young South African couple who realized there was a gap in the market for something as cool as hassle-free camping. Intrigued? Yeah, I was as well! I quickly got in touch with the good folks over at [Campr](https://www.campr.ae/)**. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-2.jpg)

**Booking Your Campr Experience**

The process is surprisingly straightforward. Here’s how it works:

**Step 1:** Visit the **Campr Dubai** [website](https://www.campr.ae/) and choose your camping package. Options range from one-night stays to four-weekend experiences. We chose the Campr2 package for one night. You’ll also select the date, number of guests, and location—desert, wadi, or mountain.

We opted for the desert to maximize our chances of star-gazing and soaking in the early “winter” chill.

**Step 2:** Wait for a response. Christine from the team replied in less than two hours, providing all the details including what’s included and payment instructions.

**Step 3:** Review costs and inclusions.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-3.jpg)

Desert stay in Dubai: AED 950

- Wadi or mountain stays: AED 950 + AED 300 for transportation outside Dubai
Wadi or mountain stays: AED 950 + AED 300 for transportation outside Dubai

- Optional add-ons like food, drinks, and mobile toilets are extra. If you’ve done other camping trips, you’ll know that toilets aren’t always necessary for a one-night desert escape. 
Optional add-ons like food, drinks, and mobile toilets are extra. If you’ve done other camping trips, you’ll know that toilets aren’t always necessary for a one-night desert escape. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-4.jpg)

A lot of people asked me about the toilet situation - there are two options you can add on for a cost; a mobile toilet unit and a split toilet/shower unit. Having used both these options in [New Zealand](/post/comprehensive-camper-van-guide-to-spending-winter-in-new-zealand), I don't find them very comfortable. There are rarely any toilets needed out in the wild. We are used to this thanks to our other camping trips, so it was no issue for us (since we just stayed the night).  

**Step 4:** You’re all set. The Campr Dubai team sends reminders of what to pack—coffee, milk, sugar, etc.—and three days before your trip, you’ll receive an approximate location. The exact spot remains a small surprise, which we loved.

**The Desert Experience**

We got our precise location a few hours before departure. Forty-five minutes later, we arrived at a stunning desert landscape. The team had set everything up, including a massive tent that easily fit the two of us. Christine gave us a quick walkthrough of the inventory and tent setup.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-5.jpg)

The wind was strong initially but calmed by 6 p.m., allowing us to enjoy the crisp evening air. The tent was sturdy, spacious, and cozy. Unlike most camping trips where you wake up sore, the queen mattress was plush and surprisingly comfortable. Don’t forget your pillows, bedsheets, and quilt, though!

Special mention to the massive tent set up that comes up with the package. We could have sat there for hours just taking in the fresh brisk air and endless sand dunes. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-8.jpg)

We spent hours taking in the endless sand dunes, clicking photos, and playing frisbee before the sunset. The desert truly felt like a secluded oasis, with not another soul in sight—a perfect escape for anyone craving peace and solitud. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-10.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-12.jpeg)

You can almost think of it as a desert oasis where time stand stills. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-14.jpg)


### **Sunset and Dinner**

Before sunset, I cozied up inside the tent while my partner wandered the dunes. The view was phenomenal, and it reminded me why **Dubai overnight camping** is such a unique experience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-15.jpg)

The queen mattress was plush and extremely comfortable; I'll be honest, we usually end up with a sore back after a night out camping but not this time around. We slept like babies. 

**Don't forget to carry your own pillows, bedsheet and quilt. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-18.jpg)

The sunset itself was a sight to behold.. with work keeping us busy, we hardly get to appreciate all the amazing views our city of wonder offers. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-20.jpeg)

For dinner, we opted for a pre-packed homemade meal rather than lighting a fire. Post-dinner, the stars appeared in full glory. Fall weather in Dubai can be rare, but the chill added to the experience. We stayed up until 11 p.m., marveling at the galaxy above us. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-21.jpg)

Post dinner, the stars were out and how. It was COLD - fall weather in Dubai is usually a rarity unless you venture this far out of the city. So glad we packed our jackets. It was around 11 p.m. before we called it a night. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-22.png)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-24.png)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-26.png)


### **Sunrise and Morning Walk**

The next morning, I woke up early to catch the sunrise—a chilly but rewarding start to the day. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-30.jpeg)

Took a long walk along the dunes before coffee and more down time under the tent. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-32.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-34.jpeg)


### **Why We Love Camping in Dubai with Campr**

While glamping has its appeal, we prefer rustic setups where we handle everything ourselves. That said, **Campr Dubai** offers the perfect blend of convenience and adventure. There’s no stress about permits or equipment, and the experience feels intimate and untouched by modern life.

The vast desert, endless stars, and hassle-free setup made us appreciate just how much **Dubai weekend getaways** can offer beyond the city’s glittering skyline. Many assume Dubai is all glam, but adventures like these prove that raw beauty is never far away if you’re willing to explore a little deeper.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-36.jpg)


### **Tips for Your Dubai Desert Camping Trip**

- **Plan according to the season:** Desert days can be harsh in summer; winter offers cooler nights perfect for **Dubai overnight camping**.
**Plan according to the season:** Desert days can be harsh in summer; winter offers cooler nights perfect for **Dubai overnight camping**.

- **Pack essentials:** Jackets, blankets, pillows, and a quilt. Add food and drinks if not included.
**Pack essentials:** Jackets, blankets, pillows, and a quilt. Add food and drinks if not included.

- **Book early:** Popular weekends fill up quickly, especially if you’re eyeing **Dubai weekend getaways**.
**Book early:** Popular weekends fill up quickly, especially if you’re eyeing **Dubai weekend getaways**.

**Embrace the simplicity:** No Wi-Fi, no noise—just you, the sand, and the stars.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-37.jpeg)

**Takeaway: **

I am definitely a sucker for vast lonely landscapes mostly untouched by humans. A lot of folks feel Dubai is all glam, but truth be told, so are most cities. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-38.jpeg)

Dig a little deeper and you are never far away from finding stunning places like this hidden gem. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-39.jpg)

Not having to fret about the set up or permits was a refreshing change! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-42.jpg)

Sleeping under gazillion stars, the chilly weather, the hassle-free set up, being one with nature.. it took us back to our time in New Zealand. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-44.jpeg)

I would highly recommend this experience if you are looking to escape the boring humdrum city life for a night or two. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/dubaidesertcamping-45.jpg)

If you’re looking to escape the city’s routine for a night or two, I can’t recommend this enough. Whether it’s a last-minute **Dubai weekend getaway** or a planned **Dubai overnight camping** experience, this is a perfect option for anyone seeking adventure without the hassle.

Dubai Desert Camping

What a year, eh? Honestly, this blog has been gathering dust while life—and work—kept us busy. At the start of the year, we had ambitious plans to hit nine countries. Now, if we manage even one, I’ll consider the year partially redeemed.

For those following along, the UAE—especially Dubai—began reopening in July. Life isn’t completely back to normal yet: masks and social distancing remain mandatory indoors and outdoors when around others. But that hasn’t stopped us from making a few exceptions to explore the beauty that surrounds us.

Our latest adventure? Camping in Dubai—but not just any camping. This was a star-lit, hassle-free desert experience organized by Campr Dubai, a service founded by a young South African couple who realized there was a gap in the market for convenient, well-planned desert escapes. Intrigued? So were we! I quickly reached out to the Campr team to plan our getaway.

I came across a cool article on Whatson Dubai about a young South African couple who realized there was a gap in the market for something as cool as hassle-free camping. Intrigued? Yeah, I was as well! I quickly got in touch with the good folks over at Campr**. **

Booking Your Campr Experience

The process is surprisingly straightforward. Here’s how it works:

Step 1: Visit the Campr Dubai website and choose your camping package. Options range from one-night stays to four-weekend experiences. We chose the Campr2 package for one night. You’ll also select the date, number of guests, and location—desert, wadi, or mountain.

We opted for the desert to maximize our chances of star-gazing and soaking in the early “winter” chill.

Step 2: Wait for a response. Christine from the team replied in less than two hours, providing all the details including what’s included and payment instructions.

Step 3: Review costs and inclusions.

Desert stay in Dubai: AED 950

  • Wadi or mountain stays: AED 950 + AED 300 for transportation outside Dubai Wadi or mountain stays: AED 950 + AED 300 for transportation outside Dubai

  • Optional add-ons like food, drinks, and mobile toilets are extra. If you’ve done other camping trips, you’ll know that toilets aren’t always necessary for a one-night desert escape. Optional add-ons like food, drinks, and mobile toilets are extra. If you’ve done other camping trips, you’ll know that toilets aren’t always necessary for a one-night desert escape.

A lot of people asked me about the toilet situation - there are two options you can add on for a cost; a mobile toilet unit and a split toilet/shower unit. Having used both these options in New Zealand, I don’t find them very comfortable. There are rarely any toilets needed out in the wild. We are used to this thanks to our other camping trips, so it was no issue for us (since we just stayed the night).

Step 4: You’re all set. The Campr Dubai team sends reminders of what to pack—coffee, milk, sugar, etc.—and three days before your trip, you’ll receive an approximate location. The exact spot remains a small surprise, which we loved.

The Desert Experience

We got our precise location a few hours before departure. Forty-five minutes later, we arrived at a stunning desert landscape. The team had set everything up, including a massive tent that easily fit the two of us. Christine gave us a quick walkthrough of the inventory and tent setup.

The wind was strong initially but calmed by 6 p.m., allowing us to enjoy the crisp evening air. The tent was sturdy, spacious, and cozy. Unlike most camping trips where you wake up sore, the queen mattress was plush and surprisingly comfortable. Don’t forget your pillows, bedsheets, and quilt, though!

Special mention to the massive tent set up that comes up with the package. We could have sat there for hours just taking in the fresh brisk air and endless sand dunes.

We spent hours taking in the endless sand dunes, clicking photos, and playing frisbee before the sunset. The desert truly felt like a secluded oasis, with not another soul in sight—a perfect escape for anyone craving peace and solitud.

You can almost think of it as a desert oasis where time stand stills.

Sunset and Dinner

Before sunset, I cozied up inside the tent while my partner wandered the dunes. The view was phenomenal, and it reminded me why Dubai overnight camping is such a unique experience.

The queen mattress was plush and extremely comfortable; I’ll be honest, we usually end up with a sore back after a night out camping but not this time around. We slept like babies.

**Don’t forget to carry your own pillows, bedsheet and quilt. **

The sunset itself was a sight to behold.. with work keeping us busy, we hardly get to appreciate all the amazing views our city of wonder offers.

For dinner, we opted for a pre-packed homemade meal rather than lighting a fire. Post-dinner, the stars appeared in full glory. Fall weather in Dubai can be rare, but the chill added to the experience. We stayed up until 11 p.m., marveling at the galaxy above us.

Post dinner, the stars were out and how. It was COLD - fall weather in Dubai is usually a rarity unless you venture this far out of the city. So glad we packed our jackets. It was around 11 p.m. before we called it a night.

Sunrise and Morning Walk

The next morning, I woke up early to catch the sunrise—a chilly but rewarding start to the day.

Took a long walk along the dunes before coffee and more down time under the tent.

Why We Love Camping in Dubai with Campr

While glamping has its appeal, we prefer rustic setups where we handle everything ourselves. That said, Campr Dubai offers the perfect blend of convenience and adventure. There’s no stress about permits or equipment, and the experience feels intimate and untouched by modern life.

The vast desert, endless stars, and hassle-free setup made us appreciate just how much Dubai weekend getaways can offer beyond the city’s glittering skyline. Many assume Dubai is all glam, but adventures like these prove that raw beauty is never far away if you’re willing to explore a little deeper.

Tips for Your Dubai Desert Camping Trip

  • Plan according to the season: Desert days can be harsh in summer; winter offers cooler nights perfect for Dubai overnight camping. Plan according to the season: Desert days can be harsh in summer; winter offers cooler nights perfect for Dubai overnight camping.

  • Pack essentials: Jackets, blankets, pillows, and a quilt. Add food and drinks if not included. Pack essentials: Jackets, blankets, pillows, and a quilt. Add food and drinks if not included.

  • Book early: Popular weekends fill up quickly, especially if you’re eyeing Dubai weekend getaways. Book early: Popular weekends fill up quickly, especially if you’re eyeing Dubai weekend getaways.

Embrace the simplicity: No Wi-Fi, no noise—just you, the sand, and the stars.

**Takeaway: **

I am definitely a sucker for vast lonely landscapes mostly untouched by humans. A lot of folks feel Dubai is all glam, but truth be told, so are most cities.

Dig a little deeper and you are never far away from finding stunning places like this hidden gem.

Glamping is not our style, we like to go rustic and set up things ourselves but we are not complaining. It felt like a whole other world with not another soul in sight - this is what we aim for on every trip.

Not having to fret about the set up or permits was a refreshing change!

Sleeping under gazillion stars, the chilly weather, the hassle-free set up, being one with nature.. it took us back to our time in New Zealand.

I would highly recommend this experience if you are looking to escape the boring humdrum city life for a night or two.

Get in touch with Campr to book your getaway this winter.

Final Takeaways

We are always drawn to landscapes untouched by humans. Desert camping with Campr Dubai gave us exactly that—space, solitude, and a chance to reconnect with nature.

If you’re looking to escape the city’s routine for a night or two, I can’t recommend this enough. Whether it’s a last-minute Dubai weekend getaway or a planned Dubai overnight camping experience, this is a perfect option for anyone seeking adventure without the hassle.

Book Your Adventure

Get in touch with Campr Dubai to plan your next desert adventure. Trust me, it’s worth every moment.

West Africa Travel Guide: Ghana, Togo & Benin Cultural Itinerary
This trip was our first taste of West Africa. A region filled with flamboyant festivals, ancient belief systems, vibrant art, and diverse...

This trip was our first taste of West Africa. A region filled with flamboyant festivals, ancient belief systems, vibrant art, and diverse ethnic groups—it’s a part of the continent still largely under the radar for most travelers. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-cover.jpg)

But if you’ve already done the more touristy parts of Africa, the nuanced culture here is a refreshing, raw experience. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-4.jpg)

Think Accra meets Nairobi with a little more soul, or voodoo temples and floating villages that feel pulled straight from a different world. If you're the kind of traveler who enjoys going off the beaten path, read on.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-6.jpg)

**Highlights: **

**Ghana: **

Accra. Black Star Monument. Jamestown. Aburi. Adom Waterfall. Batik Making Workshop. 

**Togo: **

Togoville. Lome City. 

**Benin: **

Ganvie. Sacred Forest. Python Temple. 

- [FAQ ](#viewer-7c3nb) 
[FAQ ](#viewer-7c3nb) 

- [Guide Information](#viewer-7v78l) 
[Guide Information](#viewer-7v78l) 

- [Costs](#viewer-crv4c) 
[Costs](#viewer-crv4c) 

- [Where We Stayed ](#viewer-ajc9s) 
[Where We Stayed ](#viewer-ajc9s) 

- [Highlights](#viewer-aqh74)
[Highlights](#viewer-aqh74)

- Final Thoughts 
Final Thoughts 

**Visa Requirements (for Indian passport holders):**

- **Ghana**: Visa required. €220, issued via embassy in India.
**Ghana**: Visa required. €220, issued via embassy in India.

- **Togo**: Visa on arrival. €45 (single) or €60 (multiple).
**Togo**: Visa on arrival. €45 (single) or €60 (multiple).

- **Benin**:[ E-visa](https://evisa.bj/) available online. €50, single entry, processed in 1 hour.
**Benin**:[ E-visa](https://evisa.bj/) available online. €50, single entry, processed in 1 hour.

**Yellow Fever Vaccine:** Mandatory for all 3 countries. 

**COVID Entry Requirements:** Proof of vaccination needed.

**Language:**

- Ghana: English
Ghana: English

- Togo & Benin: French (Google Translate is your friend)
Togo & Benin: French (Google Translate is your friend)

**Best Time to Visit****:** December to March (dry season)

** Costs & Accommodation**

- **Ghana**: Accommodation overpriced, but local food and activities are fairly affordable.
**Ghana**: Accommodation overpriced, but local food and activities are fairly affordable.

- **Togo & Benin**: Cheaper than Ghana, but still higher than East Africa for basic lodging.
**Togo & Benin**: Cheaper than Ghana, but still higher than East Africa for basic lodging.

- **Transport:** Fuel is expensive; private guided trips can be costly but worth it for comfort and efficiency.
**Transport:** Fuel is expensive; private guided trips can be costly but worth it for comfort and efficiency.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-9.jpg)

**Should You Hire a Guide?**

We always lean toward hiring local guides in [Africa](/africa). For this trip, we went with **Isaac** (+233 50 524 3557), highly recommended and extremely accommodating. He navigated us through everything, even after flight delays via São Tomé.

Independent travel is possible, but language barriers and logistics can make it difficult.

**Ghana Highlights	**

**Accra City & Black Star Monument**

A modern capital buzzing with markets and history. Start with the Black Star Square—a symbol of Ghana’s independence.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-13.jpg)

**Jamestown**

Old colonial streets meet vibrant murals and a raw, real energy.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-15.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-17.jpg)

 A boxing center, street art, and friendly faces make it a highlight. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-19.jpg)

**Aburi & Adom Waterfall**

Escape the city heat. Stroll through Aburi Botanical Gardens and hike to Adom Waterfall. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-24.jpg)

The cool plunge after a sweaty trek is a reward in itself.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-26.jpg)

**Batik Workshop**

Try your hand at traditional **batik printing** with **Aunty Esther**, who runs a women's empowerment collective.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-27.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-29.jpg)

. A culturally enriching and hands-on experience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-31.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-35.jpg)

It was such an enjoyable experience for me. Not to mention a fantastic souvenir to use at home. If you are in Ghana, definitely recommend trying something unique and culturally enriching.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-37.jpg)

**Togo Highlights**

**Togoville: The Home of Voodoo**

A deep dive into Africa’s indigenous religion. Sacred trees, voodoo sculptures, and centuries-old traditions await.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-40.jpg)

**Note**: Forget the Hollywood version of voodoo. This is a spiritual, community-led practice rooted in ancestral wisdom.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-42.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-46.jpg)

Forget the movie stereotypes—voodoo here is a religion born during the slave-trade era. The experience can be unusual but deeply rewarding. As I always say, no two journeys are alike; you have to see it for yourself.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-48.jpg)

**Lomé City**

Coastal vibes, local markets, Catholic churches, and a peek into Togo’s Central African artistic influences.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-49.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-51.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-53.jpg)

**Benin Highlights**

**Ganvie: The Venice of Africa**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-55.jpg)

A **stilt village** dating back to the 16th century, Ganvie is home to 40,000 Tofinu people who escaped the slave trade.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-56.jpg)

 Glide past bamboo huts, floating schools, and fishers casting nets using palm branches.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-61.jpg)

**Sacred Forest of Ouidah**

This serene space is filled with symbolic voodoo sculptures.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-67.jpg)

 The **infinite loop serpent**, representing life after death, was a personal favorite.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-69.jpg)

**Python Temple**

Yes, there are actual pythons here—completely harmless and sacred. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-70.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-72.jpg)

This temple highlights the role of animals and earth elements in voodoo practices.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-74.jpg)

**Final Thoughts**

If you're in search of big-game safaris or luxury lodges, this might not be your spot. But if raw, unfiltered culture excites you—**West Africa will steal your heart**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-75.jpg)

From conversations in Jamestown to ceremonies in Togoville and the serenity of Ganvie, Ghana, Togo, and Benin offer a cultural immersion like no other.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-76.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-78.jpg)

**Is it safe?** Yes. These are stable countries despite proximity to hotspots like Burkina Faso. Travel smart and stay aware, as you would anywhere.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ghanatogobenin-80.jpg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

[The National News ](https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/money/2022/07/28/how-to-save-money-so-you-can-afford-to-travel-more/),[Khaleej Times ](https://www.khaleejtimes.com/travel/uae-how-to-travel-the-world-despite-a-weak-passport-expats-share-tips),[Mint / Livemint](https://www.livemint.com/news/trends/this-visited-108-countries-while-holding-full-time-job/amp-11746251898742.html), [Times Now News](https://www.timesnownews.com/travel/travelogues/solo-trip-india-why-not-travel-vlogger-ankitha-rajendaran-on-travelling-solo-to-109-countries-and-the-joy-of-embracing-unseen-destinations-article-152114732)[,Outlook Traveller](https://www.outlooktraveller.com/destinations/international/travel-to-syria-uzbekistan-and-iran), [Hindustan Times ](https://www.hindustantimes.com/lifestyle/travel/woman-who-travelled-solo-to-109-countries-7-continents-shares-her-top-travel-hacks-every-female-should-know-101747298629619.html),[MSN India](https://www.msn.com/en-in/money/topstories/meet-ankita-rajendaran-the-woman-who-travelled-to-108-countries-while-working-full-time/ar-AA1DUMAu?ocid=spr_trending&businessvertical=News), [The Established ](https://www.theestablished.com/self/health/the-best-company-is-your-ownand-indian-youth-is-agreeing)[NDTV](https://www.ndtv.com/video/news/news/couple-who-have-explored-over-80-countries-share-tips-for-budget-travel-729052), [Times of India](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/life-style/travel/destinations/7-countries-you-should-visit-if-you-want-to-travel-differently/photostory/121286098.cms), [SheThePeople](https://www.shethepeople.tv/interviews/ankitha-rajendaran-why-not-book-interview-9426506)

This trip was our first taste of West Africa. A region filled with flamboyant festivals, ancient belief systems, vibrant art, and diverse ethnic groups—it’s a part of the continent still largely under the radar for most travelers.

But if you’ve already done the more touristy parts of Africa, the nuanced culture here is a refreshing, raw experience.

Think Accra meets Nairobi with a little more soul, or voodoo temples and floating villages that feel pulled straight from a different world. If you’re the kind of traveler who enjoys going off the beaten path, read on.

**Highlights: **

**Ghana: **

Accra. Black Star Monument. Jamestown. Aburi. Adom Waterfall. Batik Making Workshop.

**Togo: **

Togoville. Lome City.

**Benin: **

Ganvie. Sacred Forest. Python Temple.

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Guide Information Guide Information

  • Costs Costs

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

Visa Requirements (for Indian passport holders):

  • Ghana: Visa required. €220, issued via embassy in India. Ghana: Visa required. €220, issued via embassy in India.

  • Togo: Visa on arrival. €45 (single) or €60 (multiple). Togo: Visa on arrival. €45 (single) or €60 (multiple).

  • Benin: E-visa available online. €50, single entry, processed in 1 hour. Benin: E-visa available online. €50, single entry, processed in 1 hour.

Yellow Fever Vaccine: Mandatory for all 3 countries. 

COVID Entry Requirements: Proof of vaccination needed.

Language:

  • Ghana: English Ghana: English

  • Togo & Benin: French (Google Translate is your friend) Togo & Benin: French (Google Translate is your friend)

Best Time to Visit**:** December to March (dry season)

** Costs & Accommodation**

  • Ghana: Accommodation overpriced, but local food and activities are fairly affordable. Ghana: Accommodation overpriced, but local food and activities are fairly affordable.

  • Togo & Benin: Cheaper than Ghana, but still higher than East Africa for basic lodging. Togo & Benin: Cheaper than Ghana, but still higher than East Africa for basic lodging.

  • Transport: Fuel is expensive; private guided trips can be costly but worth it for comfort and efficiency. Transport: Fuel is expensive; private guided trips can be costly but worth it for comfort and efficiency.

Should You Hire a Guide?

We always lean toward hiring local guides in Africa. For this trip, we went with Isaac (+233 50 524 3557), highly recommended and extremely accommodating. He navigated us through everything, even after flight delays via São Tomé.

Independent travel is possible, but language barriers and logistics can make it difficult.

**Ghana Highlights **

Accra City & Black Star Monument

A modern capital buzzing with markets and history. Start with the Black Star Square—a symbol of Ghana’s independence.

Jamestown

Old colonial streets meet vibrant murals and a raw, real energy.

 A boxing center, street art, and friendly faces make it a highlight. 

Aburi & Adom Waterfall

Escape the city heat. Stroll through Aburi Botanical Gardens and hike to Adom Waterfall.

The cool plunge after a sweaty trek is a reward in itself.

Batik Workshop

Try your hand at traditional batik printing with Aunty Esther, who runs a women’s empowerment collective.

. A culturally enriching and hands-on experience.

It was such an enjoyable experience for me. Not to mention a fantastic souvenir to use at home. If you are in Ghana, definitely recommend trying something unique and culturally enriching.

Togo Highlights

Togoville: The Home of Voodoo

A deep dive into Africa’s indigenous religion. Sacred trees, voodoo sculptures, and centuries-old traditions await.

Note: Forget the Hollywood version of voodoo. This is a spiritual, community-led practice rooted in ancestral wisdom.

Forget the movie stereotypes—voodoo here is a religion born during the slave-trade era. The experience can be unusual but deeply rewarding. As I always say, no two journeys are alike; you have to see it for yourself.

Lomé City

Coastal vibes, local markets, Catholic churches, and a peek into Togo’s Central African artistic influences.

Benin Highlights

Ganvie: The Venice of Africa

A stilt village dating back to the 16th century, Ganvie is home to 40,000 Tofinu people who escaped the slave trade.

 Glide past bamboo huts, floating schools, and fishers casting nets using palm branches.

Sacred Forest of Ouidah

This serene space is filled with symbolic voodoo sculptures.

 The infinite loop serpent, representing life after death, was a personal favorite.

Python Temple

Yes, there are actual pythons here—completely harmless and sacred.

This temple highlights the role of animals and earth elements in voodoo practices.

Final Thoughts

If you’re in search of big-game safaris or luxury lodges, this might not be your spot. But if raw, unfiltered culture excites you—West Africa will steal your heart.

From conversations in Jamestown to ceremonies in Togoville and the serenity of Ganvie, Ghana, Togo, and Benin offer a cultural immersion like no other.

Is it safe? Yes. These are stable countries despite proximity to hotspots like Burkina Faso. Travel smart and stay aware, as you would anywhere.

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki
