Best Time to Visit Thailand: Phuket Travel Guide & Itinerary
A guide to planning four days in Phuket, Thailand

By now you know we are not big on beaches or beach vacations. Laying on a beach for four straight days is not our travel style. We like active trips. That being said, when we did this trip back in summer of 2017, we had already planned a whirlwind trip to Europe — which meant we just needed a relaxing getaway.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-1.jpg)

And that’s how **Thailand happened**. It was the perfect trip to escape the Dubai heat. As always, we skipped the capital city and went straight to the islands. If you’re planning your own

**Thailand itinerary**, this blog doubles as a **Phuket travel guide** with highlights like **James Bond Island tour**, **Ko Phi Phi Islands**, and **Old Phuket Town**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-2.jpg)

- Bangkok → Phuket → Patong
Bangkok → Phuket → Patong

- Local sightseeing in Phuket
Local sightseeing in Phuket

- **James Bond Island tour**
**James Bond Island tour**

- **Ko Phi Phi Islands**
**Ko Phi Phi Islands**

Local viewpoints & attractions including **Big Buddha Phuket** and **Old Phuket Town**

**Best Time to Visit Thailand**

We went in June — it was pretty rainy during that time but we got lucky with the weather. The **best time to visit Thailand** is usually from November to February, when the skies are clear and the seas are calm. No matter when you travel, always carry a poncho or umbrella, especially in Phuket where showers come suddenly.

We flew direct with [Thai Airways](https://www.expedia.com/) from Dubai. Be sure to pre-order your vegetarian meal — there won’t be any default vegetarian option.

Thailand has **visa on arrival for Indians**. Just pay cash at the visa counter when you arrive and clear immigration. It’s quick and simple.

**Language**

Thai is the official language. Being such a touristy country, most people speak good English, especially in **Phuket travel hotspots**.


**Currency**

Baht is the official currency. Be sure to carry local currency as cash. They also accept dollars at most places, especially around Patong beach and tour counters.  

We stayed at the [Memory Patong](http://booking.com/hotel/th/memory-patong.en-gb.html?checkin=2024-02-10;checkout=2024-02-15;dest_id=-3406238;dest_type=city;dist=0;group_adults=1;group_children=0;hapos=1;hpos=1;no_rooms=1;req_adults=1;req_children=0;room1=A;sb_price_type=total;sid=2c91221561facfee3d6615d3d1424f9e;soh=1;sr_order=popularity;srepoch=1706193212;srpvid=47ad665d6ce9027f;type=total;ucfs=1&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-4f7e83742692444f9e818cafd-437527_cjevent-7d34c697986e11f08147029d0a82b82a&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287#no_availability_msg) Hotel. It was pretty close to a big mall — super convenient for lunch or dinner. The breakfast was delicious with fresh fruits and juice. If you’re looking for tips on the **best areas to stay in Phuket**, Patong is the liveliest, while Kata and Karon are more relaxed, and Old Phuket Town has a cultural charm.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-3.jpg)

Depending on where you’re flying in from, Thailand can be cheap or expensive. You can haggle for every single thing — including cabs, tours, and street-side shopping. Be sure you bargain for what you think is the best value. Alternatively, [book your tours and packages](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g293915-Activities-c42-Thailand.html) ahead of time for peace of mind.

We had no issues with safety, but be mindful of your belongings at all times. Phuket and Patong are generally safe for tourists.


There wasn’t a lot of street food we could enjoy since most of the food had meat. In the city, we stuck to big brands like Subway and Pizza Hut. Highly recommend trying the Thai Pineapple Fried Rice — served in a pineapple shell — a highlight for vegetarian food lovers in Phuket.


We landed in Bangkok in the morning and boarded our flight to Phuket. From the Phuket City airport to Patong took about an hour. Checked in at around 11:30 a.m. at the hotel.

After freshening up, we headed to Jungceylon Mall, just a 15-minute walk from the hotel. Post lunch, we ventured into the streets near the Hard Rock Café in Patong, where we booked our tours for the next two days.

You can opt for the **Big Boat Tour** or the **Long Tail Boat Tour**. There is a difference in price but the routes are similar. I recommend the Big Boat for comfort.

Note: there are plenty of agents around — most are similar. We walked around before picking one random tour operator since the rates were nearly the same everywhere. Expect to spend upwards of 1,500 Baht per day per person.

By 3 p.m., we were back at the hotel. Later, we walked down to **Patong beach** to catch a stunning sunset. Paragliding is an option, but it’s pricey here.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-6.jpg)

At 9 p.m., we had a booking for the **Phuket Fantasea show**, arranged via the same tour agent. It was a fun way to spend a few hours outside the city. The show goes on until midnight, after which you’re dropped back at your hotel.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-12.jpg)

Pick-up next morning was at 8:30 a.m. from the hotel. The day was spent exploring caves, rocks, and multiple small islands in Phang Nga Bay. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-18.jpg)

After a short boat ride, we sailed past picturesque limestone karsts before dropping anchor in Phang Nga Bay. The highlight was **Khao Phing Kan**, famously known as **James Bond Island**. The island got its name from the iconic film “The Man with the Golden Gun,” and its massive leaning limestone rock is a sight to behold.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-24.jpg)

Post lunch, we set out in canoes. An eerie silence descended as we glided through limestone caves, hearing nothing but the gentle splash of water. A magical moment in nature.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-28.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-30.jpg)

Another day, another stunner.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-32.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-36.jpeg)

The **Ko Phi Phi Islands** were more relaxed, with lots of swimming, sunbathing, and scuba diving options. This was a slower day compared to the James Bond Island adventure but equally rewarding.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-42.jpg)

After breakfast, we rented a taxi from the hotel (300 Baht for 3 hours) to check out local attractions.

Our first stop was the peaceful and utterly gorgeous **Big Buddha Phuket**. The 45-meter-high statue is a highlight, free to enter, and you can easily spend an hour here soaking in the views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-48.jpg)

Next stop: the stunning **Leam Phromthep viewpoint**, with Karon beach en route. This is the southern tip of Phuket and one of its most photographed spots. The short 0.3-mile walk to the top was worth it for the panoramic views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-50.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-52.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-54.jpg)

This is easily the island's most photographed view point. Did a short 0.3 mile walk to the top and the panoramic views were stunning. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-cover.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-58.jpg)

We then explored **Old Phuket Town**, with its Sino-Portuguese architecture, colorful buildings, and vibrant street art. If shopping and culture interest you, this is the perfect place to end your day.

In the evening, relax with a Thai massage (haggle hard and don’t pay more than 500–1,000 Baht).

Tip: if you have the energy, Karon beach is amazing for sunrise views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-60.jpg)

Tip: if you have the energy, I highly recommend checking out **Karon beach **for incredible sunrise views.  

This was our first island getaway, and we made the most of our short trip. While many people stick to **Patong beach**, there’s so much more to see in Phuket. From the **James Bond Island tour** to the **Ko Phi Phi Islands**, the peaceful **Big Buddha Phuket**, and exploring **Old Phuket Town**, it’s a destination with layers of experiences.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-61.jpeg)

Give yourself four to five days to hit all the major spots. Tours make it easy and affordable compared to planning on your own.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-62.jpg)

If you’re planning your next **Thailand itinerary**, use this blog as your personal **Phuket travel guide**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/four-day-guide-to-island-hopping-in-besttimetovisitthailand-65.jpg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

 Anki

In the evening, relax with a Thai massage (haggle hard and don’t pay more than 500–1,000 Baht).

Tip: if you have the energy, Karon beach is amazing for sunrise views.

Tip: if you have the energy, I highly recommend checking out **Karon beach **for incredible sunrise views. 

This was our first island getaway, and we made the most of our short trip. While many people stick to Patong beach, there’s so much more to see in Phuket. From the James Bond Island tour to the Ko Phi Phi Islands, the peaceful Big Buddha Phuket, and exploring Old Phuket Town, it’s a destination with layers of experiences.

Give yourself four to five days to hit all the major spots. Tours make it easy and affordable compared to planning on your own.

If you’re planning your next Thailand itinerary, use this blog as your personal Phuket travel guide.

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

 Anki

Lalibela Churches & Ethiopia Travel Guide: Solo, Safe, & Spiritual
Discover the awe-inspiring Lalibela Churches in Ethiopia, carved with divine precision from solid rock. Immerse yourself in the rich history

Since Africa is my favorite continent, I go through the Addis Ababa airport at least ten times a year, it was only fair that I took time out to explore the one place in this massive (commercialized) country that has been on my list: the Lalibela churches. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-1.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-3.jpg)

In November 2023, I got lost in the time capsule that is Lalibela, where ancient tales whisper through the carved stone walls.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-5.jpg)

These spectacular rock-hewn wonders aren’t just buildings; they’re living legends etched into the soul of this mystical town. The history dates back to the 12th century, King Lalibela envisioned a ‘New Jerusalem’ and voila, his dream manifested into these awe-inspiring churches, sculpted with divine precision from solid rock. Each crevice tells a story, and each corner echoes with the footsteps of centuries. Let's dive in. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-8.jpg)

**Flights & Visa for Ethiopia**

I flew in from Djibouti via **Ethiopian Airlines**, arriving late at night. The **Ethiopian e-visa** was seamless—30-day single-entry visa cost $80, processed online via the[ ](https://www.evisa.gov.et/)[official portal](https://www.evisa.gov.et/).


**Language in Ethiopia**

With over **80 languages**, Ethiopia is incredibly diverse. **Amharic** is widely spoken, and **English** is common in cities like Addis Ababa and Lalibela.

**Best Time to Visit Ethiopia**

Plan your trip between **November to February**, the dry season ideal for visiting **Lalibela churches** and enjoying comfortable weather across the **highlands of Ethiopia**.

**Currency in Ethiopia**

US Dollars and cards work in major cities, but always carry some **Ethiopian Birr** for rural areas and local markets.


**How Long to Spend in Lalibela**

Minimum **2 full days** in Lalibela is essential. For a broader Ethiopian adventure (Simien Mountains, Omo Valley, etc.), budget **3 weeks**.

**Internal Flights: Addis to Lalibela**

Book your **Addis Ababa to Lalibela flights** at least 2–3 weeks in advance. Window seats are highly recommended for breathtaking aerial views of the Ethiopian highlands.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-14.jpg)

**Where I Stayed**

- **Addis Ababa**: A small hotel near Bole airport—perfect for a short transit.
**Addis Ababa**: A small hotel near Bole airport—perfect for a short transit.

- **Lalibela**: **Tukul Village Hotel**—beautifully local, though I was the **only guest due to regional tensions**.
**Lalibela**: **Tukul Village Hotel**—beautifully local, though I was the **only guest due to regional tensions**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-20.jpg)

Ethiopia is one of the best destinations for **vegan and vegetarian food in Africa. **The national dish **Injera** is always vegan, and meals are bursting with lentils, greens, and spices.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-24.jpg)

**Costs & Local Guide**

I booked through a **trusted local guide** who ensured safety, cultural immersion, and smooth logistics. Highly recommend a guide during times of unrest. DM me for contact!

**Solo Travel & Safety in Ethiopia**

In **Addis Ababa**, be alert like in any big city—avoid flashing valuables. I hired a **local driver** to maximize my short stay safely.

In Lalibela, while a guide isn’t mandatory, it’s **strongly recommended** due to potential tensions. **Flights can get canceled** last-minute, so travel with flexibility and patience.

**Highlights of Lalibela**

**Lalibela **

Lalibela’s rock-hewn churches in Ethiopia are the ultimate portals to another era. These architectural wonders, carved with divine precision in the 12th century, stand as living testaments to faith, craftsmanship, and a touch of magic.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-30.jpg)

** Bete Maryam**

The House of Mary rises like a stone symphony, filled with ancient frescoes and silence that speaks volumes.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-34.jpg)

My fingers traced the cool contours of ancient symbols etched into the walls, each glyph a silent prayer whispering through millennia. Here, beneath the sun's embrace, I wasn't just a tourist; I was a pilgrim, following the dusty footsteps of faith laid a thousand years ago.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-6.jpg)

**Bete Golgotha Mikael**

Descending into this cave-like church, candlelight flickers on monk robes, hymns echo off the cool rock walls—it’s a full **spiritual immersion**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-37.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-39.jpg)

Monks in flowing white robes materialized from the shadows, their voices filling the cavern with ancient hymns that sent shivers down my spine. These **11 medieval churches of Lalibela**, carved inside and outside rock, defy architectural norms and tell stories of **Christian faith**, resilience, and ancient genius.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-41.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-43.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-45.jpg)

**Addis Ababa Highlights**

On my final day, I explored **Ethiopia's capital**—from the[ ](https://nationalmuseumofethiopia.gov.et/)[National Museum of Ethiopia](https://nationalmuseumofethiopia.gov.et/) to coffee shops where **Bunna T’et** (coffee ceremonies) turn a drink into a **cultural ritual**.

**Must-Visit Spots:**

- National Museum of Ethiopia
National Museum of Ethiopia

- Merkato Market
Merkato Market

- Holy Trinity Cathedral
Holy Trinity Cathedral

- Bole Medhane Churchh 
Bole Medhane Churchh 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-47.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-49.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-51.jpg)

**Bunna T’et – Coffee as a Ritual**

Forget your quick espresso shots—**Ethiopian coffee culture** is a spiritual experience, with rounds symbolizing happiness and blessings.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-53.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-55.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-57.jpg)

**Final Thoughts**

**Lalibela's rock-hewn churches** aren’t just a UNESCO attraction—they’re a **spiritual and cultural journey**. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-59.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-61.jpg)

This trip offered me a rare mix of history, resilience, and divine artistry. If you ever find yourself in East Africa, don’t miss this **offbeat and soul-stirring destination**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/ethiopia-s-rock-hewn-wonders-exploring-the-lalibela-churches-63.jpg)

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Gorilla Trekking in DRC via Rwanda: A Soulful Solo Journey into Africa's Untamed Wild
In the depths of my soul-stirring wanderlust, I found myself irresistibly drawn to a destination shrouded in complexity and enigma —a...

In the depths of my soul-stirring wanderlust, I found myself irresistibly drawn to a destination shrouded in **complexity and enigma**—a place long misunderstood, often called the heart of darkness. But behind the headlines, the **Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)** reveals itself as one of the most **underrated trekking** destinations in the world, rich with raw nature, resilient people, and unforgettable wildlife.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-1.jpg)

My second adventure of August 2023 was a **solo trip to Rwanda and DRC**. Most of my travels are offbeat—chosen because they challenge, move, and transform me. I crave that slight discomfort, that shift from the everyday into the unknown. This was one of those journeys.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-6.jpg)

In this ultimate travel blog for **gorilla trekking in DRC**, I’ll share practical tips, the **realities of solo travel in Africa**, and my emotional journey through a complex, beautiful land.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-8.jpg)

**Itinerary**

**Day 1**: Arrive in Kigali, Rwanda. Explore the city.

**Day 2**: Drive to Nyungwe. Overnight in a border town.

**Day 3**: Cross the **Rwanda-Congo border**. Gorilla Trekking in **Kahuzi Biega National Park**.

**Day 4**: Tchegera Island escape.

**Day 5**: Goma – visit refugee communities and local NGOs.

**Day 6**: Cross back into Rwanda and fly out.

**Flights + Visa for Congo**

Fly into Kigali—**Rwanda to Congo border crossings** are easy with the right documents. For **Indian passport holders**, gorilla trekking visas for DRC can be arranged for $100 with an invitation letter from a local agency. Visiting other regions of DRC? Then the **DRC embassy visa** is required (~$200), which takes longer.


**Language**

English is widely spoken in Rwanda. In **Democratic Republic of Congo**, French dominates. Have **Google Translate** ready!

**Best Time to Visit**

Dry seasons: June–September and December–February. These are ideal for **gorilla trekking in DRC** and travel across **Central Africa**. 


**Currency**

Rwanda: Card-friendly.  Congo: Use **Congolese Franc**. USD accepted, but bills under $5 are refused.

**Where I Stayed**

I stayed at **budget-friendly backpacker lodges** near the Bukavu border. Comfortable, simple, and perfect for **backpacking in Africa** solo.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-9.jpg)

**Getting Around Rwanda**

Rwanda is incredibly safe and well-paved. I recommend hiring a car or a **local driver**. I chose a reliable guide for my journey.

**Costs + Local Guide in DRC**

For DRC, I traveled with **Romuald from Ubuntu Voyages**. Honest, flexible, and well-connected—highly recommended!

Expect to pay **$1500+** for a 5-6 day trip including **gorilla permits**, visa processing, basic stays, and food. **Solo travel in Africa**, especially in DRC, isn’t cheap—but it’s deeply enriching.

** Safety for Solo Travel in DRC**

**Safety in DRC for tourists** is complex. While parts of the country remain volatile, the trekking areas near Bukavu and **Virunga National Park** are stable with the right guides. Having local support is **non-negotiable**. I never felt unsafe—just aware, alert, and deeply respected.

**Gorilla Trekking Permits in Congo**

**Gorilla trekking permits in DRC** cost **$400**, the cheapest among Uganda and Rwanda. This was my **second encounter with Eastern Lowland Gorillas**, and I arranged the permit through a local agency.

For what to pack, check my detailed[ Uganda Gorilla Trekking Guide.](/post/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda)

**Highlights**

** **

**Kigali, Rwanda**

Coffee culture at its finest—don’t miss the rich, **Rwandan dark roast**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-10.jpg)

Visit the haunting **Genocide Museum** for historical context.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-15.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-19.jpg)

**Nyamirambo Women’s Center** - an NGO for Rwandese women in the community. You can join a cooking, sewing or basket weaving class and support their cause. I weaved myself a earring. 🫶🏽

Visit the iconic **Hotel Rwanda** filming location. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-21.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-24.jpg)

** ****Nyungwe, Rwanda**

Known for **chimpanzees and colobus monkeys**, **Nyungwe Forest** is a haven for **wildlife lovers**. I skipped the $100 canopy walk and instead explored the tranquil **tea estates**, where I met welcoming locals and soaked in the serenity.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-32.jpg)

For the intrepid hiker, there are pristine trails that lead to hidden waterfalls, each step an opportunity to connect with nature. If you are not venturing onwards to Uganda or Congo, then Nyungwe is also renowned for its diverse primate population, including chimpanzees and colobus monkeys, making it a prime destination for wildlife enthusiasts. 


**Kahuzi Biega National Park – Gorilla Trekking in DRC**

I have now had the privilege of experiencing this ethical wildlife encounter twice and I couldn’t recommend it enough! 🥲


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-34.jpg)

Trekking through **Kahuzi Biega National Park** was the highlight of my journey. The trail is steep, muddy, and wild. But when I finally stood face-to-face with a **silverback gorilla**, everything faded—except the raw, soul-piercing gaze of this magnificent creature.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-37.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-39.jpg)

Just like our hike in Uganda’s Impenetrable forest, my trek to find the endemic Eastern Lowland gorillas in Congo was an arduous one. The path was at times steep and unforgiving… but as soon as I encountered these gentle giants, my physical discomfort faded away. In their presence, I felt a profound respect for the delicate balance of nature, and I understood the urgency of protecting these near endangered creatures. Yet, my joy was tinged with sorrow as I realized the threats these magnificent beings faced. Poaching, habitat destruction, and political instability loomed large, challenging the very existence of these gorillas. My heart ached, knowing that their survival hung in the balance, and I vowed to share their plight with the world, hoping to ignite a spark of conservation in the hearts of others. So go, to help protect their sanctuary! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-41.jpg)

The **Eastern Lowland Gorillas** are endangered. I felt humbled, privileged, and heartbroken—realizing how close they are to disappearing due to **poaching, conflict, and deforestation**. This is **ethical wildlife travel in Africa** at its most powerful.

**Permits:** the tracking permits cost $400 in DRC and have to be secured ahead of time. Best to have a local agency sort it our for you, to save time.

**Rules**:

- Maintain 7-10 meters distance
Maintain 7-10 meters distance

- Stay silent
Stay silent

- Masks are mandatory
Masks are mandatory

- Don’t panic if approached—rangers will guide you. 

Don’t panic if approached—rangers will guide you. 


**Tchegera Island**

This was my respite after the trek. **Tchegera Island**, the only tourist-accessible part of **Virunga**, is a volcanic crescent in **Lake Kivu** with surreal views of **Mount Nyiragongo**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-42.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-46.jpg)

Its pristine turquoise waters offer solace and a sense of tranquility that is hard to come by in the mainland's chaos. I marveled at the untouched beauty of nature, and for a moment, the worries of the outside world seemed distant. This is the only part of Virunga National Park that is now open to public, so it’s the only way you can see the gorgeous volcanic Mount Nyiragongo.Unfortunately, due to ongoing conflict, hiking Nyiragongo is currently closed. But just **being here**, surrounded by water and silence, was healing.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-48.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-50.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-52.jpg)

Goma is a city you’ll never forget. Government websites issue **travel warnings**, yet life pulses vibrantly. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-54.jpg)

People smile, music plays, **chukudus** roll down streets, and markets buzz. I visited **refugees in Congo** and orphanages—stories that crushed and inspired me. Amid instability, I saw a **community’s deep yearning for normalcy**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-55.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-59.jpg)

Violence is temporary and overpowering, but in the fine details, and the long run, always loses in the face of daily life. Danger isn’t an ever-present thing. Things are ok and then suddenly, they aren’t. The will of a community for stability is what stuck with me. People so helpful and ready with a smile and a bonjour madame. The human need to create a sense of “normal”


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-61.jpg)

**FINAL THOUGHTS**

Traveling through the **Democratic Republic of Congo** is not for the faint of heart. But if you're an **intrepid traveler**, hungry for **off-the-beaten-path African safaris**, it will be one of the most transformative experiences of your life.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-62.jpg)

Family warned me when I told them about my plans. “Ebola”, followed later by “Crazy, you cannot go alone to the land of rebels”. Hubs had guerrillas on his mind. Me? All I could think about was gorillas, dark roasted coffee, undiluted nature and the raw authentic energy of Africa.I thought about the smell of the earth and endless rolling hills. I pictured the most intense wildlife encounters, the smell of morning ocean, the gut wrenching stories of displaced refugees and orphans.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/gorilla-trekking-and-exploring-rwanda-and-drc-s-pristine-wilderness-63.jpg)

This is a country of **conflicting realities**—majestic gorillas, haunting refugee stories, raw landscapes, and warm, welcoming people. I cried, laughed, reflected, and grew.[ ](http://grew.To)[To](http://grew.To) those considering it—**don't just go for the gorillas**. Go for the stories. The humanity. The silence. The chaos. Go because it's time to **redefine how we see Africa**.

Lots of love,

Anki

In the depths of my soul-stirring wanderlust, I found myself irresistibly drawn to a destination shrouded in complexity and enigma—a place long misunderstood, often called the heart of darkness. But behind the headlines, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) reveals itself as one of the most underrated trekking destinations in the world, rich with raw nature, resilient people, and unforgettable wildlife.

My second adventure of August 2023 was a solo trip to Rwanda and DRC. Most of my travels are offbeat—chosen because they challenge, move, and transform me. I crave that slight discomfort, that shift from the everyday into the unknown. This was one of those journeys.

In this ultimate travel blog for gorilla trekking in DRC, I’ll share practical tips, the realities of solo travel in Africa, and my emotional journey through a complex, beautiful land.

Itinerary

Day 1: Arrive in Kigali, Rwanda. Explore the city.

Day 2: Drive to Nyungwe. Overnight in a border town.

Day 3: Cross the Rwanda-Congo border. Gorilla Trekking in Kahuzi Biega National Park.

Day 4: Tchegera Island escape.

Day 5: Goma – visit refugee communities and local NGOs.

Day 6: Cross back into Rwanda and fly out.

Flights + Visa for Congo

Fly into Kigali—Rwanda to Congo border crossings are easy with the right documents. For Indian passport holders, gorilla trekking visas for DRC can be arranged for $100 with an invitation letter from a local agency. Visiting other regions of DRC? Then the DRC embassy visa is required (~$200), which takes longer.

Language

English is widely spoken in Rwanda. In Democratic Republic of Congo, French dominates. Have Google Translate ready!

Best Time to Visit

Dry seasons: June–September and December–February. These are ideal for gorilla trekking in DRC and travel across Central Africa.

Currency

Rwanda: Card-friendly.  Congo: Use Congolese Franc. USD accepted, but bills under $5 are refused.

Where I Stayed

I stayed at budget-friendly backpacker lodges near the Bukavu border. Comfortable, simple, and perfect for backpacking in Africa solo.

Getting Around Rwanda

Rwanda is incredibly safe and well-paved. I recommend hiring a car or a local driver. I chose a reliable guide for my journey.

Costs + Local Guide in DRC

For DRC, I traveled with Romuald from Ubuntu Voyages. Honest, flexible, and well-connected—highly recommended!

Expect to pay $1500+ for a 5-6 day trip including gorilla permits, visa processing, basic stays, and food. Solo travel in Africa, especially in DRC, isn’t cheap—but it’s deeply enriching.

** Safety for Solo Travel in DRC**

Safety in DRC for tourists is complex. While parts of the country remain volatile, the trekking areas near Bukavu and Virunga National Park are stable with the right guides. Having local support is non-negotiable. I never felt unsafe—just aware, alert, and deeply respected.

Gorilla Trekking Permits in Congo

Gorilla trekking permits in DRC cost $400, the cheapest among Uganda and Rwanda. This was my second encounter with Eastern Lowland Gorillas, and I arranged the permit through a local agency.

For what to pack, check my detailed Uganda Gorilla Trekking Guide.

Kigali, Rwanda

Coffee culture at its finest—don’t miss the rich, Rwandan dark roast.

Visit the haunting Genocide Museum for historical context.

Nyamirambo Women’s Center - an NGO for Rwandese women in the community. You can join a cooking, sewing or basket weaving class and support their cause. I weaved myself a earring. 🫶🏽

Visit the iconic Hotel Rwanda filming location.

I have now had the privilege of experiencing this ethical wildlife encounter twice and I couldn’t recommend it enough! 🥲

Trekking through Kahuzi Biega National Park was the highlight of my journey. The trail is steep, muddy, and wild. But when I finally stood face-to-face with a silverback gorilla, everything faded—except the raw, soul-piercing gaze of this magnificent creature.

Just like our hike in Uganda’s Impenetrable forest, my trek to find the endemic Eastern Lowland gorillas in Congo was an arduous one. Yet, my joy was tinged with sorrow as I realized the threats these magnificent beings faced. Tchegera Island

This was my respite after the trek. Tchegera Island, the only tourist-accessible part of Virunga, is a volcanic crescent in Lake Kivu with surreal views of Mount Nyiragongo.

Its pristine turquoise waters offer solace and a sense of tranquility that is hard to come by in the mainland’s chaos. I marveled at the untouched beauty of nature, and for a moment, the worries of the outside world seemed distant. This is the only part of Virunga National Park that is now open to public, so it’s the only way you can see the gorgeous volcanic Mount Nyiragongo.Unfortunately, due to ongoing conflict, hiking Nyiragongo is currently closed. But just being here, surrounded by water and silence, was healing.

Goma is a city you’ll never forget. Government websites issue travel warnings, yet life pulses vibrantly.

People smile, music plays, chukudus roll down streets, and markets buzz. I visited refugees in Congo and orphanages—stories that crushed and inspired me. Amid instability, I saw a community’s deep yearning for normalcy.

FINAL THOUGHTS

Traveling through the Democratic Republic of Congo is not for the faint of heart. But if you’re an intrepid traveler, hungry for off-the-beaten-path African safaris, it will be one of the most transformative experiences of your life.

Family warned me when I told them about my plans. “Ebola”, followed later by “Crazy, you cannot go alone to the land of rebels”. Hubs had guerrillas on his mind. Me? All I could think about was gorillas, dark roasted coffee, undiluted nature and the raw authentic energy of Africa.I thought about the smell of the earth and endless rolling hills. I pictured the most intense wildlife encounters, the smell of morning ocean, the gut wrenching stories of displaced refugees and orphans.

This is a country of conflicting realities—majestic gorillas, haunting refugee stories, raw landscapes, and warm, welcoming people. I cried, laughed, reflected, and grew. To those considering it—don’t just go for the gorillas. Go for the stories. The humanity. The silence. The chaos. Go because it’s time to redefine how we see Africa.

  • Features: Wood stoves, hot tubs, countryside vistas Features: Wood stoves, hot tubs, countryside vistas

  • Unique touch: Serene landscapes straight out of a paintingThink steaming cups of tea, sunsets behind grassy hills, and crackling fires that invite you to unwind. Unique touch: Serene landscapes straight out of a painting

Think steaming cups of tea, sunsets behind grassy hills, and crackling fires that invite you to unwind.

  • Luxury glamping in the UK doesn’t get more authentic than this—comfort, charm, and countryside calm all in one. Luxury glamping in the UK doesn’t get more authentic than this—comfort, charm, and countryside calm all in one.

3. Amsterdome – Rooftop Glamping Domes in Europe in Amsterdam

Forget everything you know about city stays— Amsterdam flips the idea of urban accommodation on its head.

  • Best for: Urban explorers and digital nomads Best for: Urban explorers and digital nomads

  • Features: Rooftop views, mirrored dome, minimalist interior Features: Rooftop views, mirrored dome, minimalist interior

  • Unique touch: Tranquility above Amsterdam’s lively streets Unique touch: Tranquility above Amsterdam’s lively streets

Perched atop a building, this glamping dome in Amsterdam offers a surreal mix of city energy and private calm. Use it for mindfulness sessions, breathwork, or just a night above the noise.

4. Rik’s Glamping Retreat – Planet-Themed Domes in Belgium

Belgium’s countryside meets cosmic comfort at Rik’s Glamping Retreat, where each dome is named after a planet—Mars, Venus, Saturn.

  • Best for: Slow travel and foodies Best for: Slow travel and foodies

  • Features: Farm-to-door breakfast, panoramic field views Features: Farm-to-door breakfast, panoramic field views

  • Unique touch: Locally sourced treats delivered each morning Unique touch: Locally sourced treats delivered each morning

This is eco-luxury glamping at its simplest: rustic elegance, starry skies, and the sweet rhythm of doing absolutely nothing.

5. Northern Lights Dome – Arctic Glamping in Norway

For a bucket list experience under the auroras, head to Northern Norway, where a transparent dome offers front-row seats to the Northern Lights.

  • Best for: Aurora chasers and winter lovers Best for: Aurora chasers and winter lovers

  • Features: Transparent dome, attached cabin, no Wi-Fi Features: Transparent dome, attached cabin, no Wi-Fi

  • Unique touch: Watch the Northern Lights from your bed Unique touch: Watch the Northern Lights from your bed

Attached to a traditional wooden cabin, this Arctic glamping dome lets you stay warm while marveling at the night sky. It’s remote, magical, and deeply peaceful.

Experience Nature in Style

These European glamping domes aren’t just accommodations—they’re immersive experiences. Whether you’re stargazing in Sweden, sipping wine in Belgium, or floating above Amsterdam, each one invites you to slow down, reconnect with nature, and rediscover what travel can be.

Rethink Your Next Adventure

Are you ready to rethink the way you travel? Glamping offers you unique stays that combine the best of nature and comfort. Every location provides an escape from the usual grind, letting you savor the beauty around you. Remember that **luxury glamping in the **UK does not merely mean being pampered; it means engaging deeply with the landscape, the atmosphere, and yourself.

Consider including one of these stunning resorts in your next travel plans. Each offers not only a place to stay but also a chance to create unforgettable memories. Your adventure awaits, with unique experiences waiting at every turn.

Wayfarer Footprints has captured the imagination of explorers and dreamers across the globe.

Our travel journey-filled with immersive adventures, honest insights, and practical tips-has resonated deeply with readers who seek meaning beyond the map. From solo travel stories to offbeat discoveries, we’re proud to have been recognized by both Indian and international media. Back from our fifth trip of 2021 but, if you noticed, it hasn't been without a theme. This year has been all about discovering off beat unconventional countries. We started our year in [Uganda,](/post/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda) then went to [Kyrgyzstan](/post/how-to-make-the-most-of-7-days-in-kyrgyzstan) for Spring. That was followed by a non-beachy to [Seychelles](/post/a-five-day-guide-to-going-off-the-beaten-track-in-seychelles), and a whirlwind trip to four Central American treasures and now - Albania. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-1.jpg)

Nestled in the Balkans and bordered by Montenegro, Kosovo, North Macedonia, and Greece, Albania is a destination that blends ancient history, Mediterranean beaches, Ottoman charm, and untamed landscapes. From the fairy-tale streets of **Berat**, the UNESCO-protected city of a thousand windows, to the wild hikes of **Theth National Park** and the glittering coastline of the **Albanian Riviera**, this country is as diverse as it is captivating.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-2.jpg)

Over the course of **8 days**, I road-tripped through Albania, stopping in mountain villages, seaside towns, and historic cities. This guide is packed with practical tips — **visa info, travel costs, transport, vegetarian food options, safety, and culture** — along with my own experiences to help you plan your perfect itinerary.


So grab your backpack (or suitcase, no judgment!), and let’s dive into Albania.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-3.jpg)

My 8-Day Albania Itinerary

This itinerary is perfect if you want a **balanced mix of mountains, history, and beaches**.

- **Day 1:** Tirana (Arrival + city highlights)
**Day 1:** Tirana (Arrival + city highlights)

- **Day 2:** Kruje & Shkoder
**Day 2:** Kruje & Shkoder

- **Day 3–4:** Theth National Park (hiking + homestays)
**Day 3–4:** Theth National Park (hiking + homestays)

- **Day 5:** Lake Ohrid (Pogradec)
**Day 5:** Lake Ohrid (Pogradec)

- **Day 6:** Berat
**Day 6:** Berat

- **Day 7:** Albanian Riviera (Himare, Dhermi, Ksamil)
**Day 7:** Albanian Riviera (Himare, Dhermi, Ksamil)

- **Day 8:** Gjirokastër & Departure from Tirana

**Visa Information**

Most nationalities (including EU, UK, US, Canada, Australia and most recently Indians) can enter Albania visa-free [Albania Ministry of Foreign Affairs – Visa Info](https://punetejashtme.gov.al/en/consular-services/visa/)

**Currency**

The currency is the **Albanian Lek (ALL)**. Euros are sometimes accepted in tourist areas, but always carry Lek for local shops and buses. [ ](https://www.xe.com/currency/ALL-Albanian-Lek)[XE Currency Converter – Albanian Lek](https://www.xe.com/currency/ALL-Albanian-Lek)

**Getting Around**

- **Car rental** → Best way to explore. Roads are winding but scenic.
**Car rental** → Best way to explore. Roads are winding but scenic.

- **Buses (furgons)** → Cheap but unpredictable schedules.
**Buses (furgons)** → Cheap but unpredictable schedules.

- **Taxis** → Affordable within cities.

**Taxis** → Affordable within cities.


**Language**

Albanian (Shqip) is the official language. English is spoken in tourist areas. 👉[ ](https://www.ethnologue.com/language/sqi)[Ethnologue – Albanian Language](https://www.ethnologue.com/language/sqi)

**Costs**

- Budget travelers: €25–40/day
Budget travelers: €25–40/day

- Mid-range: €50–80/day
Mid-range: €50–80/day

- Luxury: €100+
Luxury: €100+

[Budget Your Trip – Albania Costs](https://www.budgetyourtrip.com/albania)

**Guide Information** 
If you're a history enthusiast seeking an immersive, expert-led journey through Albania, I’d love to assist. Send me your [travel dates and a rough budget](/contact-us), and I’ll personally connect you with a local guide to ensure your trip is as deep and enriching as ours! 


**Best Time to Visit**

- **Spring (April–June)** → Lush landscapes, fewer tourists.
**Spring (April–June)** → Lush landscapes, fewer tourists.

- **Summer (July–August)** → Hot, busy on the Riviera.
**Summer (July–August)** → Hot, busy on the Riviera.

- **Autumn (September–October)** → Ideal for hiking + warm beaches. [ ](https://climateknowledgeportal.worldbank.org/country/albania/climate-data-historical)[Albania Climate Data](https://climateknowledgeportal.worldbank.org/country/albania/climate-data-historical)
**Autumn (September–October)** → Ideal for hiking + warm beaches. [ ](https://climateknowledgeportal.worldbank.org/country/albania/climate-data-historical)[Albania Climate Data](https://climateknowledgeportal.worldbank.org/country/albania/climate-data-historical)

**Safety**

Albania is **safe for travelers**. Petty theft is rare. Roads can be tricky — drive carefully.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-4.jpg)

** Accommodation**

- Budget: Homestays & guesthouses (€15–30/night)
Budget: Homestays & guesthouses (€15–30/night)

- Mid-range: Boutique hotels in towns like Berat (€40–70/night)
Mid-range: Boutique hotels in towns like Berat (€40–70/night)

- Luxury: Riviera resorts (€100+/night)
Luxury: Riviera resorts (€100+/night)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-5.jpg)

**HIGHLIGHTS**

**TIRANA**

We landed around noon in Tirana, after a quick immigration procedure, we were out. Our driver was waiting for us. After a quick stop for lunch, we made our way to Shkoder. This small town which forms a natural boundary between Albania and Montenegro, has a sleepy laidback vibe, not unlike many other similar smaller European towns. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-6.jpg)

We were tired from our journey.. so it was a very light day. Our first stop was a visit to the Shkoder Lake - a massive lake, one of the largest in the Balkans. In the summer, you can kayak or hike around the lake. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-11.jpg)

We made our way to the city center around sunset... where there was definitely more activity. We also stopped at the St. Stephen’s Cathedral. There was also a mosque and a mausoleum not two blocks away from this church. That was one of the most remarkable things about Albania - the religious tolerance. 

**Fun Fact:** did you know Mother Theresa was born Albanian? She last visited this country in the early 90s. You will find shrines of her all around the capital city. You can also find a cafe where George Bush II had coffee when he visited Albania. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-15.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-17.jpg)

For dinner, we just took a walk around the old bazaar. That is, after all, the charm of Europe.. the endlessly picturesque pedestrian streets. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-19.jpg)

We stayed at **Garden Hotel B&B. **

Our first stop was the Mesi Bridge. This is one of the largest bridges that still remain from the Ottoman period. It is called mesi aka middle because it is centrally located. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-20.jpg)

It is a treasured Albanian monument. It is also extremely picturesque with stunning blue crystalline waters of the river flowing below. Amazes me how architecture survives for centuries even after weather, war and destruction. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-25.jpg)

After an hour, we started making our way up the scenic roads to the Northern Alps, towards Theth. This is easily one of the best places to chase fall colors in Albania. This jaw-dropping scenic village is a must-see on any trip to Albania. We made a hundred stops every 5 seconds because look! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-27.jpg)

The drive alone was quite a sight to behold with a breathtaking view of the Albanian alps on every corner. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-30.jpg)

After a mediocre lunch, we made our way towards the famous Theth Church. This is probably the picture you see of Albania on every travel magazine. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-32.jpg)

We started our hike towards Grunas Waterfall. More than the waterfall, I actually enjoyed the hike up. It was seriously postcard perfect. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-37.jpg)

The hike itself is a simple 4km track which can be done in about 2 hours at a medium pace. The waterfall isn't very big but definitely worth a visit to cool off on a hot day. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-41.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-43.jpg)

Don't miss the little bridge on your way back. Cutest picture spot! I recommend taking your time to relax in this picture-perfect village. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-47.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-49.jpg)

The rest of the evening was spent chilling at the small lodge - **Thethi Paradise. **

**THETH TO KRUJE **

The highlight of our morning was waking up to some of the most magical views we have seen in a while. Fall colors galore, with the clouds and sun playing hide and seek. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-51.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-54.jpg)

Doesn't get more pretty than this and it is probably the only reason I would recommend staying at Thethi Paradise. 

After skipping breakfast, we made our way towards Kruje, a historic city in Central Albania. While this town is small, it is also extremely significant, given that it is the birth place of the Albanian hero Skanderberg. There are a few things to do here like visiting the Skanderberg Castle and Museum. I recommend going with your guide to get a better understanding of how and why he is still a revered figure in Albanian culture. If you are not into history, then definitely go for the panoramic views. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-56.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-58.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-60.jpg)

Last stop for lunch was the Old Bazaar. You will find plenty of trinkets and street food here. It was similar to the bazaars in Istanbul but smaller. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-62.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-63.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-65.jpg)

We had to drive back to Tirana but we stopped by the city center before heading to our B&B for the night. 

Today we were supposed to be in Macedonia but as racism would have it, we were denied entry because of random bit of made up information. What was supposed to be a simple cross over the border turned into a tremendous waste of time and energy. Nevertheless, we didn't let it deter our plans in anyway. The guide quickly reshuffled helping us make it to all the posts we were supposed to see in Macedonia. 

First stop was Lake Ohrid, the largest lake in the Balkan's region. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-67.jpg)

Then we stopped at the city of Pogradec for a coffee and more scenic views. While nonsensical racism let us down, the weather did not disappoint. As we drove up to our final point, we were welcomed by everything from rainbow to misty clouds for miles. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-68.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-70.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-72.jpg)

By the time we reached the Prespa Lake, the weather had finally cleared. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-74.jpg)

With a surface area of 300 sq km, the Prespa lake is massive, shared between three countries. Split in two, these beautiful lakes here have kept each other company for millions of years. They are the heart and soul of this incredibly off beat town of Albania!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-77.jpg)

Though there are over a thousand lakes in Albania, not all of them are natural. So why did we choose Lake Prespa over the other 200 odd natural lakes? Well for one, unlike the more popular Lake Ohrid, the two Prespa lakes are much more secluded and off beat. 

You won’t find hordes of tourists here.. making it the perfect place for a peaceful getaway in nature. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-79.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-81.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-83.jpg)

Would you look at that golden hour reflection? We had all it to ourselves the entire three hours we spent there. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-85.jpg)

What a fabulous way to turn around a ridiculous travel day, eh? You not just welcomed us back but also gave us a stellar day when we returned. Albania, you stunner! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-86.jpg)

**KORCA**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-87.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-89.jpg)

Yet another early start in Korca as we headed out to the old bazaar for coffee and byrek. 

The drive was stunning as well.. seemed like we had walked into a giant Van Gogh painting. We stopped on the way to visit the second longest lake in Albania - Vjose. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-91.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-93.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-95.jpg)

Our first stop for the day was an exciting one - the Benja Thermal Bath. Located at a short distance from town or Permet inside the Kruje National Park. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-97.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-98.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-100.jpg)

There are three pools here - a main one and two smaller ones. The main pool obviously looks the best but it is always busy. We avoided it and went straight for the smaller one. The water is perfect to swim in on that chilly fall day. Don't forget to pack your swimsuits and dive in. 

After another couple hours, we made it to our final destination for the day - Gjirokaster. This is an extremely picturesque museum town perched on top of a hill facing the Drino river.  This city, a UNESCO site, offers a few different things to do like the castle and museum.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-102.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-104.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-106.jpg)

The castle itself is impressive, sitting on top of a majestic hill facing the old town. As soon you enter, you will find huge tanks from the world war. The castle is not huge but it is not small either, if you like history, expect to spend a couple hours. You must definitely visit both the arms museum and the Gjirokaster museum. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-108.jpg)

The castle was built on top of a plateau,  making it the best place to capture the old and new towns converging against the stunning mountainous landscape. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-109.jpg)

We finally made it back down and walked to our beautiful hotel in the middle of the old bazaar. Spent the night taking in the moonlight and the hustle bustle of a quiet European town. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-110.jpg)

**BLUE EYE**

What a view to wake up to. This was just the most wonderful mood with the pastel hues and foggy clouds. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-111.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-113.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-115.jpg)

After a delicious breakfast, we left the mountains to head towards the West where we were welcomed by wide salty seas and got ready for sandy toes. Our first stop for the day was the famous Blue Eye - one of the most touristy places in Albania. Don't let the tourist trap tag deter you from going here - it is quite the natural phenomenon. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-117.jpg)

This is a deep, fresh water spring that looks like a glowing human eye. The color is something you have to see to believe. Go as early as possible in the day to avoid the tourist buses. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-118.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-120.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-122.jpg)

Our next stop was in Saranda. During the summer, all three are extremely popular among the affluent Albanian families. Saranda is smaller than Vlore but bigger than Himare and is known for clean beaches, a vibrant town, amazing seafood and ocean cruises. So if a laid back beach holiday is your thing, this should be your next summer destination. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-124.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-126.jpg)

Definitely visit the Saranda castle for great view of both Saranda and Korçë in Greece at a distance. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-128.jpg)

Our last stop for the day was the Butrint National Park. My history obsessed husband fell in love with this little gem of a place. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-129.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-131.jpg)

With a picturesque mountainous setting and lagoon at the center, the scenery alone makes this park a must visit. Add to some history and mystery, you have got yourself an unmissable sight on your hands. What we saw was an amalgam of monuments spanning over two thousand years of history from the 4th century to the Ottoman era in the 19th century. From the sculptures to the scripts on walls, the signs are unmissable and fascinating. 

Don't miss the theatre from the Roman era, and after Julius Caesar and Augustus founded a colony here the city was extended through building of a bridge, causing commerce to boom!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-133.jpg)

As we explored the crumbling ruins we had truly stepped back 2400 years. The ancient city of Butrint was one of the finest and most beautiful cities in all of the Roman Empire. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-134.jpg)

We got back to our room for a light dinner and enjoyed a moon lit walk by the Sarande promenade. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-135.jpg)

**VLORE. HIMARE. SARANDA.**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-136.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-138.jpg)

On our trip, we planned to hit all of the Southern Coastal towns from Sarande to Vlore to Himare. Though Himare is smaller than Saranda, it is still a cute lazy beach town that comes alive in the summer. It has a distinct Greek influence - don't miss those blue roofs on your drive up through the Llogora Pass. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-140.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-142.jpg)

The Llogara Pass is one of the highlights of your drive through the South-West region of Albania. It is a winding road reaching up to a height of 1027m overlooking the Ionian Albania riveria. 

After a quick stop at Vlore and onwards to the Karavasta Lagoon to pay a visit to Johnny, the humungous dalmation pelican. Another highlight here are the lovely mangrove forests - if you go during certain times of the day, you can ride a boat. It was closed when we went, so we climbed the observatory tower. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-144.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-146.jpg)

Around 4 p.m. we made it to the incredibly stunning Berat. where we will be spending two days exploring the UNESCO town filled with Ottoman style houses, ancient churches and mythical castles. Berat decided to gift wrap a special sunset for us! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-148.jpg)

**BERAT**

Our last full day started with an unbelievably delicious breakfast. After finishing our PCR test, we headed straight up the holy mountains of Tomorr. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-149.jpg)

Mount Tomorr is not just the highest mountain in Albania but also a place of holy significance for the locals who visit the alps during the summer months to perform sacrifices. Apparently, the mountain tops are permanently covered in fog but you can't help but be impressed by the height of this giant mountain. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-150.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-152.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-154.jpg)

Once you make it to the top, you will be left wonderstruck with the sight of the scenic roads and awe-inspiring fall colors. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-156.jpg)

Next up was a rustic hike to the Bogove Waterfall. Unfortunately the path leading up to the falls was under construction but we still made it there. It is a small fall but still worth a visit. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-157.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-159.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-161.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-163.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-165.jpg)

Final stop for the day was a hike up to the Berat Castle. We were surrounded by views on either side of the Osum River - the traditionally orthodox Gorica and the Ottoman inspired Mangelam. 

Gratefully ended our trip with yet another astounding view of the Berat city at dusk. It felt like Albania was bidding us adieu in top style. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-167.jpg)

**FINAL THOUGHTS **

This was a trip where we went on to uncover one of the last few stellar gems left in eastern Europe. From the stunning peaks of the Albanian North to the pristine beaches of the Albanian Riviera in the west, to the historic South and East.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-168.jpg)

The husband loved soaking up the crazy dose of history. Of course, the highlight for me was the unfiltered nature minus the maddening European summer crowds. Ignored by the Instagram crowd in favor of the more popular Montenegro, Croatia or Serbia, Albania is still untouched due to its turbulent history. It was our kind of place. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-169.jpg)

It was not only safe and cheap but shockingly beautiful. The road trip across the countryside was one of the highlights for me. It is a photographers dream. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-170.jpg)

Albania is also a hotpot of cultures - not unlike Turkey. Yet what struck me most was the religious tolerance. Ancient mosques sit next to massive churches, while modern and quirky cafes line the colorful streets leading up to traditional houses. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-171.jpg)

Berat is, rightfully, one of the most popular cities in the country, which means people often only make it to Berat. I wouldn't blame them. It is not just charming but is also brimming with history. You will find Ottoman influences blending in with the characteristic orthodox structures. My two cents - as much as I loved Berat, don't just go here to do the touristy bits, there is so much more to this country. Give Albania a chance, it can be mesmerizing, eye-opening and jaw-dropping! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/guidetoalbania-150.jpg)

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

Back from our fifth trip of 2021 but, if you noticed, it hasn’t been without a theme. This year has been all about discovering off beat unconventional countries. We started our year in Uganda, then went to Kyrgyzstan for Spring. That was followed by a non-beachy to Seychelles, and a whirlwind trip to four Central American treasures and now - Albania.

Nestled in the Balkans and bordered by Montenegro, Kosovo, North Macedonia, and Greece, Albania is a destination that blends ancient history, Mediterranean beaches, Ottoman charm, and untamed landscapes. From the fairy-tale streets of Berat, the UNESCO-protected city of a thousand windows, to the wild hikes of Theth National Park and the glittering coastline of the Albanian Riviera, this country is as diverse as it is captivating.

Over the course of 8 days, I road-tripped through Albania, stopping in mountain villages, seaside towns, and historic cities. This guide is packed with practical tips — visa info, travel costs, transport, vegetarian food options, safety, and culture — along with my own experiences to help you plan your perfect itinerary.

So grab your backpack (or suitcase, no judgment!), and let’s dive into Albania.

My 8-Day Albania Itinerary

This itinerary is perfect if you want a balanced mix of mountains, history, and beaches.

Currency

The currency is the Albanian Lek (ALL). Euros are sometimes accepted in tourist areas, but always carry Lek for local shops and buses.  XE Currency Converter – Albanian Lek

Getting Around

  • Car rental → Best way to explore. Roads are winding but scenic. Car rental → Best way to explore. Roads are winding but scenic.

Taxis → Affordable within cities.

Language

Albanian (Shqip) is the official language. English is spoken in tourist areas. 👉 Ethnologue – Albanian Language
