Offbeat Africa destinations for Indian travellers in 2026 aren’t about ticking boxes on a safari checklist. They’re about the moment a Maasai vendor in a Tanzanian bazaar invites you for chai. The afternoon you discover textiles in African markets rival some of the world’s finest craftsmanship. The evening you’re eating Egyptian ful medames and realizing it tastes surprisingly familiar to Indian palates.

This is the real Africa—where Indians have traded, lived, and travelled for over 2,000 years along historic trade routes, and where 2026 offers unprecedented visa access and infrastructure improvements for travellers from India.

Having explored more than 40 African countries over the past decade, this guide shares real experiences from street markets, local guesthouses, remote villages, and off-the-beaten-path destinations—not tourism board brochures.

Why African Travel is Different for Indian Passport Holders in 2026

India maintains strong diplomatic and economic ties across Africa. In 2026, this means:

• Simplified visa procedures
• Better flight connectivity
• Stronger cultural exchange programs
• Growing Indian communities across major African cities
Many African nations now offer visa-on-arrival or e-visa facilities specifically for Indian passport holders.

More importantly, Indian travellers are generally welcomed as trade partners, students, entrepreneurs, and cultural visitors rather than traditional tourists.

Benefits for Indian travellers include:
• Familiarity with Indian cuisine
• Large Indian diaspora communities
• Shared business and trade traditions
• Easy access to vegetarian food in many regions

Jambo!! You will hear this a million times during your trip. The warm smiles and welcoming nature of people in Zanzibar is an entire VIBE. Visit and find out for yourself.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-cover.jpg)

If you read about our [Maldives](/post/planning-a-trip-to-maldives) trip, you already know it was one hell of a relaxing vacation (not how we are used to traveling). I had people asking me on IG if the two trips were similar – yes & no. Though both have beaches aplenty, there are differences. I also planned for Zanzibar to be far more active than our Maldivian escape. Keep reading for my final verdict on the two trips.o trips. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-2.jpeg)

I am not big on island getaways… at least until the weird year that was 2020. Not only did I do touristy island trips within 15 days of each other, I actually enjoyed it. Who woulda thunk it, eh?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-3.jpg)

Here is my **Zanzibar travel guide** for planning a five-day trip to the small island off the coast of Tanzania.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-4.jpg)

### **Flights ****+**** Visa**** ****Stone Town Zanzibar**

I flew direct with [Fly Dubai](https://www.expedia.com/). The flight was 5 hours and 45 minutes.

Visa is on arrival for most nationalities, including Indian citizens. **Zanzibar visa for Indians** costs $50 per person. No other documents are needed. Best to carry cash. You can also submit your visa application online before you travel.


The **best time to visit Zanzibar** is June to October due to slightly cooler weather. December to February is also dry but the afternoon heat can hit hard.

Everyone is helpful and fairly fluent in English. USD is accepted at most resorts.

USD accepted at all resorts. 

I prefer hiring a local guide/driver in [Africa.](/africa) This supports the local economy, is safer, and gives access to hidden gems and history.

After much deliberation, I hired a local taxi. The car was a comfortable SUV in great condition, stocked with water. He was professional, neat, punctual, and knew all the best spots. Costs vary depending on your activities.

The car was a comfortable SUV in great condition, stocked with water, which you will need a lot of.** **Felt completely safe with him - he was perfectly dressed, neat, professional and extremely punctual. The cost depends on what you choose to do during your trip. 

I traveled with a friend, but also explored solo. Luxury properties made me feel safe. There was some minor catcalling in Stone Town, but walking with purpose and following your instincts ensures safety. Avoid wandering alone at night.

**Where I Stayed**

**Stone Town Zanzibar:** [Park Hyatt Zanzibar](https://www.booking.com/hotel/tz/park-hyatt-zanzibar.en-gb.html?dist=0;group_adults=1;group_children=0;keep_landing=1;no_rooms=1;sb_price_type=total;sid=2c91221561facfee3d6615d3d1424f9e;type=total&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-48c8828c586041c281b3467ba-437527_cjevent-48e53fb2964b11f082a9009b0a82b82d&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287), a UNESCO heritage building, is a gorgeous choice. Perfectly located, just 10 minutes from the ferry if arriving from Dar Es Salaam. The beach is crowded but fantastic.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-5.jpg)

**Kendwa Beach:** [Zuri Zanzibar](https://www.booking.com/hotel/tz/zuri-zanzibar.en-gb.html?dist=0;group_adults=1;group_children=0;keep_landing=1;no_rooms=1;sb_price_type=total;sid=2c91221561facfee3d6615d3d1424f9e;type=total&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-e77904b7a6be43c08ff02eaa7-437527_cjevent-a58ddea2964b11f081b300950a82b820&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287) is an elegant, eco-chic resort. Other options considered were The [Residence](https://www.booking.com/hotel/tz/the-residence-zanzibar.en-gb.html?aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go%20Travel%20Un%20Limited_clkid-a4ddcb40fc134f25a011678cd-437527_cjevent-ac41ba2c964b11f081b300950a82b820&sid=f08c842dbab42a93655ecaba13793263&checkin=2024-02-10&checkout=2024-02-15&dest_id=-2565924&dest_type=city&dist=0&group_adults=1&group_children=0&hapos=1&hpos=1&no_rooms=1&req_adults=1&req_children=0&room1=A&sb_price_type=total&soh=1&sr_order=popularity&srepoch=1706198389&srpvid=7ca07076dbe002e7&type=total&ucfs=1&#no_availability_msg), Melia, and Tulia. Zuri’s location makes it one of the better **Zanzibar beaches** for sun, sand, and privacy.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-6.jpg)

Zanzibar is more affordable than Maldives. Food outside resorts is cheap, especially street food. Resorts are the most expensive part.

Five days in two five-star properties cost around $1600 per person (peak season).

Vegetarian options at Park Hyatt and Zuri were excellent. With half-board at Zuri, breakfast and dinner are included; lunch is around $30 per person. Fresh bread accompanies every meal.

Lesson learnt - the choice and veggie-friendly options for food at Park Hyatt and Zuri were outstanding. If you opt for the half board option at Zuri like I did, you will be eligible for breakfast and dinner. The lunch would be about $30 per person for a soft drink and a main; you get fresh bread with every meal.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-9.jpg)


### **Day 1 – Jozani Forest Zanzibar & Stone Town**

After breakfast, a one-hour drive took us to **Jozani Forest Zanzibar**, home to red colobus monkeys. Guides help you spot the monkeys and bushbabies safely.

Fun fact: Colobus monkeys have only four fingers and use their hands, not tails, to move between trees.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-15.jpg)

This tour is not a safari so please don't mistake it for one. That being said, the monkeys are sneaky.. there will be some of coordination between the guides. It doesn't take very long to spot the little guys up on trees. They are wild animals and it is recommended you stand atleast 5ft away from them **(please be mindful if you a flu or cold as you could pass this on to them!).** They carry on with their business in a relaxed manner as if you weren't around.  If you are lucky, you can also spot some bushbaby. 

Afterward, return to Park Hyatt Zanzibar to explore the winding streets of **Stone Town Zanzibar**. The hotel merges contemporary design with old-world charm, offering stunning views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-21.jpg)

**Pro Planning Tip: if you have an extra day or time to kill, think about including this forest on your way to the Paje beach. **

The guide was wonderful. He had so much information about the place and monkeys. For instance, what makes the colobus monkeys unique is that they only have four fingers. Also, unlike other species of monkeys, they don't jump from tree to tree using their tails. Their tails don't have muscles which means they use their hands. Cool huh? 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-23.jpg)

The forest isn't very big but you will need a guide to help you find the monkeys. On your tour you will likely cover the forest and the mangroves nearby. A boardwalk with swamp on either side. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-24.jpg)

This place alone warrants a visit to the Jozani area. I will forever be in awe of nature and it's tranquility. Needless to say, the mangroves play a very important role in the ecological balance for this island with medicinal plants and ecosystems in place to protect the island from flash floods. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-27.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-29.jpg)

Drove back to the room at about 3 in the afternoon. 

Park Hyatt is a quintessential stop to discover the bustling winding streets of the cultural center of Zanzibar. The hotel itself has all the old world charm plus the luxury you would come to expect from a Park Hyatt. The bath tub and shower were too comfortable for words. All equipped with high quality, locally sourced skin and hair products. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-31.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-33.jpg)

They also have the best views in stone town. Who wouldn't want to wake up here every day? 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-35.jpg)

It feels less like a hotel from outside but the rooms are elegantly decked with beautiful hints of classic Omani architecture. Park Hyatt Zanzibar is a fine example of merging contemporary designs with old world culture. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-36.jpg)

As with most of Zanzibar, I found it to be a blend of Indian, Oman and few European influences. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-37.jpg)

The sunset was lovely but nothing like the isolated heavenly feeling I experienced in Maldives. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-42.jpg)

Skipped dinner and hit the bed early after a long day. 

Sunrise at the beach, followed by a dip in the infinity pool.

Head to Kizimkazi for dolphin watching. Go early to maximize sightings. Snorkeling is optional; tides can be rough. Private tours are recommended.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-44.jpg)

Transfer to **Kendwa Beach** for check-in at Zuri Zanzibar. Afternoon spent exploring the resort and enjoying a free spice tour. The garden showcases local spices like cardamom, ylang-ylang, and cinnamon – a sensory delight.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-47.jpg)

Evening: Relax at the **Zanzibar beaches** and watch the sunset.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-49.jpg)

Post breakfast, all set for a fun-filled activity to Kizimkazi. It was time to watch the dolphins. The roads are covered with dense forests on either side, so very different for what I usually see in sandy dry Dubai. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-50.jpg)

You will head out to the ocean on a private boat (along with 20 other boats). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-51.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-53.jpg)

I would have preferred to head out to the sea earlier since by afternoon the water is too warm and the dolphins deep diver under to escape the heat. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-55.jpg)

Nevertheless, if you head there by 9-ish, you will still get to witness these magical creatures putting up a show. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-56.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-58.jpg)

**Things to note: **

1. While you can snorkel here, a boat tour to watch them serves the purpose just as well. The tides tend to be rough at certain times of the month so be mindful of this if you decide to go swimming.

2. This is NOT a full day activity. If you want to spend more time in the ocean, you are better off adding an additional day and doing the **Safari Blue at Mnemba or Fumba Islands.  **

3. Ally set all the tours up, it was all private. Be sure to check with your tour agent about this when you pay. 

The beach itself was a stunner that cloudy morning. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-60.jpg)

I particularly loved the reflections here. African postcard moment. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-61.jpg)

By 12, we set off towards Kendwa (a good 90 minutes away), reaching the resort at 12 30. Leave some time for check in and with all the new safety measures in place. No complaints though since the reception area is incredibly picturesque with swings and a library. **Zuri takes it's pandemic safety seriously - I loved that. **You have to wear a mask in all common areas except the beach. Wear a mask. Hakuna Matata! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-62.jpg)

Checked in to the room.. look at that balcony. I will circle back to the story on the resort later. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-65.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-67.jpg)

The rest of the afternoon was spent having a late lunch and exploring the property. The highlight of my stay in the property was the free spice tour. Once again, the guide was full of incredible information. His enthusiasm was infectious. We got to play a little guessing game too - so much fun. Know what this spice is? 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-69.jpeg)

The sprawling acres of green goodness is just a quick 10 minute from the villa. From almond to wild cardamom to ylang ylang flowers, they have it all. Ocean winds often kick up scents from the nearby garden and it is a green oasis. A great place to learn about all the incredible spices on the island - mostly a fusion of flavors used in African, Middle Eastern and Asian cultures. It is a riot on your senses.. you get to smell cinnamon that is actually clove. 
Mind == blown!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-70.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-72.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-76.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-78.jpg)

Not into spices? The lush garden is perfect for a stroll or just to lounge around in these colorful nooks. Oh to be outside in nature. Don't miss the cutest swing! 

Last stop for the day.. the beach.. a cloudy evening awaits..


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-80.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-84.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-88.jpg)

but no shortage of chill vibes. 

That's a wrap of day two! 

Morning spent at the beach and Baraka Natural Aquarium in Nungwi. Swim alongside rescued turtles for an unforgettable experience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-90.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-91.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-93.jpg)

Flowers blooming, birds chirping, trees swinging... off I went to spend the morning at the beach. 

Afternoon: Lounge at Zuri Zanzibar, exploring the eco-conscious, garden-like resort. Yoga deck, local artifacts, and thatched roofs provide a perfect zen experience. Sunset at the beach with dhows sailing in the distance.

Dinner: Four-course vegetarian meal highlighting local flavors


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-95.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-97.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-99.jpg)

Early mornings are my favorite time to be at the beach. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-101.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-103.jpg)

The water is just the right temperature, there are hardly any people.. plus if you are lucky enough to experience low tide, no better time to snorkel. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-105.jpg)

I particularly liked having these cabanas and lounges to myself.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-106.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-108.jpg)

Post a hearty breakfast, I went to the **Baraka Natural Aquarium**, a natural pond which is a sanctuary for turtles. The aquarium costs $10 to enter. This is in Nungwi, ask your resort to set up the drive. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-110.jpeg)

The small pond serves as a temporary home for these creatures accidentally injured when they get caught in fishing nets.. this gives them a chance to recuperate before being released back into the ocean again. To my utter surprise I had the place to myself at 9. BLISS. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-111.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-113.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-115.jpg)

The turtles are playful and get very close. You feel almost ticklish as they brush up close to you.. but it’s just for a few minutes. Before long you start to get the hang of swimming around in the murky waters alongside these gentle animals. Running your fingers along their smooth shells as you feed the hungry little ones is a pinch-me-now kinda feeling. I was not nibbled at or bitten. Maybe I was lucky? Either way, this was one of two highlights of my trip to the island. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-117.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-119.jpg)

![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-123.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-125.jpeg)

Things to keep in mind - the turtles will be hungry but they don't mind being touched (trust me, you **will** know if they have a problem). Be kind and gentle. Try not to stomp on them. Turtles DO tend to bite or nibble - the choice to dive in with them is yours to make. 

With the busy morning out of the way, I had the rest of the day to lounge around and take in the outstanding elements on the property - the main reason I chose it. There are plenty of equally good 5* resorts in Zanzibar but my heart was set on choosing this afro-modern resort. I was not disappointed. You will find natural local materials used in every corner. There are also artifacts made by local craftsmen. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-127.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-129.jpg)

There is abundance of social awareness. Zuri is best experienced while staying in a garden-like landscape.. complete with thatched roofs, surrounded by mini-rainforests and crawling wildlife. I also didn’t find any single-use plastic (except the bottles, which resorts worldwide now have to use by law due to the pandemic). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-131.jpg)

This is a property that is both ecologically sensitive. Special mention to the yoga deck. The set up with the water and lush garden was zen perfection. Zuri means beautiful in Swahili and it aptly summarizes the experience to be had over here. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-135.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-137.jpg)

Took the morning to explore few more mazing areas inside Zuri - like this pool overlooking the beach. It does tend to get crowded by 10.. early in the morning, you can have it all to yourself.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-150.jpg)

I have never been big on fancy hotels because we hardly spent any time indoors on trips. Last December I had two slow-ish getaways giving me ample time to soak in the atmosphere at the resorts. I love places with personal touches. While the little five stars next to the name definitely help during a pandemic that is not what makes a resort special. It is about the story, sustainability agenda, and graceful staff. This is something you can get that for 10 or 1000 bucks a night. This was the view at sunrise. Worth waking up for this.

### **Day 4 – Swimming with Horses**

Early morning pool and beach exploration. Miles of soft white sand leading into turquoise waters.

Afternoon: Swim with horses on Nungwi beach (60-minute experience, $50). The rescued horses are treated compassionately, ensuring a safe and ethical experience.

Sunset: Relaxed evening on the beach and resort grounds.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-171.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-173.jpg)

I did my research to make sure the adopted horses are treated compassionately and the interactions are sensitive. I chose not to climb on Bob until we got to the beach. I walked alongside, hoping he would trust me. When he took his time and chose to roll around in the sand instead, I remember being very nervous. What had I done to make him cautious? This is something I wanted to make certain, no animal should be forced to engage in an activity. After 10 minutes, he was excited about the waves but I was still weary about climbing on; tbh I was overwhelmed by this (powerful) little guy. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-175.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-177.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-179.jpg)

I held on lightly so as to not cause his back any harm (fell off a fair few times in the process). I was on edge, making sure he was comfortable in the high tides, I kept speaking softly. My confidence was bolstered when he didn’t waver for a minute, swimming bravely with me by his side. At the end of it, he had endeared to me.. we even did a little head bump. Moments forever etched in my heart. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-181.jpg)

I cannot tell you what a meaningful experience this was, free of saddles, bonding with this magnificent animal. It was about being respectful and treating him as an equal. I simply let Bob lead the way. I only followed - not the other way around! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar-stonetownzanzibar-182.jpg)

Back to the resort at 4 for a super late lunch.. and then beach for the last sunset.

**Maldives vs Zanzibar:**

- **Maldives:** Private, exclusive, incredible snorkeling, and ethereal sunsets.
**Maldives:** Private, exclusive, incredible snorkeling, and ethereal sunsets.

- **Zanzibar:** Cultural experiences, history, adventure, and decent beaches. More affordable, and can combine with a safari in Serengeti.
**Zanzibar:** Cultural experiences, history, adventure, and decent beaches. More affordable, and can combine with a safari in Serengeti.

Pristine white sands, turquoise waters, enchanting spices, and vibrant culture make[ Zanzibar](https://www.zurizanzibar.com/?utm_source=) a magical [destination](/post/howtoplanatriptolesotho). Snorkel, paddleboat, go on spice tours, shop, and meet amazing people.

Jambo!! You will hear this a million times during your trip. The warm smiles and welcoming nature of people in Zanzibar is an entire VIBE. Visit and find out for yourself.

If you read about our Maldives trip, you already know it was one hell of a relaxing vacation (not how we are used to traveling). I had people asking me on IG if the two trips were similar – yes & no. Though both have beaches aplenty, there are differences. I also planned for Zanzibar to be far more active than our Maldivian escape. Keep reading for my final verdict on the two trips.o trips.

I am not big on island getaways… at least until the weird year that was 2020. Not only did I do touristy island trips within 15 days of each other, I actually enjoyed it. Who woulda thunk it, eh?

Here is my Zanzibar travel guide for planning a five-day trip to the small island off the coast of Tanzania.

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Getting Around + Driver Information Getting Around + Driver Information

  • Where I Stayed Where I Stayed

  • Costs Costs

  • Vegan + Vegetarian Food Vegan + Vegetarian Food

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

Flights + Visa** **Stone Town Zanzibar

I flew direct with Fly Dubai. The flight was 5 hours and 45 minutes.

Visa is on arrival for most nationalities, including Indian citizens. Zanzibar visa for Indians costs $50 per person. No other documents are needed. Best to carry cash. You can also submit your visa application online before you travel.

The best time to visit Zanzibar is June to October due to slightly cooler weather. December to February is also dry but the afternoon heat can hit hard.

Everyone is helpful and fairly fluent in English. USD is accepted at most resorts.

USD accepted at all resorts.

I prefer hiring a local guide/driver in Africa. This supports the local economy, is safer, and gives access to hidden gems and history.

After much deliberation, I hired a local taxi. The car was a comfortable SUV in great condition, stocked with water. He was professional, neat, punctual, and knew all the best spots. Costs vary depending on your activities.

The car was a comfortable SUV in great condition, stocked with water, which you will need a lot of.** **Felt completely safe with him - he was perfectly dressed, neat, professional and extremely punctual. The cost depends on what you choose to do during your trip.

I traveled with a friend, but also explored solo. Luxury properties made me feel safe. There was some minor catcalling in Stone Town, but walking with purpose and following your instincts ensures safety. Avoid wandering alone at night.

Where I Stayed

Stone Town Zanzibar: Park Hyatt Zanzibar, a UNESCO heritage building, is a gorgeous choice. Perfectly located, just 10 minutes from the ferry if arriving from Dar Es Salaam. The beach is crowded but fantastic.

Kendwa Beach: Zuri Zanzibar is an elegant, eco-chic resort. Other options considered were The Residence, Melia, and Tulia. Zuri’s location makes it one of the better Zanzibar beaches for sun, sand, and privacy.

Zanzibar is more affordable than Maldives. Food outside resorts is cheap, especially street food. Resorts are the most expensive part.

Five days in two five-star properties cost around $1600 per person (peak season).

Vegetarian options at Park Hyatt and Zuri were excellent. With half-board at Zuri, breakfast and dinner are included; lunch is around $30 per person. Fresh bread accompanies every meal.

Lesson learnt - the choice and veggie-friendly options for food at Park Hyatt and Zuri were outstanding. If you opt for the half board option at Zuri like I did, you will be eligible for breakfast and dinner. The lunch would be about $30 per person for a soft drink and a main; you get fresh bread with every meal.

Day 1 – Jozani Forest Zanzibar & Stone Town

After breakfast, a one-hour drive took us to Jozani Forest Zanzibar, home to red colobus monkeys. Guides help you spot the monkeys and bushbabies safely.

Fun fact: Colobus monkeys have only four fingers and use their hands, not tails, to move between trees.

This tour is not a safari so please don’t mistake it for one. That being said, the monkeys are sneaky.. there will be some of coordination between the guides. It doesn’t take very long to spot the little guys up on trees. They are wild animals and it is recommended you stand atleast 5ft away from them (please be mindful if you a flu or cold as you could pass this on to them!). They carry on with their business in a relaxed manner as if you weren’t around. If you are lucky, you can also spot some bushbaby.

Afterward, return to Park Hyatt Zanzibar to explore the winding streets of Stone Town Zanzibar. The hotel merges contemporary design with old-world charm, offering stunning views.

**Pro Planning Tip: if you have an extra day or time to kill, think about including this forest on your way to the Paje beach. **

The guide was wonderful. He had so much information about the place and monkeys. For instance, what makes the colobus monkeys unique is that they only have four fingers. Also, unlike other species of monkeys, they don’t jump from tree to tree using their tails. Their tails don’t have muscles which means they use their hands. Cool huh?

The forest isn’t very big but you will need a guide to help you find the monkeys. On your tour you will likely cover the forest and the mangroves nearby. A boardwalk with swamp on either side.

This place alone warrants a visit to the Jozani area. I will forever be in awe of nature and it’s tranquility. Needless to say, the mangroves play a very important role in the ecological balance for this island with medicinal plants and ecosystems in place to protect the island from flash floods.

Drove back to the room at about 3 in the afternoon.

Park Hyatt is a quintessential stop to discover the bustling winding streets of the cultural center of Zanzibar. The hotel itself has all the old world charm plus the luxury you would come to expect from a Park Hyatt. The bath tub and shower were too comfortable for words. All equipped with high quality, locally sourced skin and hair products.

They also have the best views in stone town. Who wouldn’t want to wake up here every day?

It feels less like a hotel from outside but the rooms are elegantly decked with beautiful hints of classic Omani architecture. Park Hyatt Zanzibar is a fine example of merging contemporary designs with old world culture.

As with most of Zanzibar, I found it to be a blend of Indian, Oman and few European influences.

The sunset was lovely but nothing like the isolated heavenly feeling I experienced in Maldives.

Skipped dinner and hit the bed early after a long day.

Sunrise at the beach, followed by a dip in the infinity pool.

Head to Kizimkazi for dolphin watching. Go early to maximize sightings. Snorkeling is optional; tides can be rough. Private tours are recommended.

Transfer to Kendwa Beach for check-in at Zuri Zanzibar. Afternoon spent exploring the resort and enjoying a free spice tour. The garden showcases local spices like cardamom, ylang-ylang, and cinnamon – a sensory delight.

Evening: Relax at the Zanzibar beaches and watch the sunset.

Post breakfast, all set for a fun-filled activity to Kizimkazi. It was time to watch the dolphins. The roads are covered with dense forests on either side, so very different for what I usually see in sandy dry Dubai.

You will head out to the ocean on a private boat (along with 20 other boats).

I would have preferred to head out to the sea earlier since by afternoon the water is too warm and the dolphins deep diver under to escape the heat.

Nevertheless, if you head there by 9-ish, you will still get to witness these magical creatures putting up a show.

**Things to note: **

  1. While you can snorkel here, a boat tour to watch them serves the purpose just as well. The tides tend to be rough at certain times of the month so be mindful of this if you decide to go swimming.

  2. This is NOT a full day activity. If you want to spend more time in the ocean, you are better off adding an additional day and doing the **Safari Blue at Mnemba or Fumba Islands. **

  3. Ally set all the tours up, it was all private. Be sure to check with your tour agent about this when you pay.

The beach itself was a stunner that cloudy morning.

I particularly loved the reflections here. African postcard moment.

By 12, we set off towards Kendwa (a good 90 minutes away), reaching the resort at 12 30. Leave some time for check in and with all the new safety measures in place. No complaints though since the reception area is incredibly picturesque with swings and a library. **Zuri takes it’s pandemic safety seriously - I loved that. **You have to wear a mask in all common areas except the beach. Wear a mask. Hakuna Matata!

Checked in to the room.. look at that balcony. I will circle back to the story on the resort later.

The rest of the afternoon was spent having a late lunch and exploring the property. The highlight of my stay in the property was the free spice tour. Once again, the guide was full of incredible information. His enthusiasm was infectious. We got to play a little guessing game too - so much fun. Know what this spice is?

The sprawling acres of green goodness is just a quick 10 minute from the villa. From almond to wild cardamom to ylang ylang flowers, they have it all. Ocean winds often kick up scents from the nearby garden and it is a green oasis. A great place to learn about all the incredible spices on the island - mostly a fusion of flavors used in African, Middle Eastern and Asian cultures. It is a riot on your senses.. you get to smell cinnamon that is actually clove. Mind == blown!

Not into spices? The lush garden is perfect for a stroll or just to lounge around in these colorful nooks. Oh to be outside in nature. Don’t miss the cutest swing!

Last stop for the day.. the beach.. a cloudy evening awaits..

but no shortage of chill vibes.

That’s a wrap of day two!

Morning spent at the beach and Baraka Natural Aquarium in Nungwi. Swim alongside rescued turtles for an unforgettable experience.

Flowers blooming, birds chirping, trees swinging… off I went to spend the morning at the beach.

Afternoon: Lounge at Zuri Zanzibar, exploring the eco-conscious, garden-like resort. Yoga deck, local artifacts, and thatched roofs provide a perfect zen experience. Sunset at the beach with dhows sailing in the distance.

Dinner: Four-course vegetarian meal highlighting local flavors

Early mornings are my favorite time to be at the beach.

The water is just the right temperature, there are hardly any people.. plus if you are lucky enough to experience low tide, no better time to snorkel.

I particularly liked having these cabanas and lounges to myself.

Post a hearty breakfast, I went to the Baraka Natural Aquarium, a natural pond which is a sanctuary for turtles. The aquarium costs $10 to enter. This is in Nungwi, ask your resort to set up the drive.

The small pond serves as a temporary home for these creatures accidentally injured when they get caught in fishing nets.. this gives them a chance to recuperate before being released back into the ocean again. To my utter surprise I had the place to myself at 9. BLISS.

The turtles are playful and get very close. You feel almost ticklish as they brush up close to you.. but it’s just for a few minutes. Before long you start to get the hang of swimming around in the murky waters alongside these gentle animals. Running your fingers along their smooth shells as you feed the hungry little ones is a pinch-me-now kinda feeling. I was not nibbled at or bitten. Maybe I was lucky? Either way, this was one of two highlights of my trip to the island.

Things to keep in mind - the turtles will be hungry but they don’t mind being touched (trust me, you will know if they have a problem). Be kind and gentle. Try not to stomp on them. Turtles DO tend to bite or nibble - the choice to dive in with them is yours to make.

With the busy morning out of the way, I had the rest of the day to lounge around and take in the outstanding elements on the property - the main reason I chose it. There are plenty of equally good 5* resorts in Zanzibar but my heart was set on choosing this afro-modern resort. I was not disappointed. You will find natural local materials used in every corner. There are also artifacts made by local craftsmen.

There is abundance of social awareness. Zuri is best experienced while staying in a garden-like landscape.. complete with thatched roofs, surrounded by mini-rainforests and crawling wildlife. I also didn’t find any single-use plastic (except the bottles, which resorts worldwide now have to use by law due to the pandemic).

This is a property that is both ecologically sensitive. Special mention to the yoga deck. The set up with the water and lush garden was zen perfection. Zuri means beautiful in Swahili and it aptly summarizes the experience to be had over here.

A few hours chilling in the room and it was already time for sunset.. time flies when you are having fun.

Though these sunsets were not nearly as charming as the ones in Maldives, I think my favorite part was the dhows setting sailing at a distance. They make for an outstanding frame.

Dinner was a four course vegetarian meal - the highlights were the amuse bouche and dessert. That is a wrap of day three!

Took the morning to explore few more mazing areas inside Zuri - like this pool overlooking the beach. It does tend to get crowded by 10.. early in the morning, you can have it all to yourself.

I have never been big on fancy hotels because we hardly spent any time indoors on trips. Last December I had two slow-ish getaways giving me ample time to soak in the atmosphere at the resorts. I love places with personal touches. While the little five stars next to the name definitely help during a pandemic that is not what makes a resort special. It is about the story, sustainability agenda, and graceful staff. This is something you can get that for 10 or 1000 bucks a night. This was the view at sunrise. Worth waking up for this.

Back to the beach again. The miles of soft white sand leading to the turquoise blue clear Indian Ocean is a sight you cannot get enough of. The sand in Zanzibar, unlike Maldives, felt more smooth. I could easily walk barefoot..

What I liked about it - each morning, the waves and morning light would be different.

Enjoy the palm tree, swinging hammock cabana life..

Day 4 – Swimming with Horses

Early morning pool and beach exploration. Miles of soft white sand leading into turquoise waters.

Afternoon: Swim with horses on Nungwi beach (60-minute experience, $50). The rescued horses are treated compassionately, ensuring a safe and ethical experience.

Sunset: Relaxed evening on the beach and resort grounds.

I did my research to make sure the adopted horses are treated compassionately and the interactions are sensitive. I chose not to climb on Bob until we got to the beach. I walked alongside, hoping he would trust me. When he took his time and chose to roll around in the sand instead, I remember being very nervous. What had I done to make him cautious? This is something I wanted to make certain, no animal should be forced to engage in an activity. After 10 minutes, he was excited about the waves but I was still weary about climbing on; tbh I was overwhelmed by this (powerful) little guy.

I held on lightly so as to not cause his back any harm (fell off a fair few times in the process). I was on edge, making sure he was comfortable in the high tides, I kept speaking softly. My confidence was bolstered when he didn’t waver for a minute, swimming bravely with me by his side. At the end of it, he had endeared to me.. we even did a little head bump. Moments forever etched in my heart.

I cannot tell you what a meaningful experience this was, free of saddles, bonding with this magnificent animal. It was about being respectful and treating him as an equal. I simply let Bob lead the way. I only followed - not the other way around!

Back to the resort at 4 for a super late lunch.. and then beach for the last sunset.

This was by far the prettiest evening. Fitting finale to wrap up the phenomenal stay at Zuri.

Late breakfast, then head to Prison Island, home to giant tortoises (some rumored 157 years old).** **

Explore Stone Town Zanzibar with a guide to learn about its Arab, Indian, and European influences. Don’t miss ornate doors, Darjani Bazaar, and Freddie Mercury’s house.

The island tends to be particularly crowded, but the experience is a fun one.. you can feed the giant tortoise or watch a penguin strut around in all its glory.

The only way to do a trip around Stone Town is with a guide. He will tell you all about the Arab, European and Indian influences on Zanzibar. Walk around the tiny streets and soak in the energy of the fun-loving people.

Of course, the ornate doors are the main highlight of this bustling area.

Don’t miss the house of** Freddie Mercury, Darjani Bazaar and **grab fresh coconut water while you are at it!

2020 brought humility and gratitude. A five-day trip planned in record time showed that Zanzibar is more than just beaches.

Maldives vs Zanzibar:

  • Maldives: Private, exclusive, incredible snorkeling, and ethereal sunsets. Maldives: Private, exclusive, incredible snorkeling, and ethereal sunsets.

  • Zanzibar: Cultural experiences, history, adventure, and decent beaches. More affordable, and can combine with a safari in Serengeti. Zanzibar: Cultural experiences, history, adventure, and decent beaches. More affordable, and can combine with a safari in Serengeti.

Pristine white sands, turquoise waters, enchanting spices, and vibrant culture make Zanzibar a magical destination. Snorkel, paddleboat, go on spice tours, shop, and meet amazing people.

No matter when you go, Stone Town Zanzibar and its surrounding areas will captivate your heart.

I share this with less nostalgia and more with gratitude for the experiences afforded to me in the last month. Moments like these have me recommitting my energy to discovering the most incredible wildlife experiences in Africa this year.

No matter when you go, prepare yourself to fall in love with Zanzibar. Pristine white sand beaches. Clear turquoise blue ocean. Vibrant people. Enchanting spices. Diverse culture. This East African island is full of beautiful contrasts.

This is a special place. You can snorkel, paddleboat, go on spice tours, shop and meet incredible people along the way. You will want to come back as soon as possible.

Maldives is private, exclusive with the most outstanding beaches and snorkeling reefs. Zanzibar is ideal for people looking for more of a cultural experience, along with decent beaches. If you don’t mind the crowd, this is the place for you!

Drop your comments below and show some love. Feel free to shoot me an email with your feedback.

Cheers,

Anki

Japan is a country where beauty reigns in every corner. Looking at our pictures from Japan never fails to lift my spirits. This **Japan**[ travel](https://www.japan.travel/en/?utm_source=)** guide** will take you through our unforgettable nine-day journey across Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, and Mount Fuji. This country floored me—it’s ridiculously crowded yet amazingly organized, minimal yet extravagant, modern yet rooted in tradition, fragile yet wild.

- Best Time to Visit
Best Time to Visit

Japan is beautiful. Period. For a truly otherworldly experience, I recommend visiting during the cherry blossom season* (subject to change every year) but it is mostly from mid to end of March, sometimes running into the first week of April. 


### **Flights + Visa**

We flew into Tokyo with Emirates, but you can also connect through Asia.

Getting a **Japan **[visa](https://www.mofa.go.jp/j_info/visit/visa/short/novisa.html?utm_source=)** for UAE residents** is straightforward. Simple paperwork, and we got ours in 3 days. UAE residents can now apply for an e-visa valid for 90 days.


### **Language**

Japanese is the official language. Knowing a few words helps, as small store owners don’t always speak English. Despite this, locals are incredibly helpful.


### **Currency**

The Japanese Yen is the official currency. Most places accept cards, but carrying cash is recommended for metro and bus travel.

- **Tokyo**: Hotel [Monterey Akasaka](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/monterey-akasaka.html?dksc=2;pbsource=conf_email_hotel_name&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-a40a3b1a84e14cafb3d97c94e-437527_cjevent-c293c809988911f0814b02fa0a82b82c&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287). Excellent rooms, short walk to metro, and each room had a cellphone with data—no need for a separate plan.
**Tokyo**: Hotel [Monterey Akasaka](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/monterey-akasaka.html?dksc=2;pbsource=conf_email_hotel_name&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-a40a3b1a84e14cafb3d97c94e-437527_cjevent-c293c809988911f0814b02fa0a82b82c&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287). Excellent rooms, short walk to metro, and each room had a cellphone with data—no need for a separate plan.

- **Kyoto**: One night in a traditional **Japan ryokan stay**, then [Sakura](https://www.booking.com/hotel/jp/sakura-terrace-minami-ku-kyoto.html?dksc=2;pbsource=conf_email_hotel_name&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-c0747d8425b8423fb4a328a06-437527_cjevent-cce4c736988911f082a903040a82b82d&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287) Terrace for the remainder. Cozy, central, and convenient for metro access.kfast.

Cozy, central, and convenient for metro access.kfast. 

Japan is not cheap. We mostly used metro and bus systems. Extremely punctual and efficient. **Japan metro travel tips**: always carry enough loose change.


### **SAFETY**

Japan is hands down one of the safest countries for tourists.

### **Day 1 – Tokyo City Part 1**

**Places covered**: Asakusa street, Senso-ji Temple, Tokyo Metropolitan Building, Ueno Park, Chiyoda, Imperial Palace


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-11.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-15.jpg)

Welcome to Tokyo’s world of cherry blossoms. **Things to do in Tokyo** include shrine hopping and blossom hunting. Asakusa’s Senso-ji Temple and Nakamise-dori are iconic. **Japanese temples and shrines** are scattered throughout Tokyo.

Next, **Sumida Park** is perfect for cherry blossom viewing. Tokyo has dozens of parks where you can enjoy **Japanese cherry blossom season**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-17.jpeg)

**Pro Tip**: Explore temple grounds and pagodas for a more immersive experience.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-22.jpg)

Our next stop was the beautiful **Sumida park** for more blossoms and amazing views of the iconic **Skytree Tower**. It's a huge park and one of the best places for hanami.. much quieter than some of the other parks around the city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-28.jpg)

Post lunch, we started making our way through the massive **Ueno park** - it's a must-see for hoards of trees lined on either side of the path.. great place to do that midday picnic. We spent a good three hours here. The lake in the middle of the park with ducks and framed by the blossoms is a treat to the eyes. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-32.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-34.jpg)

Our last stop for the day was another historical monument - the Imperical Palace. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-40.jpeg)

Take a bus from Tokyo or Shinjuku to Kawaguchiko station. Spend your day exploring **Mount Fuji Kawaguchiko** and nearby lakes. **Best time to visit Japan** for cherry blossoms is March–April.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-41.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-43.jpeg)

After an early (cloudy) start to the day, we got lucky at around 3 p.m. Wish we had seen more blossoms but still pretty thrilled with the view.  After reading so much about people not seeing the elusive mountain after multiple tries, our only thought  was we have got to try our luck with this...and the rest was just pure Japanese magic. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-47.jpg)

Did you know there are actually five Fuji lakes? Lake Kawaguchiko, Lake Yamanakako, Lake Saiko, Lake Shojiko and Lake Motosuko, located at the northern base of Mt. Fuji, which is also one of the best places to view Mt. Fuji from a close distance. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-49.jpg)

For the best sunset views of Mount Fuji, head to Lake Kawaguchiko. It's a wonderful place to see the mountain in all its splendor, as well as enjoy cherry blossom trees (if you visit during April).   


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-50.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-54.jpg)

**Pro Fuji Tip**: Lake Kawaguchiko offers the best sunset views and cherry blossom photo opportunities.. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-56.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-58.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-60.jpg)

**Places covered**: Yoyogi Park, Meiji Shrine, Harajuku Street, Shinjuku, Chiyoda, Yasukuni Shrine, Chidorigafuchi Park

Stroll through **Yoyogi Park** and Meiji Shrine during the **Japanese cherry blossom season**. **Things to do in Tokyo** include exploring Harajuku for street food and quirky shops.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-62.jpg)

Tokyo is home to dozens of stunning parks. If you happen to visit during the Cherry Blossom season, you will be spoilt for choice. If you are looking for the best park to catch the blossoms, you can't miss **Yoyogi Park**. Though this place is right in the middle of urban Tokyo, it is such a quiet place to unwind and relax. Walk around the park to experience the celebratory vibe as locals make the most of the season..


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-65.jpg)

Next up, the iconic symbol in the middle of the city - **Meiji Shrine**. Right here you will see that this is one of the few countries where the past, present and future co-exist in perfect harmony. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-67.jpeg)

Next we hit the **Harajuko street **between the Shinjuku and Shibuya lines**. **This street is famous for it's endless rows of trendy stores and street bites. Be prepared for a serious overdose of hello kitty merchandise. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-68.jpeg)

Post lunch we hit the Chiyoda area where we stopped to visit the **Yasukuni shrine** at sunset. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-69.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-71.jpg)

Our last stop for the evening was the **Chidorigafuchi Park**. The garden is famous for being home to over 1300 cherry blossoms.. but the best part is that the blossoms glow at night. Yozakura aka cherry blossoms at night is another amazing way to enjoy hanami. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-73.jpg)

This was easily the highlight of our day. Though every inch of the park was packed, we could not get enough of the views from here. It was spellbinding with the lake. Unfortunately, given the time of the day, we could not do the boat ride but it's highly recommended (come super early in the day if you want to do the boat ride). 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-74.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-76.jpeg)

Travel via Shinkansen—**Japan bullet train tips**: book early or on the spot for flexibility.

Stay at a **Japan ryokan stay** to experience traditional culture. Participate in a tea ceremony for a deeper understanding of Japanese customs.

**How to get to Kyoto? **

We did not reserve our train tickets in advance. Just went to the Tokyo station and booked a two-way Shinkansen bullet train to Kyoto.  

**Travel Tip: **As soon as you get to the station, you will be welcomed by tonnes of information on day tours and such. Take your pick. We decided to skip this and did it on our own - but if you are pinched for time, these tours are pretty good at covering all the spots around town. 

Since it was well past noon when we reached so the rest of the afternoon was spent visiting smaller shrines and immersing ourselves in the culture. Just from the get-go, Kyoto felt so old world and traditional…it was truly a different experience. There were parts of Kyoto that felt like a bustling city, and then you’d wander down a tiny alley and be transported back a 100 years. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-78.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-80.jpeg)

There is no better way to do that than with a stay at a **ryokan** - **a traditional Japanese-style inn**. It was the much needed escape into the quieter Japanese countryside, to complement days of exploring the crazy busy Tokyo city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-84.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-86.jpg)

Tip: don't miss the traditional **tea ceremony** at the ryokan. It's a quick demonstration of one of Japan's oldest traditions - of course, we got to taste the refreshing (read strong) tea after.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-88.png)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-90.png)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-92.png)

**Places covered**: Arashiyama Bamboo Forest, Path of Philosophy, Ginkaku-ji, Yasaka Shrine, Chion-In Temple, Fushimi Inari-Taisha

Take the **Kyoto itinerary** seriously: Arashiyama Bamboo Forest is serene early morning. The **Kyoto bamboo forest tips**: go at dawn for photos without crowds. Visit temples for the ultimate **Japanese temples and shrines** experience.

**Travel Tip:** To get to the forest, take the **C6 Bus towards Arashiyama **from the Kyoto station. You will be dropped at the bus stop right in front of the forest with clear directions to lead you towards the entrance. ca

Having gone to the Arashiyama Bamboo Forest at dawn, we had seriously amazing views of the place. As someone who loves being outdoors, I just stood there mesmerized by the light making its way through the trees. I mean would you look at this?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-94.jpeg)

I also read that it's best to visit the bamboo grooves in the morning, when it’s slightly windy because the tall bamboo stalks look dreamy and mesmerizing when they rock back and forth. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-95.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-97.jpg)

Our trip to Kyoto was going to be consumed by temples and shrines.. so being surrounded by all the green was already feeling like a great start. It makes you feel as if you stepped into another world... though small, Arashiyama is incredibly peaceful (when there are no tourists around). I highly recommend getting the FIRST bus to the forest if you want good photos of yourself without the crowd. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-99.jpg)

After we left the Bamboo forest, we took the loop city bus to the Ginkakuji area. We started walking **The Path Of Philosophy**. A long canal stretches along the path leading to the temple.. it's a wonderful place away from Kyoto city center. It was still pretty early in the day so we thoroughly enjoyed the peace and quiet.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-100.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-102.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-104.jpg)

The atmosphere of **Ginkakuji** temple was just brilliant. While the temple itself was beautiful, the path around the temple is a stunning garden.. it enhanced the feeling of Zen and there was an air of mysticism all around. The rhythm of the small stream in this picture created a peaceful background sound, highlighting the blending of Japanese architecture with its natural surroundings. Don't forget to walk to the top for panoramic view of Kyoto. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-106.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-108.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-110.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-112.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-114.jpg)

No trip to Kyoto is complete without a visit to the **Yasaka Shrine** and the** Chion-in Temple**. Both are big and spectacular - expect to spend a few hours here.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-116.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-118.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-120.jpeg)

Our last stop was the **Fushimi Inari** Gates. Needless to say, it was way too crowded at 6 in the evening but there are places inside the temple where you can get pictures without 67261292 people around you.. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-122.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-124.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-126.jpeg)

The  torii gate is believed to be the division between the physical and spiritual worlds. The best time to visit this stunning shrine is at Twilight. We visited just before sunset, walked around the famous vermilion torii gates and followed the path through the wooded forests to the green Inari mountains. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-128.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-130.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-132.jpg)

**Places covered**: Kiyomizu-dera, Gion Street, Ryozen Kannon Temple, Nanzenji Temple, To-ji Temple

Explore **things to do in Kyoto** beyond the tourist spots. Enjoy cobbled alleys, traditional wooden buildings, and stunning **Japanese cherry blossom season** views.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-134.jpg)

Next we made our way to this historic Gion area in Kyoto. With it’s picturesque wooden buildings lining the streets, this place is perfect for strolling around and window shopping. I particularly loved the **Yasaka Dori street** - a lovely, relatively quiet path through the back streets leading to Yasaka Pagoda.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-135.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-137.jpeg)

We walked around the **Shirakawa-minami Dori**, one of Kyoto's most beautiful streets. Then made our way over a small flight of stairs and a cutesy bridge to enjoy a gorgeous and vibrant Kyoto in all its splendor. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-139.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-141.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-143.jpg)

If you are in this area, a visit to the **Ryozen Kannon Temple** is a must. Here we found a 24 meter high statue of goddess of mercy. After this stop, we walked to the **Nanzenji Temple**. This spacious zen temple is also a stone garden. The garden area is free to explore but you can enter the temple and sub-temple for a small charge. This temple and the surrounding forest is a great way to escape the crowded Kyoto area. Highly recommended. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-145.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-147.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-149.jpg)

There was no shortage of amazing places in Kyoto to catch the blossom in all it's glory. Right next to our hotel, we found this beautiful garden. we were the only ones here and boy, were we in for a visual treat. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-151.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-153.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-155.jpg)

Our final stop for the day was sunset at the **To-ji temple**. The 57 meter high Pagoda is like the spiritual center of Kyoto city.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-157.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-159.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-161.jpg)

Take the Shinkansen to Osaka & Nara.

- **Osaka**: Osaka Castle surrounded by **Osaka Castle cherry blossoms** and a beautiful park.
**Osaka**: Osaka Castle surrounded by **Osaka Castle cherry blossoms** and a beautiful park.

- **Nara**: Visit Todaiji Temple (**Todaiji Temple Nara visit**) and feed friendly deer.
**Nara**: Visit Todaiji Temple (**Todaiji Temple Nara visit**) and feed friendly deer.

**Osaka travel guide** tip: the castle park is free, entry is optional. **Nara day trip** is perfect for historical and cultural immersion.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-163.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-165.jpg)

The port city of Osaka, is Japan’s second largest city and an eclectic one at that. I felt the energy of Osaka was a lot different than that of Tokyo. Yes, there are plenty of historic castles, shopping arcades and quirky streets but the city effortlessly blended modern city life with country charm - which was missing in the bustling Tokyo. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-167.jpg)

When you think about a castle in Japan, chances are that Osaka’s majestic castle is going to be the first one that comes to your mind but did you know that the Osaka Castle is surrounded by a beautiful park with over 600 cherry blossom trees? 


**Top Tip:** the castle is 600 yen to enter BUT the park is free. If you manage to get here between the end of March and beginning of April - it is one of the most stunning places to see the blossoms in the city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-168.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-170.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-172.jpg)

Did you know that the only thing that stands in the Osaka Castle as it is now and the original castle are these massive rocks? This castle has been rebuilt so many times yet no one can figure out how these rocks got there. Nevertheless, the castle is a massively gorgeous structure and an iconic landmark of the city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-174.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-176.jpg)

For the second half of the day, we hopped on to the train to visit **Nara**... famous for it's deer park. These little ones are supposed to be friendly (they are, to some extent) but if you want to pet them be prepared to get head butted in the behind. You can buy snacks to feed them and they oh-so-cutely bow down before nearly biting your hand off. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-178.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-180.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-182.jpg)

There is lot more to this place than feeding the deer, of course. The **Todaiji-temple** is the main attraction here but you can't miss the **Isuien Garden**. It's like taking a step back in time. The garden is traditionally maintained and on a hot day, it's all the solace you will need. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-184.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-186.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-188.jpg)

Final strolls in Kyoto: Kyoto National Garden, Sensoi Palace, and hidden streams. Capture last glimpses of the **Japanese cherry blossom season**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-190.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-192.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-194.jpg)

As we walked back to our room to leave, we happened to stumble onto this jaw-dropping stream. It was the fitting finale to our epic trip. This is truly what Japan was all about - the soul stirring beauty when you least expect to find it. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-196.jpeg)

Japan is easy to fall in love with: astounding cherry blossoms, quirky innovations, delicious food, and some of the kindest people in the world. Whether exploring Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, or Mount Fuji, this **Japan travel guide** ensures you experience the best of the country.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-197.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-199.jpeg)

Remember, the **best time to visit Japan** is during the fleeting cherry blossom season. Japan is a country of extreme contrasts—ancient history, Zen temples, mind-boggling cities, and spiritual practices. **Japan safety tips for tourists**: respect local customs and enjoy responsibly.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-201.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-203.jpg)

I love so many things about Japan but most of all, I love that this a country of extreme contradictions. From the ancient history to zen temples to mad, mind-boggling quirky cities with insane tech - the country is beyond fascinating. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-205.jpg)

Want to learn how to earn respect without just taking it for granted? Learn from the Japanese. Probably the only bunch of people I have been absolutely blown away by. Nothing but massive respect. I am literally counting down days until we get to visit this place again. The way everyone and everything is hyper-organized, the people, the delicious food, the tech and of course, their humility... but what I love most is their spirituality. I am not religious and don’t believe in the idea of one superior being. Japan’s unique belief system resonated with me like no other country. I recently read that in Japan no one preaches their belief - it’s simply a way of life. The emphasis is on personal practice and believing in moral principles free of judgement. The best and most important aspect of “Buddhism” is not what you know but about how you practice. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-206.jpg)

Since this post was all about the blossoms, here are a **few fun facts** before I end the post. When people think of cherry blossoms in Japan, they assume that there is just one type of spring blossoms.. BUT there are actually over 20 different subspecies. For instance, the most common type of Sakura in Japan is the Somei yoshino or the Yoshino cherry. These five-petaled cherry blossoms are almost white, having only the vaguest hint of pink to them. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-207.jpg)

The name somei yoshino comes from a village market that existed during the late Edo era, where it is said that these flowers were first sold. Now that I have seen this in person, it’s going to be impossible to think of spring without reminiscing about our time in Japan - with its sea of cherry trees and the perfect pink blooms. 

**Domo arigato, you ****beautiful beautiful ****country. **

![Blog Image](/blog-images/planning-a-trip-to-japan-during-cherry-blossom-japantravelguide-210.jpg)

Japan is a country where beauty reigns in every corner. Looking at our pictures from Japan never fails to lift my spirits. This Japan travel** guide** will take you through our unforgettable nine-day journey across Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, and Mount Fuji. This country floored me—it’s ridiculously crowded yet amazingly organized, minimal yet extravagant, modern yet rooted in tradition, fragile yet wild.

No matter where you are, you’ll never be too far from amazing food, sake, and pure zen. Japan is truly an out-of-this-world, fascinating, mind-bogglingly unique, and inspiring country in every sense of the word.
