Prague Itinerary 1 Day: Castles, Bridges & Christmas Magic
For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to Europe. We prefer to go during the off season to avoid the crowds. This time, we...

For our fifth wedding anniversary, we decided to head to Europe. We prefer to go during the off season to avoid the crowds. This time, we covered three countries — Czech Republic, Slovakia, and Slovenia.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-1.jpeg)

We started our journey in the fairy-tale capital city, **Prague**. Since we are not big on exploring cities, we spent just one day here. Believe it or not, a **Prague itinerary 1 day** is easily doable if you get organized and prioritize what you want to see. This guide covers the best attractions like **Prague Castle, Charles Bridge, Wenceslas Square, Dancing House Prague**, and even tips on enjoying the **Prague Christmas markets** — with little pointers on food for vegetarians.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-2.jpeg)


### **Must-See Places in One Day in Prague**

- Visit neo-gothic Namasti Miru
Visit neo-gothic Namasti Miru

- Explore **Wenceslas Square** and the National Museum
Explore **Wenceslas Square** and the National Museum

- Admire the Astronomical Clock Tower in Old Town
Admire the Astronomical Clock Tower in Old Town

- Step inside one quirky museum (there are plenty!)
Step inside one quirky museum (there are plenty!)

- Spot Prague’s quirky statues scattered around the city
Spot Prague’s quirky statues scattered around the city

- Play “spot the vintage cars” in the streets
Play “spot the vintage cars” in the streets

- Visit the beautiful St. Nicholas Church
Visit the beautiful St. Nicholas Church

- Stroll across the iconic **Charles Bridge**
Stroll across the iconic **Charles Bridge**

- Hike up to the world-famous **Prague Castle**
Hike up to the world-famous **Prague Castle**

- Relax at Kampa Garden for uninterrupted views of Charles Bridge
Relax at Kampa Garden for uninterrupted views of Charles Bridge

- Stop by the Insta-famous **Dancing House Prague**
Stop by the Insta-famous **Dancing House Prague**

Soak in the festive vibes at the **Prague Christmas markets**

Summer offers pleasant weather, but the city can get crowded. Winters are extreme, with snow and temperatures dipping below freezing. That being said, visiting Europe in the winter is magical — and Prague is no exception.

If you haven’t yet experienced a snowy European Christmas, I highly recommend visiting during December. The **Prague Christmas markets** bring the whole city to life with twinkling lights, mulled wine, and festive cheer. It’s honestly the **best time to visit Prague** if you want a truly unforgettable vibe.

When we visited, we had just one night of snow in Prague, but the temperatures stayed well below freezing at night.

We flew with [Smart Wings](https://www.expedia.com/) — short, direct flights are available all week. As with most Central European countries, you’ll need a **Schengen Visa** for entry into the Czech Republic. Immigration was simple and quick.

The official language is Czech. German is the second most common, followed by Russian. In tourist areas, you’ll find plenty of English speakers, so communication is not a big issue.

Travel and medical insurance is mandatory when traveling to all Schengen countries.

Currency in Prague is the Czech Crown (CZK). While some large restaurants accept Euro, most do not. Big hotels and restaurants accept cards, but smaller cafés and Christmas market stalls often prefer cash.

We stayed at the [Ametyst Hotel](https://www.booking.com/hotel/cz/ametyst.en-gb.html?aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go%20Travel%20Un%20Limited_clkid-9fa118209d6d4a4c9d332d4f1-437527_cjevent-0f8577bd993311f0831500650a82b836&sid=f08c842dbab42a93655ecaba13793263&dest_id=-553173&dest_type=city&dist=0&group_adults=2&group_children=0&hapos=1&hpos=1&no_rooms=1&req_adults=2&req_children=0&room1=A%2CA&sb_price_type=total&sr_order=popularity&srepoch=1709011319&srpvid=4be325b73ad5010b&type=total&ucfs=1&#hotelTmpl) in New Town. The rooms were spacious for European standards and offered a beautiful city view. The staff were friendly and helpful, and the location was excellent — just a 15-minute walk to major attractions like **Wenceslas Square**.

The breakfast was repetitive but had a good spread (including vegetarian options).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-3.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-5.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/__5yy-7.jpeg)

Tip: There’s a grocery store next to the hotel — very handy after a long day exploring.

If you’re planning your trip, here are the best areas for **where to stay in Prague**:

- **Old Town:** Close to **Charles Bridge** and Astronomical Clock.
**Old Town:** Close to **Charles Bridge** and Astronomical Clock.

- **New Town:** Near **Dancing House Prague** and **Wenceslas Square**.
**New Town:** Near **Dancing House Prague** and **Wenceslas Square**.

- **Lesser Town:** Perfect if you want romantic stays near **Prague Castle**.
**Lesser Town:** Perfect if you want romantic stays near **Prague Castle**.

Prague is slightly more expensive than Eastern Europe but more affordable than Western Europe. Hotels are reasonably priced in the off season. Meals average €10–15 per person.

Cabs are pricey, but public transport (trams, metros, buses) is efficient and budget-friendly.

It’s easy to book a [taxi](https://tour.kiwitaxi.com/prague/?tpo=b8629cf7fda04e52a138408d7-437527&utm_source=travelpayouts), but always do it in advance. Street taxis can overcharge. Apps like Bolt are more reliable.

We felt safe throughout our stay. Locals were warm and helpful, unlike in some Western European cities where petty theft is more common.


### **Vegetarian Food in Prague**

If you’re vegetarian, prepare for a carb-heavy trip. Soups were often thin, and many vegetarian dishes lacked variety. Still, we managed to try some local favorites.

At the **Prague Christmas markets**, you can grab:

- **Trdelník** (chimney cake with cinnamon sugar and toppings)
**Trdelník** (chimney cake with cinnamon sugar and toppings)

- Baby potatoes with onions and spices (surprisingly filling)
Baby potatoes with onions and spices (surprisingly filling)

- Potato pancakes with cheese
Potato pancakes with cheese

For longer stays, plan ahead by searching for **vegetarian food in Prague** or look for international restaurants.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-9.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-11.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-13.jpeg)

Prague is divided into four main areas: Old Town, New Town, **Prague Castle**, and Lesser Town. To make the most of your **Prague walking tour**, start with the area closest to your hotel and work your way through.

- **Namasti Miru Church:** A neo-gothic Church of St. Ludmila built from brick, with a cross-shaped transept.
**Namasti Miru Church:** A neo-gothic Church of St. Ludmila built from brick, with a cross-shaped transept.

- **Highlights of Our One Day Prague Walking Tour**Prague is divided into four main areas: Old Town, New Town, **Prague Castle**, and Lesser Town. To make the most of your **Prague walking tour**, start with the area closest to your hotel and work your way through.
**Highlights of Our One Day Prague Walking Tour**

Prague is divided into four main areas: Old Town, New Town, **Prague Castle**, and Lesser Town. To make the most of your **Prague walking tour**, start with the area closest to your hotel and work your way through.

- **Namasti Miru Church:** A neo-gothic Church of St. Ludmila built from brick, with a cross-shaped transept.
**Namasti Miru Church:** A neo-gothic Church of St. Ludmila built from brick, with a cross-shaped transept.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-15.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-17.jpg)

**Wenceslas Square:** A historic site where protests and declarations took place. You’ll also find the National Museum here.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-19.jpeg)

**St. Nicholas Church:** A baroque masterpiece worth a quick stop.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-20.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-22.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-24.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-26.jpeg)

**Quirky Museums & Vintage Cars:** From toy museums to torture exhibits, Prague has something strange at every corner. You’ll also spot colorful vintage cars parked along the streets.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-28.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-30.jpeg)

**Charles Bridge:** Walk parallel to the bridge first to capture it in full, then cross later for the experience. Go before dawn for a quiet stroll.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-32.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-34.jpeg)

**Prague Castle:** A must-see! The climb includes 70–80 steep steps, but the panoramic views are worth it.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-36.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-38.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-40.jpeg)

**Kampa Garden:** For crowd-free views of Charles Bridge, this is the spot. In summer, bring a picnic.

**Dancing House Prague:** While the building itself was underwhelming, the riverside views nearby were beautiful. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-42.jpeg)

..because let's face it, if you head to the Charles bridge in the afternoon - this is what you will get. If you want to enjoy views from the bridge without anyone around, go before dawn.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-43.jpeg)

Our last stop was a visit to the now insta-famous dancing house. The house itself was a disappointment but the views from around here were surreal. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-46.jpeg)

We spent the rest of the afternoon exploring hidden alleys and other quaint neighborhoods in the city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-48.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-50.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-52.jpeg)

**Prague Christmas Markets:** End your day here. Skip the crowded city center markets and head to the cozy one near Namasti Miru.

Though Prague reminded me of Copenhagen in some ways, it had its own soul — from the cacophony of music at the **Prague Christmas markets**, to the screech of the old trams, and the taste of cinnamon-loaded Trdelník.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-cover.jpeg)

If you only have one day, this **Prague itinerary 1 day** will give you the best of the city: Gothic spires, quirky statues, cobbled alleys, festive markets, and world icons like **Charles Bridge** and **Prague Castle**.

Prague is truly a city that stays with you — whether you come for history, romance, or the magic of winter.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/prague-in-24-hours-all-the-must-see-and-fun-spots-around-townpraguecastle-54.jpeg)

Sri Lanka Itinerary – A 6 Day Journey Through Culture, Hills & Beaches
December 2016 For our third wedding anniversary, we decided to take a relaxing vacation to Sri Lanka. We spent 6 days going around the...

**December 2016**

For our third wedding anniversary, we decided to take a relaxing vacation to Sri Lanka. We spent 6 days going around the country.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-1.jpg)

This blog post is about what we did and a recommended **Sri Lanka itinerary** to experience much of the country in under a week. If you have longer, you can do more off-beat things.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-2.jpeg)


### **TABLE OF CONTENTS**

[FAQ](#viewer-4a9qb)

[Vegan + Vegetarian Food ](#viewer-1ve50)

**Day 1** – Arrival – Pinnawala Elephant Orphanage – Golden Cave Temple Dambulla – Relax at resort

**Day 2** – Hike the Sigiriya Fortress – Relax at resort

**Day 3** – Drive to Kandy – en route Botanical Garden and Spice Garden – Tooth Relic Temple –   Kandy Lake – Cultural Show

**Day 4** – Drive to Nuwara Eliya – Horton Plains Hike

**Day 5** – Drive to Bentota Beach

**Day 6** – Colombo City – Gangarama Temple

The weather cools down from November to March. We enjoyed amazing weather when we visited during the second week of November. 👉 Check updated weather and travel info on the[ ](https://www.srilanka.travel/)[Sri Lanka Tourism official site](https://www.srilanka.travel/).

We flew with[ ](https://www.omanair.com/)[Oman Air](https://www.omanair.com/) – short, one-stop flights all through the week.

For the visa – apply online on the official Sri Lanka government [website.](https://www.eta.gov.lk/slvisa/visainfo/center.jsp?locale=en_US) Pay $20 per person and you will get an email with the visa in less than 24 hours.


Sinhalese is the official language. People also speak English, and you should have no problem communicating in major tourist spots.

As always, best to have medical and travel insurance. Life doesn't stop happening even when you are on vacation.

Credit card machines don’t work everywhere. Carry local currency in cash.


This is definitely a country you can do on your own or with a trusted local driver. I usually find the best guides on[ ](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g293961-Activities-Sri_Lanka.html)[TripAdvisor](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attractions-g293961-Activities-Sri_Lanka.html).

You can also rent a car to drive in Sri Lanka – roads aren’t great, but if you are used to a bit of rough driving, you’ll be fine.

We had no issues with safety. Everyone we met was warm and helpful.


As always, we wanted to try the local food – and requested vegetarian versions whenever possible. As expected on a tropical island, there was a lot of coconut and fish. Still, buffets had plenty of vegetarian options – like an authentic variety of spicy chutneys.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-3.jpg)


### **Elephant Orphanage**

We landed in Colombo, quickly did our visas, and exited the airport. Our first stop was the Pinnawela Elephant Orphanage. Established in 1975, it started with seven orphan elephants. Today, some of these orphans have seen their grandchildren born in the same location


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-4.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-6.jpeg)

It was beautiful watching the elephants being fed.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-8.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-10.jpeg)

After lunch at a local joint, we visited the Golden Temple in Dambulla, the largest and best-preserved cave-temple complex in Sri Lanka. With more than 150 Buddha statues and centuries of murals, it was breathtaking.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-12.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-14.jpeg)

This temple actually sits on a small hill, so while the climb up can be a little taxing, the views more than make up for it. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-16.jpeg)

Pro Tip: Mind the monkeys!

We stayed at [The Paradise Resort](https://www.brownshotels.com/paradise/) in Dambulla – serene, secluded, with a stellar pool and recreation facilities.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-17.jpg)

After a healthy breakfast, we headed to the Sigiriya Rock Fortress, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-18.jpg)

Built by King Kashyapa, this “Lion Rock” rises 200 meters from the jungle.

The climb was steep, but the panoramic views from the top were worth it.

We ended the day biking to a small lake nearby for sunset and enjoying a delicious Italian meal at the resort.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-19.jpg)

The rock was the innermost stronghold of the 70 hectare fortified town. It was hot day and for us to visit the sheltered pocket of the rock, we had to make our way through a spiral stairway. We were told once we reach the top, the views were unmatched. So ho ahead and make the climb - watch the lush green kingdom engulf you in it's history.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-20.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-22.jpg)

We spent the rest of the afternoon relaxing in our room. We rode our bikes to a small lake nearby and watched the sunset. We then enjoyed a delicious Italian meal at the restaurant. Would highly recommend the Vegetarian Pizza and Pasta.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-24.jpg)

The next day, we drove to Nuwara Eliya, stopping by a tea plantation to learn about the process of making pure Ceylon tea.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-27.jpg)

We then visited **Horton Plains**, with its famous “World’s End” cliff and Baker’s Falls. This was a peaceful and scenic hike, one of the highlights of our trip.

Stay: The Grand Hotel – a colonial gem with the best breakfast spread.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-29.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-12.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-33.jpeg)

**Pro Tip**: If you have the time, I would also recommend visiting a theater to witness a cultural show. 

Our over night stay was at [Ozo Kandy](http://www.ozohotels.com/kandy-srilanka/). This was a trendy contemporary hotel with stunning views of the river and the city. The food and buffet was delicious as well.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-35.jpg)

Early start to the day because we had to drive all the way up to Nuwara Eliya. En-route we stopped by a small tea plantation for pictures. You can all learn about the process of manufacturing tea. You can also see how tea is graded and taste a cup of pure Ceylon tea in the factory.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-36.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-40.jpg)

As you drive past the endless green pastures of land, you know you are already in **Nuwara Eliya**, Sri Lanka's most popular hill resort. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-42.jpeg)

After we checked in to our hotel, we made our way to what I call "poor man's New Zealand" - the Horton Plains. This vast area is located on a high windswept saddle at about 6,900 to 7,500 ft above the sea level. The plains are an expanse of misty grassland with scraggy trees and ice-cold rivulets.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-43.jpeg)

The unique feature of Horton Plains is the **"World's End"**. It is considered to be the finest view in all of Sri Lanka. The awesome escarpment drops vertically for about 4,000 feet, hence the name.  When you are inside the Horton Plains, also include the picturesque **Bakers falls **on your list of things to do (it is about a 15 km walk). There will be clear signs for both the World Ends and the Baker Falls - just follow the trail and you will be just fine. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-44.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-46.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-48.jpeg)

This place was one of the highlights of our trip. We fell in love with the calmness of the [place.](https://chatgpt.com/c/68c2f170-b678-8321-995a-3049aa1a90fd) It was mostly empty, so we took our time, explored the entire length of the path and clicked tons of pictures. If you are heading to Nuwara, you have to visit this place. Nature in all it's glory! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-50.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-52.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-54.jpg)

We stayed at the [Grand Hotel](http://grandhotel.tangerinehotels.com/) for the night. This place had the best breakfast spread - massive and YUM. We loved staying here, the ancient British feel of the rooms, even the chilly weather did not bother us. Could not recommend it enough. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-56.jpg)

From the hills to the coast – we stayed at Vivanta by Taj, Bentota, for beach access and sunset views. It was the perfect relaxing end to the trip.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-59.jpg)

We stayed at the Vivanta by Taj for the exclusive beach access with the most amazing sunset views. Also, you have to try the **restaurant buffet** - worth every penny! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-61.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-65.jpg)

Needless to say, it was a flawless end to our[ vacation. ](https://chatgpt.com/c/68c2f170-b678-8321-995a-3049aa1a90fd)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-cover.jpg)

On our last day, we explored Colombo – [visiting](https://chatgpt.com/c/68c2f170-b678-8321-995a-3049aa1a90fd) the **Gangarama Temple**, strolling clean boulevards, and enjoying a final Sri Lankan meal before flying out.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-68.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-70.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-72.jpg)

We spent the afternoon at a local mall, soaking in the festive vibes, caught up with few friends and boarded our flight back to Dubai at 7 p.m.


One week will give you plenty of time to experience the different landscapes within the country - we wanted to cover the city, the hills and the beach. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-74.jpg)

If we had done this trip on our own, we would have also visited the **Nine Arch Bridge**. Perhaps a good reason to head back soon. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/srilankaitinerary-75.jpg)

Feel free to leave your thoughts in comments. 

Love,
Anki

Chobe Safari Lodge Review: A Perfect Stay in Botswana
A Memorable Stay at Chobe Safari Lodge For our third safari leg in Botswana, we picked the Chobe Safari Lodge  for our stay. This...

### **A Memorable Stay at Chobe Safari Lodge**

For our third safari leg in Botswana, we picked the [Chobe Safari Lodge](https://www.chobesafarilodge.com/) for our stay. This property is located in Kasane on the banks of the Chobe River, home to the largest elephant population in the world. One of the largest rivers in this region, it shares a border with Chobe National Park. At the lodge’s entrance, you’ll also find the meeting point of four African countries: Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, and Zambia. Isn’t that exciting?


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-11.jpg)

This is a huge property with traditional thatched roofs, set among the lush greenery of the river. The best part is its location—within just 10 minutes of [Chobe National Park](https://www.safaribookings.com/chobe). Since it’s close to the road border to Zimbabwe as well as the airport, **Chobe Safari Lodge** is an excellent place to stay if you are combining two or more countries during your trip.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-4.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-9.jpg)

As soon as we entered the lodge, we were welcomed by a massive reception area with a dining space, a bar, and a lounge decorated with hints of African architecture, from the cushions to the wall art. The high ceilings and wooden decor give the space a relaxed and inviting vibe.

On the lower level is a swimming pool surrounded by sun loungers where you can relax between activities. A terrace leads down to the boating station, offering incredible views across the river to Namibia.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-10.jpg)

We particularly loved the sunset from the pool area—with Pumba for company.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-cover.jpg)

We particularly loved the sunset from the pool area.. with Pumba for company. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-16.jpg)

The River Rooms are big, comfortable, and offer outstanding views of the Chobe River. All rooms come equipped with TV, WiFi, and air conditioning. We loved the light ethnic colors and the wide garden space just outside.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-7.jpg)

**Chobe National Park** is home to hundreds of animal species and over 3,000 species of birds. It’s truly a paradise for photographers and wildlife lovers. Since the lodge sits right on the river, you can even be picked up for a **Chobe River boat safari** directly from the property. This was, without doubt, the highlight of our trip to Botswana.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-21.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-58.jpg)

If you haven’t pre-booked activities, don’t worry—**Chobe Safari Lodge** offers plenty of options, including traditional boat cruises and land safaris in a 4x4 vehicle into the national park. The concierge team are always available to help plan and guide you.

Planning to hop across borders to Zimbabwe or Namibia? The lodge can arrange that too.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/a-trip-to-the-elephant-capital-of-the-world-botswana-2.jpg)


### **Breakfast**

The breakfast spread was surprisingly large, with a full English breakfast, plenty of meat options, and a variety of vegetarian choices for us. From hot beans, toast, and cereal to fresh fruits and flavored yogurt, the buffet had something for everyone.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-29.jpg)

For dinner, we often opted for the buffet, priced at around $27 per person. It featured salads, soup, a live noodle station, seafood, local delicacies, Indian curries, and indulgent desserts.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-30.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-32.jpg)

One of the highlights was watching local dancers from nearby villages perform while we enjoyed our meal—a truly [unforgettable experience.](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Hotel_Review-g297318-d477352-Reviews-Chobe_Safari_Lodge-Kasane_Chobe_National_Park_North_West_District.html)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-34.jpg)

We loved the proximity of the resort to **Chobe National Park**, which made multiple safaris easy to plan in a single day. After long game drives, this lodge was the perfect spot for a sundowner—watching the sunset, drink in hand, with amazing company.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-37.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-chobe-safari-chobesafarilodge-39.jpg)

Botswana is often considered an expensive[ safari](https://www.getyourguide.com/chobe-national-park-l1644/) destination, given its strong focus on sustainability and conservation. That’s why **Chobe Safari Lodge** stands out—it offers excellent value for money without compromising on comfort, safety, or game drive opportunities, both on land and on the river.

If you’re looking for a well-located, value-for-money **safari lodge in **[Botswana](https://www.botswanatourism.co.bw/), this property is the ideal choice.

A Memorable Stay at Chobe Safari Lodge

For our third safari leg in Botswana, we picked the Chobe Safari Lodge for our stay. This property is located in Kasane on the banks of the Chobe River, home to the largest elephant population in the world. One of the largest rivers in this region, it shares a border with Chobe National Park. At the lodge’s entrance, you’ll also find the meeting point of four African countries: Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, and Zambia. Isn’t that exciting?

This is a huge property with traditional thatched roofs, set among the lush greenery of the river. The best part is its location—within just 10 minutes of Chobe National Park. Since it’s close to the road border to Zimbabwe as well as the airport, Chobe Safari Lodge is an excellent place to stay if you are combining two or more countries during your trip.

As soon as we entered the lodge, we were welcomed by a massive reception area with a dining space, a bar, and a lounge decorated with hints of African architecture, from the cushions to the wall art. The high ceilings and wooden decor give the space a relaxed and inviting vibe.

On the lower level is a swimming pool surrounded by sun loungers where you can relax between activities. A terrace leads down to the boating station, offering incredible views across the river to Namibia.

We particularly loved the sunset from the pool area—with Pumba for company.

We particularly loved the sunset from the pool area.. with Pumba for company.

The River Rooms are big, comfortable, and offer outstanding views of the Chobe River. All rooms come equipped with TV, WiFi, and air conditioning. We loved the light ethnic colors and the wide garden space just outside.

Chobe National Park is home to hundreds of animal species and over 3,000 species of birds. It’s truly a paradise for photographers and wildlife lovers. Since the lodge sits right on the river, you can even be picked up for a Chobe River boat safari directly from the property. This was, without doubt, the highlight of our trip to Botswana.

If you haven’t pre-booked activities, don’t worry—Chobe Safari Lodge offers plenty of options, including traditional boat cruises and land safaris in a 4x4 vehicle into the national park. The concierge team are always available to help plan and guide you.

Planning to hop across borders to Zimbabwe or Namibia? The lodge can arrange that too.

Breakfast

The breakfast spread was surprisingly large, with a full English breakfast, plenty of meat options, and a variety of vegetarian choices for us. From hot beans, toast, and cereal to fresh fruits and flavored yogurt, the buffet had something for everyone.

For dinner, we often opted for the buffet, priced at around $27 per person. It featured salads, soup, a live noodle station, seafood, local delicacies, Indian curries, and indulgent desserts.

One of the highlights was watching local dancers from nearby villages perform while we enjoyed our meal—a truly unforgettable experience.

We loved the proximity of the resort to Chobe National Park, which made multiple safaris easy to plan in a single day. After long game drives, this lodge was the perfect spot for a sundowner—watching the sunset, drink in hand, with amazing company.

Botswana is often considered an expensive safari destination, given its strong focus on sustainability and conservation. That’s why Chobe Safari Lodge stands out—it offers excellent value for money without compromising on comfort, safety, or game drive opportunities, both on land and on the river.

If you’re looking for a well-located, value-for-money **safari lodge in **Botswana, this property is the ideal choice.

Victoria Falls Hotel Review: Ilala Lodge & Palm River Hotel
A Stay at Two Iconic Victoria Falls Hotels For our trip to Zimbabwe, we stayed at Ilala Lodge  and Palm River Hotel , two sister...

### **A Stay at Two Iconic Victoria Falls Hotels**

For our trip to Zimbabwe, we stayed at **Ilala Lodge** and **Palm River Hotel**, two sister properties situated on the banks of the mighty Victoria Falls. We were so excited to be back in this region, which was hands down one of the highlights of last year.

Both properties showcase the perfect balance of comfort, location, and authentic Zimbabwean hospitality, making them an ideal choice if you’re searching for the **best Victoria Falls hotel** for your stay.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-cover.jpg)


### **Ilala Lodge – Love at First Sight**

The Ilala Lodge hotel was love at first sight. A modern resort with the right balance between comfort and old-world charm, this family-run boutique hotel is brimming with class and elegance.

** Location, Location, Location!**

When it comes to visiting popular attractions, we prefer staying close to the action. One of the best reasons to stay here is that the lodge is within walking distance from the **Victoria Falls** and the city center. You can easily book activities, grab a sundowner, or take a stroll for sunrise or sunset.

This convenient location is what makes Ilala Lodge stand out as a top **Victoria Falls hotel**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-4.jpg)

**Lounge and Common Areas**

The shared spaces are designed with exquisite taste and elegance. Think fine dark wood, tall ceilings, ornate chandeliers, and crisp linen. The entire setting carries a colonial-era charm, giving you the sense of stepping into a heritage retreat.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-6.jpg)

** Rooms at Ilala Lodge**

There are **73 rooms and suites** at this property, catering to solo travelers, couples, and families.


### **Standard & Deluxe Rooms**

The rooms are spacious and come with modern amenities, including a fully stocked mini-bar. The highlight? Every guest gets a slice of the view—right from the balcony, you can see the world’s most spectacular natural wonder.

Our **Deluxe Room** was tastefully furnished with white linens, tapestry, dark wooden furniture, and a ridiculously comfortable bed. The bathroom was just as spacious, featuring an open shower and sustainable bath products.

Extra perks like a coffee machine, mini-bar, and fresh baked treats (all included in the rate) were especially welcome after a warm day exploring the Falls.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-9.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-11.jpg)

**Dining & Bar**

The dining area was one of my favorite spaces. Styled with impeccable finesse, it offers both indoor and outdoor seating, with unbeatable views of the **Victoria Falls** any time of day.

You can enjoy continental as well as local options from their à la carte menu, or pop over to the bar for a sundowner by the pool.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-15.jpg)


### **Breakfast with a View**

Breakfast was a real highlight. We loved the variety, with plenty of vegan and vegetarian options, alongside a grand buffet. Everything was beautifully presented and served quickly.

Arrive early if you’d like a scenic spot overlooking the Falls—it’s the best way to start your day at a **Victoria Falls hotel**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-21.jpg)


### **Service That Feels Personal**

Though boutique in size, the service standards here rival those of a luxury resort. The ever-smiling staff go above and beyond to ensure everything you need is taken care of. It’s the small, thoughtful touches that made us feel like royalty throughout our stay.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-23.jpg)

**Things to Do Around Victoria Falls**

When in Victoria Falls, there’s no shortage of activities—whether you prefer adrenaline-pumping adventures or relaxed sightseeing. Some must-dos include:

- 🌅 Sunset river cruise
🌅 Sunset river cruise

- 💦 Devil’s Pool experience
💦 Devil’s Pool experience

- 🚶 Walking tour inside Victoria Falls National Park
🚶 Walking tour inside Victoria Falls National Park

- 🌊 White-water rafting
🌊 White-water rafting

- ⚡ Ziplining across the gorge
⚡ Ziplining across the gorge

- 🚁 Helicopter ride over the Falls
🚁 Helicopter ride over the Falls

The concierge team at Ilala Lodge and Palm River Hotel are excellent. They’ll not only book activities for you but also offer competitive rates and personalized recommendations.

**Palm River Hotel – A Modern Escape**

The Palm River Hotel complements Ilala Lodge with a more modern resort-style setting along the Zambezi River. Spacious rooms, manicured lawns, and a tranquil riverside vibe make it perfect for couples and families looking for a calm base just minutes from the Falls.

Together, these two properties represent the best of both worlds—a boutique **Victoria Falls hotel** with heritage charm (Ilala Lodge) and a modern riverside escape (Palm River Hotel).


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/staying-at-the-palm-river-hotel-zimbabwe-victoriafallshotel-26.jpg)


### **Final Thoughts**

Ilala Lodge and Palm River Hotel are shining examples of getting the basics right without going over the top. Both properties offer **amazing value for money**, considering their proximity to Victoria Falls, excellent food, warm service, and inviting spaces.

If you’re searching for the best **Victoria Falls hotel**, these two sister properties should be at the top of your list. They are the perfect base for exploring one of the world’s most magical natural wonders.

Explore Taiwan's vibrant cherry blossom season with our 6-day itinerary, featuring must-visit temples, bustling Taipei night markets.

Beyond thrilled to say that this country was one hell of a surprise. Not only is Taiwan colorful, diverse and extremely beautiful, it’s also very affordable and the incredibly friendly people will undoubtedly steal your heart. Visiting for the Sakura spring season felt like stepping into Oogway’s mystical little land. It was surreal, every corner like a dream. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-1.jpg)

As always, we skipped majority of the touristy spots and ventured off to the countryside. It’s no doubt why that Taiwan has made it to my favorites list.

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-7aa73)

For Indian passport holders with a valid US visa, you can just apply for a ROC permit and enter the country for FREE. If you don't have one, then you will need to apply for an embassy visa. 


Renting a car is cheap, if your plan is to cover places outside Taipei, highly recommended. We pre-booked and picked ours up at the airport. 
**Please note that you will need to have an**[ IDP (International Driving Permit)](https://services.india.gov.in/service/detail/apply-for-an-international-driving-permit)** to drive in Taiwan. **


You can easily pick one up for $5-8 at the airport. 


Traveling solo in Taiwan is a breeze, thanks to its efficient public transport and friendly locals. The country boasts one of the safest environments for solo travelers, making it an ideal destination for adventurers. Whether you're wandering through the bustling streets of Taipei or hiking in Alishan, you’ll always feel welcome and secure. My Taiwan itinerary can easily be adapted for a solo trip. 

Taiwan is a haven for vegan and vegetarian food lovers. From street food stalls to upscale restaurants, the options are endless. In Taipei, I indulged in delicious tofu dishes, veggie dumplings, and fresh fruit juices at Shilin Night Market. In Chiayi, local eateries served up scrumptious plant-based meals that were both healthy and satisfying.

**Pro tip: buy these vegan dumplings from a small stall just across the Taipei main station. SO good!**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-10.jpg)

Straddling the border of Hsinchu and Miaoli counties, Quanhua Temple is nestled into the mountainside.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-14.jpg)

What makes this holy site SO unique that we hiked up slippery stone steps to visit? The only Taoist temple on the mountain, Quanhua Temple circa 1897 is dedicated to the Jade Emperor. Each level of Quanhua Temple and every shrine along the hiking trails are all like gems in a crown. From lion heads to pure gold buddhas carvings, there is no telling what you may come across within the temple’s walls. We were the only ones there on this overcast day, the stillness and spirituality of this place is unparalleled. It was so special and worth the 2 hour drive from Taipei. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-16.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-12.jpg)

It is a bustling metropolis, but at night, it transforms into a dazzling dizzy of neon lights and street food aroma! 

We skipped the morning rush and explored at night. 

📌 Chiang Kai-shek Memorial Hall: This iconic landmark isn’t just for history buffs.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-22.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-24.jpg)

📌 Night Markets: Prepare to have your senses overloaded in the best way possible. From steaming buns to stinky tofu, the Shilin night market in particular is a foodie paradise.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-28.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-30.jpg)

📌 Longshan Temple: This historic temple is a dazzling display of traditional architecture, all lit up like a dream at night!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-32.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-36.jpg)

📌 Dalongdong Temple: this lesser-known gem boasts a dazzling dragon mural that comes alive at night!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-38.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-42.jpg)

An easy day trip from Taipei, this former Japanese gold mining town is full of life (and people!). It’s a really charming mountain town with a little bit of everything. You can spend the entire day making your way through Jiufen Old Street because it encapsulates the best of Taiwan in one small package: rich history, atmospheric lanes and teahouses, breathtaking views, and distinctly Taiwanese snacks at every turn! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-46.jpg)

It is also a haven for food lovers. As vegetarians, our options were limited but we found a wonderful vegan restaurant serving delicious local meals. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-44.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-50.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-52.jpg)

Since we have done the tea ceremonies in Japan, we didn’t do it in Taiwan but if you haven’t tried it before Jiufen is the best place to find quaint cafes offering authentic tea ceremony experiences. If you’re looking for the best day trip from Taipei, make sure it’s this one. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-54.jpg)

As a forest lover, I’ve discovered breathtaking forests in different corners of the world. While I certainly have my favorites, Alishan is in a league of its own. This enchanted forest was the sole reason I chose Taiwan for our April trip. What makes Alishan special? The misty cloud cover that swirls in and out of the trees throughout the day, no matter the season, is simply magical. It’s something you have to see to believe. It’s like walking into a dream.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-55.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-57.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-59.jpg)

When to visit: The best time to visit is late spring, from late March to early April. I can vouch for it – it’s brilliant! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-63.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-65.jpg)

How to get there: Many people do a day trip from Taipei, but I don’t recommend that. Drive or get to Chiayi County first and spend a few days in the area. Take a morning bus from Chiayi Main Station to the National Forest. It’s a 2.5-hour journey, and the drive is beautiful.

What to do: Ride the mini trains. There are numerous trails inside the park. If you do them all lke we did, you’ll need 8 hours! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-67.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-69.jpg)

Where to eat: There are plenty of restaurants at the entrance where you can grab a bite!

Add ons: if you have few extra days, add a day to explore the tea plantations in Fenqui. 

This was my 100th country and I couldn’t be happier with the choice. There aren’t many people who list Taiwan as their favorite country, but I can’t stop singing its praises to everyone I meet. If you go, you will be convinced too. Somehow it seems to be always overlooked in favor of more popular countries in the region!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/taiwan-itinerary-71.jpg)

**BUDGET + TIPS**

Traveling in Taiwan can be affordable with careful planning. Hostels and budget hotels offer great value, and street food is both delicious and inexpensive. Consider purchasing an EasyCard for seamless travel on public transport. Visiting during the Cherry Blossom season might be pricier, so book accommodations and transport in advance to secure the best deals.

### **Why We Chose Templation in Cambodia**

After a few budget stays in Vietnam, I wanted to treat myself during our **Cambodia** leg—and **Templation by MAADS** was just what we needed. This **5-star boutique property** near Angkor Wat blends luxury, nature, and Khmer culture, making it the perfect balance between relaxation and heritage.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-1.jpg)


### **First Impressions & Check-In Experience**

A quick 20-minute drive from the airport brought us to this tranquil oasis. We were greeted with **cold towels, welcome drinks**, and a sweet treat while the staff prepped our room.

Covid safety was fully in place, and the staff's **kindness and care** stood out—especially when they checked in on me after I sprained my ankle.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-4.jpg)

The staff were so courteous, friendly and thoughtful; I sprained my leg during a hike and they kept checking on me to make sure I had everything I needed to rest my ankle. Our minds were at ease from the second we set foot here. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-6.jpg)


### **Location: 10 Minutes from Angkor Wat**

The resort's **proximity to Angkor Wat** was a game-changer. We woke at 4:45 a.m. and reached the temples by 5:00 a.m., skipping the long drives others endure. Trust me, with Cambodia’s heat, being close by matters!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-7.jpg)


### **Sustainability Practices**

Templation is part of the **MAADS group**, known for sustainability and supporting local communities. Eco-conscious decor, local staff, and minimal environmental footprint make it an ethical luxury option.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-12.jpg)


### **Entrance **

Another one of my favorite areas in the property was the waterfall pathway. As we made our way through the entrance, we first heard the waterfall before we saw it. Talk about a jungle oasis. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-16.jpg)

### **Final Thoughts**

Templation offers a **private luxury experience** that feels authentic and sustainable. Its peaceful ambiance, exceptional staff, and eco-friendly design made our Cambodia trip extra memorable.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-68.jpg)

The whole resort had a zen vibe about it. It was fantastic way for us to escape the tourist crowds at the temple, yet it was at just the right distance from the city center. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/templationangkorwatcambodia-69.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/cambodia-travel-angkor-wat-itinerary-guide-17.jpg)

**Pro tip:** Stay **at least 3 nights** to truly unwind and explore. We left feeling rested, cared for, and fully immersed in the Cambodian charm.

Africa Uganda: The Pearl of Africa Uncovered
Back from country number 41 and it was by far the most epic adventure we have had since Antarctica  in December, 2019. At the end of last...

Back from country number 41 and it was by far the most epic adventure we have had since [Antarctica](/copy-of-oceania) in December, 2019. At the end of last year, I promised myself that I would recommit my energy to supporting the wildlife and local communities in Africa. No better place to start than the wildest of off-grid places in [Africa](/africa). The untouched Pearl of Africa - Uganda. 

The Covid situation in Africa now has made traveling there a slight challenge - but I know I will be back in this continent the first opportunity I get to do so safely. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-cover.jpeg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-2.jpeg)

We chose Uganda for the simple reason that it was the safest, easiest option with all the Covid restrictions still in place. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-3.jpg)

It exceeded all our expectations. We were blown away by the endless rolling hills, the welcoming people and the lush green landscapes. When you think of Africa, people often mention wanting to see the safaris in Serengeti or [Masai Mara](/explore-destinations/4-beautiful-days-in-masai-mara-tips-to-plan-a-great-african-safari), or drive along the surreal [coast of Cape Town](/post/capetownsouthafrica) or even walk on the beaches in Mozambique. Unfortunately, Uganda doesn't often make it to the African "bucket list" but it should definitely be on there. It is rustic, authentic and beyond gorgeous. For the first time in many years, I sincerely wished we had more time to explore a single country. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-4.jpg)

Let's dive right into what you will need to plan a trip to this unique East African treasure!

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-6i37i)

Another reason we chose Uganda over Rwanda - direct flights with [Emirates](https://www.expedia.com/) from Dubai to Entebbe. There are some countries which are exempt from visas but citizens of most countries will need one to enter. Visa can be obtained on arrival for most nationalities. I chose to apply and pay for ours [online to save time at immigration](https://visas.immigration.go.ug/). Got ours emailed in less than 48 hours. Carry it with you. Straightforward process.  

You will be asked to show your **YELLOW FEVER VACCINE CARD** when you disembark at the airport. So get vaccinated, and if you already have the card, carry it on hand. This is **mandatory**. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-5.jpg)

I was pleasantly surprised to see the health measure put in place by the Ugandan government to

welcome tourists back safely. Everyone at the airport wore a mask - also found this to be the case in Entebbe and in busier towns. Our guide wore his the whole time as well.  

November to March is the best time to visit. Otherwise you can go between June to August. We went in mid February and it rained only for a few hours further down South in Bwindi. The climate was mostly warm and dry. Being on the equator, the weather here is pretty consistent but April to June you will get heavy rains, which would make the gorilla trekking and another activities a little tricky to enjoy. You don't want to go all the way only to find the trek being completely wiped out. 

Everyone was helpful, welcoming and fluent in English. 

Travel and medical insurance is always recommended. USD accepted at all resorts. Carry local currency if you plan to eat or shop in smaller stores. 

I get asked this after every trip. Honestly, duration of a trip depends entirely on your style of travel. We are **not** slow travelers - we enjoy shuffling our surroundings every other day. Most of the activities I had planned was in the South Western part of Uganda. I found that a week was just enough time to cover this side of the country. If you plan to head further up north to the Rhino Park or Murchison Falls, I would recommend spending close to two weeks. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-6.jpg)

Uganda is not a very big country by size.. but I have learnt to never underestimate the driving distances in Africa. If you follow our itinerary, be prepared for early starts and to stay in the car for 4 to 5 hours a day. Heads up, you CANNOT rush in Uganda - this place deserves time. Plan for a minimum of one week. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-7.jpg)

Since we already knew the places we wanted to cover, I put together our itinerary and contacted the tour agents who where officially registered with the Ugandan Tourism Board. All of them responded on time, with tons of information. I eventually decided to go with an amazing local guide. 

Absolutely loved them. The owner was flexible, always available and was able to offer us exactly what we wanted. Our driver was knowledgeable and always on time. His rates were by far the most competitive. He had the right connections at the resorts I had chosen. He was also able to arrange for a doctor to meet us for our tests. The entire trip was seamless from start to finish. Could not recommend them enough. 

If you are on a tight budget, you can also choose to rent a car and drive yourself. We don't prefer doing this in Africa. Having a local guide always helps. Kick back and observe the every day life of the Ugandan villages as you drive past them. 

**SOLO TRAVEL **

As with any country, abundance of safety is advised for travelers. Irrespective of your gender, things can always go wrong in a minute. I traveled with the hubs, so felt safe overall. Nevertheless, I would say Uganda is safe enough to backpack around for a solo traveler. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-incredible-untouched-pearl-of-africa-uganda-8.jpg)

- **Age limit - **this is not an easy trek by any means. You will be bitten by everything from giant mosquito to crawling red ants. There is also no telling how long the trek will take, it could take anywhere from an hour to five to find the gorillas. It is going to be exhausting irrespective of when you travel. I would not recommend taking any one less than the age of 13, even then it would be best if they have prior experience trekking long distances in challenging conditions. 

**Age limit - **this is not an easy trek by any means. You will be bitten by everything from giant mosquito to crawling red ants. There is also no telling how long the trek will take, it could take anywhere from an hour to five to find the gorillas. It is going to be exhausting irrespective of when you travel. I would not recommend taking any one less than the age of 13, even then it would be best if they have prior experience trekking long distances in challenging conditions. 


- **Is this trek**** dangerous****? How close can you get to the gorillas? Do they ****attack ****you****? **** Are they bothered by your presence?  **
The trek is about as dangerous as jumping out of an airplane to sky dive i.e. there are risks involved in every wild activity. You follow the rules to the T, no questions asked. Listen to your ranger and stick to the group at all times. Gorillas are incredibly gentle and mostly intelligent creatures. Yes, of course, there is always inherent danger when you approach the wild but there is no reason to be afraid. Don't panic if they get too close and DO NOT ATTEMPT TO RUN unless the ranger advises otherwise. We got real close to them and they didn't even blink an eye. Act mature and don't pretend to own the planet. Stay humble. Quietly observe their interactions and there is much you can learn from animals.
