  • Under 15 days: no visa fee (just a small processing fee) Under 15 days: no visa fee (just a small processing fee)

  • Over 15 days: approx $45 USD Over 15 days: approx $45 USD

  • Carry your yellow fever card just in case Carry your yellow fever card just in case

Language

French and Malagasy are official. English is rare. Download Google Translate with offline packs.

Best Time to Visit

To visit Tsingy and Morondava, go between mid-April to October. Other months are monsoon season and roads are impassable.

Currency

Carry Malagasy Ariary. Euro is accepted in cities, but avoid rural exchanges.

Guide or DIY?

Let’s be honest - this is not a budget-friendly destination. It’s more like Antarctica meets Africa. If you want a smooth ride, please consider hiring reliable local experts.

If you are serious about traveling to this country, and are looking for the guide information, feel free to email me with your contact details; I am happy to connect you to the ground team by Whatsapp. I’m sharing this because I’m a huge believer in supporting local guides. They are a hardworking local company not a giant agency, to help them manage their bookings and give you the best service, please include your confirmed travel dates and number of people when you reach out.

Estimated Cost: €1800-2000+ per person for 10–14 days (basic 3* stay, transport, internal flights, guides)

Estimated Trip Costs + Getting Around

A 10–14 day trip typically costs €1800+ per person, including basic 3-star accommodation with breakfast, transport, a guide, and return flights from Morondava. It’s more cost-effective to join a small group tour.

If you’re planning to visit Morondava or other islands, note that internal flights are required ensure these are included in your final quote from local operators.

Traveling with Young Kids**?**

Not recommended. Expect:

  • 15+ hour drives on rough roads 15+ hour drives on rough roads

  • Minimal facilities Minimal facilities

  • Heat, dust, giant insects Heat, dust, giant insects

This is best suited for experienced travelers used to rugged conditions.

We stayed in simple, convenient places since it was low season. In the capital, our stay at Boutique Hotel near the airport offered a comfy bed and a decent veggie breakfast—just what we needed after a long trip.

Most places were basic but clean. Our top stay Mantadia Lodge, Andasibe – 5-star views, vegan food, infinity pool, spa & stunning mountain views.

Highlights of the Trip

Antananarivo

Only stayed here to rest. It’s chaotic, but we found decent Indian food and boutique hotels.

Morondava

Avenue of Baobabs

Madagascar’s most iconic sight. We went three times:

  • Sunrise Sunrise

  • Sunset Sunset

  • Milky Way Milky Way

Each time offered a different kind of magic.

The moment we saw the towering trees lining the sandy roads, we were in awe. We loved it so much, we went back three times—for sunrise, sunset, and even the Milky Way. Hard to choose between dawn and dusk—both were magical.

As we walked to the village at dusk—fires crackling, goats returning, stars replacing the orange sky—I was struck by Madagascar’s wild beauty. It’s truly exotic. We visited the Avenue three times, and each moment was unforgettable. Take your time here—it’s worth it.

Kirindy Forest

Home to white Sifakas, shy Mouse Lemurs & night walks for nocturnal wildlife.

 A must for wildlife enthusiasts.

A major draw for wildlife lovers like us, these charismatic creatures amazed us with their dances, calls, and social behavior. We spent several days exploring national parks to see them up close.

Bekopaka – Tsingy National Park

A journey that tests your patience — 17 hours, 2 ferries, a bullock cart… but 100% worth it. 150-million-year-old limestone formations, steep hikes, rope bridges, and caves.

 Note: Wear proper shoes. And maybe don’t look down.

We met kids in remote villages, taught them frisbee, and shared our food. These moments hit hard — but are also the most heartwarming.

Before you get excited, get ready to hear about the arduous journey to get here. There are bad roads, and then there are roads from Morondava to Tsingy. Our journey took 17 hours. We started at 5 a.m. got on a bullock cart, got on two ferries and finally arrived at the hotel at around 1 a.m. There was one beautiful part in the journey though (swipe to the end) - we met an entire village of kids, shared our food and taught them to play frisbee for a few hours. For me, seeing kids like this is the hardest part of Africa travel, it’s gut wrenching. We often carry our frisbees on such trips and leave it behind, it’s not nearly enough but its us, trying in our own small way to bring a smile to these precious faces.

There are some wildlife experiences out there that are awe-inspiringly, blow-the-lights-out magnificent.While it’s easy to go D’aww, it’s also important to note that these species are threatened by habitat loss and deforestation. By visiting the communities and National Parks involved in protecting these species and by participating in reforestation activities when there, you can make a small but meaningful contribution and support the local conservation efforts. There’s a whole world out there, filled with weird and wonderful creatures, and observing any animal, no matter how great or small, in its ‘natural’ habitat is undeniably incredible. This the world that brings me the most joy.

There are over a 100 different species of Lemurs, found nowhere else on the planet. whilst planning a trip to incorporate them might need a little more organization - they’re worth every last memory card. Spending time with wildlife was on top of our agenda during our trip to Andasibe. This was also the most relaxing leg. We would spend hours each day in every park trying to find the shy lemurs hidden deep in the shadow of trees.

We found the** Indri, one of the largest living Lemurs** with a distinctive piercing-whalesong.

Golden Sifaka, known as dancing sifakas because of the way they move, skipping sideways

We then got very close to the shy black and white ruffed lemur having lunch..

Walked further to spot my favorites and the most cutest of the lot, the Bamboo Lemur

A mama and baby Sifaka spending time under the shade

The fourth largest island in the world is home to 5% of all known animal and plant species. There are over 150 species of Chameleon alone, imagine that.

We found Weiled and Parsons Chameleon perched precariously on branches completely camouflaged with their lime green coat.

As a David Attenborough admirer, finding the endemic Mantella frog with it’s carrot-colored skin, I was over the moon.

Madagascar is a wildlife wonderland. We planted trees in the reforestation center and donated to the privately owned NGO - given that most species I mentioned here are under threat of extinction, this trip felt more urgent, purposeful and like a force of good, there is no better reason to visit this country.

From wildlife, to diverse landscapes, towering mountains, lush rainforests, the land of baobabs and lemurs is packed with adventures for anyone up for a WILD ride. I also loved that towards the end of our trip there was a whole lot of downtime. Though it may not seem like it, our trips all about balance and not just the constant rush from place to place.

There is SO much to discover & it’s all still unexplored - just think, over 95% of the flora and fauna here cannot be found anywhere else in the world.

There are now more ways to explore the world than ever before. So if you’re looking for an adventure that’s a little different, this is the one to pick. The one thing we try to aim for on such trips is that our visit benefit local people and the planet - we got many opportunities to do just that in this country & we loved it.

Chances are, once you have ventured into something this extraordinary, you’ll be hooked for life!

Leave your thoughts and questions below. Thanks for reading.

Lots of love,

Anki
Welcome to the Youngest Country in the World: South Sudan Travel with the Mundari Tribe
Learn about the captivating rituals and customs of the Mundari people, one of the largest ethnic groups in South Sudan.

Welcome to **South Sudan**, the **youngest country in the world**—formed after a brutal 21-year battle with Sudan and finally gaining independence in 2011. My short yet powerful **South Sudan travel** experience revolved around visiting one of the most iconic communities in the region—the **Mundari tribe**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-cover.jpg)

As one of the largest ethnic groups in **South Sudan**, numbering around 750,000 people, the **Mundari South Sudan** community has preserved their fascinating cattle-centric traditions and **Mundari culture** for centuries. If you’re keen on **offbeat Africa travel**, meeting this **cattle herding tribe in Africa** is an unforgettable way to connect with the roots of the continent.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-4.jpg)

**Why the Mundari?**

I’ve been captivated by the **Mundari tribe** for years—their rich rituals, unique lifestyle, and deep connection with cattle. So finally being able to immerse myself in their daily lives was an experience I’ll always treasure. For those planning to **travel to South Sudan**, understanding the **Mundari culture** offers a rare glimpse into a resilient people whose way of life has endured despite adversity.

The **Mundari South Sudan** tribe gave me a window into a culture still thriving today. From their intricate body art and striking orange-dyed hairstyles to their shelters made from cow dung and urine, everything revolves around their sacred Ankole cattle. Their **cattle herding traditions in Africa** are among the most well-preserved on the continent.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-6.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-10.jpg)

**South Sudan Itinerary (3-Day Trip)**

**Day 1:** Arrive in **Juba South Sudan**, explore the capital, and drive to the **Mundari tribal area** by evening.

**Day 2:** Full day with the **Mundari tribe**, experiencing their routines and camping with them.

**Day 3:** Return to **Juba** and catch your outbound flight.

- [Final Thoughts](#viewer-ebg4l)
[Final Thoughts](#viewer-ebg4l)

**Flights + Visa**

I flew in with Ethiopian Airlines, though Fly Dubai is another convenient option.

 **Visa:** South Sudan now offers [e-visas](https://www.evisa.gov.ss/). Just upload your arrival date, yellow fever card, vaccination proof, and tour letter. The process costs $120 (single entry) and you can only apply 7 days before your arrival. Mine came through within hours. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-12.jpg)

** Language**

Given the heavy [UN presence](https://peacekeeping.un.org/en/mission/unmiss), English is widely understood, especially in **Juba South Sudan** and around tourist spots.

**Best Time to Visit**

**December to February** is ideal for **travel to South Sudan**, as these are the driest months—perfect for outdoor tribal experiences without rain interruptions.

. 

**Currency**

USD is accepted widely in **Juba**, but it’s helpful to exchange a small amount of local currency for tips and souvenirs.

**How Long to Spend**

My visit was a **long-weekend** trip, but if your budget allows, you can organize a **South Sudan itinerary** for 7–9 days to explore more tribes and offbeat locations across the country

**Where I Stayed**

I camped with the **Mundari tribe**, which is the most immersive way to experience **Mundari culture**. Dry season is the best time for this. In the rainy season, be prepared for roadblocks and muddy conditions.

** Costs + Local Guide**

Given that this is one of the **least visited countries in the world**, there’s little info on local guides. I recommend **Mayoum Bul from Metro Safaris**—a reliable, South Sudanese-Canadian guide who customizes trips safely and efficiently. Highly recommended!

**Solo Travel + Safety + Photography**

Traveling solo? You’ll feel safe with a guide. The locals are welcoming. However, if you're planning to bring camera gear, **apply for a photography permit** ahead of time especially for **Juba South Sudan**.

**Highlights of South Sudan**

** ****Juba South Sudan**

Overlooking the White Nile, **Juba South Sudan** blends ancient traditions with a fast-modernizing capital. At **Konyo Konyo Market**, I explored stalls filled with produce and handcrafted goods. Religious diversity was evident too—Sunday Mass at the Catholic Cathedral, and afternoon prayers at **Juba Grand Mosque**. I also visited **John Garang’s Mausoleum**, a poignant place honoring the man who led South Sudan to freedom.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-13.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-15.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-17.jpg)

**Mundari Tribe: The Cattle-Herding Warriors**

At dawn, I awoke to the sound of barking dogs and the deep bellows of cattle—the **Mundari tribe** was already in motion. These resilient people rely on their **Ankole cattle** for everything: milk, fuel (from dung), housing material, and spiritual connection. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-21.jpg)

Even **Mundari children** as young as five are entrusted with herding responsibilities.Their homes—built from dung, ash, and thornbush—are both eco-conscious and symbolic. The **ash** even serves as a natural mosquito repellent. By midday, I sat under a shelter watching **Mundari warriors** wrestle in traditional bouts, a display of strength, identity, and pride


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-23.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-27.jpg)

The cows are milked, then released to graze on the scrub grasslands under the careful watch of the boys. The dung is collected to build their mud and thornbush huts - the ash also acts as a mosquito-repellent for the cows. These cattle keep the Mundari nourished and housed, forming a symbiotic relationship.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-29.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-31.jpg)

At midday, the heavy rains drove the tribe and herd to seek shelter. The cattle provided a soothing breeze with their massive horns as the Mundari rested. I was offered fresh milk the cattle as gifts but I politely refused. The Mundari revere the cowds as gifts from the gods. As one elder told me, "If the cattle thrive, so do we." As the rain stopped, the cattle were guided back out to pasture in preparation for the evening's rituals. The Mundari are also fiere warriors and often engage in wrestling matches amongst themselves. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-35.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-37.jpg)

.Their warmth was astonishing. Even as a stranger, I was offered fresh milk and smiles. Their **duku hairstyles**, painted faces, and body markings all told stories of heritage and identity.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-39.jpg)

**Final Thoughts**

**Visiting South Sudan tribes** like the **Mundari tribe** is one of the most moving experiences I’ve ever had. Their lifestyle—rooted in ancestral wisdom—reflects the richness of **Mundari culture**, and their resilience offers hope that tradition can endure even in modern times.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-40.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-42.jpg)

From the **cattle herding rituals of Africa** to their distinctive appearance and community life, the **Mundari South Sudan** experience is unmatched.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-44.jpg)

If you're considering **offbeat Africa travel**, don't hesitate. **Travel to South Sudan** and witness the spirit of the Mundari for yourself. Their stories, rituals, and strength will stay with you long after you leave.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/the-traditions-of-the-mundari-tribe-in-south-sudan-45.jpg)

Thanks for reading. Leave your questions and comments below.

Lots of love,

Anki

Our trips are always about seeing the world differently. Going off the main tourist trail in Africa and discovering the non-touristy countries on this continent is one of my most favorite things to do. Period. Not surprisingly, it wasn’t Tunisia or Morocco but Algeria that caught this intrepid travelers attention. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-1.jpg)

Algeria is ridiculously under the radar. A country which offers some of the friendliest people in the world in places you wouldn’t expect, amazingly diverse and breathtakingly raw landscapes that you can hardly find any pictures of on the gram. I was totally sold. Just a stone’s throw from the borders of Libya and Niger, there is, of course, absolutely no way I can show you what spending six days in the splendor of Algeria’s Tadrart Rouge in the heart of the Sahara desert was like in just a short series. Still, it’s a start.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-4.jpg)

Now the biggest country in Africa, traveling through these fascinating parts of Algeria was eye-opening. The Sahara desert occupies four-fifth of the Algerian territory but if rustic camps for days are not your thing, then you still have picturesque coastal towns, historic Roman sites, and heritage of the Ottomans, French and Spanish. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-6.jpg)

It’s also completely off the beaten track compared to the Instagram high of Morocco and Tunisia. There is influence of African, Arab and Berber cultures - reflected in the music, architecture and cuisine. If you are eyeing an enriching travel experience in North Africa, this is a beautiful one to add to the list. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-7.jpg)

Exploring the geological wonders from rusty-red sandstones to the golden desert and then camping under a star-lit sky is what makes the Algerian Sahara that much more unique. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-104.jpg)

Algeria is the hidden gem in North Africa, overshadowed by Tunisia and Morocco. What we found was a place completely devoid of tourists. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-guide-to-discover-the-wonders-of-algeria-105.jpg)

Just towering sand dunes sculpted by the wind over millennia, stretched as far as the eye could see. The ever-shifting hues of the sand, from deep reds to golden oranges, created an otherworldly spectacle at sunrise and sunset. At night, the desert unveiled its true magic. We pitched a tent, just us two and slept beneath a blanket of stars, with constellations and galaxies twinkling as if they were just within reach.

It is a humbling experience, feeling so small in this vast, untamed landscape, yet also intimately connected to the natural world. Though not necessarily comfortable: there were sand storms, long hot days, long slugs up sand dunes and steep canyons. Not that we ever regret these real rustic adventures. We loved and lived every bit of it. It was a mind-bending trip!

Our trips are always about seeing the world differently. Going off the main tourist trail in Africa and discovering the non-touristy countries on this continent is one of my most favorite things to do. Period. Not surprisingly, it wasn’t Tunisia or Morocco but Algeria that caught this intrepid travelers attention.

Algeria is ridiculously under the radar. A country which offers some of the friendliest people in the world in places you wouldn’t expect, amazingly diverse and breathtakingly raw landscapes that you can hardly find any pictures of on the gram. I was totally sold. Just a stone’s throw from the borders of Libya and Niger, there is, of course, absolutely no way I can show you what spending six days in the splendor of Algeria’s Tadrart Rouge in the heart of the Sahara desert was like in just a short series. Still, it’s a start.

Now the biggest country in Africa, traveling through these fascinating parts of Algeria was eye-opening. The Sahara desert occupies four-fifth of the Algerian territory but if rustic camps for days are not your thing, then you still have picturesque coastal towns, historic Roman sites, and heritage of the Ottomans, French and Spanish.

It’s also completely off the beaten track compared to the Instagram high of Morocco and Tunisia. There is influence of African, Arab and Berber cultures - reflected in the music, architecture and cuisine. If you are eyeing an enriching travel experience in North Africa, this is a beautiful one to add to the list.

This weekend Riyadh guide is your map to unlocking the city's magic—from **must-see landmarks** to **hidden alleyways** where the real charm unfolds. It was a relaxed trip, just barely scratching the surface. We hope this **short Riyadh itinerary** will be helpful for your travel plans.

[Final Thoughts](#viewer-ebg4l)


We flew from **Dubai to Riyadh** with Flyadeal. The flight is basic but affordable—they often run amazing deals. For visas, we applied online using our **GCC residency**. You can also apply if you hold a valid **EU, UK, or US visa**. Immigration was seamless—only 5 minutes and no questions asked.

Unlike the UAE or Qatar, **English is not widely spoken** in Saudi Arabia. **Arabic is the main language**, though in touristy spots, you'll find some English speakers. Google Translate helped when needed.


The ideal time to visit Saudi Arabia is **November to March**. Cooler weather makes it perfect for exploring cultural sites and open-air spaces.

Always best to carry some **Saudi Riyals (SAR)**, especially for small purchases in souks or local markets.

. 

Even during winter, the **sun is intense**. Carry a **high-SPF sunscreen**—a must for any Middle East destination.

Riyadh is one of the largest cities in the region. You could easily spend **5–7 days** exploring various districts, historical forts, shopping avenues, and local restaurants.

We stayed at the **Radisson Blu Riyadh**—centrally located and perfect for a short trip. The **breakfast buffet** was excellent and they had great **vegetarian options** for à la carte dining.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-8.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-10.jpg)

Driving in Riyadh is intense! While **renting a car** is an option, we preferred using **Careem taxis**—affordable and convenient. Unless you're comfortable with aggressive city traffic, ride-hailing is the way to go.


Although I didn’t go solo this time, **Riyadh is safe** for solo travelers, including women. Just **dress conservatively**, but you don’t need to wear a hijab unless visiting a mosque. Always use common sense and stay in public areas.


### **HIGHLIGHTS: Top Things To Do Riyadh**

This 1865 clay-and-mud **fort** is where **modern Saudi Arabia** began.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-14.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-16.jpg)

 Outside, traditional **souqs** line the streets—great for souvenirs and street eats


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-18.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-20.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-22.jpg)

Definitely great for picking up street eats or trinkets. If you find Middle Eastern architecture interesting, then this is a place you shouldn’t miss!


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-cover.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-26.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-28.jpg)

 An absolute **must-visit in Riyadh**. This is the **birthplace of the Saudi state**, with preserved **18th-century mud-brick houses**. Get your **tickets online** before you go. As you walk through **Al-Turaif**, you'll see stunning **Najdi architecture** and cultural exhibits showcasing Saudi traditions.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-30.jpg)

This whole area is like a step back in time, where the air is filled with the aroma of delicious food. This is also the place where started, way back in the 18th century, you can still see the mud-brick houses. This is truly the grandparent and most certainly the heart of this city. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-31.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-33.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-35.jpg)

I loved the touch of sophisticated modernity as we walked through the narrow streets of Al-Turaif, spotting ornate wooden doors called “Najdi”. On the inside, you will find exhibits of the culture of Saudi Arabia. Fascinating look into its past.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-37.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-2.jpg)

It is also worth staying until evening here to catch the sun go down. 

**Pro tip:** Stay until **sunset**—the lighting is magical.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-43.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-45.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-47.jpg)

 A modern dining space next to Diriyah, with excellent options for all diets. For vegetarians, **Takya** is a must-try—authentic Saudi flavors with a twist. We absolutely loved it.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-49.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-51.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-53.jpg)

 For a panoramic city view, head to **Kingdom Tower’s Sky Bridge**. This is a **ticketed attraction** and resembles Dubai’s Frame, but with a more low-key vibe. **Visit at night** for the best experience. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-55.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-riyadh-57.jpg)

Yes, it’s safe. Follow general precautions, avoid remote areas after dark, and dress modestly.

 **Careem** for transport, **Google Translate**, and **Google Maps** offline version.

It depends—**meals and rides are affordable**, but hotels and attractions can be pricey if not booked in advance.

So that’s our **weekend Riyadh travel guide** to kickstart the year. We just dipped our toes into what this **enchanting Saudi capital** offers. From ancient forts to buzzing dining scenes and cultural revival, Riyadh is a city in transition—and it’s doing a decent job!

Thanks for reading! Leave your questions in the comments.

**Lots of love,**

** Anki**

Final Thoughts

We flew from Dubai to Riyadh with Flyadeal. The flight is basic but affordable—they often run amazing deals. For visas, we applied online using our GCC residency. You can also apply if you hold a valid EU, UK, or US visa. Immigration was seamless—only 5 minutes and no questions asked.

Unlike the UAE or Qatar, English is not widely spoken in Saudi Arabia. Arabic is the main language, though in touristy spots, you’ll find some English speakers. Google Translate helped when needed.

The ideal time to visit Saudi Arabia is November to March. Cooler weather makes it perfect for exploring cultural sites and open-air spaces.

Always best to carry some Saudi Riyals (SAR), especially for small purchases in souks or local markets.

.

Even during winter, the sun is intense. Carry a high-SPF sunscreen—a must for any Middle East destination.

Riyadh is one of the largest cities in the region. You could easily spend 5–7 days exploring various districts, historical forts, shopping avenues, and local restaurants.

We stayed at the Radisson Blu Riyadh—centrally located and perfect for a short trip. The breakfast buffet was excellent and they had great vegetarian options for à la carte dining.

Driving in Riyadh is intense! While renting a car is an option, we preferred using Careem taxis—affordable and convenient. Unless you’re comfortable with aggressive city traffic, ride-hailing is the way to go.

Although I didn’t go solo this time, Riyadh is safe for solo travelers, including women. Just dress conservatively, but you don’t need to wear a hijab unless visiting a mosque. Always use common sense and stay in public areas.

Things to Do in Seychelles: Travel Guide with Itinerary, Visa, Cost & Best Beaches
June 2021 Welcome to Seychelles - a tropical haven that gave us a reprieve from the ordinary and shoved us straight into the laps of...

June 2021 

Welcome to Seychelles - a tropical haven that gave us a reprieve from the ordinary and shoved us straight into the laps of blissful nature.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-1.jpg)

You know how some people like to spend their beach holiday not moving a muscle? Yeah, we are definitely not those people. In the short time we spent in Seychelles, we did everything from hiking to kayaking to chasing waterfalls — truly some of the best **things to do in Seychelles**


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-cover.jpg)

We woke up in the greenest of rainforests, watched swaying palm trees, walked on whitest of white sands and listened to the rhythmic sounds of waves crashing onto the iconic boulders of Seychelles. There is so much more to this country than it's supremely instagrammable **Seychelles beaches**.

This is my guide to some of the more off beat spots we discovered during our short getaway.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-3.jpg)


### **TABLE OF CONTENTS**

- [FAQ](#viewer-e443f)
[FAQ](#viewer-e443f)

- [Island Hopping](#viewer-9c31p) 
[Island Hopping](#viewer-9c31p) 

- [Hiring a car](#viewer-b8kuj) 
[Hiring a car](#viewer-b8kuj) 

- [Budget](#viewer-dq3k5) 
[Budget](#viewer-dq3k5) 

- [Vegetarian + Vegan Food](#viewer-aj9k6) 
[Vegetarian + Vegan Food](#viewer-aj9k6) 

- [Where We Stayed](#viewer-a8aac) 
[Where We Stayed](#viewer-a8aac)

[Highlights](#viewer-ajhel)

- [Final Thoughts](#viewer-92bk9)
[Final Thoughts](#viewer-92bk9)


### **FLIGHTS + VISA**

We flew direct with Emirates. NO visa required for Indian passport holders (I believe this is the case for most countries). The immigration process was quick and simple though the line was a little long. Don't forget to have your return ticket and confirmed hotel reservation on hand! For those searching, yes, there is a **Seychelles visa for Indians** option with visa-on-arrival.

On the way back, we used the airport lounge and it was a great place to spend a few hours far away from the airport crowds.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-4.jpg)


### **BEST TIME TO VISIT THINGS TO DO SEYCHELLES**

April, May, June and October, November is best due to the slightly cooler weather. This is considered the **Seychelles best time to visit** for both couples and families.

It is a French speaking country but everyone is helpful and fluent in English. 

Travel Insurance is highly recommended. USD accepted at all resorts. Carry local currency for small purchases.

No trip to Seychelles is complete without island hops to most of the most popular islands - Praslin and **La Digue island**. [Cat Cocos](https://catcocos.com/) is your friend. You can go to their website, check the schedules, pay and reserve your tickets.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-5.jpg)

The most expensive option is to do day tours from Mahe to the islands - though these tours are easily EUR 180 pp all [inclusive.](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g298572-d2037953-Reviews-Creole_Travel_Services-Victoria_Mahe_Island.html)

We also decided to stay only in Mahe, to save time and because of a new rule that was put in place at the start of June. Due to increasing cases on the main island, guests from Mahe are not allowed to stay in Praslin or La Digue.

If you plan to leave your resort and the salty margaritas behind, you have to rent a car to get around Mahe. After research, we decided to go with [Doffay Rental Car](https://www.tripadvisor.com/Attraction_Review-g297798-d20381380-Reviews-Doffay_Car_Rental-Mahe_Island.html). You can easily book online. This kind of **Seychelles car rental** makes exploring the island so much easier.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-6.jpg)

The country felt extremely safe. Should be no a problem to go solo here.

Traveling with a tight budget to Seychelles? Sounds ironic.. but it can be done.

Few small tips to reduce your **Seychelles trip cost**:

- Stay in self catered apartments
Stay in self catered apartments

- Take the public transport (SPTC)
Take the public transport (SPTC)

- Skip the tours - explore all the islands on your own
Skip the tours - explore all the islands on your own

- Find fun free activities like hiking, beach hopping or cuddling turtles
Find fun free activities like hiking, beach hopping or cuddling turtles

It makes most sense to go for half board at such resorts. The breakfast buffet was massive. The chef made us fresh green curry, pasta and pizza every night. There were even a few good vegetarian restaurants outside.

**WHERE WE STAYED**

We decided to stay at Kempinski Seychelles. This property is located on Baie Lazare beach. With limited time, we wanted relaxation and a taste of [Seychelles luxury resorts](https://www.booking.com/hotel/sc/kempinski-seychelles-resort.en-gb.html?dist=0;keep_landing=1;sb_price_type=total;sid=143bead0b8b6ff85e6a9f83cf76bdf4e;type=total&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-d14c0abb9ab14e95b0c0ed288-437527_cjevent-5192746793e211f083c1009e0a82b824&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287)[.](https://www.booking.com/hotel/sc/kempinski-seychelles-resort.en-gb.html?dist=0;keep_landing=1;sb_price_type=total;sid=143bead0b8b6ff85e6a9f83cf76bdf4e;type=total&aid=818288&label=affnetcj-11916287_pub-5108952_site-100846482_pname-Go+Travel+Un+Limited_clkid-d14c0abb9ab14e95b0c0ed288-437527_cjevent-5192746793e211f083c1009e0a82b824&utm_source=affnetcj&utm_medium=bannerindex&utm_campaign=xx&utm_term=index-11916287)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-7.jpg)

If your only plan is to laze around at this lush elegant resort, you need not worry. The property also offer a huge variety of water activities and even a giant aldabra corner where you can find these gentle giants resting in the afternoon heat. The staff, service, covid protocols and food were all top of the line. 

The room we stayed was basic but had all the amenities had such an amazing view of the sunrise, swaying palm trees and cooing birds every morning. The staff were beyond courteous and made our stay memorable. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-8.jpg)

Drive from airport to Kempinski, sea kayaking, glass kayak, hiking the resort peak.

Kayaking in Seychelles is a fantastic way to get close to the iconic coves.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-9.jpg)

As soon as we arrived, the staff were ready for us with a lemon iced tea. We completed the check-in formalities and headed for breakfast with heavenly views of the pristine beach. Stomachs full and mind at peace, we decided to explore the property. Every corner was a dream. It was truly time to swap reality for luxury relaxation.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-10.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-12.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-14.jpg)

After a power nap, we met the friendly residents and kayak! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-16.jpg)

If you are an adventure seeker, then there are so many exciting water activities available on the property. We went with Kayaking. Though we waited until late in the afternoon for the waves to die down a little, the sea was still choppy. Kayaking in Seychelles is a fantastic way to get close to the iconic coves; plus it is an eco-friendly activity. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-17.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-19.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-21.jpg)

There are many places you can kayak on the island - from Praslin to La Digue. We felt it was easiest to do it on the property. Definitely rent the glass kayak. The water is so pristine, you cannot miss the corals swooshing to the high tides. It was such a refreshing experience and a fabulous way to socially distance in open spaces. We got explore the beautiful mangroves, feel the salty air, and felt one with nature. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-23.jpg)

For sunset, we went to hike the small peak right inside property. It is a gentle 30-40 minute trail up that opens up to panoramic views of the tropical grounds that surround the property. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-24.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-25.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-27.jpg)

Once we reached the top, we had the whole place to ourselves.. just the peaceful seclusion we crave for on every vacation. 

You can even choose to do a sunset picnic or a sunrise yoga on the hill top here.This is one of the unique features of Kempinski and should not be missed.

An early start today - welcome to the magical world of **La Digue island**.

Meeting the Giant Aldabra Tortoise was unforgettable. After, we visited Anse Source d'Argent and Praslin’s Vallee de Mai with the famous coco de mer nut.

Next up - Anse Lazio, one of the most beautiful **Seychelles beaches** in the world.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-29.jpg)

Of course, the highlight is also meeting the Giant Aldabra Tortoise of Seychelles. I first set my eyes on giant tortoise in [Zanzibar](/post/plan-your-five-day-escape-to-the-land-of-spices-jambo-zanzibar) but they aren’t the same kind. These gentle giants are endemic to the Aldabra Atoll in Seychelles (they seemed bigger and way more more friendlier)! Here you will find the largest population of tortoise (yes, even more than Galapagos!)- making it one of the largest ecosystems on our planet dominated by reptiles. This has been my biggest discovery about myself in the past few months of traveling - I totally live for these little chances to interact with the amazing creatures of the world. Btw, if you stroke one of these tortoises on the neck they just love it. They will stand up on their front legs, stretch their necks and ask for more. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-30.jpg)

You will also find other enchanting species like the spider - such a cool experience to hold this guy; just when you think he is going to drop to the ground you will watch them spin a web in microseconds. WOWZA! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-31.jpg)

After walking around for a bit we finally stumbled onto the calling card of Seychelles. Presenting the most photographed beach in the world - **Anse Source d'Argent**. Does it live up to the hype? You will have to decide for yourself. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-32.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-34.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-36.jpg)

It was a tad touristy for me (though on hindsight it turned out to be far less crowded than I expected it to be)! It was big enough to lose the crowds in between these gigantic boulders.  


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-38.jpg)

What I liked most was the pace of life on this almost motor-free island - life moves at the pace of a tortoise. That alone warrants a visit. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-39.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-41.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-43.jpg)

After a few hours in La Digue, we got on to the ferry to make our way to Praslin. The water was way calmer this time around but boy was it HOT in the afternoon! Our first stop in Praslin was the **Vallee de mai **(Valley of May). This must visit destination and a UNESCO world heritage site allows you walk through a mystical palm forest, which is also home to the iconic emblems of Seychelles - the coco de mer nut and one of the rarest flora on the planet. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-45.jpg)

The paths inside the park are very well marked and there is no way you can get lost. Some of the trees insider there are actually over 200 years old. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-46.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-48.jpg)

No ifs, no buts - just the coco de mer nut that’s shaped like a butt. This rare giant nut is endemic to the island of Seychelles - a treasured cultural and botanical icon of the Seychelles. It is known to produce the largest and heaviest seeds in the world. It has an endangered status because this plant has separate male and female parts that can take almost 50 years to reach maturity. It is often sold for thousands of dollars - even illegally. Conservationists have turned to the islanders to help secure the palm’s future. If you happen to visit the island of Praslin, a trip to Vallee De Mai is a must, you can find out all about this nuts history straight from the locals! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-50.jpg)

Next up - the best beach in Seychelles (in my opinion) - **Anse Lazio**. The winding drive up to the beach is well worth the effort - this beach makes it to the top 10 beaches in the world. It is also often compared to the more instafamous beach in La Digue because of it's massive boulders bordered by coconut palm trees that dominate both landscapes, but that is where the comparison ends. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-51.jpg)

This beach was much bigger that I expected. There was also so much to do here from snorkeling to just laying on the beach working on that tan. You could easily lose the crowds too. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-52.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-54.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-56.jpg)

It was easily one of the prettiest beaches we have been to. Try to go after mid day for better pictures but the sea will be more rough. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-58.jpg)

**HIKING THE DAN GALLAS TRAIL**

One of the best hikes in Seychelles. Incredible views, granite boulders, and Robinson-Crusoe style isolation.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-59.jpg)

All of the popular hiking trail can be found inside the Morne National Park, a protected National Park since 1979. You will find endemic plants, insects and birds inside the forest.. lots of sunshine, pure air and a dense forest. The perfect recipe for a memorable natural experience. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-60.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-62.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-64.jpg)

This trail is right for you if you're looking for some adventurous trekking. As you climb up the trek, you are greeted by thick woods and granite boulders. There are steps and ladders to help you reach the summit. At the summit, you can have a spectacular birds' eye view of Victoria, Beau Vallon and the Silhouette Island on the other. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-66.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-68.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-70.jpg)

Easily makes it one of the most breathtaking hiking trails in Seychelles.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-72.jpg)

The trail itself is not very strenuous if you are an experienced BUT the path is tricky and dangerous - there were times when there was no visible trail leading up. Be mindful and tread carefully. The last 45 minutes was just climbing over massive boulders. This is also not a circular path - you take the same route back down, though the descent was tougher with slippery sand. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-73.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-75.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-77.jpg)

One of the best part of these hikes was not having hordes of tourists behind us and in fact we were the only ones on the trail,  which gave us a real feeling of Robinson-Crusoe style isolation - 

which is rare on beaches. This is not a very difficult hike but I still recommend you start early so you can get back down by mid day. Pack plenty of water, snacks and sunscreen and set off for the adventure. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-79.jpg)

We got back to the room for some much needed down time. For sunset, we drove down just a few miles from our resort to the **Police Bay beach. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-80.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-82.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-84.jpg)

The drive was exciting and took a bit longer than expected - the roads winding in and out of dense forests. It’s not really a great beach for swimming but it’s wild and beautiful all the same. The best part of course is that you will most likely have it to yourself. 

SOLITUDE. REST. RELAX. RESET. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-86.jpg)

Exploring mangroves, hidden waterfalls and the stunning Grand Anse beach, followed by the beauty of Anse Royale.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-87.jpg)

First stop the **Port Launay Mangrove Forest **- this mangrove is one of the best and last remaining mangrove forests on the island. This area is right next to the Constance Ephelia area. The mangrove forests grow to a certain size and then the lodge teams plant them in the mud, in the hope that these areas would eventually be populated with more trees. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-88.jpg)

Next stop - an extremely difficult to find **Grand Anse waterfall** in the middle of nowhere. We had to ask people about the location; we even had a local actually drive us to the spot. We finally parked our car in front of a house and took the 25 minute hike up through the lush jungle.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-89.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-91.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-93.jpg)

Note that the path is marked as dangerous and rightly so - there was basically no trail. IT was just going down a pile of rocks and climbing up. If you don't have firm footing or good shoes, I DON'T RECOMMEND THIS HIKE.  It was still a fabulous getaway in the middle of a warm summer day. Worth it, if you can find your way there.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-95.jpg)

After lunch on the way, we stopped by the gorgeous **Grand Anse beach on West Mahe**. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-96.jpg)

We later discovered that this is one of the longest beaches on the Mahe western coast. This sprawling bay is incredibly stunning, with clean sand and a pristine turquoise ocean. The backdrop is endless mountainous landscape, perfectly contrasting the flat beach below.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-97.jpg)

Our last stop for the day was my favorite - behold the beauty of **Anse Royale. **


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-98.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-100.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-102.jpg)

I was just stunned by the changing colors, the majestic boulders, the seaweed and just everything. What could be more jaw dropping than witnessing this and having it all to yourself? 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-104.jpg)

The morning was spent at Baie Lazare beach, part of our **Seychelles itinerary**.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-105.jpg)

The morning was spent at the Baie Lazare beach. What I loved about this beach was that it felt like a more private beach - it was quiet and used only by the people in the hotel - though, by law, no beaches are allowed to be private in Seychelles! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-106.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-108.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-110.jpg)

Seychelles was never a bucket list for me but turned into an unforgettable island escape. It’s expensive, but whether you’re planning a **Seychelles honeymoon**, calculating your **Seychelles trip cost**, or exploring solo, you’ll find magic


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-112.jpg)

My biggest tip is for you to decide where you wish to stay and how much you want to spend. Seychelles is expense, no two ways about it. Be extra careful when you pick the resort and island you want to stay in ahead of time. While Mahe had a plenty of activities, Praslin had the best beaches. Even if Praslin was open for staying, I would not have stayed in Praslin...  but that is just me! 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-113.jpg)

Rest assured though, it is still a remarkable place to relax and unwind. From Mahe through Praslin to La Digue, you cannot go wrong with laying on empty beaches and hiking through jungle forests. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/things-to-do-in-seychelles-114.jpg)

From Mahe through Praslin to La Digue, you cannot go wrong with relaxing on **Seychelles beaches** and enjoying the islands at your own pace.

Cheers, Anki

June 2021

Welcome to Seychelles - a tropical haven that gave us a reprieve from the ordinary and shoved us straight into the laps of blissful nature.

You know how some people like to spend their beach holiday not moving a muscle? Yeah, we are definitely not those people. In the short time we spent in Seychelles, we did everything from hiking to kayaking to chasing waterfalls — truly some of the best things to do in Seychelles

We woke up in the greenest of rainforests, watched swaying palm trees, walked on whitest of white sands and listened to the rhythmic sounds of waves crashing onto the iconic boulders of Seychelles. There is so much more to this country than it’s supremely instagrammable Seychelles beaches.

This is my guide to some of the more off beat spots we discovered during our short getaway.

TABLE OF CONTENTS

  • FAQ FAQ

  • Island Hopping Island Hopping

  • Hiring a car Hiring a car

  • Budget Budget

  • Vegetarian + Vegan Food Vegetarian + Vegan Food

  • Where We Stayed Where We Stayed

  • Highlights Highlights

  • Final Thoughts Final Thoughts

FLIGHTS + VISA

We flew direct with Emirates. NO visa required for Indian passport holders (I believe this is the case for most countries). The immigration process was quick and simple though the line was a little long. Don’t forget to have your return ticket and confirmed hotel reservation on hand! For those searching, yes, there is a Seychelles visa for Indians option with visa-on-arrival.

On the way back, we used the airport lounge and it was a great place to spend a few hours far away from the airport crowds.

BEST TIME TO VISIT THINGS TO DO SEYCHELLES

April, May, June and October, November is best due to the slightly cooler weather. This is considered the Seychelles best time to visit for both couples and families.

It is a French speaking country but everyone is helpful and fluent in English.

Travel Insurance is highly recommended. USD accepted at all resorts. Carry local currency for small purchases.

No trip to Seychelles is complete without island hops to most of the most popular islands - Praslin and La Digue island. Cat Cocos is your friend. You can go to their website, check the schedules, pay and reserve your tickets.

The most expensive option is to do day tours from Mahe to the islands - though these tours are easily EUR 180 pp all inclusive.

We also decided to stay only in Mahe, to save time and because of a new rule that was put in place at the start of June. Due to increasing cases on the main island, guests from Mahe are not allowed to stay in Praslin or La Digue.

If you plan to leave your resort and the salty margaritas behind, you have to rent a car to get around Mahe. After research, we decided to go with Doffay Rental Car. You can easily book online. This kind of Seychelles car rental makes exploring the island so much easier.

The country felt extremely safe. Should be no a problem to go solo here.

Traveling with a tight budget to Seychelles? Sounds ironic.. but it can be done.

Few small tips to reduce your Seychelles trip cost:

  • Stay in self catered apartments Stay in self catered apartments

  • Take the public transport (SPTC) Take the public transport (SPTC)

  • Skip the tours - explore all the islands on your own Skip the tours - explore all the islands on your own

  • Find fun free activities like hiking, beach hopping or cuddling turtles Find fun free activities like hiking, beach hopping or cuddling turtles

It makes most sense to go for half board at such resorts. The breakfast buffet was massive. The chef made us fresh green curry, pasta and pizza every night. There were even a few good vegetarian restaurants outside.

WHERE WE STAYED

We decided to stay at Kempinski Seychelles. This property is located on Baie Lazare beach. With limited time, we wanted relaxation and a taste of Seychelles luxury resorts.

If your only plan is to laze around at this lush elegant resort, you need not worry. The property also offer a huge variety of water activities and even a giant aldabra corner where you can find these gentle giants resting in the afternoon heat. The staff, service, covid protocols and food were all top of the line.

The room we stayed was basic but had all the amenities had such an amazing view of the sunrise, swaying palm trees and cooing birds every morning. The staff were beyond courteous and made our stay memorable.

Drive from airport to Kempinski, sea kayaking, glass kayak, hiking the resort peak.

Kayaking in Seychelles is a fantastic way to get close to the iconic coves.

As soon as we arrived, the staff were ready for us with a lemon iced tea. We completed the check-in formalities and headed for breakfast with heavenly views of the pristine beach. Stomachs full and mind at peace, we decided to explore the property. Every corner was a dream. It was truly time to swap reality for luxury relaxation.

After a power nap, we met the friendly residents and kayak!

If you are an adventure seeker, then there are so many exciting water activities available on the property. We went with Kayaking. Though we waited until late in the afternoon for the waves to die down a little, the sea was still choppy. Kayaking in Seychelles is a fantastic way to get close to the iconic coves; plus it is an eco-friendly activity.

There are many places you can kayak on the island - from Praslin to La Digue. We felt it was easiest to do it on the property. Definitely rent the glass kayak. The water is so pristine, you cannot miss the corals swooshing to the high tides. It was such a refreshing experience and a fabulous way to socially distance in open spaces. We got explore the beautiful mangroves, feel the salty air, and felt one with nature.

For sunset, we went to hike the small peak right inside property. It is a gentle 30-40 minute trail up that opens up to panoramic views of the tropical grounds that surround the property.

Once we reached the top, we had the whole place to ourselves.. just the peaceful seclusion we crave for on every vacation.

You can even choose to do a sunset picnic or a sunrise yoga on the hill top here.This is one of the unique features of Kempinski and should not be missed.

An early start today - welcome to the magical world of La Digue island.

Meeting the Giant Aldabra Tortoise was unforgettable. After, we visited Anse Source d’Argent and Praslin’s Vallee de Mai with the famous coco de mer nut.

Next up - Anse Lazio, one of the most beautiful Seychelles beaches in the world.

Of course, the highlight is also meeting the Giant Aldabra Tortoise of Seychelles. I first set my eyes on giant tortoise in Zanzibar but they aren’t the same kind. These gentle giants are endemic to the Aldabra Atoll in Seychelles (they seemed bigger and way more more friendlier)! Here you will find the largest population of tortoise (yes, even more than Galapagos!)- making it one of the largest ecosystems on our planet dominated by reptiles. This has been my biggest discovery about myself in the past few months of traveling - I totally live for these little chances to interact with the amazing creatures of the world. Btw, if you stroke one of these tortoises on the neck they just love it. They will stand up on their front legs, stretch their necks and ask for more.

You will also find other enchanting species like the spider - such a cool experience to hold this guy; just when you think he is going to drop to the ground you will watch them spin a web in microseconds. WOWZA!

After walking around for a bit we finally stumbled onto the calling card of Seychelles. Presenting the most photographed beach in the world - Anse Source d’Argent. Does it live up to the hype? You will have to decide for yourself.

It was a tad touristy for me (though on hindsight it turned out to be far less crowded than I expected it to be)! It was big enough to lose the crowds in between these gigantic boulders.

What I liked most was the pace of life on this almost motor-free island - life moves at the pace of a tortoise. That alone warrants a visit.

After a few hours in La Digue, we got on to the ferry to make our way to Praslin. The water was way calmer this time around but boy was it HOT in the afternoon! Our first stop in Praslin was the **Vallee de mai **(Valley of May). This must visit destination and a UNESCO world heritage site allows you walk through a mystical palm forest, which is also home to the iconic emblems of Seychelles - the coco de mer nut and one of the rarest flora on the planet.

The paths inside the park are very well marked and there is no way you can get lost. Some of the trees insider there are actually over 200 years old.

No ifs, no buts - just the coco de mer nut that’s shaped like a butt. This rare giant nut is endemic to the island of Seychelles - a treasured cultural and botanical icon of the Seychelles. It is known to produce the largest and heaviest seeds in the world. It has an endangered status because this plant has separate male and female parts that can take almost 50 years to reach maturity. It is often sold for thousands of dollars - even illegally. Conservationists have turned to the islanders to help secure the palm’s future. If you happen to visit the island of Praslin, a trip to Vallee De Mai is a must, you can find out all about this nuts history straight from the locals!

Next up - the best beach in Seychelles (in my opinion) - Anse Lazio. The winding drive up to the beach is well worth the effort - this beach makes it to the top 10 beaches in the world. It is also often compared to the more instafamous beach in La Digue because of it’s massive boulders bordered by coconut palm trees that dominate both landscapes, but that is where the comparison ends.

This beach was much bigger that I expected. There was also so much to do here from snorkeling to just laying on the beach working on that tan. You could easily lose the crowds too.

It was easily one of the prettiest beaches we have been to. Try to go after mid day for better pictures but the sea will be more rough.

HIKING THE DAN GALLAS TRAIL

One of the best hikes in Seychelles. Incredible views, granite boulders, and Robinson-Crusoe style isolation.

All of the popular hiking trail can be found inside the Morne National Park, a protected National Park since 1979. You will find endemic plants, insects and birds inside the forest.. lots of sunshine, pure air and a dense forest. The perfect recipe for a memorable natural experience.

This trail is right for you if you’re looking for some adventurous trekking. As you climb up the trek, you are greeted by thick woods and granite boulders. There are steps and ladders to help you reach the summit. At the summit, you can have a spectacular birds’ eye view of Victoria, Beau Vallon and the Silhouette Island on the other.

Easily makes it one of the most breathtaking hiking trails in Seychelles.

The trail itself is not very strenuous if you are an experienced BUT the path is tricky and dangerous - there were times when there was no visible trail leading up. Be mindful and tread carefully. The last 45 minutes was just climbing over massive boulders. This is also not a circular path - you take the same route back down, though the descent was tougher with slippery sand.

One of the best part of these hikes was not having hordes of tourists behind us and in fact we were the only ones on the trail, which gave us a real feeling of Robinson-Crusoe style isolation -

which is rare on beaches. This is not a very difficult hike but I still recommend you start early so you can get back down by mid day. Pack plenty of water, snacks and sunscreen and set off for the adventure.

We got back to the room for some much needed down time. For sunset, we drove down just a few miles from our resort to the **Police Bay beach. **

The drive was exciting and took a bit longer than expected - the roads winding in and out of dense forests. It’s not really a great beach for swimming but it’s wild and beautiful all the same. The best part of course is that you will most likely have it to yourself.

SOLITUDE. REST. RELAX. RESET.

Exploring mangroves, hidden waterfalls and the stunning Grand Anse beach, followed by the beauty of Anse Royale.

First stop the **Port Launay Mangrove Forest **- this mangrove is one of the best and last remaining mangrove forests on the island. This area is right next to the Constance Ephelia area. The mangrove forests grow to a certain size and then the lodge teams plant them in the mud, in the hope that these areas would eventually be populated with more trees.

Next stop - an extremely difficult to find Grand Anse waterfall in the middle of nowhere. We had to ask people about the location; we even had a local actually drive us to the spot. We finally parked our car in front of a house and took the 25 minute hike up through the lush jungle.

Note that the path is marked as dangerous and rightly so - there was basically no trail. IT was just going down a pile of rocks and climbing up. If you don’t have firm footing or good shoes, I DON’T RECOMMEND THIS HIKE. It was still a fabulous getaway in the middle of a warm summer day. Worth it, if you can find your way there.

After lunch on the way, we stopped by the gorgeous Grand Anse beach on West Mahe.

We later discovered that this is one of the longest beaches on the Mahe western coast. This sprawling bay is incredibly stunning, with clean sand and a pristine turquoise ocean. The backdrop is endless mountainous landscape, perfectly contrasting the flat beach below.

Our last stop for the day was my favorite - behold the beauty of **Anse Royale. **

I was just stunned by the changing colors, the majestic boulders, the seaweed and just everything. What could be more jaw dropping than witnessing this and having it all to yourself?

The morning was spent at Baie Lazare beach, part of our Seychelles itinerary.

The morning was spent at the Baie Lazare beach. What I loved about this beach was that it felt like a more private beach - it was quiet and used only by the people in the hotel - though, by law, no beaches are allowed to be private in Seychelles!

Seychelles was never a bucket list for me but turned into an unforgettable island escape. It’s expensive, but whether you’re planning a Seychelles honeymoon, calculating your Seychelles trip cost, or exploring solo, you’ll find magic

My biggest tip is for you to decide where you wish to stay and how much you want to spend. Seychelles is expense, no two ways about it. Be extra careful when you pick the resort and island you want to stay in ahead of time. While Mahe had a plenty of activities, Praslin had the best beaches. Even if Praslin was open for staying, I would not have stayed in Praslin… but that is just me!

Rest assured though, it is still a remarkable place to relax and unwind. From Mahe through Praslin to La Digue, you cannot go wrong with laying on empty beaches and hiking through jungle forests.

From Mahe through Praslin to La Digue, you cannot go wrong with relaxing on Seychelles beaches and enjoying the islands at your own pace.

Cheers, Anki

Here are TEN smart travel tips for beginners that will help you get started on your adventure. 


Here are TEN smart travel tips for beginners that will help you get started on your adventure.

While spontaneity sounds fun, good planning is your best travel

companion—especially for beginners. Instead of rigidly planning every hour, focus

on key essentials:

Research visa requirements early to avoid airport surprises.

Check if your destination requires vaccinations or health documents.

Use apps like Google Maps offline—download maps in advance in case you

lose connectivity. Don’t overpack your itinerary. Leave space for unexpected discoveries.

Pro Tip: If visiting multiple cities or countries, look into local holidays or events

that could affect transportation or accommodation availability[.](https://docs.google.com/document/u/0/d/170NR6lPO4_0JC79az68woqfcJdylO9egMYY-oUPdV7M/edit)

The phrase “Pack light” is overused because it’s true. But how do you actually do

it?cThe 3-2-1 rule. Pack 3 tops, 2 bottoms, and 1 pair of versatile shoes for a

week-long trip.

Layers over bulk. Lightweight jackets are better than bulky coats for varying

climates.

Multipurpose items. A scarf can be a blanket on the plane, a beach towel, or

even a makeshift pillow.

Personal Experience: Once, while backpacking through Southeast Asia, I realized

I’d packed five unnecessary shirts but forgot a rain poncho—which I desperately

needed in the monsoon season. Lesson learned: pack for the climate, not your

Instagram feed.

Scan your passport, visa, and travel insurance. Save them to Google Drive or

another cloud service.

Carry printed copies separately from the originals.

Use a money belt or hidden pouch to keep passports safe, especially in

crowded areas.

What if you lose your passport?

Contact your country’s[ embassy](https://www.embassypages.com/) immediately. File a report at the nearest police station—this will help with reissuing documents.

Beyond the Headlines: Exploring Afghanistan's Untapped Beauty
An independent, free-spirited, atheist traveling SOLO as a woman of color in a TBAN-ruled Afghanistan. Do I DARE? Yes, I do. A Frame of...

An independent, free-spirited, atheist traveling SOLO as a woman of color in a TBAN-ruled Afghanistan. Do I DARE? Yes, I do. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-1.jpg)

There is a common misconception that travelers like me who go to such countries are crazy fearless extremists. On the contrary, these are the trips I spend months preparing for - mentally and emotionally. I do a thorough study of the country: where are the danger zones, where are relatively “safe”, when I cannot go, and when there is a relative lull in the country and I CAN possibly take a chance. I am always very careful choosing the guides who will accompany me on my solo trips and with whom, in fact, I trust my life. On this trip, they risked life and limb for me, defying the TBAN on more than a few occasions. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-2.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-4.jpg)

I won’t be telling you can waltz into the country without preparation, or that everything distributed by the media is a legend. This time, some of it is true. When you’re here, on the inside, everything feels different - yes, there are a bunch of people with gu** but there are also real people, living a routine life, and they learn about all the happenings, like the rest of the world, from the news. They are not dodging bullets every second of the day, not anymore. For the first time in decades it’s almost “safe” to visit (by Afghan standards), you can go anywhere in the country, IF you have all your permits + a local guide.


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-6.jpg)

Let me also tell you that this country is not for everyone. To decide to go here I didn’t necessarily have to be brave, BUT I needed to forget everything I thought I knew about the place, strongly believe in strangers and MYSELF. The injustice to women is clearly visible, without reducing any of that, there is also another side to the country, which is what I am showing. 

The good: the history, culture, landscapes. The people. How much they go through. How different their lives are. How incredibly kind they are. It’s a completely different world out here. It’s full of untapped beauty, if you look only in that direction. 

The not-so-good: the omnipresent TBAN. When I’m traveling like this, I chose to be a traveler that just observes and learns. I’m not an activist, I go to observe the reality, not to criticize a culture that I don’t fully grasp. 


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-7.jpg)


![Blog Image](/blog-images/travel-to-afghanistan-9.jpg)

My experience was still vastly different from that of the Afghani women. I was offered courtesies most locals are not. As a tourist, who was only passing through, barely scraping the surface in 8 days, I could only gauge so much but I am determined to share an unbiased opinion, and show the reality as I experieced it. 

Day 1 Land in Tajikistan early in the morning. Cross over to Afghanistan through the Shirkhan Bandar border. Stay in old town of Kunduz. 

Day 2 Drive from Kunduz to Mazare-Sharif & Balkh. Stay in Mazare Sharif. 

Day 3 Drive from Balkh to Samangan. Stay in Samangan. 

Day 4 Travel from Samangan via Panjshir Valley to Bamiyan. Arrive late night. Stay in Bamiyan 

Day 5 Bamiyan. Stay in Bamiyan. 

Day 6 Band-E-Amir Lakes. Stay in Bamiyan. 

Day 7 & 8 - Kabul. Fly home from Kabul.
